question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the annual Joint Forum report, as outlined in the Partnership Agreement with the Community and Voluntary Sector, will be presented to the Assembly.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action he can take to ensure the Education Authority is delivering a sustainable Educational Psychology service, given that the Ards and North Down area has not had service provision since November 2025.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether all grant-maintained integrated schools receive an offer to be included in the same insurance arrangements as provided for all controlled schools, as required under section 9 (2) (c) of the Integrated Education Act (NI) 2022.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will a copy of his Department's Fair Funding Principles that underpin any grant funding arrangements with Voluntary and Community Groups funded directly by his Department, or through any arm's-length bodies that are within the responsibility of his Department.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on when she will introduce legislation to ban pavement parking in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any arrangements in place to support deafblind people to apply for, attend, and participate in interviews, as part of the Personal Independence Payment process.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any access to employment support available for deafblind people who wish to apply for jobs and participate in job interviews; and (ii) any organisations in receipt of funding from his Department to deliver this specific support.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people, issued with health service hearing aids, who are on a waiting list for audiology reassessment to assess hearing and update hearing aid equipment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any proactive national programme is in place, or is planned, to examine the sensory health and well-being of people aged 50 and over, including hearing, sight and cognitive assessments.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social housing developments which have been refused planning permission due to opposition from NI Water, in (i) 2024-2025; and (ii) 2025-2026, broken down by council area.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will meet with the Minister for Infrastructure and her officials to discuss constraints on town centre regeneration arising from NI Water's designation of areas, such as Newtownards town centre, as a closed capture area, resulting in planning applications to bring derelict buildings back into use being refused because NI Water will not to connect those buildings to the waste water and sewage network.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the Fair Funding Principles adopted by his Department to ensure Voluntary and Community Sector organisations are provided with grant funding in a timely manner that does not require recipients to take out a bank loan, or use reserves outside their published Reserve Policy.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on the process to recruit a new CEO of the Housing Executive; (ii) when he expects the arm's-length body to provide him with a list of candidates to consider; and (iii) whether he will produce the criteria he will be using to select a candidate.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of identified plots of public land which have been identified for potential use for social housing located in areas where NI Water has designated a closed capture area.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will bring forward legislation on the revised Decent Homes Standard before the end of this mandate.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any progress made towards implementing a revised Decent Homes Standard, given that the recent consultation closed on 4 March 2026.

To ask the Minister for Communities, following a recent meeting of his officials and the local community, for an update on any plans to reopen Kirkistown Castle, Cloughey, to allow public access to this heritage facility that has been closed since Covid.

To ask the Minister for Communities, following the recent introduction of the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme, whether he will (i) review; and (ii) update (a) the Social Fund rules to include a category under Relationship with person who has died for a parent of a baby miscarried before 24 weeks of pregnancy; and (b) the Child Funeral Fund to provide a one-off lump sum payment to cover the expense of a funeral of a child lost through miscarriage before the 24th week of pregnancy.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any funding streams available, through the Heritage Division, to support community associations (i) to recruit, train and support volunteers; (ii) to provide staff to open and operate heritage sites, such as Kirkistown Castle in Cloughey; and (iii) to enable public access to heritage sites that are currently closed.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will review and revise the amount awarded for a Funeral Expenses Payment, given the significant difference between the amount awarded and the current cost of funerals.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on any progress his Department has made to revitalise the Housing Executive, given there has been no approval from HM Treasury to give power to the arm's-length body to borrow against its own income; and (ii) any other options now being considered.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how (i) the changes to 26/27 Small Pockets of Deprivation (SPOD) funding processes, as notified to Neighbourhood Renewal, Areas at Risk, and SPOD voluntary and community organisations, which included confirmation programme and running costs will be claimed retrospectively following submission of claims, preferably every six months, is upholding the spirit of the Fair Funding Principles outlined in the Partnership Agreement with the Community and Voluntary Sector; and (ii) he has ensured that these organisations are not required to operate outside G
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the delivery of support for childcare providers.
answer_text: My Department is actively developing a specialised business support service for childcare providers. Officials are developing the service specification and undertaking extensive engagement with stakeholders from both within and outside government. This collaborative approach is vital to ensure the Childcare Business Support Service is tailored to reflect the sector’s diversity and to address providers’ needs effectively. Stakeholder input will help design a model that is fit for purpose and supports sustainable growth. Alongside this, childcare providers continue to benefit from ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships. These programmes fully cover training costs for occupational qualifications for staff working in the sector. Employers are eligible for incentive payments when an apprentice successfully completes all elements of a Level 2 or Level 3 framework, payable on completion at each level.   Childcare providers may also be eligible for business support from Go Succeed, the council led business support service and Invest NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the role that nature-based solutions play in Northern Ireland’s Third Climate Change Adaptation Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any meetings he has held with the Housing Executive on the Task and Finish group set up through Homeless Connect regarding the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol.
answer_text: I have not held any meetings with the Housing Executive specifically on the work of this group.The Housing Executive has informed me that while the original proposal was that a Task and Finish Group should be established, it was later agreed that it would instead establish a Homelessness Local Area Group for Belfast City Council area.The Local Area Group will bring together a range of statutory and voluntary sector representatives from across the homelessness sector and provide a long-term framework through which the Housing Executive will engage on issues relating to homelessness delivery in Belfast.What I have done is prioritise funding for all homelessness services: record levels of funding for temporary accommodation and the first ever ring-fenced allocation for homelessness prevention. This allocation is maintained in the initial 26/27 budget provided to the NIHE while departmental budgets remain to be agreed. I have invested capital funding and FTC respectively in providing new homelessness services under the New Foundations and LAMA Programmes. In the meantime I have directed officials towards detailed assurances on the NIHE’s delivery of its Out-of-Hours service both when SWEP is in place and when not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of visitors to PRONI; and (ii) a breakdown of the countries visitors have come from, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The attached statistics are taken from the annual Digest of Statistics for the Public Record Office of Northern Ireland which is validated by the Northern Ireland Statistical Research Agency. The Digest for 2024-25 is currently being collated and is not yet available, however the published figures for 2019-2024 are included below. The Digest only validates countries-of-origin for new visitors. The percentage numbers for those are attached below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he is taking, through his Department and its arm's-length bodies, to set up a multi disciplinary hub in Belfast as identified by the Homelessness Prevention Forum.
answer_text: My Department allocates approximately £4.3m annually to the Housing Executive for Community Prevention activity. This allocation provides core funding for several organisations, including the Welcome Organisation’s Support Hub.The support hub provides service users with access to basic needs including meals, clothes, showers and respite. Welcome staff provide intensive support work both on a one-to-one basis as well as via group activities. This includes housing referrals, the promotion of life skills including the setting up bank accounts and support with benefits, and clinics linking with other professional services. They also make appointments for service users with health-care professionals at their mobile health unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reason why he has not released grant funding for voluntary and community services organisations delivering homelessness prevention and support services for quarter one of the 2026-2027 financial year.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed budget I have made an initial allocation to the Housing Executive and other ALBs to allow for the continuation of vital services and provide a level of budget certainty for the first three months of 2026/27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he will provide a copy of any guidance produced by his Department or any of its arms-length bodies, that requires all exhibitions and presentations funded by public money to include fact based interpretive text; and (ii) any steps he will take ensure USA NI 250 exhibitions acknowledge that some individuals highlighted as part of that project may have been slave owners or traders, or used their position to support slavery.
answer_text: No specific guidance has been produced, and exhibitions and presentations are assessed on a case-by-case basis by the host organisation.The Department recognises the autonomy of those organisations hosting exhibitions in determining their operational and interpretive approaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide a copy of his Ministerial diary from the time he started travelling to the United States for St Patrick's Day, until his return to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 42996/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has considered the release of funding through investment of Civil Service and public sector pension funds, to finance social housing.
answer_text: My Department has direct responsibility for the Civil Service Pension Scheme. This scheme is of an ‘unfunded’ defined benefit design. This means there is no fund, or associated investments. It can be noted that the other public service schemes are of a similar unfunded design, with the exception of the Local Government Pension Scheme, which is the responsibility of the Department for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have been reimbursed for treatment costs for cataract surgery under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme (WLRS) commenced 2 June 2025.  To date my Department has approved fewer than 5 WLRS applications for cataract surgery.  We have not yet received any claims for reimbursement of cataract surgery under the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the Justice Bill (NIA Bill 07/22-27).

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he (i) will provide copies of any (a) emails; and (b) minutes of meetings held by him, or his departmental officials, in respect of Developer Contributions; and (ii) has sought an exemption from Developer Contributions for housing associations building homes using the Housing Association Grant.
answer_text: There have been no meetings held by me or my Departmental Officials in relation to the recent announcement on Developer Contributions.It is my understanding from the Department for Infrastructure’s recent announcement that Developer Contributions are to be voluntary. I have not sought an exemption from Developer Contributions for housing associations building homes using the Housing Association Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people who are on inpatient waiting lists for (a) cataract surgery; and (b) Posterior Capsule Opacification treated by Yttrium-Aluminium-Garnet laser capsulotomy, in each Heath and Social Care Trust, and (ii) the number of these surgeries or treatments that have been carried out in each quarter of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Minister for Infrastructure's statement on Developer Contributions for Wastewater Infrastructure on 18 March 2026, to detail any impact this will have on the ability of housing associations to be able to afford to build homes under the new Housing Association Grant terms.
answer_text: The Minister for Infrastructure’s statement advised that developer contributions for wastewater infrastructure would be voluntary. The Total Cost Indicators Benchmarking system which is used to establish Housing Association Grant reflects actual costs incurred by the sector. The Department will continue to monitor the costs submitted by Housing Associations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is to any policy for patients assessed through the Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service, on whether they are still required to be on an outpatient waiting list to see an Health and Social Care Trust consultant before being added to the heath service surgery or treatment inpatient waiting list.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS) was first introduced in March 2018 and was fully commissioned at a regional level from February 2019. The NI Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service (NI PEARS) provides the first point of contact for patients on the Acute Eyecare Pathway, that is, for patients who develop a sudden acute previously undiagnosed eye problem.  There is appropriate regional provision across the majority of primary care optometry practices where optometrists are required to offer the patient an appointment within 48 hours of referral.  Previous audit of the outcomes of NI PEARS provision evidenced that 80% of NIPEARS presentations are managed by the optometrist in practice without the need for onward referral. This safe and effective service frees capacity in both primary care GP practices and unscheduled ED and eye casualty settings with > 4,000 patients accessing the service each month. System Outcome Measure (SOM) data demonstrates that 99% of patients are appointed and managed within a 48hr window following triage.In the scenario where referral to secondary care eye services is indicated following an NI PEARS assessment, the primary care optometrist will complete the referral and send it to the appropriate clinical sub-speciality. On receipt of the referral, HSC Trust clinical ophthalmology teams will triage the referral and determine if an out-patient appointment is necessary; this may be provided by an HSC Trust consultant or other member of the multi-disciplinary eye care team. An out-patient appointment will not always result in a recommendation for surgical intervention but, where this is indicated, a patient will then be placed on an HSC surgical waiting list. In generating a referral, the optometrist has determined that further clinical assessment and/or intervention may be required and the provision of NI PEARS does not supersede the need for the hospital eye service to decide on the next phase of a patient’s clinical care journey. At present, neither community optometrists nor GPs directly list for ophthalmology out-patient, or, in-patient clinical care in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide a copy of the minutes from his meeting with the Disability Strategy Co-design Group on 4 December 2025, including (i) the content discussed; (ii) details of those in attendance; and (iii) the meeting location.
answer_text: Due to the need for full consideration of issues associated with UKGDPR it is not appropriate to provide a copy of the minutes from the meeting within the required timeframe. I can confirm (i) the meeting involved a comprehensive discussion on the draft Disability Strategy which I shared with members of the former Co-design Group on Friday 07 November 2025; (ii) Members of the former Disability Strategy Co-design Group attended. A list of the organisations who attended is provided below; 	Children's Law CentreOmnibus PartnershipMENCAPDisability Action NIRNIB NIRNID NIIMTAC Equality Commission NINorth-West Forum of People with DisabilitiesNorthern Ireland Human Rights Commission	Centre for Independent Living NI		and (iii) the meeting was conducted online.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the future funding for higher education institutions.
answer_text: I am committed to supporting a thriving local Higher Education sector. This is critical for students, a strong and regionally balance economy, as well as civic society. In the absence of sufficient funding from the British Government, all Departments are under significant financial pressure. In this difficult context I am focussed on maximising the impact and effectiveness of the resources available. I am currently considering how best to allocate my budget and am also acutely aware of the impact of passing cuts onto the Higher Education sector. However, there financial challenges right across my Department which spans Invest NI, colleges, tourism, the TV and film industries, and energy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work being carried out in relation to the National Association of Head Teachers' trade dispute over workload.
answer_text: Engagement has continued with NAHT officials, and the other recognised teacher unions, via the Teacher Negotiating Committee (TNC) structures. This work has been aimed at addressing excessive teacher and school leader workload.On 10 March 2026, the TNC issued a joint communication to teachers and school leaders providing an update on ongoing work and detailing areas where progress can be made to address workload in the short-term.  As engagement is ongoing to address workload through the agreed collective negotiating machinery of TNC, the Northern Ireland Teaching Council, which represents the five recognised teaching unions, has confirmed that they are not in dispute with management side.My officials are continuing to assess the recommendations from the Independent Workload Panel with a view to developing a detailed action by April 2026. This is in line with the communication that I issued to all teachers and school leaders at the start of February.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the practical changes as to how children and young people will access emotional health and wellbeing support as a result of the proposed Children’s and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.
answer_text: The Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework will deliver clear, practical improvements to how young people access support. Consultation feedback, including from more than 120 young people, highlighted inconsistent pathways, long waits and support arriving only at crisis point.A central change is the move towards a needsled, relational and traumainformed model. Young people told us that being listened to and respected mattered as much as being seen quickly. The Framework therefore prioritises personalised, contextaware support delivered by trained staff who can focus on the whole child, including family and community influences.Crucially, the Framework endorses a significant shift away from single, diagnostically led pathways for conditions such as autism or ADHD pathways that have varied considerably across Trusts. Instead, referrals will move into an inclusive, needsled neurodevelopmental pathway that responds to presenting needs rather than diagnosis as the entry point. Regionally consistent pathways will make access more predictable and reduce the postcode variation frequently raised during consultation. This will create a more consistent and equitable system across Northern Ireland that will ensure children receive help earlier, without waiting for a diagnostic process to begin. Strengthening early intervention in settings such as schools, youth services and community organisations means young people can access support sooner and from trusted adults. The Framework also promotes enhanced participation, ensuring children and young people are actively involved in decisions about their care. It places a strong emphasis on equity, with specific measures for young carers, neurodivergent children, disabled young people, careexperienced young people and others who face additional barriers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to fully staff the Regional Gender Identity Service at the Brackenburn Clinic, to reduce the length of time patients are having to wait before accessing services.
answer_text: Belfast HSC Trust are actively working on internal processes required to fully staff the Regional Gender identity service. Advertisement of the new service manager post is currently live on HSCNI recruit. The delay in progressing recruitment is due to the Trust awaiting the recent publication of Dr Cass’s Assessment of the Northern Ireland Lifespan Service prior to recruitment of posts to ensure that the proposed new service model / staffing structure aligns with recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the percentage of the operating costs for the Regional Gender Identity Service that have not been met, in each of the last five financial years.
answer_text: The Regional Gender Identity Service is delivered in Belfast HSC Trust, for the region. All of the expenditure reported by Belfast HSC Trust  in relation to the Gender Identity Service has been within the funding levels provided, for the last four financial years from 2021/22 to 2024/25. The final position for the 2025/26 financial year is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate interim support that is being made available for patients of the Regional Gender Identity Service who have been on the waiting list for five years and more.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a timeline of when he will take forward the ten recommendations made by the Public Accounts Committee's Report on Homelessness in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In accordance with established procedure, a Memorandum of Reply to the Public Accounts Committee’s report will be presented to the Assembly by 24 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to provide his Department's commitment to a neighbourhood-centred model of primary care community audiology service, as set out in the July 2025 Heath and Social Care Reset Plan, in order to reduce the audiology waiting list.
answer_text: My Department recognises the significant pressures on audiology services and the impact that waiting times have on patients and families. The Health and Social Care (HSC) Reset Plan, published in July 2025, identified the development of neighbourhoodcentred primary care models as a key area for service reform, including opportunities to expand communitybased audiology provision.Work is ongoing within my Department to scope how elements of the neighbourhoodcentred model could be applied within audiology services. This includes examining:Opportunities to deliver more routine audiology assessments and followup care in community settings, closer to where people live.Potential for partnership models with primary care and the voluntary/community sector, where clinically appropriate.New workforce and skillmix options that could support a shift in service delivery while maintaining clinical governance.The role of digital pathways, including remote support for hearing aid users, to reduce pressure on hospitalbased services.In parallel, HSC Trusts continue to implement local measures aimed at reducing audiology waiting times, including targeted backlog initiatives and service optimisation work.My Department is currently considering the findings of the scoping work referenced above. Any future development of a communitycentred audiology model would be subject to detailed service planning, workforce assessment, financial analysis, and stakeholder engagement.At this stage, no final decisions have been taken, but my Department remains focused on actions that can improve access to audiology services and support the wider transformation aims set out in the Reset Plan.At present the level of demand for audiology services exceeds the capacity available in the HSC leading to unacceptable waiting times for some patients.  A number of factors have contributed to this, including funding pressures and missed opportunities in the past to modernise how health and social care services are delivered.As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term.This engagement has included discussions with HSC Trusts, workforce leads, the British Association of Audiologists, and community and primary care providers. While some of these discussions have not resolved the challenges as yet, they have been constructive and are helping to shape thinking around a more sustainable model of care. A key consideration is maintaining the right balance between hospitalbased audiology services and those that may be safely and effectively delivered in community settings, while also ensuring workforce capacity is protected.Officials are currently exploring the development of a pilot proposal f

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following his engagement with the Welcome Organisation and their frontline delivery of services for homeless people, to detail any plans in place to (i) update the Housing (NI) Order 1988; and (ii) add to his expected Housing Miscellaneous Bill, a statutory duty to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: My recent experience of how the Welcome Organisation deliver front line services was extremely insightful.The Public Accounts Committee report on Homelessness, published on 26 February, recommends that my Department should work with the Housing Executive to explore options for strengthening homelessness prevention.I welcome the Committee’s Report.  As per protocol I will respond to its Recommendations formally through the Assembly process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide a copy of any evidence collected from environmental health inspections carried out in the eleven local council areas, that was considered by his Department in advance of the Decent Homes Standard consultation, which confirms the prevalence of damp and mould in tenancies.
answer_text: My Department did not collect evidence from environmental health inspectors.  Officials considered data obtained through the Northern Ireland Housing Condition Survey, which is a comprehensive assessment of the condition of all properties, not solely those which have been referred for inspection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide (i) a copy of the periodic surveys carried out on registered housing associations, as outlined in his response to the Tackling Disrepair, Damp and Mould private members motion on 24 February 2026, when he said his Department, as the regulator, has undertaken periodic surveys to ensure that registered housing associations appropriately manage all cases of damp and mould in their stock and have policies and procedures in place to address damp and mould; and (ii) copies of any periodic surveys carried out on Housing Executive properties,
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer part (i), the information requested has been placed in the Assembly Library.In relation to part (ii), the NI Housing Executive has not undertaken any periodic damp and mould surveys of its properties in the past five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the average price of car insurance for drivers aged 70 and over in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any work she is undertaking with insurance companies to address unfair financial consequences for older drivers,
answer_text: The regulation of Financial Services including insurance services is a reserved matter.  Complainants who contact my Department’s Trading Standards Service Consumerline about car insurance are advised to contact their insurer directly to try to resolve the issue. If they are dissatisfied with the response, then they should escalate the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS). The FOS is a free, independent service that resolves disputes between consumers and financial services firms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the average price of car insurance for drivers aged 70 and over in Northern Ireland; and (ii) any work she is undertaking with insurance companies to address unfair financial consequences for older drivers,
answer_text: (i) Motor insurance pricing is not a devolved matter. The setting of premiums is a commercial decision for individual insurers and as such my Department does not hold the requested information. The industry body, the Association of British Insurers (ABI) does not publish average motor insurance premium data broken down by age and by UK nation or region, so specific figures for drivers aged 70 and over in the North are not available. (ii) My Department has no legislative influence over motor insurance pricing, which is regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). Motor insurance pricing and underwriting decisions are entirely matters for individual insurers, based on their own risk assessments and commercial judgement.  As such, my Department cannot intervene in insurers’ commercial decisions. The British Government published the Motor Insurance Taskforce Final Report in December 2025. It sets out coordinated actions to help reduce costs for all motorists. In line with Action Point 4.4 of the report, my officials have commenced exploratory discussions with key stakeholders involved in the administration and running of the Continuous Insurance Enforcement (CIE) scheme in Britain to explore the potential benefits to all drivers and feasibility of introducing a CIE scheme here.My Department continues to monitor issues affecting motorists and engages with relevant stakeholders, where appropriate, to understand emerging concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she has had with Royal Mail about late and missing post deliveries, given the impact on businesses, government services and the wider public.
answer_text: Postal services are a reserved matter, with Ofcom responsible for regulating the postal industry in the north of Ireland. Under Ofcom’s regulatory framework, postal operators - including Royal Mail - are required to have a clear and accessible complaints procedure for consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the consultation will be launched to seek views on the priorities for PC28 for NI Water.
answer_text: Under Article 7 of the Water and Sewerage Services (NI) Order, my Department may issue guidance to the Utility Regulator on social and environmental policies in relation to water and sewerage.  The Utility Regulator shall have regard to this guidance when regulating NI Water.Draft guidance on my social and environmental policies for PC28 is currently being prepared and it is hoped that the consultation will be launched by summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any protections in place for students renting in the private rented sector.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an upgrade to the South Street Wastewater Pumping Station, Newtownards, will be included for investment under PC28.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has confirmed that there are no plans to include an upgrade to the South Street Wastewater Pumping Station (WwPS) in its PC28 business plan. NI Water had developed a solution under the Ballyrickard Drainage Area Plan, however this has been considered unfeasible and does not represent value for money, primarily due to the scale of storage required as part of the solution (c. 17,000 m³) and its estimated construction cost of c. £50m.NI Water has advised however that, during the PC28 period (2028–2033), it will carry out further investigations and hydraulic/environmental modelling to develop a more efficient and deliverable solution. Any revised approach will be dependent upon reaching agreement with NIEA on a reduced storage requirement and the availability of sufficient CDEL funding, before assessment through the Price Control business planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the People and Place – A Strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal.
answer_text: I have witnessed first-hand the impressive work delivered by our partners in the community sector as part of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme. This year I have invested over £20 million pounds in my Neighbourhood Renewal programme which makes a real impact on the lives of those in the most deprived communities. Through this investment, over 2,500 local community groups were supported, more than 5,500 adults volunteered in their communities, over 1,500 people were helped to secure employment through targeted training and employability interventions, and community groups were able to secure over £40 million in additional external funding.Of course, I want to see my Neighbourhood Renewal programme doing even more for disadvantaged communities. The Review of the ‘Neighbourhood Renewal People and Place Strategy: has now concluded and I am considering how to take it forward. Subject to my Executive colleagues’ approval, a public consultation on the Strategic Framework will take place in due course.It is not anticipated that any changes to the existing Neighbourhood Renewal programme will take place during the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme.
answer_text: It is vital that we work collaboratively in the face of the impacts of climate change and the challenges we face such as more frequent and severe storms leading to increased risk of flooding, loss of energy supply and coastal erosion along with other risks such as longer lasting wildfires which are all impacting upon our communities, businesses and natural environment. 				My Department has been leading on the development of the Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme, or NICCAP3 for short, on behalf of all departments to ensure that we collectively meet our legal responsibility under the Climate Change Act 2008.				The NICCAP3 sets out details of policies and proposals in response to the latest UK Climate Change Risk Assessment with the ultimate aim of improving our resilience to the impacts of climate change that impacts all of us here in Northern Ireland. In developing the draft Programme my Department, as well as working with all other departments, has also undertaken a co-design approach with a range of key stakeholders. 				The public consultation my Department undertook last year showed overwhelming support for the draft Adaptation Programme, and my Department published the summary analysis of responses last October. The constructive feedback received through that consultation has been used to make refinements to the draft NICCAP3 including incorporating additional actions that were proposed by a range of stakeholders.  				Unlike other regions of the UK the draft NICCAP3, in addition to the 150+ actions from departments, also contains over 120 actions from a range of key stakeholders including Local Government, environmental Non-Government Organisations, our universities, Agencies and front-line delivery organisations in recognition of the important role they play in adapting to and creating resilience to the impacts of climate change. This ‘whole society approach’ my department has taken has been welcomed by the UK Climate Change Committee.				I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to the NICCAP3 being finalised and laid in this Assembly. It is imperative that Northern Ireland has an adaptation programme in place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when the Ministerial Panel Housing Thematic Sub-group last met.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing for All Ministerial Panel – Housing Thematic SubGroup last convened on Wednesday 4 September 2025 in the Board Room of the Housing Centre, 2 Adelaide Street, Belfast.In line with the Group’s Terms of Reference, which provide for biannual meetings, the next meeting is scheduled to take place at the same venue on Wednesday 4 March 2026.The Terms of Reference also permit additional meetings to be convened on an ad hoc basis, should any member of the SubGroup request that such a meeting be arranged.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any Invest NI support available for small to medium-sized enterprises, located in town centres, which are unable to extend premises or move into more suitable town centre premises, due to the lack of wastewater and sewage connections.
answer_text: Invest NI provides a broad range of support to small and medium sized businesses across the North primarily in the form of advice, guidance, innovation grants and access to finance. 		It provides a range of finance solutions under the banner of ‘Access to Finance’. These funds include both debt and equity support to ensure that all businesses, regardless of their size, can access the funding they need to scale to the next level. 		To ensure businesses can access the support they need to innovate Invest NI also provides support through its Innovation Voucher and Business Innovation Grant schemes. These initiatives provide grants to help local small and medium sized businesses develop and bring to market new products and services, or improve existing processes, all with the aim of increasing profits and driving growth. 	The provision of wastewater and sewage connections does not fall within the remit of Invest NI. 	Details on all of the support above can be found on the Invest NI website. If the Member would like to discuss further she should contact Invest NI’s Head of Southern Regional Business, Alistair Quinn, and if any business would like to discuss the support available to them, they should contact Invest NI’s Business Support Team on 0800 181 4422.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available for local councils to (i) develop night-time economy provision; and (ii) support the retention of small to medium-sized businesses that serve the night-time economy, including the food-to-go sector, to stop the loss of these businesses from town centres, due to the inability to revitalise derelict commercial premises or expand existing premises, due to the lack of wastewater and sewage connections.
answer_text: The Minister for Infrastructure is responsible for wastewater.				My Department, alongside Tourism NI and the three Belfast Business Improvement Districts, has commissioned research to better understand the issues affecting the night-time economy. While the final report is anticipated soon, the interim findings suggest reliable late-night transport, safety, tourism and licensing are key factors affecting the success of our night-time economy. 				I was pleased to work with Minister Kimmins, Executive colleagues and Belfast City Council to secure funding for a late-night public transport pilot scheme in Belfast. Initial feedback on uptake is positive and my hope is that this pilot will lead to advancements in late-night transport provision across our region, thereby supporting our night-time economy across our local council areas.				My Department and partner organisations continue to provide a broad range of supports to businesses to help them be more competitive through energy efficiency, innovation, skills, and exploring new markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she is taking to ensure NI Water is given clear direction to prioritise investment into closed catchment areas, where no new connections are currently permitted during PC21, as outlined in NI Water's wastewater system capacity information.
answer_text: The prioritisation of investment in water and wastewater infrastructure is a matter for NI Water, however I can advise that the company prioritises its investments by focusing on the operational requirement to operate, maintain and enhance water and wastewater services to address the most urgent risks to public health, environmental compliance, and economic growth, within the approved budget allocation.Specific capital investment needs are defined are part of the Utility Regulator’s Price Control.  The Price Control is a structured regulatory process that identifies what investment is needed, what it will deliver, and what level of expenditure is considered efficient. The Regulator ultimately decides which capital outputs NI Water is funded to deliver, based on statutory obligations, customer priorities, and affordability.In terms of addressing the issues with connecting to the wastewater network, since October 2024 my department has allocated over £30m of capital funding for wastewater infrastructure, which has unlocked wastewater capacity for over 5,300 homes across the North. This is above the 4,500 which NI Water had initially stated they could connect if fully funded in PC21, and it is only 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) a breakdown of the funding for Severe Weather Emergency Protocol; and (ii) the amount of the funding that is spent by the Housing Executive, on their operational costs.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Funding for the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP) is managed within the Strategic Prevention annual budget that forms part of the Homeless programme. The expenditure for the last five financial years is detailed in the table below:2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25SWEP Spend-£5,190£35,873£9,837£51,523When implementing SWEP, the Housing Executive will utilise a range of options that seek to provide additional emergency capacity. These options include engaging with a range of partners seeking additional emergency units that are delivered at no cost to existing contractual arrangements and other options which require additional payments. The table above details those additional payments. There are no additional operational costs to the Housing Executive for SWEP as all staff requirements are considered part of business as usual.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department is undertaking with Translink, Belfast City Council and the organisers of the Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann, to (i) provide safe public transport to and from Belfast city centre,; and (ii) reduce or remove cars from travelling through this event.
answer_text: Translink is working closely with Belfast City Council and the organisers of Fleadh Cheoil na hÉireann to develop a comprehensive and safe transport plan for the event. This includes both bus and rail provision.Translink’s initial assessment indicates that additional park-and-ride capacity on key approaches to the city may be required, alongside enhanced public transport services, including lateevening operations, to support safe travel to and from the city centre.A detailed transport plan will be published once there is greater clarity around the event programme, locations, timings and projected attendance. This will ensure that the appropriate measures are in place both to provide safe, reliable public transport and to minimise car travel through the event.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any policy she used to determine whether households can or cannot afford to contribute towards infrastructure needs, such as wastewater and sewage.
answer_text: The current NI Executive policy is to provide a subsidy to NI Water in lieu of domestic water charging.    I will not add to the financial burden faced by people in the North by introducing domestic water charging to hard pressed workers and families in the midst of a cost-of-living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 2942/22-27, to detail the reason why the interim Regional Planning Commission's report has still not been published.
answer_text: My officials and I are currently considering the Commission’s report. This process takes time to ensure a thorough understanding of the issues and recommendations. I will publish the report once my review is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether it is NI Water's policy to refuse all planning applications across the Strangford constituency, in order to negotiate a better budget, during the multi-year budget period.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that it is a statutory consultee to the Local Planning Authorities and, as such, provides guidance to planners as part of the planning process. Ultimately, the decision to approve, refuse, or condition planning applications rests solely with the relevant planning authority, not with NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 2939/22-27, whether she has completed any (i) risk assessment; and (ii) rural needs assessment, of her active travel programme, to encourage children to cycle to schools in rural communities.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the Climate Change Act, the main focus of my department with respect to encouraging more people to walk, wheel or cycle has concentrated on developing the Active Travel Delivery Plan and enhancing my department’s design guidance for the delivery of active travel schemes. A rural needs impact assessment was carried out on the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan. The prioritisation of high-quality active travel infrastructure in rural areas requires a different approach to that applied in an urban context. Once work on the Active Trave Delivery Plan is complete, officials will consider how best to prioritise active travel investment to maximise benefits in rural areas.As outlined in my answer to AQO 2939/22-27, my department is also currently developing a toolkit of measures that can be implemented around schools to encourage walking, wheeling or cycling for the school run. Some of the measures designers might consider include reduction in speed limits, greater pedestrian priority in the vicinity of schools, or measures to discourage inconsiderate parking that can cause difficulties for those walking, wheeling or cycling. Risk assessments are carried out as part of the design process for any individual project of this nature brought forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has had any engagement with the Minister for Communities and the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to support for businesses that cannot avail of premises due to lack of wastewater capacity, making the redevelopment of derelict properties inaccessible.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will be following the Welsh Housing Quality Standard, from April 2026, to ensure protection for all tenures across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I will not be following the Welsh Housing Quality Standard from April 2026.  My Department is currently consulting on proposals to revise the Decent Homes Standard, which is the standard that all social housing providers are required to meet in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36330/22-27, to provide copies of the minutes of meetings (i) he; or (ii) his officials, have had with Hospitality Ulster, where the University of Stirling's recommendations following the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle were discussed, including the (a) dates; and (b) locations, of the meetings; and (c) attendees and their positions within his Department.
answer_text: I have arranged for the information you requested to be placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of complaints submitted through the official complaints system to the Housing Executive, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 38211/17-22, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions his Department has taken to ensure all existing social housing stock is transitioned from home heating oil, before the end of the 2022- 27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: My Department is currently consulting on proposals for a revised Decent Homes Standard, which is the standard that all social housing is required to meet. The consultation includes proposals to support the transition towards low carbon heating. I aim to publish the revised Decent Homes Standard later in 2026, but a period of transition will be required for social housing providers which will extend beyond the mandate.  In relation to the Housing Executive, I have sought the requested information from the NIHE Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“It will not be possible to transition the Housing Executive’s remaining oil heating systems to gas or no/low carbon alternatives by this date.  The Housing Executive currently has some 24,305 oil boiler heating systems in the dwellings in its stock.  On average each year we are changing 980 of these to gas or other heating systems, as allowed by the funding that is available (given all of our stock investment priorities) and the delivery contracts that are in place.In addition, a high number of these oil heating systems are in properties in rural areas where the gas infrastructure is not available.  Our intention is to eventually change these to air or ground source heat pumps, and we have been piloting these in a number of schemes - primarily the Low Carbon Heating Programme - to inform the review of our Heating Policy and our intended introduction of a new Landlord Decarbonised Heating Policy (as noted in our Corporate Sustainable Development Strategy & Action Plan).  However, we are not yet in a position – in both delivery and funding terms – to roll out the scale of programme that would be required to address all of these off the grid properties, and certainly not by 2027.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the new powers being introduced in Scotland that give landlords the ability to remove domestic abusers from tenancy agreements from August 2026; and (ii) whether he has any plans to introduce similar legislation.
answer_text: The relevant Scottish regulations will allow social landlords to apply for court orders to end a tenancy in cases involving abusive behaviour, enabling a victim of domestic abuse to remain in their home. The powers under Schedule 3 of the Housing (NI) Order 1983, includes domestic violence as a potential ground for possession if specific criteria are met. The NIHE, as part of the Housing Executive’s Domestic Abuse Action Plan, is examining the issue of action to be taken against perpetrators of domestic abuse, including evaluating the current options and considering the approach taken in other areas of the UK. It aims to produce a report with recommendations by April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) new social home business cases which have been approved, but are awaiting planning permission in order to proceed; and (ii) homes agreed in each business case, in the Strangford constituency, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are three proposed new social housing schemes (with an associated 24 new social homes) included in the 2025/26 Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for the Strangford Parliamentary Constituency:2-6 North Street, Newtownards (Arbour Housing – 10 units).Castle Street, Comber (Radius Housing – 13 units).Parsonage Close, Kircubbin (Rural Housing – 1 unit). For all three scheme proposals, Applications for Project Approval (with associated Economic Appraisals/Business Cases) have been submitted to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group (DPG). These proposals are currently under assessment and Project Approval has yet to be confirmed. Planning Approvals for all three scheme proposals are already in place. Please note that the Housing Executive does not record SHDP data by District Electoral Area.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of all Ministerial Direction decisions taken by him, during his tenure as Minister for Communities.
answer_text: I have made no Ministerial directions during my tenure as Minister for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any plans in place to improve soundproofing around the Members' Dining Room, to ensure those attending meetings in Room 115 can properly hear proceedings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission recognises the importance of ensuring that Members and others attending meetings in Room 115 are able to hear proceedings clearly, and understands the concerns that have been raised. The building’s Grade A listed status places significant constraints on the type of alterations that can be made, which greatly limits the scope for further soundproofing, however  the Assembly Commission will explore practical, non-intrusive ways to minimise noise disruption between the Members’ Dining Room and Room 115.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether (i) she is aware of the change to disability transport classification; and (ii) the Driver and Vehicle Agency can be supported with this new provision.
answer_text: I can advise that I am unaware of any change to disability transport classification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social homes that are projected to be delivered through the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: When a final Budget for 2026-2029/30 has been agreed by the Executive, I will prioritize the capital allocation for my Department and will provide an allocation for the Social Housing Development Programme.It will then be for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as the commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, to consider how the budget may best be used to meet identified housing need before any figure for starts in 2026-29/30 can be confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of meetings his Department has had with NI Water concerning the capacity issues holding back the delivery of new social homes, including delivery of the housing supply strategy.
answer_text: My senior officials attend the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) 'Housing Strategy Meetings’, alongside NI Water and other stakeholders, which are focused on unlocking capacity for new social homes.Departmental officials also meet regularly with DfI colleagues, including through the Supply Strategy action planning process, to develop strategic actions which can address wastewater capacity issues and prioritise housing supply. Alongside this, the Housing Executive maintains on-going engagement with NI Water to review the status of all proposed new social housing schemes currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). In addition to the on-going assessment of programmed new social housing schemes, the Housing Executive also engages with NI Water regarding new housing delivery beyond the current 3-Year SHDP in the context of long-term housing need and NI Water’s next Price Control period (PC28).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new social housing developments that have been (i) objected to; or (ii) refused by, NI Water at planning application stage.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not hold a definitive figure for the number of new social housing developments objected to or refused by NI Water at planning application stage. This is because development proposals are at various stages of the planning process, and NI Water’s position can change as conditions are addressed, or solutions identified, through the Wastewater Impact Assessment (WWiA) process. Some schemes receive planning approval with NI Water conditionality, which is later resolved. Others face initial refusal pending WWiA outcomes, but approval may follow once solutions are agreed.In summer 2025, NI Water carried out a high-level risk assessment of the 3-year Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) ‘New Bids’. Based on data provided in August 2025:2,751 units: Solution possible, no issues envisaged.1,302 units: Requires developer engagement to determine if a solution is possible.649 units: Requires NI Water capital works upgrade.Current Position for 2025/26The table below summarises NIHE’s understanding of the position for proposals within the 2025/26 SHDP year:Planning / NI Water PositionNr SchemesNr UnitsApproval not required1555Approval in place591,080Planning Approval TBC / NI Water Approval in place20842Planning Approval / NI Water Approval TBC3339Total972,316NIHE continues to engage with NI Water and other stakeholders to manage these risks proactively.”

NI Water advice in respect of the member’s question is detailed in the table below. This information relates to responses that NI Water has provided to the Ards and North Down Council Planning Department, which may include reasons for refusal. It remains within the remit and responsibility of the Local Planning Authority, as to whether NI Water’s recommendations are accepted. Information only started being collected from June 2021.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount he included for benefit cap mitigations in his bid for the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
answer_text: In the budget exercise my Department submitted a bid for Welfare Mitigations which included Benefit Cap Mitigations of £12.7million in 2026-27, £13.0million in 2027-28 and £13.4million in 2028-29.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work of the Interim Regional Planning Commission.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an updated Departmental organisational chart detailing the new senior management team following a number of recent retirements.
answer_text: An organisational chart detailing my Department’s senior management team will be confirmed following the appointment of a new Permanent Secretary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a review of NI Water's objectives has been carried out to ensure they are aligned to the key priorities contained in the Programme for Government, such as new housing and support for businesses, especially small and medium-sized enterprises.
answer_text: Effective water and sewerage infrastructure is central to the delivery of Programme for Government outcomes in terms of providing more social, affordable and sustainable housing, protecting Lough Neagh and the environment and growing a globally competitive and sustainable economy.  In relation to protecting Lough Neagh, NI Water has invested £60m in its infrastructure in the first 4 years of PC21 and has committed a further £36m in the remaining 3 years of the Price Control. In terms of enabling housing connections, since autumn 2024, my Department has provided an additional £30m of capital funding to enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity for 5,300 additional domestic and non-domestic properties across the North.  This is above the 4,300 properties that NI Water originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control Period in 2028. I am steadfast in my commitment to ensuring that NI Water deliver the vital infrastructure to achieve the Programme Government outcomes and I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to deliver for all.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether the £63million of unclaimed benefits delivered via the Make the Call Wraparound Service, as outlined in his Department's press release on 16 Dec 2025, has been delivered from the Make the Call Wraparound Service alone, or if any other partner agencies were involved in supporting those accessing welfare support.
answer_text: The £63million of unclaimed benefits was generated through direct engagement with the Make the Call Wraparound Service. While referrals are received from partner organisations, the recorded outcomes reflect the Department’s direct assessment, advisory and outreach support, which led to the successful uptake of benefit entitlements.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) their action plan; (ii) budgetary allocation; and (iii) a timeline, as to how they intend to respond to the recommendations of the commissioned research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
answer_text: We are currently carefully considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse. Officials remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors to ensure they are kept informed of developments

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when they will publish the reports of independent experts on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, including the set of recommendations agreed by the inter-departmental working group, survivors and public interest groups.
answer_text: We are currently carefully considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse. Officials remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors to ensure they are kept informed of developments

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why 11 individuals were unable to access emergency shelter under the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol on Sunday 4 January 2026, due to capacity related issues; and (ii) any steps his Department will take to prevent this happening again.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive put in place SWEP arrangements on 4th January to support those sleeping rough to secure emergency accommodation.  While one of the SWEP providers did face capacity and logistical challenges on the night of the 4thJanuary, the Housing Executive’s Out of Hours homelessness service engaged throughout the night with our partner organisations to address issues of rough sleeping, as would normally be the case in such instances.SWEP is a distinct additional and non-statutory arrangement put in place by the NIHE, in partnership with voluntary and community sector partners to meet emergency accommodation needs for anyone sleeping rough.  It is not unusual for SWEP to be in place for prolonged periods during the winter months, as indeed it was last year, and the NIHE is grateful to voluntary and community sector partners for working so effectively with us. Our teams will continue to work alongside those voluntary and community sector partners around the clock to ensure provision is maintained.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they (i) have taken, since the beginning of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate; or (ii) intend to take, to engage with those who have been subject to sexual abuse, in church settings.
answer_text: Historical clerical child abuse (HCCA) has had a devastating impact on those affected and we express our deepest sympathy and solidarity to all victims and survivors of this terrible abuse. In this current mandate, Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors, listening to their stories and hearing their views on what is needed.  This has included attending meetings of the Reference group of victims and survivors in September 2024 and May 2025 and meeting with individual victims and survivors. In addition, officials have liaised closely with the Reference group of victims and survivors to develop the terms of reference for the 3 research projects that were completed last year, regularly update on progress of the work, and discuss the findings and recommendations. We are very grateful to all those who have participated and shared their experiences.We remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors, whose views we will take into account as we move forward in our deliberations on this complex and sensitive issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding allocated to the Housing Executive for providing services when the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol is triggered, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP) is managed within the Strategic Prevention annual budget that forms part of the Homeless programme. The expenditure for the last five financial years is detailed in the table below:”2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25SWEP Spend-£5,190£35,873£9,837£51,523

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will review the Disabled Facilities Grant to include provisions to meet the cost of energy efficiency adaptations for people who are (i) immobile; or (ii) have a serious or terminal illness, and are impacted by fuel poverty.
answer_text: Assistance may be available via a Disabled Facilities Grant for individuals who are immobile or have a serious terminal illness where such measures are identified as a clinical need and recommended by an Occupational Therapist as part of an adaptation to meet the needs of an individual.  Disabled Facilities Grants are available to eligible private home owners, private landlords and tenants for works necessary to fulfil an Occupational Therapist’s recommendation, ensuring a disabled person’s home is safe and accessible. Affordable Warmth Scheme grants assist low-income households living in private accommodation by providing grants for energy efficiency to address the effects of fuel poverty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Disabled Facilities Grants awarded, broken down by (i) the total amount of grant funding issued; (ii) the average grant awarded per application; and (iii) the average amount applicants have contributed to the costs of works, in each council area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 37516/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in Strangford.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 37514/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average time taken from a report being made, to completion of repairs or replacement, as a result of tenant-reported heating system problems, in Housing Executive properties, broken down by council area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I am advised by the Housing Executive that the requested information is not readily available. This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Domestic heating faults vary extensively. When a tenant reports any heating fault, it is categorised into one of three groups – Emergency, Urgent or Routine – dependant on the nature of the fault. EMERGENCY call out – to be responded to by telephone with a triage call within 15 minutes, made safe within 2 hours where applicable and completed within 24 hours.URGENT work – appointment made with the tenant and work completed within 4 working days.ROUTINE work – appointment made with the tenant and work completed within 15 working days.Tenants are not left without any form of heat. Temporary Room Heaters are made available in the event of a repair not being possible at the time of attendance and, where alternative hot water provision is available in the form of an immersion heater, it is ensured that this is operational before leaving the property.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how the Neighbourhood Model of Care will bring care closer to home for under-served communities.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department has in place to measure the effectiveness of the Disabled Facilities Grant (i) in meeting Occupational Therapist recommended adaptations; and (ii) in how it is managed and operated by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: My Department is currently carrying out a review of NIHE-Administered Private Sector Grants, including the Disabled Facilities Grant, and in this regard is working closely with the Department of Health and the Housing Executive to identify improvements. A public consultation on proposals for change will take place this year. (i) Health Trust Occupational Therapists (OTs) are involved throughout the Disabled Facilities Grant process to ensure the recommended adaptations are met and are appropriate to meet the needs of a disabled person. OTs review all plans and any issues are addressed through engagement with the applicant and their architect where necessary, until both the Housing Executive and the OT are satisfied that the client’s needs are met. An OT must formally approve the plans before any grant approval is issued. Upon completion of the works, the OT is required to inspect and confirm that the adaptations meet their client’s needs before the payment of grantaid.(ii) The Housing Executive has a structured Disabled Facilities Grant process and measures performance and delivery against defined service standards. Customer satisfaction surveys are completed and survey results reviewed on a monthly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the publication of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy was published for public consultation on 17 December 2025. The strategy focuses on three themes: supporting children in their early years, helping families with their child’s learning and childcare costs, and strengthening the early years sector and workforce.The strategy builds on £80 million already invested by the Executive over the past two years, which is delivering real benefits for thousands of children, families, and providers. One of the most successful initiatives has been the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme. Since its launch in September 2024, eligible working parents have saved almost £19 million, and combined savings are estimated at £40 million.We have also begun moving all pre-school provision to full-time to ensure every child can access 22.5 hours of age-appropriate learning before starting school. So far, over 2,500 additional full-time places have been funded, with a further 2,200 places approved to move to full time provision from September 2026. This will mean that approximately 50% of pre-school provision, in every area of Northern Ireland, will be full time. I have also invested significantly in existing early years programmes, many of which provide targeted support to children facing disadvantage or with additional needs and disabilities.  These early actions lay the foundation for long-term progress. I strongly encourage stakeholders to respond to the consultation available on my Department’s website by the closing date of 24 March 2026.  The comments will be used to inform the final version of the strategy and delivery plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any actions he is taking to (a) prevent; and (b) reduce, the number of deaths of people experiencing homelessness as reported by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) in their Deaths Of Homeless People Report 2024; and (ii) any actions he can take to ensure the number of deaths of people experiencing homelessness are included in future publications by NISRA.
answer_text: Within the Programme for Government there is a commitment to focus on the prevention of homelessness, by working collaboratively and prioritising housing solutions for those most in need.I have also provided a ringfenced an allocation of £2.5m for strategic homelessness prevention, alongside £4.3m for the continuation of community prevention activities.Following the release of the ‘Deaths of Homeless People in Northern Ireland’ report on 11th December, NISRA have confirmed that they plan to publish this report annually.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any urgent or emergency communications in place to ensure (i) school transport services; (ii) schools; (iii) parents and guardians; (iv) and pupils, are informed of road closures and disruptions to home to school journeys, particularly in coastal areas during storms, in a timely and effective manner.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport. It has advised me that where advance notice is available regarding road closures or transport disruption, every effort is made to communicate this effectively through schools’ parent messaging systems and social media channels. However, the EA does not currently operate a dedicated transport notification service that can provide real-time alerts to all service users, such as schools, pupils, parents or guardians, about urgent or unexpected changes to school transport services. This may include situations in which transport services are disrupted at short notice due to severe weather conditions or sudden road closures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any risk assessment processes used by Education Transport services to determine if it is safe for Home to School transport to be delivered along a coastal route during a storm; and (ii) any contingency processes enacted to ensure all Home to School transport providers use alternative routes in coastal areas to ensure the safe carriage of pupils when coastal roads, like the A20 Portaferry Road, is experiencing the impact of severe stormy weather, making driving conditions treacherous.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that during periods of severe weather and in emergency situations, its transport service works alongside the EA Emergency Planning Service to maintain situational awareness and coordinate response efforts. This enables dynamic risk assessments to be carried out to reduce risk and mitigate impact. If emergency structures are activated, the EA coordinates with multi-agency partners and in terms of road safety, it acts on advice from the PSNI and Department for Infrastructure (DfI) Roads Service who have responsibility for closing roads.
