Where Schools remain open during severe weather and pupils need transport, the EA collaborates with service providers such as Translink to arrange where possible for pupils to be transported home safely. Transport decisions are guided by severe weather warnings from the Met Office and road closure notifications from PSNI or DfI. If necessary, alternative routes are considered locally, but in some scenarios, transport services may be disrupted at short notice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any process used to coordinate a multi-agency approach to close coastal roads during storms; and (ii) when a decision is made to close a coastal road, how the (a) public; (b) schools; and (c) businesses are informed of the closure.
answer_text: When road closures are necessary in an emergency situation such as a risk to life or danger to or from traffic, my departmental officials work closely with the PSNI who have the responsibly for closures in these instances.During storm events my departmental officials attend multi agency co-ordination meetings, to update blue light services, the Education Authority, Translink and local councils on issues impacting the travelling public.  This information is also disseminated widely through the use of my department’s social media and its TrafficwatchNI website. Where roads are closed, signage is placed on site and diversion routes will be signed where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the successful pilot by Civic Initiative, which trialled the concept of deliberative democracy, to detail whether they have reviewed the resulting report.
answer_text: We recognise and fully support the role that civic society plays in supporting policy making, through co-design and co-production approaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any percentage uplift for the Supporting People Programme funding that has been included in his Department’s calculations for the multi-year budget starting in April 2026.
answer_text: The Department is continuing to work on budget plans for years beyond 2025/26, including provisions for the Supporting People Programme. At present, funding for future periods has not yet been agreed and remains subject to confirmation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the number of serious adverse incidents occurring in hostels and temporary accommodation, in this mandate.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Provider organisations carry out their own analysis of incidents to ensure that staff training needs, safeguarding considerations, and any identified risks are appropriately addressed. Major Adverse Incidents, along with other incident types, are discussed at Contract Management meetings and during Quality Monitoring visits undertaken by the Supporting People team with provider organisations.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been carried out to ensure all services being delivered under the Supporting People Programme are paying salaries at the National Living Wage.
answer_text: Neither my Department or the Housing Executive have a role in setting salaries or employment terms and conditions for staff employed by Supporting People Scheme Providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the Participation and the Practice of Rights policy brief, published on 1 December 2025, on the Fundamental Review of Allocations of Social Housing; and (ii) how he will ensure this is taken into consideration the Housing Executive in its review of the points system.
answer_text: I have been clear that we must deliver on the Programme for Government commitment to increase the supply of social homes, but we must also ensure that our allocation system prioritises households in greatest objective need. The Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations underwent full public consultation and equality impact assessment and all proposals were supported. Implementation of the agreed changes has included ongoing stakeholder engagement, led by the Housing Executive, to inform implementation. My officials have analysed the brief referred to and continue to work with the Housing Executive on implementation of the remaining recommendations.  My officials work closely with the Housing Executive to ensure that all evidence-based stakeholder contributions are considered through established consultation and evidence-gathering processes when preparing to implement the recommendations.  This evidence is considered by my Department in respect of each recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when an independent scrutiny panel will be established to review the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations; and (ii) the number places on that panel to be filled by people (a) with lived experience of waiting for housing; or (b) who have been awarded Full Duty Applicant status.
answer_text: My Department has publicly consulted on proposals for change, published an outcome report and tasked the Northern Ireland Housing Executive with implementing the proposals from the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations (FRA). The Housing Executive has put structures in place to inform implementation, including a Stakeholder Advisory Group. I have sought further  information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“A Stakeholder Advisory Group was established to assist the Housing Executive throughout the duration of the FRA Project to successfully implement the proposals for change in a way which will achieve the best possible outcomes for customers and social landlords and provide assurance on the implementation approach. The Group comprises stakeholders who have the highest interest in and influence on the project, including Housing Rights and Homeless Connect who represent the views and interest of customers who require housing and homelessness assistance.People with lived experience of being on the housing waiting list and receiving homelessness assistance are on a number of FRA working groups. In addition, the FRA Project secures valuable input from people with lived experience through focus groups and workshop sessions organised at key stages of the project and for specific proposals. The FRA Project engages regularly with a wide range of tenant and customer representative bodies, including the Central Housing Forum and Housing Community Network, Housing Policy Panel, Rural Residents Forum and Disability Forum.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the success of the New Foundations programme in delivering cross-departmental collaboration, to detail any steps his Department plans to take to ensure this approach continues, and is expanded to reduce homelessness.
answer_text: My officials will continue to work closely with other departments to explore opportunities for meaningful joint working and to enhance cross-departmental collaboration such as that which delivered New Foundations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he is taking to ensure (i) the Housing First model will be incorporated into his Department’s homelessness prevention policy; and (ii) that suitable investment will be provided in the multi-year budget.
answer_text: The Housing First model is incorporated in the Housing Executive Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, available via https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/73313718-aa0e-4aae-b122-6573dcab88c7/Ending-Homelessness-Together-Homelessness-Strategy-2022-27.pdf.  The Housing Executive has recently published a report ‘Housing First Feasibility Study: Expansion of Services in Northern Ireland’, which is available via https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/6820a1fd-f47e-4119-812b-bdd639b68c62/Housing-First-Feasibility-Study-December-2025.pdf.Work on budget profiling beyond 2025 / 26 is continuing, and funding remains to be confirmed.I will continue to make the case for increased funding for Housing and Homelessness prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with (i) the Minister for Finance; and (ii) the Executive, to request any Barnett Consequential funding resulting from the energy support budget allocated in Great Britain, to help families living in fuel poverty in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Tackling empty homes is a priority for my Department as part of its wider work to increase housing supply, support regeneration and improve outcomes for communities. My officials are taking forward work to identify potential policy interventions that could be utilised in Northern Ireland to bring vacant properties back into use.My officials have engaged with their colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the Department of Finance (DoF) in support of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the reason Housing First has not moved forward in Northern Ireland, since the 2012 report by the Housing Executive; and (ii) any steps he will take to overcome barriers to move the policy forward.
answer_text: Housing First has been operating on a small scale in Northern Ireland since 2013, funded by the Housing Executive’s Supporting People Programme with the support of DePaul and Simon Community.  Expansion of Housing First is included in the Housing Executive Homelessness Strategy 2022-27.  Increased housing supply is vital to supporting this model, as outlined in the Housing First Feasibility Study, available via:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/6820a1fd-f47e-4119-812b-bdd639b68c62/Housing-First-Feasibility-Study-December-2025.pdfMy Department will work, in collaboration with other Departments and the Housing Executive, to address the constraints identified in this Feasibility Study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the bid his Department has put in to the multi-year budget to fund New Foundations over the next three years.
answer_text: My Department has allocated £3m to the Housing Executive for the New Foundations programme in 2025/26. Work on budget profiling beyond 2025/26 is continuing, and funding remains to be confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the proposed governance structure for the New Foundations Pilot Programme; and (ii) any (a) community; and (b) voluntary sector, organisations that will be involved in the programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The governance arrangements for the New Foundations programme are currently under development and, subject to approval, will incorporate consideration of appropriate community and voluntary sector involvement.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail where the properties being bought through the New Foundations programme will be based.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The specific locations of properties to be acquired under the New Foundations Programme is yet to be finalised.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of young people who will be supported through the New Foundations programme, in its first year.
answer_text: The New Foundations programme aims to have secured 30 units of accommodation along with tailored support packages in the first year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of small businesses in Strangford that have availed of support from Invest NI, in the past five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with the year ending 31st March 2025 being the most recent available.  Over the period 1st April 2020 to the 31st March 2025 Invest NI has supported 148 businesses within the Strangford Parliamentary Constituency area, 127 (86%) of these were small businesses.		In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress made to ban the advertisement of all types of vapes (i) near schools; and (ii) by confectionary retailers.
answer_text: Advertising of nicotine vapes is restricted in the UK, under the Tobacco and Related Products Regulations 2016, which makes it is unlawful to advertise nicotine-containing products in a manner that promotes their use or appeals to children. There are currently no restrictions on the advertising of non-nicotine vapes and other nicotine products such as pouches. Under the Tobacco Advertising and Promotion (Display) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012 and The Tobacco Advertising and Promotion (Display of Prices) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012, it is illegal to display tobacco products and the prices of tobacco products, in the course of business in Northern Ireland. However, there are no current restrictions on the display of vaping or other nicotine products in retail settings.This will be addressed by the UK-wide Tobacco and Vapes Bill, which is currently progressing through Parliament.  The Bill, which has the support of the NI Assembly, aims to address the current gaps in legislation, by banning the advertising and sponsorship of all vape and nicotine products and by providing powers to regulate flavours and packaging, to reduce their appeal to children. The Bill will also introduce regulation-making powers to prohibit point of sale displays for vapes and other nicotine products. It is expected that the Bill will progress to the House of Lords Report Stage in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the actions her Department is taking to transition Northern Ireland away from the dependence on fossil fuels through; (ii) alternative fuels in public transport; (iii) further development of the electric vehicle charging network; and (iv) updates to planning to modernise requirements to meet climate change objectives.
answer_text: The actions my Department is taking to decarbonise the transport sector are outlined in the draft Climate Action Plan and focus on three policies. The ‘Switching Fuels’ policy is key to reducing dependence on fossil fuels in the transport sector. The ‘Shift’ policy aims to reduce vehicle emissions by shifting modes of transport away from private car journeys to sustainable travel alternatives, including public transport.  The ‘Reduce’ policy considers the role of the planning system.  My Department continues to support the ongoing decarbonisation of Translink’s bus fleet and provided funding of £66m to enable Translink to purchase 145 zero and low emission buses.  A further £88m was provided in 2022 for the purchase of an additional 100 zero emission buses and associated EV charging infrastructure.  My Department has also supported investment of approximately £19.2m in hydrogen bus technology.The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North.  Major EV infrastructure providers, understanding the commercial benefits available, are making significant investments in developing EV Infrastructure that meets both current and future need. My Department has also provided funding to support the further development of the EV charging network, including match-funding for a Local Council consortium (9 councils) application to the Office for Zero community-based’s On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme to install 250 charging points and support for funding through the Shared Island Initiative, including the Sports Club EV Charge Point Scheme to rollout community based charging points at 48 sports clubs and the Green Hydrogen Refuelling Corridor project in collaboration with the Department of Transport.  In addition to financial support, my Department has also been trailing EV charging solutions for residents without driveways to allow safe cable routing across footways.My third key transport decarbonisation policy aims to reduce vehicle emissions by reducing the need and length of journeys. I recently consulted on my Draft Transport Strategy 2035 which covers the key role of the planning system in supporting transport policies and the integration of transport and land use planning.  Responses to the consultation are currently being considered.The planning system also has a role to play in supporting the wider efforts of government in contributing to climate requirements. For example, my Department recently published on 11 December 2025 a revision to the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) on the subject of renewable and low carbon energy. This new policy framework can help enable appropriate development and associated infrastructure that can make a positive contribution towards reducing emissions and decarbonising energy use across the region in the long-term public interest. Furthermore, last year my Department undertook a Call for Evidence on a potential foc

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps his Department can take to assist Northern Irelands transition away from its dependence on fossil fuels through; (i) reducing carbon emissions; and (b) increasing the use of renewable energy, across the Northern Ireland Civil Service estate.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) office estate represents less than 1% of the built assets recorded on the Government Land and Property Register and, as such, has a minimal impact on the total public sector energy usage. That said, I am determined that my Department does everything possible to reduce carbon emissions and increase the use of renewable energy across the estate. DoF is currently progressing an ambitious Office Estate Strategy, Phase 1 of which is aimed at ‘right sizing’ our portfolio by reducing the in-scope footprint by approximately 40% by 2028. Up to the 31 March 2024, energy consumption across the estate had been reduced by 12,287,443 kWh. Phase 2 of the Strategy aims to deliver a greener, modern NICS Office Estate by 2035. This will be achieved through improved energy efficiency in our retained estate and establishing signature regional buildings to enable staff to work in offices closer to home, reducing travel time and associated emissions, while helping promote regional balance. In addition, a number of projects have also been initiated that aim to reduce carbon emissions.  LED lighting has been installed in many buildings across the estate, which use significantly less energy and has a longer lifespan. In April 2025, the natural gas powered plant used for heating/cooling in Lanyon Plaza, one of our larger buildings, were replaced with electrically driven units.Consolidation projects in Omagh, Ballymena and the Coleraine areas are also aligned with the Departments net zero ambitions which will involve recovering roofs, replacing windows, upgrading the mechanical and electrical systems as well as lining exterior walls all of which will improve the ‘U’ value, a measure of how well a material or building component (like a wall, window, or roof) insulates against heat loss, making the buildings more energy efficient.On the Stormont Estate, procurement is underway to deliver a 400kVA photovoltaic installation by 2027, which will provide renewable energy to the DoF managed buildings. At Castle Buildings, a refurbishment project is ongoing that will include the replacement of the roof at Block B with enhanced insulation to meet net zero standards. Work has also been initiated to investigate and develop options to upgrade both the Castle Buildings exterior “envelope” and systems, to move to net zero standards.My Department is also commencing the procurement of a carbon baselining exercise for the retained Office Estate. This will help inform and enable additional efficiency projects with a view to further reducing energy usage and de-carbonising our offices.  For the longer term, my Department has been supporting the Department for the Economy in a pilot project to investigate the potential future application of shallow geothermal technology to provide sustainable low carbon renewable heating and cooling systems. It is hoped that this will inform the potential for a future geothermal heat network to replace fossil fuel heating system

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to transition Northern Ireland away from the dependence on fossil fuels through (i) her work with businesses; (ii) improvements and expansion of the electricity grid; and (iii) attracting investment to deliver programmes, such as a retrofit roll-out across all tenures.
answer_text: My Department leads on the Energy Strategy - The Path to Net Zero Energy, which continues to progress the transition away from fossil fuels towards self-sufficiency in affordable renewable energy and net zero emissions by 2050. (i) Supporting Businesses:The Business and Industrial Processes sector plan, included in the draft Climate Action Plan (CAP) published in June 2025, sets out the policies and proposals to support businesses’ transition away from fossil fuels. These include fuel switching, electrification, and resource and energy efficiency measures. One key policy is Invest NI’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Programme, funded by my Department, which provides financial support to businesses to implement resource efficiency, energy efficiency, and decarbonisation measures within their operations. (ii) Grid Improvements and Expansion:On 26 November 2025, I published a decision paper on fairer grid connection costs. This decision will remove unfairness and enable homes and businesses to connect to the grid at a fair price, encouraging new investment in low carbon technologies and renewables. The Utility Regulator will work to modify NIE Networks’ licence, with a new statement of connection charges to be published in the new year and changes taking effect in the first half of 2026. The North South Interconnector remains a strategic priority for my Department, essential for lowering costs for electricity consumers, strengthening grid reliability, and enabling greater integration of renewables. Construction began in late 2024, with estimated completion in 2031. Smart meters will provide electricity consumers with the opportunity to lower their bills and support renewable integration. Following public consultation, my officials have analysed stakeholder feedback and are working with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks on a project plan for delivery. Procurement is planned for 2026, with first installations in 2027. A Grid Development Monitoring Group has been established by my Department, including the Utility Regulator, SONI, and NIE Networks, to monitor delivery of network developments, identify barriers, and accelerate progress on grid upgrades. (iii) Attracting Investment and Retrofit Programmes:My Department is developing a Net Zero Accelerator Fund (NZAF) to support local enterprises in accelerating projects that contribute to emissions reduction targets and to act as a catalyst for attracting and complementing private investment in the green economy.Earlier this year, my Department consulted on future support for low carbon heating in residential properties. Officials are finalising analysis of responses to inform policy decisions, including heat pump installations and future scheme design for energy efficiency measures and low carbon heat. This year, I have allocated just over £2m of additional funding into NISEP to support low-income households to install energy efficiency measures this winter, building on previous years’ work which w

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any strategy to (i) transition Parliament Buildings away from dependence on fossil fuels; and (ii) further increase production of renewable energy on site.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission’s Sustainable Development Office continues to work with partners to progress the transition of Parliament Buildings away from dependence on fossil fuels. As part of a framework agreement with DoF, all of the electricity used within Parliament Buildings is generated from renewable energy sources.  In addition, officials are in collaboration with the Department for the Economy on the development of geothermal technology across the wider Stormont Estate and the investigation of potential low-carbon heating options for Parliament Buildings. These explorations form part of the Assembly Commission’s wider sustainability work aimed at reducing reliance on fossil fuel–based systems.The Assembly Commission has implemented a range of measures to reduce its carbon footprint. Due to the limited land within the Assembly Commission’s curtilage, the scope for installing large-scale renewable technologies on site is restricted. However, the Assembly Commission has already installed photovoltaic panels, solar thermal tubes and rainwater-harvesting systems, and continues to keep opportunities for additional on-site renewable energy generation under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he will provide copies of any written communications between Hospitality Ulster and his Department, including those sent (a) directly to him; (b) to his Private Office; (c) to any person working in his Department; and (d) to his Special Advisor, with respect to the University of Stirling’s Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle; and (ii) their subsequent recommendations.
answer_text: Any request my Department receives to release information will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when (i) the objections process will be reviewed; (ii) a consultation will be carried out; and (iii) any updates or changes will be made, as indicated in his statement on the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle, on 18 November 2025.
answer_text: In my response to the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland, I stated that my officials will work with key stakeholders and relevant delivery partners to establish the scale of any potential problem, and if necessary, explore potential solutions. The need for consultation, any potential future changes, and the timing of such, will be determined by the outcome of that process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with other Departments to ensure the Fair Funding Principles contained within the new Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector in Northern Ireland will be adhered to.
answer_text: My Department supports engagement on the Partnership Agreement and Fair Funding Principles through the Joint Forum, which includes representatives from all Government Departments who are fully aware and engaged. While my Department facilitates the Forum, responsibility for applying the Fair Funding Principles rests with individual Departments.The Joint Forum has a key role in ensuring the principles are communicated and understood. Cross-sectoral working groups set up through the Joint Forum are now progressing key priorities, including a communications plan as well as developing an evaluation framework and reporting mechanism.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review legislation and regulations to enable Department funded Community Transport operators to collect and drop off passengers in Translink operated bus stations, to enable improved connectivity and multi-modal transport opportunities.
answer_text: The policy for access to stations is operated by Translink is set out in the Public Service Agreement (PSA) between Translink and my Department.  This allows eligible operators of public passenger transport services to request access to Translink stations in line with these procedures.Whilst I recognise the importance of community transport and how it can contribute to multi modal transport opportunities, there is no plan to review this section of the PSA, or legislation, to enable organisations, grant funded by my department, to collect and drop off passengers in Translink operated bus stations at present. However, this can be kept under consideration when the PSA is reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide a copy of the minutes of any meetings he has had with Hospitality Ulster, where he discussed the University of Stirling's recommendations following the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle, to include the (i) dates; and (ii) locations, of the meetings; and (iii) attendees positions within his Department.
answer_text: Any request my Department receives to release information will be considered on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the legislation and regulations, to enable DFI Roads to install disabled bays outside pharmacies located along the roadside as standard, rather than requiring an application.
answer_text: My Department’s powers to provide parking places are set out in Articles 10 and 13 of the Road Traffic Regulation (Northern Ireland) Order 1997.Comprehensive guidance on requests for accessible parking bays for businesses, including the relevant application criteria, is provided in RSPPG E056: Provision of Parking Places RSPPG E056.  The application process is a key part of this policy to ensure that the context and constraints for each location are fully considered. As such my Department has no current plans to review the legislation or regulations to permit the provision of accessible parking bays outside pharmacies or other businesses without the completion of an application.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 33735/22-27, whether this is a review of the operation of existing permits or whether she is exploring the effectiveness of Section 10B permits, compared to other permits operated in GB.
answer_text: The review is looking at the operation of the existing permits processes, and will identify any potential changes which may be made to the current system.  My Officials will shortly be providing me with a full report on their findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions she is having with independent breweries and local drinks producers, to develop opportunities to grow their business, both in Northern Ireland and for export.
answer_text: Invest NI provides a range of support to local drinks producers to help them grow and expand into export markets. This includes financial assistance for capital investment and market development, innovation and skills development, and non-financial assistance such as specialist advice, introductions to international buyers and promotional activity through a network of business development teams across 25 global markets.		By way of example, over the last three financial years (2021/22-2024/25) Invest NI has provided support totalling £5.13m to the sector, leveraging almost £30m of investment.		In addition, InterTradeIreland welcomes applications from businesses in the north within the Beverage Manufacturing sector and has a range of programmes to support their business growth through innovation, collaboration and by increasing cross-border sales. Since 2023, Intertrade Ireland has supported 22 beverage manufacturers based in the North through its programmes and events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a copy of the evaluation or rationale, explaining why the proposal to replace the Strangford Ferry with a bridge, was not previously considered.
answer_text: The 2013 Strategic Review of the Strangford Lough Ferry Service considered alternative proposals to the ferry service, including the option of a bridge however, the review concluded a bridge was not an economically viable solution. The subsequent business case for the replacement of MV Strangford did not consider the provision of a bridge given that its central objective was to enable the Strangford Lough Ferry Service to continue to operate the current scheduled service.The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day, from early morning to late-night serving both the local community and local tourism through the provision of a reliable service and is considered the most economical transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she will take to (i) ensure all sections and divisions within her Department use the spelling of Cloughey, for the village on the Ards Peninsula Irish Sea coast, and (ii) stop the use of and replace signs, with the incorrect spelling of Cloghy and Cloughy.
answer_text: My officials advise that they are aware of several older directional signs that use the spelling Cloghy.  As the Member will be aware, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.  For this reason, the spelling will be amended as and when signage comes to the end of its natural life and is in need of replacement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the option to build a bridge over Strangford Lough was rejected during business case consideration, before the last ferry was purchased.
answer_text: The 2013 Strategic Review of the Strangford Lough Ferry Service considered alternative proposals to the ferry service, including the option of a bridge however, the review concluded a bridge was not an economically viable solution. The subsequent business case for the replacement of MV Strangford did not consider the provision of a bridge given that its central objective was to enable the Strangford Lough Ferry Service to continue to operate the current scheduled service.The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day, from early morning to late-night serving both the local community and local tourism through the provision of a reliable service and is considered the most economical transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 32515/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 32515/22-27 was answered on 9 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps taken to ensure that employers taking part in the JobStart Scheme are providing jobs identified by other Departments as necessary to meet public service delivery needs.
answer_text: JobStart is open to employers from across all sectors who are encouraged to provide a wide range of diverse opportunities, enabling choice for participants whilst meeting the wider business needs of employers and the Northern Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the spirit of cross departmental working, whether he will consider extending the JobStart Scheme employer criteria to include jobs needed in (i) the education sector, such as (a) classroom assistants; and (b) technicians; and (ii) the health sector, such as domiciliary care roles.
answer_text: The JobStart scheme is open to employers across the public, private and voluntary sectors. This already provides the potential for opportunities to be created in the roles listed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of JobStart Scheme employment opportunities identified within the Department of Communities, and in each of the other Departments.
answer_text: Figures are not yet available for the number of JobStart opportunities available across the NICS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of employers who have signed up to the JobStart scheme, to date; and (ii) where current job opportunities are located, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that the volume of employers and job opportunities is growing every day. The following information is the position as of 30 November 2025:653 employer applications had been received. The local council location of job opportunities advertised on JobApplyNI on that date isCouncil AreaNo. of JobStart OpportunitiesAntrim and Newtownabbey24Ards and North Down38Armagh City Banbridge and Craigavon32Belfast172Causeway Coast and Glens31Derry City and Strabane86Fermanagh and Omagh20Lisburn and Castlereagh38Mid and East Antrim32Mid Ulster12Newry Mourne and Down35Total520

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any evidence a claimant on Universal Credit will be required to produce to access financial support for a service listed in the Help with Health Costs Scheme from 1 December 2025.
answer_text: Universal Credit claimants that fall within the thresholds for automatic passporting to the Help with Health Costs scheme will be advised that they can show their UC award notice or their UC statement, either a hard copy or digital format on their phone or device as proof of entitlement to help with health costs.The delivery of the Help with Health Costs scheme rests with the Department for Communities and my officials, policy and operational, have worked closely with the Department for Communities to ensure that relevant operational processes in addition to external and internal guidance are updated to facilitate a smooth rollout of the new policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process his Department will use to process claims for the reinstated Help with Health Costs Scheme from 1 December 2025.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs scheme remains unchanged, however the legislation has been amended from 1 December 2025 to enable automatic passporting to certain Universal Credit recipients. Over 195,000 current recipients of Universal Credit in Northern Ireland will be eligible for automatic passporting to Help with Health Costs from 1 December 2025. The underlying threshold eligibility criteria for the Help with Health Costs have not changed, rather, some individuals who may have previously accessed support through the Low-Income Scheme will now be automatically passported. Those in receipt of Universal Credit who are not automatically passported from the 1 December 2025 will continue to have to apply through the Low-Income Scheme. The legislation which makes provision for the Help with Health Costs scheme falls within the remit of the Department of Health, however operational responsibility for the delivery of the Help with Health Costs scheme rests with the Department for Communities.My officials, both policy and operational have worked with the Department for Communities to ensure that relevant operational processes in addition to external and internal guidance are updated to facilitate a smooth rollout of the new policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline how the recently reconvened Coastal Forum will help support greater coastal resilience and adaptation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the number of jobs in the Community and Voluntary Sector, at risk of redundancy, due to the lack of certainly regarding the future of the UK Shared Prosperity Fund and the Local Growth Fund.
answer_text: As these funds are administered directly by the UK Government, my Department is unable to assess in detail the impact of the potential loss of funding on the Voluntary & Community Sector (VCS). We have, however, been provided with information from NICVA which estimates that approximately 650 staff from the Sector were involved in project delivery up to 31 March 2025. I remain extremely concerned that the current Local Growth Fund financial allocation from April 2026 will have a negative impact on our VCS who currently deliver Economic Inactivity interventions which support those furthest from the labour market.My Department has been working closely with the Department of Finance, the Northern Ireland Office and the UK Government to seek clarity on the transition to the Local Growth Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the reporting commitments contained in the Partnership Agreement with the Community and Voluntary Sector, will provide details from every Executive Department, setting out how they are delivering the Fair Funding arrangements.
answer_text: The process for evaluating the implementation of the Partnership Agreement, including the Fair Funding Principles, is currently being developed by the Joint Forum’s Evaluation Working Group. While annual reports are expected to draw on contributions from Executive Departments, local government, and the Voluntary and Community Sector, the scope of reporting in the first year has not yet been agreed. This reflects the ongoing work by the Joint Forum to raise awareness of the Partnership Agreement, promote shared understanding, and establish the evaluation framework. The scope of the first annual report will be confirmed once the Evaluation Working Group has completed its work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to review the budget for Supporting People, with an aim to uplift the budget, in order to ensure providers pay staff a fair and equitable salary for the services delivered for vulnerable people.
answer_text: I have confirmed a further allocation of £1.98m in-year funding to the Supporting People Programme to address staffing and inflationary costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the principles of fair funding arrangements, as agreed by the Executive in the Partnership Agreement with the Community and Voluntary Sector, launched July 2025, are required to be included by each Department of the Executive, as part of the current planning and submissions to his Department for the upcoming multi-year budget.
answer_text: The fair funding principles set out in the Partnership Agreement with the Voluntary and Community Sector are intended to enable effective use of public funding and deliver greater benefits to our communities.These principles should be considered alongside and do not override existing accountability requirements, financial and budgetary guidance, procurement policy or legislation.It is the responsibility of each Minister to determine how best to incorporate these principles into their departmental decision-making processes when preparing bid submissions to my department as part of in year exercises or the multi-year budget information-gathering exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the location of each of the 600 homes being purchased by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive for temporary accommodation, broken down by local council area, including the year of purchase or expected purchase.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.	I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:	“The principal considerations in the geographical distribution of these units will include:Geographical areas where exposure to high-cost temporary accommodation is most prevalent.The nature of the temporary accommodation client groups (single persons, couples, families).The availability of suitable property available to purchase that align with the above criteria.Value for money considerations.No properties have yet been purchased under this scheme however we estimate a purchasing profile of: 25 Yr1 2025/26, 125 Yr2 2026/27, 225 Yr3 2027/28, 225 Yr4 2028/29”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on delivery of three rounds of IVF, as set out in the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF. You may be aware it was included following a request by my party.Although the expansion in 2024 to one full cycle was welcomed, it was not possible to expand the service further due to funding constraints.My Department is continuing to work with the Regional Fertility Centre to explore the feasibility of expanding current capacity from within available resources. This is extremely challenging, as any expansion must be both affordable and deliverable without detriment to current provision and waiting times, which the team has worked hard to maintain after the disruption of the Covid pandemic. While I am cautious about raising expectations at this stage, I am assured that all feasible options for moving to a second cycle are being actively considered. While planning work is ongoing, it is not possible to give a definitive implementation date at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to remove the second religion question from future censuses, that asks respondents, who confirm they have no faith, to confirm what religious community background they may have come from.
answer_text: The content and design of the Census 2031 questionnaire will be shaped by user need and legislative requirements.  As such NISRA, which has responsibility for the planning and conduct of a census here, launched a public consultation on Census User needs on 28 October this year. That consultation will run for 14 weeks until February 2026 – and will support the next phase to design, test and recommend questions to be included.After the close of the consultation on 4 February 2026, all responses will be evaluated to inform NISRA’s recommendation for topics to be included in the 2031 Census questionnaire.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who have not yet been transferred on to Universal Credit, broken down by type of benefit.
answer_text: The table below shows the number of people who have been issued with a migration notice but who have not yet claimed Universal Credit, broken down by type of benefit.Benefit combinationNumber of claimants ESA and HB7,050ESA 5,800HB50IS10HB and JSA10ESA and IS*JSA*Total12,910Notes:Data Source: Universal Credit weekly Move to UC tracker file at 19/11/2025.Figures are at the individual claimant level, so will be greater than the number of households.Figures are the number of claimants who have received a Move to Universal Credit notification, but have yet to make a UC claim and have not had their claim stopped or a claim pending, as of 19/11/2025.There are a number of cases that have not yet received a Move to Universal Credit notification as of 19/11/2025. These are not included in the figures.Figures are rounded to the nearest 10 and totals may not sum due to rounding. Figures below 5 are marked as *.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an update hearing test and replacement hearing aid, following an issue with the first hearing aid, in each Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, has been progressed; and (ii) where the evidence of the religious background of teachers, per school, is reported and published.
answer_text: To ensure the smooth implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors.This group worked in conjunction with the Equality Commission and guidance for schools was published with the support of Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee. The Equality Commission delivered a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance. The target audience for the webinars was those school governors, principals, heads of departments involved in the recruitment and selection of school teachers. The Chair of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) management side wrote to all schools explaining the requirements for the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.The religious background of teachers in a particular school is not reported or published. The Education Authority is responsible for preparing and submitting the annual monitoring return to the Equality Commission for teachers in controlled schools and CCMS is responsible for carrying out this function for teachers in Catholic maintained schools. All other schools with over ten staff are required to submit monitoring returns to the Equality Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any allocation being provided to Northern Ireland, from the UK Government’s launch of a new £27 million veterans strategy, to fund a network of support centres intended to provide veterans with advice on housing and employment.
answer_text: No specific allocation has been made to Northern Ireland under this programme as it is an open call for funding. Existing organisations or consortiums can apply for funding to the Office for Veterans Affairs within the Ministry of Defence before 14 January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rational for veterans not being listed as a specific category in the Housing Executive's Homelessness Guidance Manual, Chapter Five: Priority Need.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for providing homelessness assistance in Northern Ireland. When exercising its functions in relation to housing allocation, homelessness and disabled facilities grants, due regard must be given to the principle of the Armed Forces Covenant that service members, veterans, and their families are treated fairly and receive the support that they need, including access to social housing in Northern Ireland. This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the information requested from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:“Article 5 (1) of the Housing (NI) Order 1988 sets out six distinct categories of persons having a priority need for accommodation. The Homelessness Guidance Manual is policy guidance for staff and reflects the legislative parameters within which the Housing Executive operates. Veterans are therefore not listed as a distinct category as this is not provided for in the relevant legislation.In comparison to England, whereby the Housing Order 1996 allows Local Authorities in England to give additional Priority to members of the Armed Forces if they are identified as in urgent housing need, no such provision exists within Northern Ireland, or within the Housing (NI) Order 1988 which would allow the Housing Executive to do so.Where an applicant is a former member of the Armed Forces, any and all circumstances/needs related to their service history should be taken into consideration. This may include any physical or mental health issues which a member of the armed forces may have (as a result of their service) and these will be taken into consideration in terms of establishing vulnerability in line with investigation of their homeless application.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any processes that have been put in place by his Department to confirm which Universal Credit claimants are eligible for the Help with Health Costs scheme, once passporting is introduced from 1 December 2025; and (ii) the form that claimants, who are in receipt of Universal Credit, but are under the threshold for automatic passporting, should use to apply for the low income scheme.
answer_text: Over 195,000 claimants who are not declaring earned income or are under certain earned income thresholds will be automatically passported to help with health care costs and will no longer need to complete an application for Help with Health Costs. Their eligibility for help can be confirmed via their Universal Credit statement.Claimants who are under the earned income threshold for automatic passporting will not be required to complete any forms.  Those who are above the earned income thresholds and require Help with Health Costs can continue to apply for support through the Low-income Scheme by completing form HC1 'Claim for help with health and travel costs'.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the last time school meals were reviewed to ensure they met the recommended dietary content, promoted by Diabetes UK.
answer_text: My Department completed a review of the nutritional standards for school food in 2021. This was informed by a working group of health and educational school food experts to ensure alignment with up-to-date government guidance on healthy eating such as the Eatwell Guide. Whilst revised nutritional standards were agreed, due to budgetary pressures, the necessary funding to take forward their implementation has not yet been available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether they will promote the changes to Help with Health costs for claimants of Universal Credit, coming into effect on 1 December 2025, through the online portal to ensure all are aware they can access help towards dental treatment, eye sight tests, glasses or contact lenses, and travel costs for health and social care treatment.
answer_text: My Department will promote the changes to the Help with Health Costs scheme by issuing a notification to every existing Universal Credit claimant. In addition we are amending the Universal Credit journal welcome message, providing signposting in our 35 Jobs and Benefits offices and via telephone, and reflecting the changes on nidirect, a key source of information for Universal Credit claimants.  Finally, we are updating external products such as the leaflets and posters distributed to opticians and dental practices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will promote the changes to Help with Health Costs for claimants of Universal Credit to ensure that they are aware they can access help towards (i) dental treatment; (ii) eye sight tests; (iii) glasses; (iv) contact lenses; and (v) travel costs for health and social care treatment.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce a health and wellbeing programme for carers, delivered through the Health and Social Care Trusts, that includes an annual carers' health assessment and advice on diet and exercise, mental health and the importance of self-care.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rational for a blood test not being included as standard practice during annual learning disability assessments, given that this test could highlight issues such as diabetes in a cohort of people who may have difficulty communicating symptoms.
answer_text: Annual Health Checks for people with a learning disability are delivered through the Northern Ireland Contract Assurance Framework (NICAF), which requires General Practices (GP) to use a recognised protocol such as the Cardiff Health Check. These protocols include recommended blood tests, such as glucose and lipid profiles, where clinically indicated.Blood tests are not mandated for every individual because venepuncture is an invasive procedure that may cause distress, particularly for those requiring desensitisation or best-interests processes where capacity is limited. Current clinical guidance therefore supports an ‘offer and clinical indication’ model rather than universal testing.GPs undertake blood tests where symptoms, risk factors or existing conditions require investigation, or where individuals or carers consent to testing. This approach ensures that clinical judgement, proportionality and patient rights remain central to the assessment.The draft Learning Disability Service Model and its Service Delivery Plan outline actions to improve the quality and uptake of Annual Health Checks including: a regional communication plan.strengthened Health Facilitationdevelopment of a monitoring database, andenhanced support for reasonable adjustments within primary care.These measures will improve early identification of health needs, including through appropriate use of blood tests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a copy of his, or his Special Adviser’s, instruction to departmental officials to promote his activities in Israel on the Department of Education social media channels during his visit in October 2025.
answer_text: On Monday 27 October 2025, I asked the Press Office to draft a press release about the visit to Ofek School in Jerusalem.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to provide a copy of all minutes or notes taken at any ministerial meetings and interactions that took place in Israel in October 2025; and (ii) to confirm the number of civil servants in attendance to take those minutes or notes, as required in the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: Pursuant to the Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021, the legislation sets out specific obligations for Ministers regarding transparency, accountability, and the conduct of official business. These obligations apply only to “relevant meetings,” defined as pre-arranged meetings with third parties to conduct official business. Having reviewed all engagements during the Israel visit and consulted with officials, I am satisfied that none of them meet this definition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a copy of any invitation he received (i) into his Private Office; (ii) directly to his MLA or ministerial email address; (iii) through his Special Adviser, to visit Israel in October 2025, outlining (a) the date and time received; (b) the source of the invitation; and (c) any responses provided through his MLA or Private Office email addresses regarding any invitations.
answer_text: At 14.53 on 15 September, I received an invitation, to my MLA account, from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Israel inviting me to be part of a devolved administration delegation to Israel from the 24–31 October 2025. I asked my Private Office to provide a response, which I approved and they issued.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given the findings of Simon Community’s recent report, Childhood Adversity and Homelessness in Northern Ireland: Breaking the Cycle, to detail any plans they have to drive better cross-departmental collaboration, to tackle and prevent homelessness.
answer_text: My Department already leads on the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan and officials will seek to expand on this work to ensure greater collaboration and engagement at a strategic level across Departments to address Homelessness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has a centralised, publicly-accessible, database of buildings identified as needing remediation in regards to fire safety.
answer_text: My Department holds a database of all buildings that have undergone a Fire Risk Appraisal of External Walls as part of the Cladding Safety Scheme where the assessment has demonstrated the need for remediation.  This list is not publicly available.It is intended to publicly publish details of awards made under the Northern Ireland Cladding Safety Scheme in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure the golden thread of information is (i) embedded into building management; and (ii) applied consistently to Northern Ireland projects.
answer_text: My Department is currently co-designing policy with citizen’s, industry and public sector experts that will ensure that Golden Thread information is embedded into building management. It is intended that the regulatory function proposed within the policy will ensure consistency across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how his Department is working with local councils to ensure consistent enforcement of fire safety standards across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My officials are currently drafting a high-rise residential building policy safety policy that will aim to ensure consistent enforcement of fire safety standards across Northern Ireland. Significant engagement has already taken place and will continue with council officials in the development of the policy to ensure consistency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure all high-rise buildings meet fire safety regulations, particularly in relation to cladding, balcony materials and fire doors.
answer_text: My officials are currently drafting a high-rise residential building safety policy that aims to improve fire safety in new build and existing high rise residential buildings in line with recommendations from the DoF Expert Panel report, the Hackitt reports and the Grenfell Enquiry.  The policy will address all related building products mentioned in your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount being paid to Cooperation Ireland to manage the NI Community Infrastructure Fund,; and (ii) is this cost is being deducted from the £4.32 million available for the fund.
answer_text: Co-operation Ireland are being grant funded £432k over three financial years to administer the Northern Ireland Community Infrastructure Fund. No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any (i) advice; and (ii) guidance, provided to each Department of the Executive, to ensure regional economic balance is considered before any award of contract, tender or grant monies are decided.
answer_text: DoF has responsibility for policy around procurement and I do not have a remit to provide guidance to other Ministers on this. I am, however, glad to see a growing awareness of the importance of regional balance, which has been raised as a whole government issue within the new Programme for Government. I look forward to working with colleagues across the Executive to make sure this continues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the work of the Residential Building Safety Team.
answer_text: Following a period of research and engagement, officials are finalising a draft policy paper which will be issued for public consultation later this year.  Subject to Executive agreement, my Department intends to introduce a Building Safety Bill early in the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to align Northern Ireland’s building safety regime with the Building Safety Act 2022 in England, and the Building Safety Regulator.
answer_text: The Department has researched arrangements in all UK jurisdictions and in Ireland, with a view to the creation of bespoke policy and legislation to effectively regulate Residential Building Safety in Northern Ireland, subject to Assembly approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether an annual report is produced detailing how each Department is delivering the Executive’s commitment to regional economic balance.
answer_text: Regional balance has been raised as a whole government issue within the new Programme for Government (PfG).A PfG Delivery Report will be published by the Executive on an annual basis. This report will set out progress made towards the PfG Priority “A Globally Competitive and Sustainable Economy”. This will include an update on work related to Regional Balance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the waiting time for a child, with an urgent referral from Scrabo Children's Centre, to get a first appointment with an Ear, Nose and Throat specialist in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting times for children accessing a first appointment with Ear, Nose and Throat, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 20813/22-27, and further to the release of a substantial area of land in Bangor, County Down by the Department of Education, to detail (i) whether his officials have worked with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to identify this land for housing; and (ii) any actions that have been taken with Executive colleagues to ensure such opportunities are acted on quickly.
answer_text: To date, the Housing Executive has not been notified of any surplus land within the Education Authority’s estate in Bangor. The Housing Executive is a statutory partner in the Ards and North Down Community Planning Partnership and actively contributes to the Community Planning Estate and Land Sub-Group. They will raise the status of this site to assess its potential to meet housing needs. Where a viable opportunity is identified, the Housing Executive will express interest and nominate a Housing Association to explore the feasibility of delivering housing on the site.The Housing Executive identifies and communicates social housing requirements through the Annual Housing Development Commission (formerly known as the Commissioning Prospectus), which also guides housing associations in their site search activities. Surplus public sector assets—whether identified through the Public Sector Trawl or directly by asset owners—are assessed against local housing need and development potential.I am also progressing work to bring a paper to the Executive around making better use of public land to support the supply of affordable housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of requests to utilise public land, made available from another Executive department that have been made, in each of the past ten years, in each council area; and (ii) if the requests have been successful; and (iii) what the land has since been used for.
answer_text: ANSWERThere is no centralised database that records requests to utilise public land owned by Executive Departments. Departments are responsible for managing their estates, which may include considering approaches from other Departments, wider public sector or external parties, regarding the use of land or buildings.The Strategic Asset Management Unit (SAMU) in Land & Property Services (LPS) has a role in the disposal of surplus public sector land and buildings as set out in the Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property policy. SAMU maintains records of completed disposals but does not track assets beyond completion of a sale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance pursuant to AQW 20813/22-27, to detail (i) when the land of the former Department for Education car park at Rathgael House, Bangor was made available to Executive colleagues; and (ii) if any of the Executive or their Departments have indicated an interest in this land.
answer_text: ANSWERThe car park adjacent to the Rathgael House site has not yet been deemed surplus as it is currently in use whilst the ground works on the Rathgael site are ongoing. These ground works should be completed by the end of October 2025 which will allow the Department to declare the adjacent car park surplus under the Land and Property Services ‘Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property’ policy. This process will allow other public bodies to express an interest in acquiring the land. At this stage, my Department has not been made aware of any interest from other departments or public sector bodies, however this will be formalised when the disposal process is initiated.

answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has the management, oversight, and advisory role in relation to the disposal of surplus public sector assets under the D1 disposal process. Over the past ten years, there have been six recorded instances of transfers of surplus assets between Departments. These transactions are outlined in the table below and includes the relevant council areas.
