question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the outcomes for the total number of individuals supported to access services funded by the Supporting People programme in each of the last five financial years, broken down by (a) thematic area; (b) primary client group; and (c) type of service; and (ii) this number as a percentage of the total number of individuals supported recorded for each of the outcomes set out in the Supporting People Outcomes Framework.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals his Department estimates work in services provided through the Supporting People programme, broken down by (i) Supporting People thematic area; and (ii) primary client group.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Major Adverse Incidents recorded by Supporting People providers, in each of the last five years, including 2024-25, broken down by (a) type of adverse incident; (b) Supporting People thematic group; and (c) primary client group.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to seek funding for the Provider Innovation Fund under the Supporting People programme in (i) 2025-26; and (ii) in bids for the upcoming multi-year budget.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Housing Executive continues to bid for Provider Innovation Funding, and I will continue to support these bids. However, due to the constrained budget the bids for 2025-26 have been unsuccessful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of unspent Supporting People funding from 2024-25 that has been recouped in the 2025-26 financial year, broken down by (a) thematic area; (b) primary client group; and (c) type of service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Recoupments from 2024/25 have not yet occurred in the 2025/26 year.  The Supporting People team are in the process of managing 2024/25 provider financial returns and recoupments of unspent Supporting People grant funding will be considered following this process.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of individuals; (ii) the number of providers; and (iii) the number of services, supported through Supporting People-funded services, in each of the last five financial years, broken down by (a) Supporting People thematic group; (b) primary client group; and (c) local council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of individuals who have accessed the Public Health Agency needle exchange service pilot in four temporary accommodation hostels, in each financial year since 2023-24; and (ii) whether his Department plans to continue with the pilot.
answer_text: The provision of harm reduction services within Northern Ireland is facilitated by the Public Health Agency (PHA). The PHA work alongside other statutory agencies, community pharmacies, community and voluntary organisations, low threshold services, and hostels to provide the Needle and Syringe Service (NSES) and Take Home Naloxone (THN) programme across the region. PHA use an online Harm Reduction Information System called Neo360 to capture live data on needle exchange and naloxone transactions across all sites. All data captured is anonymous and non-identifiable.The PHA have opened needle exchanges within 3 temporary accommodation hostels, and one drop-in facility, for people experiencing homelessness. This service is not public facing and is only accessible to the service users of the relevant organisation.The Welcome Organisation, Ann’s Gate Hostel installed two token-operated vending machines in July 2024 which dispensed needle exchange kits. These vending machines have been relocated with the service to Glenview in 2025. The number of individuals who use this service is not monitored as part of the Harm Reduction Information System for Northern Ireland.The Welcome Organisation Drop in Service was an out-of-hours, outreach only service which had been in place since October 2020. The planned expansion of this service to include a stationary site at Townsend Street was halted with the site closing in October 2024. Following relocation and additional staff training this site is now preparing to re-open needle exchange.Queen’s Quarter Housing opened a needle exchange in May 2023, however, due to high staff turnover the site closed in March 2024. This site has now re-engaged with PHA and are preparing to re-open needle exchange.Extern’s Ormeau Centre opened needle exchange in July 2024 and remains opened.In 2023-2024, 602 unique service users accessed these sites for needle exchange.In 2024- 2025, 447 unique service users accessed these sites for needle exchange.These sites are no longer within the pilot phase. The pilot has demonstrated the viability of NSES being placed within temporary accommodation provision. The PHA continues to work with all hostels where there is evidence of a need for needle and syringe exchange services and to consider the most appropriate type of service provision to reduce the spread of blood borne viruses, reduce needle discards and engage people who inject drugs in harm reduction services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when the last review was undertaken on (a) the number of chefs or cooks, per central production kitchens; and (b) their workload; and (ii) what is the policy on the number of schools, per chef or cook permitted.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the employment of its catering staff, including determining the number of staff required, their working hours and their deployment based on the number of children and young people using its school meals service.  The EA aligns its delivery of these responsibilities with its relevant guidelines. An EA school meals kitchen that produces up to 200 meals per day will be allocated a catering manager. For every additional 200 meals produced in a kitchen, an additional cook will be allocated by the EA. This allocation of staff resource is based upon the number of meals to be produced by the kitchen and is not influenced by whether these meals are for consumption onsite or transported to another school. The EA reviews the numbers of meals produced in each school kitchen on a monthly basis to ensure appropriate allocation of staffing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when the last review of school meals was completed, to test whether meals provided by a central kitchen are arriving at an appropriate temperature, as per The Food Hygiene Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006; (ii) where the records confirming delivery temperature are kept; and (iii) whether these records are available for public scrutiny.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Catering Service carry out temperature checks every day as per the HACCP (Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point) guidelines, in line with Food Standards Agency requirements and the requirements of the Food Hygiene Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006. Temperature checks are carried out on the delivery, storage, preparation, holding and service of all food products. In addition, transported food items are temperature checked on arrival at the dining centres and before they are served.In accordance with environmental health requirements, temperature records are kept in the relevant production kitchen and the receiving dining centre for a 12-month period.  Temperature records form part of internal audits carried out the by EA Catering’s Quality Assurance Team. Councils’ Environmental Health Officers will also check on temperature records during inspection visits. Anyone wishing to view temperature records should engage in the first instance with EA Catering.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the standard of transport boxes procured for school meals; (ii) whether the transport boxes are solely insulated boxes or the type that can be powered to reheat; (iii) the number of each type of box, used to transport school meals across Northern Ireland, broken down by central production kitchens.
answer_text: The Food Standards Agency (FSA) does not stipulate the specifications of a box used to transport food. The FSA states the appropriate temperatures that different food type should be at when its holding state. All food products supplied by the Education Authority (EA), either chilled, ambient or hot, are transported in insulated sealed boxes which are specially designed for the transport of meals from production centres. The EA uses both insulated and digital reheat boxes for the delivery of transported meals.All transport boxes use by the EA are of the highest specification available with one-piece seamless, double walled, high density polyethylene construction.  All boxes have a sealable lid to ensure temperature control is maintained. The boxes keep products at the required holding temperature for four hours.The EA has 579 production kitchens delivering meals to 494 dining centres across Northern Ireland. The number of boxes used each day is dependent on the menu.  The EA estimates that it has 12 transporting boxes per dining centre and therefore up to a total of 5,928 boxes are in use within the EA at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether he will review the number of catering staff in each central production kitchen; and (ii) the number of school hubs being catered for per chef or cook.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the employment of its catering staff, including determining the number of staff required, their working hours and their deployment based on the number of children and young people using its school meals service.  The EA aligns its delivery of these responsibilities with its relevant guidelines. An EA school meals kitchen that produces up to 200 meals per day will be allocated a catering manager. For every additional 200 meals produced in a kitchen, an additional cook will be allocated by the EA. This allocation of staff resource is based upon the number of meals to be produced by the kitchen and is not influenced by whether these meals are for consumption onsite or transported to another school. The EA reviews the numbers of meals produced in each school kitchen on a monthly basis to ensure appropriate allocation of staffing resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all school meals, transported by bus or taxi, are contained in appropriate transport boxes that comply with Food Standards Agency requirements.
answer_text: My Officials and I are continuing to engage with key stakeholders and carry out research on the phased review of taxi policy and legislation. Officials are planning on having an options paper, on potential changes, for me to consider by the end of the year and a full consultation on any proposed changes should be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of the amount spent (i) to maintain the fleet of school buses operated by the Education Authority (EA); and (ii) on new buses for the EA fleet, in each of the past ten years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that due to its retention policy, financial records are only available for the past seven financial years. (i) The amount spent to maintain the EA school bus fleet in each of the last seven financial years is detailed in the table below:Financial YearMaintenance costs for EA school bus fleet 2018/2019£5,058,9502019/2020£5,643,9762020/2021£3,899,6962021/2022£4,664,8912022/2023£5,020,7502023/2024£5,751,3042024/2025£6,642,178(ii) The amount spent on purchasing school buses for the EA fleet in each of the last seven financial years is detailed in the table below: Financial YearAmount spent on purchasing new EA buses2019/2020£2,099,0002020/2021£13,248,0002021/2022£19,735,0002022/2023£5,744,0002023/2024£75,0002024/2025£2,594,000

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail if not-for-profit organisations applying to the Olympic Legacy Fund can chose not to use Crowdfunder UK to raise their 35 per cent portion of costs.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI, which will be responsible for delivering the Olympic Legacy Fund on behalf of my Department. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Not-for-profit organisations applying to the Olympic Legacy Fund must use Crowdfunder UK to raise 100% of project costs.  Subject to a project meeting the conditions of award, Sport NI will pledge 65% of project costs to an applicant’s crowd funding campaign.  The remaining 35% must be raised through pledges from the applicant’s ‘crowd’ (or community).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to clarify the term rolling funding, as promoted during his responses to questions on his statement on the Olympic Legacy Fund on 16 September 2025; and (ii) to detail if he has agreement that any underspent funding can be taken forward across financial years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI, which will be responsible for delivering the Olympic Legacy Fund on behalf of my Department. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Olympic Legacy Fund is a rolling programme within this financial year (2025/26).  Unlike a traditional grant programme with a short application window, the Olympic Legacy Fund will remain open from now until the end of the financial year, or until the full programme budget has been exhausted.  However, all funding must be committed by 31st March 2026. “Sport NI do not anticipate any underspent funding from the Olympic Legacy Fund.  However, there is no agreement that any underspent funding can be taken forward across financial years.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what procurement processes were followed to identify and decide Crowdfunder UK as the fundraising platform the Department for Communities is using for the Olympic Legacy Fund.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI, which will be responsible for delivering the Olympic Legacy Fund on behalf of my Department. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“On 1st April 2024, Sport Northern Ireland awarded a contract to Crowdfunder UK for cloud-based software/hosting to support grant administration.  Crowdfunder UK were procured using Crown Commercial Services ‘G-Cloud 13 Call-Off Contract’ and have been contracted for 36-months (until 31st March 2027) with an optional 12-month extension.  The Olympic Legacy Fund has been appended to services delivered, as per terms and conditions of contract.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm what support will be provided to football clubs that do not have a home ground and were unsuccessful in applying for stadia funding under the Northern Ireland Football Fund, such as Ards FC.
answer_text: All clubs who were not selected in the prioritised cohort have been offered the opportunity to contact my officials to arrange a meeting to discuss their applications and receive feedback. I have also offered to meet those unsuccessful clubs who feel such a meeting would be beneficial and am due to meet representatives from Ards football club shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will provide a copy of the job description and terms and conditions for the position of Director of Mechanisms of Government.
answer_text: A copy of the Candidate Information Booklet for IRC316271, Director of Machinery of Government is attached separately and can be viewed in the Assembly Library. This booklet contains information about the main duties and responsibilities of the role (P10) and terms and conditions (P17-20).Further information is also available in the Civil Service Handbook available at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/articles/northern-ireland-civil-service-handbook.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide a copy of the business case detailing the rationale for the addition of the post of Director of Mechanisms of Government for the Department for Communities.
answer_text: The Director of Machinery of Government post was established in May 2023 following the realignment of business areas within the Department.  A business case was not required, as the role was funded through the suppression of other posts that were deemed surplus to requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he will take to support the increasing number of adults and children who are living with food insecurity, as confirmed in the Trussell report on Hunger in Northern Ireland published in September 2025.
answer_text: My Department takes the following actions to support those living with food insecurity:	Leading on the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy which contains a range of interventions which will help mitigate impacts, reduce the risk of falling into poverty and support all those impacted to exit poverty, including those who are living with food insecurity.	Provides annual funding in the region of £7.6m to the independent advice and debt sector which play a critical role in addressing poverty. 	Consulting on a refreshed Support Framework for Independent Advice and Debt Services. This will strengthen collaboration and improved referral pathways with other community supports including food banks and social supermarkets to ensure citizens have early access to advice services for holistic sustainable outcomes.Allocating in the region of £2.86 million for 2025/26 to support Social Supermarket projects in each Council area. Administers the unique Discretionary Support Scheme to provide short-term financial support by way of interest free loans or non-repayable grants, to help with living expenses and essential household items as per below. https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support	Delivers a wide range of employability support through our frontline Jobs and Benefits Offices and employment programmes. 	Offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check potential entitlements to social security benefits, additional support and services through Make the Call Service. 	The NI Housing Executive assists tenants who are struggling financially and can trigger the delivery of the Making Your Money Work service to mitigate the immediate impacts of financial issues which create food insecurity/poverty.	The NI Housing Executive administers the Sustaining Tenancies Grant Funding Programme to voluntary, community and social enterprise organisations to deliver projects that support the sustainability and resilience of Housing Executive tenancies.	The NI Housing Executive administers the Homeless Prevention Fund to alleviate and prevent homelessness for customers presenting to front line teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide a copy of any guidance provided to (i) the Executive; or (ii) their Departmental Finance officers regarding (a) cross departmental programmes; and (b) related budget lines.
answer_text: ANSWERIn April 2025 my officials published Guidance to Departments on Pooling of Budgets, however there are other guidance documents available that may relate to (a) cross departmental programmes; and (b) related budget lines. These are listed below:Managing Public Money NIConsolidated Budgeting Guidance 2025-20262025-26 In-Year Monitoring of Public Expenditure GuidelinesDear Accounting Officer letters Finance Director letters

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when a review of speeding fines will be undertaken; and (ii) why a speeding fine is less than an on-street parking fine.
answer_text: I have recently received briefing and advice from officials on a number of speeding related measures, including a review of penalty fines for speeding. I am now considering the next steps and way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will reinstate the Northern Ireland Environment Agency signs confirming the Area of Special Scientific Interest that stretches from behind the New Quays, Portavogie to the short walkway and on to South Shore at Ratallagh, Cloughey.
answer_text: Earlier NIEA signage at this location was temporarily erected at Outer Ards Area of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI), due to a number of fly tipping incidents and to provide details for the public to report any concerns.  While one sign on the dunes at the Southern Shore was removed in April 2023, after falling over due to the exposed location and unstable substrate, the other signs were checked recently and remain in position. NIEA is not required under the Environment Order to install or replace ASSI signage. My officials are engaging with Ards and North Down Borough Council (ANDBC), which has advised of its plans to install permanent environment and educational signage along the Outer Ards Coast, including Portavogie Dunes, Ratallagh. ANDBC has agreed with NIEA officials to include details on its coastal signage for reporting any concerns about Outer Ards ASSI to NIEA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commit to (i) reviewing the Home to School transport arrangements for children living in temporary accommodation, to ensure these children are able to access school without facing additional costs; and (ii) updating this as an action in the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) is responsible for the Home to School Transport Policy and the Education Authority (EA) for the operation and delivery of school transport. The EA assesses applications for transport assistance based on a pupil’s current home address, in line with the DE policy, and when families move house or school during the school year they must submit a new application for transport. The DE transport policy acknowledges that the application of the criteria in the general transport policy may not always be appropriate and therefore it enables the EA to consider cases where transport may need to be provided in exceptional circumstances, which can include where a family is forced to relocate to a temporary address at short notice for reasons beyond their control. Each family's circumstances are different and I am content that the EA’s exceptional circumstances process is an appropriate way in which it can consider each case on an individual basis and where needed, provide transport assistance for pupils experiencing homelessness and those living in temporary accommodation. I will, however, consider if further actions relating to the support provided for children and young people to enable them to access education in these difficult circumstances should be included in the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a copy of the assigned responsibilities given to each of the Junior Ministers, in this mandate.
answer_text: Junior Ministers are appointed under a Determination made in accordance with Section 19 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 which specifies that the function of the Junior Ministers is to assist the First Minister and deputy First Minister in the exercise of their functions in relation to The Executive Office.  They may therefore be involved in all aspects of the department’s work.  Junior Ministers have specific responsibility for taking the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill through its Assembly legislative stages.  They also chair the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Oversight Board. The Board reviews the implementation of the Strategic Framework, related Delivery Plans, and manages interfaces across government Departments and the public sector. In addition, Junior Ministers work has included two visits to The Executive Office in Brussels; speaking to Motions in the Assembly; answering Oral Questions during Question Time to the First Minister and deputy First Minister; attending Business Committee on behalf of the Executive; and meeting with stakeholders associated with key Executive objectives such as Good Relations projects and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls strategy.  They regularly engage in significant work with NSMC and BIC Institutional bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work being carried out with banks to create a more streamline and relevant application process for organisations in the Voluntary and Community Sector to update and open accounts.
answer_text: ANSWERLegislative authority on banking matters is the responsibility of the Treasury.I have raised the difficulties the community and voluntary sector are experiencing in maintaining and opening banks accounts directly with the then Chief Secretary to the Treasury in March 2025, when I wrote to him sharing my department’s published 2024 Banking Roundtable summary report. Given its legislative authority on banking matters, I asked the Treasury to work to provide solutions to the issues being encountered by the Voluntary and Community Sector.  Through my officials’ engagement with UK Finance, the banking industry body, it has advised that it launched the Voluntary Organisation Banking Guide in July last year. This is a dedicated website developed in collaboration with several charity and voluntary groups, regulators, and 13 banks offering tailored guidance on structuring, opening, and managing accounts. In addition, the Charity Finance Group (CFG) hosts a Charity Banking Forum whereby banks meet on a quarterly basis with a network of charity and voluntary organisations, to discuss ongoing challenges that the banks and sector can collaborate on. I understand that NICVA and the local Chairty Commission are both represented on that. In July this year, the CFG published a new practical toolkit designed to support those charities that need to raise an issue or concern with their banks. However, I understand that the Community and Voluntary sector is still experiencing a range of banking issues. While NICVA have acknowledged to my officials that some banks are taking steps in the right direction, they have indicated that it is still too early to determine whether these changes will result in meaningful improvements for voluntary organisations.I will raise this issue at my Banking Roundtable event later this year where stakeholders from the Voluntary and Community and banking sectors will be in attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) an update on the review of the operation and charging mechanisms of property management companies and letting agencies; (ii) what penalties are being considered; (iii) how the industry will be monitored and (iv) who will be responsible for such monitoring.
answer_text: My Department conducted a detailed scoping report relating to the issue of shared property management including the operation of property management companies. That report explored the issue of service charges and outlined options as to how the industry could be regulated. I have considered this work and accept that reform is desirable. I am engaging with my officials to determine what further progress can be made on the regulation of shared property matters.The operation and charging mechanisms of letting agencies, including how they may be monitored is a matter for the Minister for Communities. I understand that there are no plans for regulation in this area at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on how his Department will meet climate change objectives, particularly moving social homes away from home heating oil usage.
answer_text: In line with the requirements of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, my Department will exercise its functions, so far as is possible to do so, in a manner that is consistent with the achievement of the emissions targets of the Act.In relation to social homes, a review of the Decent Homes Standard is underway. Currently, the Standard requires social housing providers to ensure that homes have ‘efficient heating’ but does not specify the heating system type. If a social housing provider is updating a heating system in a property, then the provider must comply with current Building Regulations, which are the responsibility of the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with (i) the Minister of Finance; and (ii) Minister for Infrastructure, to ensure building regulations are updated to (a) reduce carbon emissions, (b) meet the highest practicable Energy Performance Certificate standards and (c) deliver climate change requirements.
answer_text: While I have not had any direct discussions with the Ministers for Finance or Infrastructure about Building Regulations, I would refer you back to my response on AQW 29871/22-27, which detailed numerous regular cross departmental meetings during which this subject was discussed.Updating Building Regulations is however a cross-cutting subject which impacts several departments.  Any proposed updates should therefore come before the Executive for consideration and agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the preparations being put in place to invest in appropriate measures to provide accommodation for people who are homeless before winter 2025-26.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive will continue to meet its statutory duties under the Housing (NI) Order 1988. Anyone who is statutorily homeless will be provided with temporary accommodation. The Housing Executive is regularly engaging with a range of partners to expand its portfolio of temporary accommodation.The Housing Executive also engages with partners to deliver the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP). The SWEP ensures availability of accommodation to individuals sleeping rough when there is an increased risk of death due to the weather e.g. temperatures fall near or below freezing. The Housing Executive monitors weather conditions as part of SWEP arrangements and upon the requirement for a SWEP, will engage with providers to deliver additional provision via options such as crash beds.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for a review of the impact of his removal of intimidation points from the social housing selection scheme; and (ii) to provide any evidence that the decision has led to appropriate ranking and allocation of social housing for those experiencing violence or risk of violence, or domestic abuse.
answer_text: The removal of Intimidation Points from the Housing Selection Scheme has ended the overriding priority that intimidation had over similar serious and enduring reasons for homelessness, enabling all those who are victims of violence or at risk of violence or abuse for any reason, including domestic abuse, to access the same categories of points in the Scheme. I am advised by the Housing Executive that a total of 226 Applicants were awarded Intimidation Points during 2023/24 out of a waiting list of 47,312 households, accounting for less than 0.5% of the Waiting List. At the time of removal of Rule 23 from the Scheme on 1st April 2025, 137 applicants were in receipt of Intimidation Points. As of 5th September 2025, 95 applicants were remaining on the Waiting List with Intimidation Points. Further changes to the Housing Selection Scheme, including a review of Primary Social Need points, are planned as part of the ongoing Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations and the cumulative impact of all changes will be examined when fully implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what actions are being taken to ensure that waiting lists for children awaiting assessments for (i) autism; and (ii) ADHD are being reduced in each Health and Social Care Social Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount paid to patients in out-of-court settlements in each year since 2010, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the religion, including those who are of no faith, of children in every school, broken down by (i) church or named religion, (ii) school name; and (iii) school type including: (a) Controlled, (b) Maintained, (c) Integrated, (d) Controlled Integrated, (e) Voluntary Grammar, (f) Controlled Grammar, (g) Irish Medium; and (h) Controlled Irish Medium.
answer_text: The answer has been placed in the Assembly Library. Religion is classified as special category data under data protection legislation and therefore requires enhanced safeguards. Due to the risk of potential identification of individuals, the information at school level has been provided using five broader categories.Information broken down by all religious categories has been provided by the specific management/school types requested for part (iii).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the negotiations with Treasury that he has indicated will enable the Housing Executive to finance a new build programme.
answer_text: The June 2025 Spending Review announcement confirmed that the UK Government and Northern Ireland Executive have agreed to immediately begin negotiations on a full Northern Ireland Executive Fiscal Framework, the scope of which will include Housing Executive borrowing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why the response to AQW 27036/22-27 has been provided in a different way than an almost identical question asked in 2024 AQW 27036/22-27; and (ii) to ask that the information is updated and presented in the same format as the 2024 answer.
answer_text: AQW 27036/22-27 asked only for individuals whereas AQW 19059/22-27 asked for people and households; and AQW 27036/22-27 asked for a breakdown by household type which was not sought in AQW 19059/22-27.Since this was the case, the answers given and the format used to present the information was necessarily different and was presented by the Housing Executive in the most appropriate format based on the information you had requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 27036/22-27, as the deposited paper for this answer does not include the second set of tables referred to could he ensure the requested details are deposited in the Library.
answer_text: I can confirm that an updated response to AQW 27036/22-27, which now includes the second set of tables referred to, is being laid in the Assembly Library to replace that provided on 29th August 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) which business area within the Department for Communities paid for the research into the Vice President of the United States of America's genealogy and lineage; (ii) any subsequent publishing costs; and (iii) the total amount spent on this exercise including any Civil Service staff time.
answer_text: The Public Record Office of Northern Ireland paid for the research into the Vice President of the United States of America’s genealogy lineage, the total amount spent for research and publication: The Family Footsteps of J.D. Vance was £2,251. The total amount spent on the exercise was £5,589 including Civil Service staff time.This project was part of the Department’s work around US250 which highlights the people who travelled from Ulster to the USA and made a huge contribution in shaping the country we know today. The story of JD Vance highlights the influence and spirit of the Scots Irish and whilst record keeping in the 1700s isn’t of the quality that it is today, it has not deterred people like the Vice President from declaring their Scots Irish roots.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of politicians from the United States of America who have received funding form his Department for genealogy and lineage research in the past 10 years; and (a) the name of each USA politician whose genealogy was researched, (b) the amount spent for each piece of research, (c) when it was published; and (d) where it can be accessed by the public.
answer_text: Zero.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department maintains a record of the number of parking penalty charges issued by each local council.
answer_text: From 1st April 2024 local councils have taken responsibility for the enforcement of their own off-street car parks.The Department do not maintain records of Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) issued by local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the amount collected in parking penalty charges by her Department, in each year since 2015, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The amount of revenue generated since 2016/17 from Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) is shown in the table below. This figure is taken from the Department's finance system which cannot categorise PCN revenue by geographical location. The Department’s finance system does not hold data prior to the inception of DfI as a Department, therefore figures are only available from 2016/17 onwards.Year£m16-17 6.5 17-18 6.9 18-19 6.8 19-20 6.4 20-21 3.2 21-22 5.4 22-23 6.8 23-24 7.4 24-25 8.4

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when he met the residents whose homes were targeted during racially-motivated disorder in Ballymena in June 2025; and (ii) the names and position of all elected representatives, officials and Civil Servants present at such meetings.
answer_text: Whilst no meetings took place with the residents whose homes were targeted in Ballymena, I did make an offer via the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to assist in whatever way possible and offered to meet with those affected. I condemned these racially motivated attacks and will continue to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the cut-off date by which capital money released through monitoring rounds can be efficiently and effectively spent on housing in this financial year.
answer_text: There is no firm cut-off date associated with funding confirmation, however, as you will know, it gets much more challenging to deliver programme outputs at short notice later in the year.Ideally, the Social Housing Development program should be fully funded from the outset at the beginning of each financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions being taken to secure additional money for (i) Supporting People; and (ii) homelessness prevention, given the growing number of people awarded Full Duty homeless status.
answer_text: As outlined in my Department’s Budget 2025/26 EQIA I have allocated an additional £3.7m above the 2024/25 Budget position to help bring forward new and extended services which align to the 3 year Supporting People Strategy and aim to prevent homelessness.Of this, £2.7m has been allocated for strategic homelessness prevention and £1m has been provided to Supporting People for new services and the extension of current services.I also bid for an additional £2.2m funding in the June Monitoring Round to cover the increase in Employee National Insurance Contributions incurred by Supporting People scheme providers in 2025/26, however, this bid was not met.I awarded £2.8m of capital and £0.2m resource to the New Foundations Project, to enable young people leaving care and who are at risk of homelessness to be identified earlier, with the aim to provide a safe and secure accommodation solution. £4m of Financial Transaction Capital has also been allocated for the Loan to Acquire Move-on Accommodation (LAMA), to enable homelessness charities to purchase safe, secure, private rental properties to facilitate faster movement thought the homelessness system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the meetings his Department have had with the Departments of Infrastructure and Finance, regarding review and update of building standards, since the introduction of The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As my Department leads on the residential buildings sector for Northern Ireland’s Climate Action Plan my officials have discussed building standards on a number of occasions via the Residential Decarbonisation Coordination Group, which is co-chaired by officials within my Department and includes representatives from the Department of Finance.  There have also been numerous regular cross departmental meetings since the introduction of the Climate Change Act (2022) Northern Ireland, during which the subject of building standards was discussed.  As these meetings are too numerous to list, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Disability Strategy and Disability Work Strategies will be put out for public consultation.
answer_text: I can confirm that both the Executive’s Disability Strategy and my Department’s Disability and Work Strategy will be issued for public consultation. The timing of these consultations will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a breakdown of the monies allocated in this financial year for (i) Ulster Scots culture and language; and (ii) Irish language.
answer_text: This table represents funding supplied by DfC and National Museums NI which is a DfC ALB. The Irish Government provides additional funding to support both the Irish Language and the Ulster Scots Language, Culture and Heritage.DfC Programme Funding£Language Ulster-Scots Agency2,000,000Ulster-ScotsUlster-Scots Development Programmes103,000Ulster-ScotsForas na Gaeilge3,600,000IrishIrish Language Development Programmes103,000IrishLíofa50,000IrishGaeltacht Quarter Economic Development Programme136,000IrishALB Funding from National Museums NI6,500Ulster-Scots10,550IrishTotal6,009,050

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the compensation costs incurred following the decision on the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: The judge quashed the decision and the orders, taking effect from the 27 June 2025.  To date there have been no compensation costs incurred.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of meetings there have been between her Department and the Crown Estate regarding the release of licenses for off-shore wind opportunities, since the start of the 2016 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: At present, seabed leasing for offshore renewable energy is not possible as The Crown Estate (TCE) require a decommissioning regime for a leasing round to take place. My Department has therefore had no meetings with TCE regarding the release of areas for leasing. My Department continue to work in close collaboration with other departments and key stakeholders, such as TCE, to develop and deliver the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP). This plan outlines the policy frameworks required to enable Offshore Renewable Energy development in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, should the A5 allocation become available for redistribution, to detail the mechanisms which can be deployed to ensure the money is used in an effective and efficient way.
answer_text: The reallocation of any reduced requirements from the A5 scheme, should these arise, will be a matter for the Executive to consider and agree upon as part of both the in-year monitoring process and the wider Budget 2026–29 process, to ensure that any budget resources that do become available, are directed towards the Executive’s highest priority areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the last date by which the allocation for the A5 project can be reasonably spent within the 2025/26 financial year.
answer_text: Department for Infrastructure (DfI) will need time and space to work through the recent ruling in relation to the A5 in order to fully understand its implications, including in relation to the use of resources allocated for the scheme for 2025-26.Any funding allocated in 2025-26 which is not required should be returned to the centre for reallocation at the earliest opportunity. The reallocation of any Executive-earmarked reduced requirements will be a matter for the Executive to consider and agree upon as part of the in-year monitoring process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline why both the Cookstown and Ballynahinch bypasses were redesigned following the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, when the A5 Western Transport Corridor was not redesigned.
answer_text: I can confirm that neither the Cookstown nor Ballynahinch bypasses were redesigned following the introduction of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.The A5WTC has been continually refined, throughout its design and development, to adopt policies around reduced emissions as far as possible, including contractual commitments, for example PAS 2080:2023 Carbon Management in Buildings and Infrastructure.Recommendations 14 -17 of the Departmental Statement detail my Departments approach to Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  Departmental Statement 2024 (v3.0) | A5 Western Transport Corridor Hub Site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to the introduction of a Young Carers Covenant that agrees to deliver a fair future for all young carers and young adult carers, in line with Carers Trust ten outcomes and as adopted by the Scottish Government, Department for Work and Pensions and Department of Health and Social Care.
answer_text: My Department fully recognises the vital role of young carers and remains committed to supporting young carers wherever possible.  However, many of the challenges faced by young carers extend beyond the remit of health and social care.  A Covenant of the kind proposed would involve cross-departmental consideration and require the input and agreement of other Executive Ministers.   Whilst my Department cannot solely commit to introducing a Young Carers Covenant, I have asked my officials to raise this matter for discussion at the next Supporting Carers Senior Officials Cross Departmental Group, with appropriate involvement of young carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to provide further clarification to an answer to a supplementary question about energy support schemes during Question Time on Monday 30 June 2025, when he referred me to the Minister for the Economy's Energy Strategy; (ii) whether his Department provide energy support through Affordable Warmth Scheme and other funds; and (iii) whether he will consider adding technological solutions that harness surplus renewable energy.
answer_text: Delivering the Fuel Poverty Strategy requires working in collaboration with other Executive Ministers, including the Minister for the Economy in relation to the Energy Strategy. As part of this work on Fuel Poverty, consideration is being given to harnessing renewable energy in a way that directly supports households most in need. I have recently written to EnergyCloud NI to meet with them to hear more about their work and the role it can play in addressing fuel poverty by using excess renewable energy in Northern Ireland. My Department currently provides energy support to low-income households through the Affordable Warmth Scheme and is developing the Warm Healthy Homes Scheme that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. The Fuel Poverty Strategy public consultation was used to consult on key aspects of the new scheme including the use of technological solutions such as low carbon heating, photovoltaic solar panels and battery storage. The consultation responses will be used to inform the scheme design.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to clarify the process for allocation of Intermediate Rental properties, given that a representative from the company operating the scheme on the Department’s behalf confirmed tenants will be chosen based on being drawn out of a hat at the Committee for Communities on 19 June 2025.
answer_text: The Member is incorrect. The representative did not confirm this but simply stated that it is one example of an approach that could be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of child carers that have been assessed for support in the 2024-25 financial year, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Young carer assessments can be completed by the Trusts or the commissioned Regional Young Carers Service. The number of young carer assessments completed during the 20024-25 financial year as reported by each Health and Social Care Trust and the regionally commissioned young carers services as follows: Belfast Northern South EasternSouthernWestern Number of Young carers assessments completed as reported by Trusts in Statutory Function Report83no data available 29604Number of Young Carers assessments completed as reported by Action for Children or Barnardo's 43823603Work is ongoing to differentiate the number of assessments conducted by the contracted regional service providers (Action for Children and Barnardo’s) and those by the Trusts. My officials are working with Northern Trust on their data.Last year the contracted regional service providers delivered direct support for 605 Young CarersBelfast – Action for Children - 109 young peopleNorthern – Barnardo’s – 128 young peopleSouth Eastern - Action for Children 81 young peopleSouthern - Action for Children - 216 young peopleWestern – Barnardo’s - 71 young peopleThe Department provides funding to commission and deliver a Regional Young Carers Service. this is currently provided by Action for Children and Barnardo’s who as part of their service delivery undertake assessments of Young Carers on behalf of HSC Trusts.Barnardo’s operate in the Western and Northern Trust areas, Action for Children operates in Belfast, Southern and South Eastern Trust areas.The following services are commissioned across all five HSC Trusts – Day short breaks- this is a large group activity designed to offer young carers a short break with a focus on fun and socialising with peers. 1-1 therapeutic work – this is 1-1 face time with a young carer. It may take place as part of their assessment, review or ongoing service delivery (including 1-1 therapeutic work or a 1-1 short break session) Group work – this includes 2 or more young carers attending a group where there is a specific focus in response to identified need e.g. mental health group, sibling group. Action for Children also offer Overnight short breaks in Belfast, Southern and South Eastern Trust areas. This is a residential activity that includes an overnight away from home designed to offer a short break with fun and socialising with peers. This may also include focussed group work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what support and interventions are available for young carers in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Young carer assessments can be completed by the Trusts or the commissioned Regional Young Carers Service. The number of young carer assessments completed during the 20024-25 financial year as reported by each Health and Social Care Trust and the regionally commissioned young carers services as follows: Belfast Northern South EasternSouthernWestern Number of Young carers assessments completed as reported by Trusts in Statutory Function Report83no data available 29604Number of Young Carers assessments completed as reported by Action for Children or Barnardo's 43823603Work is ongoing to differentiate the number of assessments conducted by the contracted regional service providers (Action for Children and Barnardo’s) and those by the Trusts. My officials are working with Northern Trust on their data.Last year the contracted regional service providers delivered direct support for 605 Young CarersBelfast – Action for Children - 109 young peopleNorthern – Barnardo’s – 128 young peopleSouth Eastern - Action for Children 81 young peopleSouthern - Action for Children - 216 young peopleWestern – Barnardo’s - 71 young peopleThe Department provides funding to commission and deliver a Regional Young Carers Service. this is currently provided by Action for Children and Barnardo’s who as part of their service delivery undertake assessments of Young Carers on behalf of HSC Trusts.Barnardo’s operate in the Western and Northern Trust areas, Action for Children operates in Belfast, Southern and South Eastern Trust areas.The following services are commissioned across all five HSC Trusts – Day short breaks- this is a large group activity designed to offer young carers a short break with a focus on fun and socialising with peers. 1-1 therapeutic work – this is 1-1 face time with a young carer. It may take place as part of their assessment, review or ongoing service delivery (including 1-1 therapeutic work or a 1-1 short break session) Group work – this includes 2 or more young carers attending a group where there is a specific focus in response to identified need e.g. mental health group, sibling group. Action for Children also offer Overnight short breaks in Belfast, Southern and South Eastern Trust areas. This is a residential activity that includes an overnight away from home designed to offer a short break with fun and socialising with peers. This may also include focussed group work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the role of young carers in developing and updating regulations and legislation on the provision of support for carers; and (ii) what new regulations or legislation (a) has been introduced; and (b) is planned to be introduced as a direct result of this involvement, before the end of the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The Social Care Collaborative Forum has been established to take forward a number of key priorities including a review of carers legislation (inclusive of young carers) to be carried out by 2028.  Young carers interests are fully embedded in this work through the participation of Barnardos and Action for Children in the Forum’s Supporting Carers workstream, however no new carers legislation, or changes to existing carers legislation, will be brought through in the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline (i) what actions he and his Department are taking to identify and provide support for young carers who attend school; and (ii) how is he ensuring every school sector is providing equitable access for young carers to meet his objectives.
answer_text: Departmental officials work closely on an ongoing basis with Action for Children and Barnardo’s Northern Ireland, both of which provide support for young carers throughout Northern Ireland, in order to help raise awareness and facilitate a better understanding within schools around the role of young carers, the challenges they face, and the support available.The Department and the Education Authority’s (EA) Education Welfare Service (EWS) supported Action for Children and Barnardo’s Northern Ireland with the launch of a Young Carers in Education Digital Awareness Pack. The pack, which was circulated to all schools in 2024, included: a letter from the Permanent Secretary to all education settings;a digital poster designed by Young Carers;  EA and Health and Social Care Board (HSC) Guidance - Supporting Young Carers in School; and   Young Carers’ Education Research by Action for Children.In August 2025, the Department will be supporting the launch of Action for Children’s Young Carers’ video, “Teach the Teachers”, which is designed to help raise awareness of the role of young carers among teachers. It shows how to identify young carers in their classroom and what support they can offer to a Young Carer.  The EA has agreed to share this video at School Principal events, with the intention that it will be shared further and throughout schools in Northern Ireland.The EA is introducing a new specialist post to support Young Carers regionally.  The postholder, who is expected to be appointed in July 2025, will aim to ensure consistency of approach and positive outcomes for Young Carers in our educational settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of regulations of short-term rental properties in Northern Ireland, following the reported request made by the Minister for Communities at a Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council meeting in February 2025.
answer_text: My Department’s and Tourism NI’s powers to regulate the short-term let visitor accommodation sector are set out in the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Tourism Order”). The 1992 Tourism Order requires all visitor accommodation in the North to be certified by Tourism NI before they can operate, as well as once every four years, to ensure that specified minimum quality standards are met.My Department and Tourism NI are reviewing the visitor accommodation certification scheme and its associated statutory criteria regulations which outlines these required minimum quality standards.The 1992 Tourism Order does not provide any power for my Department or Tourism NI to limit, cap or control the number or spread of properties providing accommodation for visitors. Our role is focused on minimum quality standards.That being said, following the publication of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy, DfC officials have contacted relevant Departments to discuss a collective approach on related issues. My officials are engaging in this process.DfC will take forward research on the issue of short-term/holiday lets, their potential impacts, and their interaction with the planning process. This work will be done in conjunction with the Department for Instructure and my Department.I agree with the Communities Minister that it is important that we have a proper evidence base to understand what impact holiday lets have on local housing supply, if any, before we make any policy decisions on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) programmes; or (ii) actions, her Department is currently delivering or planning with employers, to encourage and promote employment of people with disabilities.
answer_text: My department offers a range of practical, financial and other support to those with disabilities across the further and higher education sectors, as well as to those participating on vocational training programmes, to enable them to build the skills and qualifications needed to compete for employment opportunities at all levels.  We have also been working closely with the Department for Communities (DfC) on the development of a Disability and Work Strategy which will aim to improve employment outcomes for disabled people and people with health conditions. In terms of specific programmes and actions my department is currently or planning to deliver, with employers, Breaking Barriers in education supports learners to gain qualifications and work experience to enable progression into the labour market. Working with local community-based organisations the programme provides additional support, both in skill development and overcoming barriers for those with a disability or who are neurodiverse. These career progressive programmes include supported work placements and guided learning in skills required to sustain longer term employment. We have a strong apprenticeship offering but whilst there are over 13,000 apprentices in the north, people with disabilities are currently underrepresented. I want to minimise and, where possible, remove barriers to take-up of apprenticeships by those individuals. My Department's Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund will support two dedicated disability projects with allocations of up to £100,000 per project over the next 18 months to address these inclusion gaps. Assured Skills Academies provide pre-employment training courses for individuals aged 18 and over who meet the eligibility criteria and therefore are open to people with disabilities. The training is industry-recognised and on completion of the academy a job interview is guaranteed for existing vacancies with the participating companies. My Department also delivers Skills for Life and Work, a vocational education and training programme focused primarily on Entry Level and Level 1, for young people aged 16–17 (extended age eligibility for young people with a disability and young people who are care experienced - up to 22 and up to 24 respectively) who have left school with low or no qualifications. Typically, more than half of participants present with a registered disability. To support those participants who have a disability, there is an extra training premium, additional time on programme, and the Department also currently contracts separately for Disability Support Services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department is monitoring (i) the Education Authority; and (ii) Controlled Schools Council, to ensure both are complying with the Integrated Education Act 2022 and supporting the intentionality of integrated education and integrated ethos.
answer_text: Only the Education Authority (EA) has a duty to encourage, facilitate and support Integrated Education. Neither organisation has a specific duty to support the intentionality of the Integrated ethos. The EA and the Controlled Schools Support Council (CSSC) both have a strong history of working in partnership with those in Integrated Education to support and develop the sector. For example, in 2025 the EA and NICIE launched ‘The Framework for Integrated Education’, a self-evaluation toolkit specifically designed for Integrated schools to help them evaluate the nature, extent, and quality of their Integrated ethos and set targets for its development.The Department monitors compliance with these actions through routine engagement with each body, approval and monitoring of organisational business plans by DE policy leads and through the regular arm’s length body accountability arrangements.  The Department sought and used input from both organisations during development of the Section 10 report on Integrated Education published on 26 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the number of Airbnb properties registered, broken down by local council area, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: Tourism NI holds information on the number of certified properties by accommodation category and by Council area. The eight accommodation categories set out in the legislation are Hotels, Guest Houses, Bed & Breakfast Establishments, Self-Catering Establishments, Hostels, Bunk House, Campus Accommodation and Guest Accommodation. Tourism NI, however, does not hold any information on the number of accommodation providers who promote their properties on particular marketing platforms such as Airbnb.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what actions she is taking to develop a structured approach to employer engagement to enhance and support the number of people with disabilities into employment, as called for in Mind the Gap, Supporting people with disabilities into employment, Summary Report - June 2025.
answer_text: My department offers a range of programmes, many of which are in conjunction with employers.  We have also been working closely with the Department for Communities (DfC) on the development of a Disability and Work Strategy which will aim to improve employment outcomes for disabled people and people with health conditions and the SEDI sub-committee of the Skills Council includes membership from both the business community and disability advocates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children with a statement of Special Educational Needs that will be enrolling in Primary 7 at the start of the new academic year 2025-26, broken down by council area.
answer_text: The Department does not yet have access to information regarding the Special Education Needs of pupils who will be enrolling in Primary Year 7 in September 2025.  This data will be collated as part of the 2025 school census data collection on 10 October 2025.School Census information in the table below shows the number of pupils enrolled in Year 6 in primary schools as of census day 2024 broken down by Local Government District (school location).  While pupils will continue to undergo the statementing process throughout the year, the available information may be used as an indicative estimate of the upcoming Year 7 cohort. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Supported Employment model based on the Supported Employment Quality Framework is being integrated into future strategies and action plans, in order to address the gap in the number of people with disabilities in the workplace compared to people without disabilities.
answer_text: A draft Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland is currently with the Executive for consideration. The draft Strategy proposes actions the support personal journeys towards, into and progressing in employment; and recognises the importance of the Supported Employment Model amongst a wider portfolio of employability support for disabled people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of the evaluation of the Employment Strategy for People with Disabilities that ended in March 2021.
answer_text: The Department did not publish an evaluation of the previous Employment Strategy for People with Disabilities: ‘Supporting Equality Through Inclusive Employment’.In co-developing a new Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland, the Department undertook a dedicated workshop with members of the Disability Employment Stakeholder Forum in June 2021, to review and identify lessons learned from the previous Strategy and its implementation. The outputs of this workshop shaped the forward approach to co-production of a new Strategy for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the careers (i) guidance; and (ii) support, provided by her Department in special schools to encourage employment options for young people with disabilities.
answer_text: My Department’s Careers Service offers impartial and personalised guidance and support on an all-age, all-ability basis, provided by professionally qualified Careers Advisers. This support empowers clients to make their own learning and career decisions, based on up to date labour market information, knowledge of career pathway options and an understanding of their own strengths, talents, skills and interests.Through established partnership working with post-primary schools, including special schools, careers services are prioritised for pupils with barriers and those who are considered vulnerable and most in need. This includes those with a disability, a medical condition and a statement of Special Educational Need. For young people with a statement of Special Educational Need, Careers Advisers attend Transition Plan Meetings and their subsequent Annual Reviews at the invitation of the school. This support allows young people, and their parents and guardians, to make informed decisions about the options available to them on the range of educational, training and employment opportunities. Careers Advisers continue to work with pupils beyond school, assisting with next steps and planning of their career pathway to ensure they are fully supported in the transition from school to adult provision and employment. The provision of appropriate, timely and specialised careers guidance is key to enabling individuals of all ages and abilities to make informed career decisions. The recently published Careers Action Plan recognises this and sets out a programme of collaborative work which will improve the careers ecosystem for the benefit of all individuals. Building on dedicated initiatives already under way, specific action will be taken to strengthen the support for children and young people with special educational needs and to improve pathways to appropriate learning and employment opportunities. My Department’s Careers Service has a key role in this work. You may also be aware of the proposals for reform that I outlined to Assembly colleagues on 16th June which, with the right investment, will improve and enhance the support and provision for learners with special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 28695/22-27, whether he will establish a data sharing arrangement with the Department of Health to establish the numbers of children and young people on the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service waiting list experiencing (i) chronic; and (ii) severe chronic, school absence.
answer_text: Both my Department and the Education Authority would welcome any data sharing agreement which might assist policy and service areas to work collaboratively to support children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline when the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015, Part 2 Installation of Domestic Microgeneration Equipment, C1 (b) will be reviewed and updated to provide permitted development for the installation of more than 14 square metres of stand-alone, ground level, solar panelling for a domestic premises.
answer_text: I have currently no plans to review the permitted development rights for the installation of Domestic Microgeneration Equipment at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there are any actions he can take to accelerate Foundation Programme Allocations for 2025; and (ii) what actions will he take for future years.
answer_text: I have assumed that your question relates to the Medical Foundation Training Programme. My officials work closely with colleagues in the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency, who manage this programme on my Department’s behalf, to ensure that Medical Foundation training posts are allocated as quickly as possible to applicants, as soon as posts are made available. Medical Foundation training posts are allocated on a UK-wide basis via a national 4 nation recruitment and allocation process, and NIMDTA work closely with other UK jurisdictions in relation to allocations to the Northern Ireland programme. My officials and NIMDTA will continue to work closely with UK counterparts to ensure Foundation Training Programme places for future years are allocated as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Independent Review of Environmental Governance in Northern Ireland - Interim Report.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to clarify (i) the terms and conditions for the Housing Policy Panel; and (ii) how appointments to the panel are made.
answer_text: The Housing Policy Panel was established as part of the Tenant Participation Strategy to represent social housing tenants in the development of departmental policies that are relevant to their interests. No parameters were set for its operation or formation by the Department. As the appointed Independent Tenant Organisation, Supporting Communities NI were responsible for establishing the Housing Policy Panel and for developing its Terms of Reference as part of the first action plan for the Tenant Participation Strategy. Tenant representatives are nominated by Registered Housing Associations from their tenant engagement groups and from the Housing Executive’s Central Housing Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline if those found guilty of participation in violent disorder in early June against the Community in Ballymena, Portadown and other areas will be investigated by the Minister’s benefit fraud team, in order to ensure that any person who has been found guilty of taking part in such disorder has not claimed they have limited capacity for work.
answer_text: My Department will consider information provided by law enforcement agencies and will initiate benefit fraud investigations on those found guilty of participation in the recent violent disorder across Northern Ireland, where there is credible evidence suggesting that those individuals have been fraudulently claiming benefits. If an individual is suspected of benefit fraud, reports can be made in confidence online via nidirect or by contacting the Benefit Fraud Hotline on 0800 975 6050.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the quality standards adopted by their Department, in relation to how many days they take to respond to letters received by Committees of the Assembly.
answer_text: We endeavour to provide an agreed response within the timescales requested by committees and our internal timescales will be set to reflect this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm if he is considering to permit for-profit businesses to operate in the social housing market.
answer_text: I am not considering permitting for-profit businesses to operate in the social housing market. It is specified in legislation that Registered Housing Associations may not trade for profit.However, Registered Housing Associations may leverage private investment.  As part of my work on the Housing Supply Strategy I am considering how to develop further sources of investment to better augment social housing delivery.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to confirm what plans are in place to (i) regrout or (ii) appropriately rejoint the cobblestones around Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is committed to maintaining Parliament Buildings and the surrounding grounds in keeping with its Grade A listed status. As part of this ongoing effort, work will take place during the summer recess to repoint the cobblestones and replace damaged flagstones.These repairs have been scheduled to follow the recent specialist stone cleaning, as some of the damage was worsened by the use of the cherry picker during that process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, following HM Treasury's Green Book Review 2025, whether he will implement a similar update to appraisals in his Department to enable the more effective assessment of place-based interventions such as the (i) roll out of a national retrofit programme; or (ii) placement of new build developments.
answer_text: My officials have been liaising with their Treasury counterparts on the Green Book Review and I understand that Treasury intends to publish the updated Green Book early in 2026. The Green Book is the foundation of my Department’s economic appraisal guidance. If there are any updates to the Green Book that could be applied here to aid more effective assessments, including those relating to place-based interventions, my officials will consult with colleagues in other departments when considering the implications for appraisal guidance here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department has evidence of any negative impact on the (i) educational attainment; and (ii) attendance levels, of children who are on a waiting list to see Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service, broken down by Health Trust area.
answer_text: Neither my department nor the Education Authority holds the data by Health Trust Areas on waiting lists for CAMHS. If a child is awaiting an assessment from CAMHS and cannot attend school while awaiting assessment, schools will follow the Code of Practice and utilise services such as Education Psychology and Education Welfare Service to support need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Chancellor of the Exchequer's intention to introduce a 10-year rent settlement, to detail any plans he has to replicate these rent controls and align increases in social housing with Consumer Price Index plus 1 per cent from 2026.
answer_text: Rent setting for Registered Housing Associations (RHAs) lies outside of my remit and it is a matter for individual Housing Association Boards to determine appropriate rent levels and to consider and approve any rent increases.With regards to rent setting for NIHE, increase requests are considered by my Department and DoF on an annual basis and are subject to evaluation and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following HM Treasury's Green Book Review 2025, whether he will implement a similar update to appraisals in his Department to enable the more effective assessment of place-based interventions such as the (i) roll out of a national retrofit programme; or (ii) placement of new build developments.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to update the approach to appraisals in my Department. The HM Treasury Green Book Review was published on Wednesday 11 June 2025. The review notes that HM Treasury will establish a new joint taskforce to develop the approach to place-based business cases and oversee their implementation. It is too early to say if, or how, the approach to appraisals within the Department for Communities may change. Appraisal guidance for NICS Departments issues centrally from the Department of Finance and my Department will engage with the Department of Finance on the issue.
