answer_text: Health and Social care Trusts follow the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP), to manage autism referrals. IEAP is a regional guidance document issued by the DHSSPS, updated in June 2020.(i) Table 1 below details on the number of children awaiting anADHD assessment in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in each of the last ten years, broken down by years waiting as requested.Within the South Eastern H&SC Trust, there are a number of children who may be awaiting assessment for ADHD who are on the Autism Neuro-developmental waiting list, this is a list for children and young people who are being assessed for Autism, but based on referral information have an increased level of complexity; this may be due to trauma and or possible ADHD presentation.Table 1.Wait List- Year End (31 March)< 1 year> 1 year> 2 years> 3 years> 4 years> 5 years> 6 yearsTOTAL2015/16113      1132016/17143<5     1442017/1819611     2072018/19227229     4562019/2021498<5    5202020/21354113     4672021/224561556112022/23 423342<5    7662023/24*56749810    10752024/25*582652377<5   1613(ii) The South Eastern H&SC Trust does not remove young people from the new patient ADHD waiting list because they have turned 18years of age; one consultant will see and assess these young people if they choose to remain on the Children’s services waiting list. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the barriers to identifying modern slavery and human trafficking in the context of child criminal exploitation.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 14	 - AQO 2226/22-27 – WITHDRAWN  ANSWER  On 11th June, my Department launched the findings of our co-commissioned research project examining barriers to recognising Child Criminal Exploitation (CCE) as a form of Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking (MSHT) in Northern Ireland.   The report highlights that, despite increasing awareness, formal identification through the National Referral Mechanism (NRM) remains limited. It points to Northern Ireland’s unique social and political context, where harm linked to paramilitary and organised crime is often normalised, which presents a significant challenge in recognising exploitation among victims, families, and professionals. It also notes that practitioners may lack confidence in using the NRM, particularly where child protection systems already exist, and that safety concerns further deter referrals. The report suggests that the current definition of trafficking, with its emphasis on movement, may not fully reflect the nature of CCE in this context.  My Department acknowledges these findings and will carefully consider the recommendations as part of our ongoing work to strengthen the response to CCE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities,pursuant to AQO 2109/22-27, (i) to clarify comments he made which intimated the the Mears Group are housing illegal immigrants; and (ii) for his assessment of the legal obligation for the UK to protect refugees and asylum seekers.
answer_text: Your assertion is incorrect, and I would refer you to the answer I gave to AQO 2109/22-27Immigration is an excepted matter and the lead government department with responsibility for legal obligations to asylum seekers across the UK is the Home Office.  Where an individual has been accepted by the Home Office as a refugee and given leave to remain in the UK, in most cases this will bring an entitlement to access public services including housing and homelessness assistance for which my Department is responsible in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Help with Health Costs Scheme consultation.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the progress of the Review of Higher Education Funding.
answer_text: It is clear that the current funding model does not work for our local universities and university colleges, and it does not work for students. That is why I am committed to a review of Higher Education Funding. I want to work in partnership with the five higher education institutions in the north and related stakeholders to address the funding challenges faced going forward. My officials have therefore been engaging with key stakeholders over the past number of months regarding the potential scope and approach to the review.  Officials are currently finalising the draft terms of reference and will brief me in the coming weeks. The review is likely to involve three specific strands of work:an assessment of financial sustainability, with a first stage comprising a financial needs assessment; the teaching funding model; and support for students.The Financial Needs Assessment is expected to be the first element of the review to commence. This will assess the financial needs of each of the five local higher education institutions for the coming three to five years.I intend to ensure key stakeholders including the institutions, students and staff are fully involved as the Review progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the next phase of the review of (i) energy efficiency requirements and; (ii) related areas of Building Regulations, will be consulted upon.
answer_text: My Department consulted on a discussion document on amendments to the Building Regulations to improve energy efficiency and address related areas in new buildings or where buildings are substantially modified. The consultation ended in December 2023 and since then officials have been working with key stakeholders in other Departments and our Building Regulations Advisory Committee (NIBRAC) to analyse the responses to the discussion document and bring forward proposals for potential amendments to the Building Regulations. This will provide an indication, to the public, industry and others with an interest, of the proposed approach across those areas and timeframes for future uplifts. I plan to seek Executive approval for publication of the response to the discussion document before the summer recess with a full legislative consultation on the subsequent proposed uplifts in these areas anticipated by the second half of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the preparatory work for updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004; and (ii) when the legislation will be brought forward.
answer_text: The public consultation on the proposed eligibility criteria for passporting Universal Credit (UC) recipients closed on 10 March 2025. I welcome the openness, feedback and engagement from all who have participated in the consultation. 		Officials are currently analysing the responses and compiling a Consultation Summary Report which will be published on the Departmental website in the coming weeks. It is intended that the legislative process will be completed by summer.				My aim is to ensure that those most in need automatically receive help to access these very important health services. 				As you are aware, whilst the legislative process is ongoing, an interim measure remains in place for UC recipients who need financial assistance with their health costs. This is via the Health Service Low Income Scheme (LIS), where UC claimants are required to apply annually to gain a potential exemption or remission from their health costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many occupational therapists have received training on Housing our Ageing Population Panel for Innovation design principles, to ensure appropriate age-friendly and accessible standards are included when considering housing adaptations as part of their assessment for the Disabled Facilities Grant.
answer_text: The principles of the Housing Aging Population for Innovation Design Principles (HAPPI) are embedded in core training provided to Occupational Therapists at undergraduate level in Ulster University. However, there is no specific training related to HAPPI principles. Consequently, it is not possible to quantify the number of occupational therapists who have received training.The Department has also worked with Ulster University in the development of a postgraduate module covering Housing Adaptations and Design for Community Living which is delivered for 20 Occupational Therapists each year. This module focuses on internal and external access to/from and within the home to help therapists identify design solutions to inform their clinical decision making as part of the assessment process for housing adaptations. It is relevant to all housing tenures and is not explicit to the assessment for Disabled Facilities Grant which is available for private sector homes only.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the draft revised design standards for housing for older people (category 1 and category 2) will be implemented and made mandatory.
answer_text: In addition to the current draft design standards for Category 1 and Category 2 housing, my Department is piloting ‘Housing our Ageing Population: Panel for Innovation’ (HAPPI) design recommendations which provide for generous space standards, provision of natural daylight, good ventilation, ample storage, and design of communal spaces to encourage social interaction. Once sufficient cost and post-occupancy data is available from additional schemes, a decision will be made whether to bring this forward for approval, subject to Social Housing Development Programme budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the second religion question will be removed from the census in line with the rest of these islands.
answer_text: NISRA, which has responsibility for planning and delivering a census here, plans to consult with users in advance of the next census to identify their need for future statistics relating to population and housing. This will include development of census questions for 2031.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the 2024 Good Relations Indicators report will be published.
answer_text: The 2023 report was published on 12th December 2024 and the Good Relations Indicators 2024 report is planned for publication in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the funding mechanism used for (a) the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland; and (b) the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People; and (ii) whether there is a ring-fenced funding allocation for the commissioners in the budget rather than dependence on monitoring rounds.
answer_text: My Department funds the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People through Grant-In-Aid.  There is no ring-fenced funding allocation for either Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans in place to action the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People recommendations for Own Motion Powers to represent children and young persons.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People’s recommendations for Own Motion powers are currently under consideration.  This is being taken forward in the context of ongoing work to address the recommendations of the ‘New Decade, New Approach’ review of NICCY.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any progress has been made on an independent review of the legal duties owed to homeless people and the development of legislation for a statutory duty to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: Whilst I have not made any commitment to a comprehensive independent review of the legal duties owed to individuals experiencing homelessness, there may be opportunity to change the underpinning legislation to better support our policy aims.		My officials continue to engage with the Housing Executive and stakeholders in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) people; and (ii) households, who are (a) on the social housing waiting list; (b) in housing stress; and (c) with Full Duty Applicant Status, on 31 March and on 30 September in each of the last ten years, broken down by local council area and age of person or household.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW/27036/22-27 the information has been placed in the assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) people; and (ii) households, living in temporary accommodation on 31 March and 30 September in each of the last ten years, broken down by (a) accommodation type and (b) age of person or household.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024	Data on placements in temporary accommodation from 2018, by household and accommodation type, is available in Section 3 of the accompanying tables. However, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what his Department estimates the additional cost in: (i) Employer National Insurance Contributions; and (ii) National Living Wage increases will be, in 2025/26 for organisations funded through the Supporting People programme and broken down by thematic group.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘Please see estimates detailed below in respect of the Employer National Insurance contribution increase and the National Living Wage increase in 2025/26 for organisations funded through the Supporting People programme, broken down by Supporting People Thematic Group.             SP Thematic GroupEmployer National Insurance Element (£k)National Living Wage Element (£k)Disability 9011,351Homelessness8901,335Older People 246369Young People 1782672,2153,322’ For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his Department estimates for what the additional cost in: (i) Employer National Insurance Contributions; and (ii) National Living Wage increases, will be in 2025/26, for projects funded through the homelessness services budget.
answer_text: My Department does not hold staffing and payroll details of organisations that it grant funds to provide homelessness services on its behalf.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the additional £1 million proposed in the 2025/26 budget for pipeline schemes for the Supporting People programme can solely be used to fund new schemes, or can it be used to address currently existing cost pressures in Supporting People projects.
answer_text: The proposed additional allocation of £1.0m will help bring forward new and extended services which align to the 3 year Supporting People Strategy and aim to prevent homelessness. These additional services will provide more housing support to vulnerable people and provide the opportunity to improve their quality of life and achieve greater independence within this valued Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the outcome and summary of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment within the Department for Communities 2024-25 Budget Consultation will be published.
answer_text: My Department has now published its Budget 2024-25 Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) Final Decisions Report and a Summary of Responses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what financial support, for costs related to increased employer National Insurance contributions, is being provided by your Department for those Community and Voluntary and Social Enterprise organisations delivering services to meet your priorities
answer_text: Given the rising cost pressures facing the Voluntary and Community Sector, my Department’s funding for community development, infrastructure support, advice and debt advice services will increase by £2.8 million in the 2025/26 financial year.  This represents a 9% uplift on the 2024-25 funding level.My Department is not the employer for any Voluntary and Community organisations it funds; however, organisations are encouraged to avail of the substantial increase in HMRC Employers Allowance announced in the Budget.  This is expected to offset some of the increase to Employers National Insurance Contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what financial support, for costs related to increased employer National Insurance contributions, is being provided by his Department for those Community and Voluntary and Social Enterprise organisations delivering services to meet his priorities
answer_text: Unfortunately, given the resource pressures my Department is facing in 2025-26, and the inadequate level of additional funding being provided to address the increased national insurance costs, I am unable at this time to provide any additional support to assist those Community and Voluntary and Social Enterprise organisations delivering services on behalf of my Department.My Department currently estimates that it is facing c£300m of inescapable pressures for 2025-26.  When other high priority and desirable pressures are included Education’s budget shortfall increases to c£360m.The estimated cost to my Department and its arms-length bodies of the National Insurance changes which took effect in April 2025 substantially exceed the additional funding the Department of Finance is proposing to allocate to meet this pressure for 2025-26.  I will continue to make the case to both the Northern Ireland Executive and to HM Treasury that Education in Northern Ireland remains underfunded, and I will continue to seek additional funding via monitoring rounds during 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what financial support, for costs related to increased employer National Insurance contributions, is being provided by his Department for those Community and Voluntary and Social Enterprise organisations delivering services to meet his priorities
answer_text: ANSWEREach public sector contract will have its own specific terms and conditions and therefore any contract modifications necessary to ensure delivery whilst achieving best value for money will be considered by the Department on a case by case basis. Such modifications can include increases to prices required by Voluntary, Community and Social Enterprise (VCSE) organisations delivering services, as a result of the increases in employer National Insurance Contributions (NICs).Decisions on increases in grant funding to meet pressures arising from the increase in employer NICs would also be taken on a case by case basis by the relevant public body.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what financial support for costs related to increased employer National Insurance contributions is being provided by his Department for those community and voluntary and social enterprise organisations delivering services to address his priorities.
answer_text: My recent announcement on 13 May outlined funding being made available to help support non-statutory providers of health and social care to meet their additional National Insurance bills. This package provides a projected £40m covering areas such as social care providers and hospices as well as family health service providers (GPs, dentists, community pharmacies and opticians). Within this package, some projected £2.1m of funding has been made available to support Community and Voluntary organisations with these additional costs.  A significant element of this funding relates to contracts held between HSC Trusts and individual Community and Voluntary organisations and the application of funding will be based on these local contractual arrangements.My announcement sets out that the provision of this funding is not without a financial hit to my Department, but I have taken this action rather than leave our health and social care partners to deal with the financial pressure of increases to Employer National Insurance contributions by themselves.This is in line with my three year plan for Health and Social Care which includes stabilisation of services amid the current severe financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is considering bringing ground mounted solar photovoltaic panels for domestic buildings into the scope of building inspectors.
answer_text: The scope of building inspectors is not within the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any work being carried out to make the Northern Ireland Civil Service recruitment processes more flexible in order to fill the number of vacancies across all Departments more effectively and efficiently.
answer_text: ANSWERIn the last year my Department has filled over 3,000 vacancies across the Civil Service to support departments. The NICS has recently launched its People Strategy for 2025 -2030. One of the key initiatives of this strategy is to simplify and streamline the recruitment process to ensure we continue to recruit the best people and address capability gaps ensuring a continued supply of talent in the Civil Service. This work has commenced with several improvement initiatives and a review of relevant HR Policy already underway.Work is also progressing to design future processes to support the implementation of a new integrated Finance and HR system as part of the Integr8 programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail any work being carried out to address the Fiscal Council’s observations on departmental income through fees and charges; and (ii) for his assessment of how transparency can be improved.
answer_text: Whilst each department is responsible for the regular review of their fees and charges, the Executives’ Budget Sustainability Plan included a commitment for the Executive to agree to the regular strategic consideration of income generation measures. Now that the 2025-26 Final Budget has been agreed officials have commenced substantive work on the Strategic Income Review and will consider information gathered from departments on their income generation measures.It is expected that this Review will improve the transparency of departmental income, including fees and charges, and will be completed in advance of a multi-year budget being set.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how many victims of Domestic Violence are currently housed in temporary accommodation; and (ii) how many are (a) living with children and (b) not living with children, broken down per council area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024Section 3 of the accompanying tables contains a breakdown of temporary accommodation placements by household type, as well as the number of children housed in temporary accommodation by type and age. However, I am unable to provide a breakdown of the number of domestic violence victims currently housed in temporary accommodation by council area, or whether they are living with children or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions are being taken to update the permitted development planning (i) fees; and (ii) criteria to include larger ground mounted solar photovoltaic panels for domestic use.
answer_text: Part 2 of the Schedule to the Planning (General Permitted Development) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 sets out the permitted development rights for the installation of domestic microgeneration equipment. Class C provides permitted development rights for the installation, alteration or replacement of stand alone solar within the curtilage of a dwellinghouse, subject to certain conditions and limitations, including a 2 metre height restriction, without requiring a planning application. Permitted development does not attract a planning fee.I have currently no plans to review these permitted development rights at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm the average amount of time a victim of Domestic Violence is housed in temporary accommodation before securing a permanent tenancy.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024Table 3.5 contains details on the duration of temporary accommodation placements; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown of the average length of stay for victims of domestic violence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions are being considered to resource safe spaces within homes, of any tenure, for victims of Domestic Violence.
answer_text: The Housing Executive operates the Sanctuary Scheme, which is a multi-agency initiative to enable households, at risk of domestic abuse, to remain in their homes by installing additional security measures which may include a “Sanctuary Room” within the property. 		This scheme is currently only available to Housing Executive tenants. However, the Housing Executive recently published a Domestic Abuse Action Plan which includes plans to explore the potential of extending the Sanctuary Scheme to Housing Association tenants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm if he will bring forward legislation to enable social housing providers to legally transfer a tenancy to a victim of Domestic Violence, in situations where their abuser is the named tenant.
answer_text: Whilst bringing forward new legislation would be for me to consider, I have sought input from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive on the current position and their view on the need for such a change. They have advised me as follows.“The power to assign a tenancy from one person to another (on a non-voluntary basis) has been vested in the courts. Social Housing Providers will follow any court order to facilitate a statutory assignment of tenancy. This is referred to as assignment by Court Order. A court may issue an order for a secure or introductory tenancy to be assigned from one person to another under:Article 26 of the Matrimonial Causes (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 (property adjustment orders in connection with matrimonial proceedings) Article 21 of the Matrimonial and Family Proceedings (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (property adjustment orders after overseas divorce, &c.)Paragraph 2 of Schedule 1 to the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (orders for financial relief against parents), or Part II of Schedule 2 to the Family Homes and Domestic Violence (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 (orders transferring certain tenancies on divorce, etc., and on separation of cohabitees)Part 2 of Schedule 15, or paragraph 9(2) or (3) of Schedule 17, to the Civil Partnership Act 2004 (property adjustment orders in connection with civil partnership proceedings or after overseas dissolution of civil partnership, etc.)In addition, the existing tenant may make a request to assign the tenancy voluntarily or in the case where the tenant (in a domestic abuse case) terminates the tenancy the social landlord has discretion to consider any remaining partner for an exceptional award of tenancy. Verification of the circumstances will be required.Where the tenant has left the property, possession may also be sought on the basis for non-occupation, but this would be a last resort in the absence of a suitable agreed solution. When possession has been granted the landlord may consider any remaining victim for an exceptional award of tenancy.There have been a small number of cases brought to the Housing Executive’s attention involving joint tenancies in which the Social Landlord cannot unilaterally vary the tenancy, the courts would remain the (correct) option in those cases. In these cases, the victim remained in the property with the alleged abuser absent (in law the joint tenancy endures so long as one of the joint tenants continues to occupy the property as their principal home). It would be the Housing Executive’s view that the power to unilaterally vary tenancies has been correctly vested in the courts and it would not be seeking such a power.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how the work with the Department of Health on carers and recognition of unpaid carers will be included in his upcoming Disability Strategy.
answer_text: Work is underway to ensure that the Disability Strategy reflects the current needs of Deaf and disabled people alongside the Executive’s priorities. I can confirm that as part of this process, engagement is taking place with the Department of Health on a range of issues that will be reflected in the draft Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of all minutes, or reports, of meetings his Department has had with LGBTQAI+ representatives in relation to plan 2 of the LGBTQIA+ Strategy, or Sexual Orientation Strategy, since 2020, including (i) dates; and (ii) a list of attendees, including departmental role.
answer_text: No meetings have taken place in relation to a Stage 2 plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the definition of vulnerable, as used in his recent Fuel Poverty Strategy consultation.
answer_text: While the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy does not provide a precise definition of vulnerable, it refers to households with low incomes, a long-term health condition or illness exacerbated by cold and damp conditions in their home. These households are most at risk of fuel poverty and most likely to be negatively impacted by it. Good quality, warm, secure housing is vital to both mental and physical health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department plans to identify vulnerable households in relation to the proposals outlined in the recent consultation on a Fuel Poverty Strategy to target and improve energy efficiency in vulnerable households.
answer_text: In the draft Fuel Poverty Strategy, I recognise the importance of collaborating and building partnerships to identify and provide advice and long-term sustainable support to those most in need.   To raise awareness and identify vulnerable households to support, I will utilise and build upon collaborative partnerships and referral pathways between Government, local Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector. I am committed to building a partnership approach with the voluntary and community sector and health professionals, raising awareness, utilising best practice and providing specialist support when needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in line with commitments his Department made to the recommendations from the LGBTQI+ Strategy Expert Advisory Panel in December 2020, (i) whether work on the development of the stage 2 plan is progressing; and (ii) whether he will provide the Assembly with an update on progress of the stage 2 plan.
answer_text: An Expert Advisory Panel was set up in 2020 to make recommendations to the Department on key themes for development of a Sexual Orientation Strategy.  One of the panel’s recommendations was to develop an action plan for delivery of the Strategy, once it had been agreed by the Executive. This Action Plan would be reviewed and refreshed after five years leading to the development of a Stage 2 plan.  Following consideration of the work previously carried out to develop each of the Social Inclusion Strategies, alongside unprecedented pressures on resources across Executive departments, I decided to move forward on development of the Strategies on a phased basis, with work now well progressed on the Anti-Poverty and Disability Strategies. Further detail on the timetable for the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy will be confirmed shortly. The Expert Panel’s recommendations will be considered as part of this development, including future review arrangements. Final decisions on content of the Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he or his Department have discussed carer poverty for those citizens who continue to provide over 35 hours of caring per week, but are no longer able to claim or apply for Carers Allowance due to having reached State Pension age.
answer_text: Where a person has reached State Pension age overlapping benefit rules prevent Carer’s Allowance being fully payable at the same time as another income replacement benefit, such as the State Pension.  I met with Carers NI earlier in the year and a number of issues were discussed, including, carer poverty and overlapping benefit rules.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the Speaker’s comments on 7 April 2025 on Assembly Questions relating to arm’s-length bodies, whether he will reconsider advising Members to go to an arm’s-length body directly for any future queries when responding to an Assembly Written Question.
answer_text: I will continue to comply with the Speaker’s ruling on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any proactive support being provided in 2025-26 to support unemployed people over the age of 50 into employment.
answer_text: I am committed to helping people into meaningful employment, including those aged 50+.  My Department’s highly successful JobStart 50+ pilot scheme has supported 52% of participants to gain permanent employment by the end of their time on the scheme.  Unfortunately, budget for this Treasury funded pilot programme has not been recurrent in 2025-26. I am in the process of agreeing my Department’s 2025-26 Budget allocations. In allocating my Budget, one of my priorities is employment support, to help people of all ages to overcome barriers faced in moving into, retaining and progressing in employment.  My Budget allocation plans will be announced in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the Assembly Pension Trustees will circulate an updated pension statement to all Members, outlining any changes to individual pensions as a result of the US trade tariffs announced by President Trump and subsequent market fluctuations on pension investments.
answer_text: As set out in the Assembly Commission’s response to AQW 11190/22-27, the annual pension benefit statements for Members are a matter for the Trustees of the Assembly Members’ Pension Scheme.  However, the Assembly Commission is aware that the 2024-25 annual benefit statements were discussed by the Trustees at their meeting on 2 April 2025, and work has already commenced on collating the information for this year’s exercise.  The Trustees will issue the annual statements in due course.Regarding the impact on accrued pension benefits due to the uncertainty in global markets, this is also matter for the Trustees.  However, it may be helpful to note that the Assembly Members’ Pension Scheme is a Career Average Revalued Earnings, defined benefit scheme, where benefits accrue at a fixed percentage, currently 2%, of annual earnings.  Accrued benefits are then revalued each year in line with inflation (CPI).  The pension benefits payable on retirement, are therefore not dependent on the level of contributions made by a Member, or the final size of the pension pot.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of how many social homes will start in 2025-26 following the budget announcement, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The Executive agreed its final Budget 2025-26 on 3 April. I am currently prioritising the capital allocation for my Department and will provide an allocation for the Social Housing Development Programme.It will then be for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, as commissioning body for the Social Housing Development Programme, to confirm the location and amount of starts that can be achieved this year with the available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why his decision to announce the funding for groups to mark the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence was made in the media rather than in the Assembly Chamber under Standing Order 18A or Standing Order 18B.
answer_text: During Question Time on 31 March 2025, I indicated that I was continuing to explore opportunities within my Department to support organisations participating in the semiquincentennial commemorations, and that I intended to make further announcements in the near future.  The announcement was made during a meeting of the US/NI Cultural Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of students undertaking sign language courses in (a) Queen's University Belfast; and (b) Ulster University; (ii) how many teaching hours are provided; and (iii) the curriculum content for each.
answer_text: In the current academic year 2024/2025 there are currently a total of 160 student registrations on Sign Language Courses in the Language Centre at Queen's University.The teaching hours for each course are:Level 1 Award in British Sign LanguageBSL 101 - 20 hours of in-person teaching BSL 102 - 22 hours of in-person teaching BSL 103 - 22 hours of in-person teaching Level 2 Certificate in British Sign LanguageBSL 201 - 30 hours of in-person teaching BSL 202 - 35 hours of in-person teaching BSL 203 - 35 hours of in-person teaching Currently, all sign language courses in The Language Centre are linked to the examining body 'Signature's' course outline, with Signature accrediting the exams at the end of each level of the course. The curriculum content can be found at:https://www.signature.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/BSL1-2023.pdfhttps://www.signature.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/BSL2-2023-1.pdfAt present Ulster University does not have any sign language courses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to confirm the process to introduce Sign Language to the key stage two curriculum.
answer_text: Within the statutory requirements there are a range of opportunities for schools to include Sign Language within their curriculum provision.The Area of Learning ‘Language and Literacy’ places the value on the ways in which learning another language such as sign language can improve children’s communication and literacy skills and can help them to develop cultural understanding, which is one of the key elements that underpin the Northern Ireland Curriculum objectives.Schools can currently choose to teach Makaton, British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language. They have the opportunity to access resources and support from organisations including the National Deaf Children’s Organisation and Action Deaf Youth. Additionally, schools can use their delegated budget to engage the services of any organisation for delivery purposes.My Department recognises the importance of British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) in fostering inclusivity and accessibility within our education system. A Sign Language Bill was introduced to the Northern Ireland Assembly on 10 February 2025, fulfilling a commitment in the Executive’s New Decade, New Approach agreement. My officials will want to consider the impact and operational practicalities of the proposals, including the impact on the curriculum. I will continue to evaluate the role of sign language in supporting communication and accessibility for all learners. It should be noted that any changes to the statutory curriculum would require legislative amendment to the Curriculum Minimum Content Order, which would require consultation and engagement with relevant stakeholders.Furthermore, I expect to receive a report in the coming weeks on the curriculum review, following which I will reflect on any recommendations therein.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the revised code of practice on obtaining information under the Social Security Administration Act 1992 will be published
answer_text: The revised Code of Practice will be laid before the Assembly by 30 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any progress made to develop and deliver a Citizens' Assembly process, as committed to in New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: We recognise and fully support, the role that civic society plays in supporting policy making, through co-design and co-production approaches.Ministers have recently written to Executive colleagues to seek their views on the proposed reestablishment of the Civic Forum on a compact, flexible and agile basis, which would complement the growing direct involvement of stakeholders in co-design in policy formulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the report resulting from the consultation on Gender Pay Gap, which closed in February 2025, will be published.
answer_text: My officials are analysing the responses to the public consultation on Gender Pay Gap Reporting regulations and they will then present the findings to me.  My Department’s response to the consultation will be published over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many of the 600 homes being purchased by the Housing Executive are former Housing Executive homes previously sold under the House Sales Scheme.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 25142/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any engagement they have proactively taken to examine the work done by Civic Initiative, specifically in relation to its proof of concept of a Citizens' Assembly.
answer_text: We  recognise and fully support, the role that civic society plays in supporting policy making, through co-design and co-production approaches.Ministers have recently written to Executive colleagues to seek their views on the proposed reestablishment of the Civic Forum which would complement the growing direct involvement of stakeholders in co-design in policy formulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any review of the Student Maintenance Grant has taken place to align it with the living wage to help students to meet the increasing cost of living in Northern Ireland, such as rent.
answer_text: I understand the financial pressures that students face and am pleased that in January my predecessor announced a 20% uplift in maintenance loan support for full-time undergraduates for the coming academic year.The review of higher education funding is due to commence in the coming months and will consider, among other issues, the financial support available to students including the maintenance grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will ensure a purpose built student accommodation regulation will be included in the development of private rented sector regulations to protect tenants, including enabling students to end a contract by giving one months' notice and to require building owners or a management company to provide at least 24-hour notice before entering rooms.
answer_text: My Department does not regulate purpose-built student accommodation. The Department for Economy has responsibility for higher education policy in relation to teaching and research, university managed student accommodation including halls of residence and purpose-built student accommodation.As this is outside the scope of private rented sector regulations, I will not be including the suggestions put forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount their Department has been spent on (i) solicitors' fees; and (ii) court costs in relation to legal challenges on the Irish Language Strategy in the last ten years.
answer_text: The amount spent by the Executive Office in the last ten years on legal challenges in relation to the Irish Language Strategy is set out in the table below. It is not possible to disaggregate solicitors’ fees and court costs incurred by the department. Figures have been rounded to the nearest pound and are net of VAT.Financial Year2016/2017£3,4702017/2018£44,4742018/2019£1472021/2022£2,4562022/2023£38,262TOTAL£88,809

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) why the funding mechanism for Foras na Gaelige, stemming from an agreement decided at North South Ministerial Council, has not yet appeared on the agenda for the Executive; and (ii) for their assessment of evidence provided to the Committee for Communities at its meeting on 13 March 2025 that the funding mechanism has been blocked six times from being discussed at the Executive.
answer_text: While there is no paper in circulation which refers specifically to the Foras na Gaeilge funding model, this matter is part of a wider range of issues concerning the funding mechanisms for North South bodies. The recommendations which have been made to the Executive, following a discussion at NSMC to explore, remain under consideration

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to provide a copy of the processes and procedures used by his Department with third parties involved in co-designing and co-producing policy; (ii) to detail any mechanisms in place to measure (a) the effectiveness of the co-design and co-production process; and (b) feedback from organisations, bodies and individuals who have worked with his Department, for example on any of the social strategies or the Irish Language Strategy.
answer_text: There is no central Departmental policy and procedure for policy areas and projects involved in co-design and each will have their own, tailored to their specific requirements. Similarly, each area will have their own review and feedback mechanisms in place – for example the Dept of Finance’s Business Consultancy Services (BCS) was commissioned to undertake a review of the Social Inclusion co-design process. A copy of the BCS Review will be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any progress that has been made to replace Section 10b permits for community transport operators with permits similar to Section 19 and Section 22 of the Transport Act 1985.

To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for a timeline confirming the progress of the support for low carbon heat in the residential sector consultation; and (ii) when her Department's report will be published.
answer_text: My department consulted on future support of low carbon technologies in residential properties, which closed 10th January 2025. The views provided will support my department in the development of policy to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat. Officials are currently analysing the responses provided and my department plans to communicate a feedback report on the consultation through the department's website and social media platforms this summer. Any support scheme to emerge from the consultation would fall under the usual due diligence of government policy making. This is a multi-stage process of which this public consultation is an initial step.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the outcome of the support for low carbon heat in the residential sector consultation will feed into the (i) review of planning legislation; and (ii) Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My department is fully committed to working across government and playing its part in supporting the development of long-term sustainable energy solutions that support the delivery of Climate Change targets and growing our green economy. The Path to Net Zero Energy provides the policy framework to decarbonise energy and to mobilise the skills, technologies and behaviours required to transition our economy to net zero.  The recent consultation on support for low carbon heat in residential buildings will ultimately seek to introduce a support scheme like those operating in Britain and south of Ireland. As part of this policy development process officials from my department worked with Department for Infrastructure colleagues to amend permitted development rights for domestic heat pumps with changes coming into effect from July 2023.  This policy initiative aims to stimulate the heat pump sector which remains in its infancy. The development of skills and support for investment in the low carbon heating sector will be one component to help feed into the delivery of the Housing Supply Strategy however other policy initiatives will be required to drive change in how we build new private and social homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce legislation, similar to the Planning and Infrastructure Bill going through Westminster, to remove the barriers to development that is limiting housebuilding in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that the planning system in the North operates in an efficient and effective way. My department has been working collectively with local government, who are responsible for over 99% of all local and major planning applications, and other stakeholders to improve the planning system through the Planning Improvement Programme.The programme has already delivered positive changes including targeted updates to legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists to improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process. My department is also conducting a review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to enable the planning application process to be further streamlined. Ongoing work with statutory consultees has already resulted in performance improvements and this engagement will continue.My officials are currently engaging with individual council planning authorities and other key stakeholders in the planning system with a specific focus on identifying challenges in current development management (planning application process) practice and procedures, as well as individual performance related matters, to inform further opportunities for efficiencies and improvements to the system.My officials will engage with officials in the British Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government regarding this Bill and any potential learnings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when welfare mitigations will be reviewed to consider if the existing mitigations are meeting needs or if there are any gaps in support.
answer_text: The Executive agreed, on 5 December 2024, to extend the existing mitigations schemes for another 3 years. There is no current timetable for a further review of mitigations.It will be for the Executive to decide on any future mitigation package of new measures and to consider the allocation of the additional funding such a package might require.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any cross-departmental actions that have been agreed with each Department, though the Housing Supply Strategy, to reduce the future cost to mitigate the Social Sector Size Criteria.
answer_text: Following publication of the Executive Housing Supply Strategy in December, cross-Departmental structures have been established to begin work on the first short term Executive Action Plan. This work will consider the range of actions to be prioritised over the next few years to address the whole system issues impacting on housing supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the proposed parking fines by each Health and Social Care Trust will be included in Penalty Charge Notice or Private Parking Charges legislation.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks, including those at Health and Social Care Trust sites, nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23336/22-27, to detail how her response complies with the Executive's concordat with the Community and Voluntary Sector to deliver effective partnership working and delivery of volunteering opportunities.
answer_text: The licensing arrangements for drivers of not-for-profit community transport providers are a legal requirement in support of road safety. They are not altered by the Executive’s concordat with the Community and Voluntary sector to deliver effective partnership working and delivery of volunteering opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to an answer provided during the Urgent Oral Question on 10 March 2025, to provide a list of the dates he raised the anti-poverty strategy at the Executive.
answer_text: I wrote to Executive Colleagues on 24 September 2024 and 17 December 2024. My department does not retain records of occasions the strategy may have been discussed under AOB.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) who is responsible for the delivery of strategies that are published as an Executive Strategy; and (ii) how the Executive Committee plans to ensure openness and transparency across all Departments to ensure an Executive Strategy is delivered.
answer_text: Executive Strategies are developed by a lead minister, in consultation with other relevant Ministers, and brought to the Executive Committee for approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how toileting across the health service will be inclusive if single sex-only provision is maintained in hospitals and other facilities, given that most carers have to help the person they care for, who may be of the opposite sex, with toileting needs especially when attending health appointments.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts provide accessible toilets across its facilities and these are gender neutral. They facilitate access for disabled people (both visible and invisible), those with long term conditions or those who require support from a carer (regardless of their sex).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he will report and update the Committee for Communities on the delivery of future social strategies, including the anti-poverty strategy, specifically on the areas that are being delivered by other Executive Departments.
answer_text: Each of the Social Inclusion Strategies will include reporting and monitoring mechanisms as appropriate, however these mechanisms will be a matter for the Executive to approve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of adults registered for NHS dentistry in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The number of adult registrations at 31st December 2024, the most recent publicly available statistics, is outlined in the table below:Year (as at 31 Dec)No. of Adults Registered2024832,194Source: Business Services Organisation, Information and Analysis Directorate, Department of Health (provided by L Baird – 14 March 2025)Information on the number registered for Health Service dentistry in Northern Ireland by age group is routinely published on a quarterly basis in our Accredited Official Statistics publication: FPS General Dental Services Statistics Quarterly Update.https://bso.hscni.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/dental_quarterly_tables_q3_24-25_published.xlsx

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the 2025-26 draft budget allocation for Resource DEL for languages of £8.5 million is intended to be spent, including a percentage breakdown of the intended spend on (i) Irish; (ii) Ulster-Scots; (iii) Sign Language; and (iv) other languages.
answer_text: All funding requirements will be considered against statutory commitments and ministerial priorities within the final budget settlement. All budget decisions will be subject to equality assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the source of the £11.6 million for Fresh Start Shared Education and Housing, as listed on page 27 of the 2025-26 Draft Budget; (ii) what percentage of this amount is for (a) Shared Education; and (b) Shared Housing; and (iii) why the Department has removed references to Integrated Education from the references to Fresh Start.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Education bid for a total of c.£59.9 million for the remaining Fresh Start Shared and Integrated Education projects in Budget 2025-26.  As only £48.3 million of Treasury funding remains available, the Executive agreed to allocate the additional £11.6 million to Education from mainstream Capital DEL  to meet the bid in full. The shortened title of Fresh Start Shared Education & Housing, reflects the title used by Treasury when providing the funding. It does not preclude the funding being used for integrated education projects. Indeed, £26.7 million of the funding allocated was for integrated schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the UK Government committing to legislate to ban no fault evictions and introducing Awaab's law, to detail any actions he will take during the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate to ensure tenants are not left behind by this legislation not being replicated in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I have no plans to introduce legislation to ban no fault evictions. As you are aware, my department is already taking forward work, as mandated by the Assembly, to increase security of tenure through the introduction of much longer notice to quit periods.  Also, my department is closely following the progress of Awaab’s law and officials are monitoring developments to consider the most appropriate approach here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of reform of the Housing Executive, including plans to split the landlord function away from the policy setting and decision-making authority function.
answer_text: I am continuing to actively push and prioritise a solution on NIHE Revitalisation. The Department of Finance leads on engagement with His Majesty’s Treasury on this matter and I understand it is currently under consideration by HMT officials. This engagement reflects a preference for minimal change to the status of the NIHE and which may not require any further structural reclassification, split or separation of the Landlord and Strategic Housing Authority functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how he will ensure the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) is able to meet the needs of tenants with disabilities, given the 2025-26 Draft Budget does not include a capital allocation for disability adaptations for NIHE homes; (ii) whether he will commit to carrying out an equality impact assessment with NIHE to understand the impact to tenants of failing to provide disabled adaptations; (iii) how many homes are known to need disabled adaptations, broken down by local council area; and (iv) any proposals the NIHE have provided to fund dis
answer_text: Work is ongoing on options to live within the proposed 2025-26 Budget outcome and the impacts of doing so.  As part of this work, an internal exercise to refresh the Department’s forecast 2025-26 requirements has been completed. The information gathered from this exercise will inform my decisions on how the proposed budget should be allocated within the Department.Departments are expected to conduct their own equality screenings and (where necessary) EQIA consultations, based on the outcome of the draft Budget. Work has begun to assess the impact of my Department’s proposed allocations and fulfil all necessary equality obligations. Points (iii) and (iv) are operational matters for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Major Adaptations only - There are 359nr of MAPD’s (Major Adaptations For Persons With A Disability) awaiting starts broken down by council (Pre-Site). This includes schemes at Project Inception, Briefing and Commissioning, Awaiting Statutory Approvals, Pre-site initiation, Pending Procurement, Tender evaluation, On hold, and OT Referral Stage. There are currently 51 on site being delivered.Minor Adaptations only - There are currently 739 minor adaptions which are live jobs with our Contractors to undertake works. These are broken down by Council Area below. CouncilMajor Adaptation TotalMinor Adaptation TotalBelfast74197Derry City and Strabane58106Causeway Coast and Glens46178Ards and North Down3825Newry Mourne and Down3442Armagh City Banbridge and Craigavon2833Lisburn and Castlereagh2446Mid and East Antrim2340Mid Ulster1325Fermanagh and Omagh1126Antrim and Newtownabbey1021Grand total359739Historically the NIHE adaptation programme has been funded by virtue of DfC returning funding generated from NIHE house and land sales back to the NIHE. However, due to DFC funding pressures in the current financial year, this has not happened. Accordingly, the NIHE funded the adaptations programme from rental income receipts in those years. In the event that DfC is again unable to fund the adaptations programme in 2025/26 (as yet unknown), then NIHE could again consider funding this from rental income receipts. In this eventuality, it is possible that investment in the 2025/26 adaptations programme may have to be scaled back (in the wider context of the NIHE Landlord having insufficient financial capacity to meet medium term priority stock investment requirements, as highlighted as part of the NIHE Revitalisation review).”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will (i) commit to ensuring all GP services provide a professional phone line or email for health care staff to enable and improve communications about patient care; and (ii) ensure such proactive communications are built in to future GP contracts.
answer_text: GPs are independent contractors and, as such, are responsible both for making appropriate clinical decisions about patient care and for decisions about the day to day running of their Practice.  This includes the use of telephony.It is recognised that facilitating better communication and clarity of responsibility at the interface between primary and secondary care fosters strong, constructive working relationships and supports a more efficient use of staff time and medical resources, as demand for services continues to grow.My Department has led engagement with primary and secondary care providers to help improve communication and collaborative working across the Primary and Secondary care interface.A key output of this work has been the development of the ‘Working Better Together Consensus and Guidance on the Primary and Secondary Care Interface in Northern Ireland’. The guidance, which builds upon good practice that is already in place, provides a set of clinically led principles to guide how healthcare professionals should engage with each other across new and existing patient pathways and to promote good quality, patient-centred care. The underlying intent of the guidance is to improve relationships and communication, remove unnecessary administrative burdens and bring about a more efficient system to improve patient flow, care and experience.One of the key principals in the guidance is to facilitate easy, prompt communication between primary and secondary care professionals, including encouraging the sharing of administrative contact directories.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Inclusion Health Service will have a permanent location in Belfast.
answer_text: I recognise the important work that Inclusion Health Service offers to those individuals who are homeless and who need access to timely health and social care provision. It is vitally important that the new permanent location is safe for both those accessing and delivering services.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has been actively looking for suitable permanent premises. This work has involved engagement with a range of key stakeholders including the Belfast City Council, Northern Ireland Housing Executive, Community and Voluntary groups and Land and Property Services, as well as with staff and service users of the Inclusion Health Service to get their input on the optimum location for the service. The Trust has also been actively viewing a number of properties and recently one has been identified as potentially being suitable. Trust representatives are currently engaging with the relevant landlord to discuss potential lease of the property, including any specific refurbishment work that would need to be completed before this could be agreed. Given these talks are currently ongoing I am not yet in a position to provide the member with a definitive timescale for when the Inclusion Health Service will be relocated to a new centre but I can assure you this will be done as soon as practicable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the barriers to healthcare experienced by people who are homeless, including the requirement for photographic identification by GPs when, in many cases, identification can only be provided to a person with an address.
answer_text: My Department is committed to understanding and responding to the healthcare needs of those who are homeless in Northern Ireland and the barriers they face.   In January 2025 the Public Health Agency (PHA) initiated a procurement for a contractor to undertake the initial stage of a Homeless Health Needs Assessment across Northern Ireland to ultimately determine strategic priorities for the medium and long term. Unfortunately, no contractors submitted a bid for this work; however, to ensure that some progress is made, PHA has commenced a more limited exercise to ascertain a Health Needs Assessment from the perspective of service providers and that report is expected to be completed by September 2025. Officials from my Department are also involved in the Department for Communities (DfC) led work on the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan (IDHAP) which addresses gaps in non-accommodation services including local enhanced general practitioner services.  One of the actions within the IDHAP relates to the Belfast Inclusion Health Service implementation of the three-year service delivery plan focusing on specific areas of Health and Social Care needs and taking into account the future strategic direction of homelessness and health, including the NICE Guidelines 2022 for integrated health and social care for people experiencing homelessness.A further development in this area is the Complex Lives initiative established by the PHA, Belfast City Council, and the Housing Executive, which is operating and providing support to those who are homeless and at risk in the Belfast area. With a Multi-Disciplinary Team in place, it is providing a whole system, accountable care approach to supporting people experiencing multiple disadvantages with the aim of helping people engage, stabilise, and find a new way forward with their lives. With regards to the requirement for photographic identification by GPs; in the circumstances where a patient would not be in possession of photographic identification (e.g. those experiencing homelessness), the GP surgery can discuss with the patients or their representative(s) what alternative documents are available that would be acceptable. The surgery may contact the Medical Registrations team in Business Services Organisation (BSO) to seek assistance, if required. Where the GP Practice contact BSO about a case like this, BSO would advise the Practice to make contact with the Homeless shelter as it would normally be in a position to verify the person and supply a letter to this effect. My Department continues to review and improve access to healthcare by working alongside other key stake holders to remove barriers experienced by the homeless where possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions she is taking to replicate the driver licensing requirements used in Great Britain for drivers of not-for-profit community transport providers, rather than the need for full commercial driver licenses.
answer_text: My officials have previously engaged with the sector on this issue. At this point in time, I have no plans to change the current licensing requirements for drivers of not-for-profit community transport providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how her Department is reviewing the community transport sector when she only funds a small number of community transport operators; and (ii) what investigation has been carried out on those organisations operating under a Section 10B permit, but do not receive any funding from the Rural Transport Fund or Transport Programme for People with Disabilities.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a phased review of community transport.  The review will only consider the community transport operators and schemes funded by my Department and does not extend to the wider community transport sector across the North of Ireland.  The Department is also reviewing the operation of the Section 10B permits scheme. The Section 10B permits review will examine the efficiency and effectiveness of the permits scheme across the community transport sector, including both funded and non-funded organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Review of Housing Associations Grant will be published.
answer_text: The most recent Total Cost Indicator/Housing Association Grant review has been completed, and I am currently giving my consideration to it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the Barnett consequentials he expects Northern Ireland to receive following the UK Government’s announcement of an additional £350 million in funding for existing affordable housing programmes.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Executive will not receive any additional Barnett consequentials from the announcement of £350 million for affordable housing, as it is funded from existing Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) average; (ii) median; and (iii) longest number of days spent in temporary accommodation by households in the last ten financial years, including 2024-25, broken down by (a) Bespoke Facility of Temporary Accommodation; (b) Crash Dispersed Intensively Managed Emergency Accommodation; (c) Houses in Multiple Occupation; (d) Housing Association temporary accommodation; (e) single lets; (f) Dispersed Intensively Managed Emergency Accommodation; (g) voluntary sector hostels; (h) Housing Executive hostels; (i) hotel and bed and breakfast accommodat
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024The accompanying tables at Section 3 contain details on temporary accommodation placements by banded length of stay and accommodation type; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his objectives and funding for the arts sector from 2025-26 will be based on co-production and co-design with the Arts Council and organisations and individuals delivering the arts; and (ii) to provide the evidence of this consultation.
answer_text: My department works closely with the Arts Council to understand the community value and economic impact that can be delivered by arts activity. This work, along with engagement with Executive Departments, district councils and other stakeholders, is informing the development of the Arts Policy, with which Arts Council strategies, activities and funding will be aligned. The draft Policy will be subject to public consultation. Recognising that the Policy remains under development, I have written to the Arts Council to lay  out my priorities for the Council’s activities for 2025-26. Within those parameters, and in line with its Strategic Plan, the detailed allocation of its funding is a matter for the Arts Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Letter of Expectation, sent by his Department to the Arts Council in week commencing 3 February 2025, means there will be a delay to funding for the Arts Sector from 1 April 2025.
answer_text: The alignment of Arts Council activity with my priorities may have implications for some of its plans and programmes. More generally, the timing of budget allocations to departments, and the need for a 12 week public consultation on an Equality Impact Assessment, mean that final allocation letters to Arm’s Length Bodies are unlikely to be issued until after 1 April 2025. My officials will work with the Arts Council to minimise any delays and disruption.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any research his Department has carried out to review the average income for individuals in the arts sector, such as artists, performers and craftspeople, including where the data can be accessed.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland commissioned research undertaken by Perspective Economics to inform the report ‘The Arts Council of Northern Ireland Working and Living Conditions of Artists in Northern Ireland’. This study sought to understand, as of 2024, what the income determinants and challenges are for artists in Northern Ireland. The report can be accessed via the Arts Council website - https://artscouncil-ni.s3-assets.com/Working-and-Living-Conditions-of-Artists-In-Northern-Ireland.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will make a bid for capital funding for 2025-26 to enable and improve (i) accessibility; and (ii) health and safety, for arts working spaces and audience areas, including studios, theatres and office spaces.
answer_text: The Arts Council of Northern Ireland, as my Department’s Arms-Length Body responsible for the arts, provides advice to my Department of capital needs arising in relation to the arts sector. My decisions on the allocation of my Department’s capital funding allocation are made after analysis and prioritisation of various capital funding proposals in a very difficult budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any issues preventing him from publishing an Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: It remains my intention to present a paper on the Anti-Poverty Strategy to the Executive before the end of March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the damage to forests and country parks as a result of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Many of my Department’s forests have suffered significant damage from Storm Éowyn and the impacts are widespread throughout the estate.  Forests which have been particularly affected include; Drum Manor, Gosford, Tardree, Mount Stewart, Ballysallagh, Tollymore, Castlewellan, Lough Navar, Favour Royal, Mullaghafad and Kesh. Forest Service’s assessment of the extent of woodlands damaged is based on initial reports from local managers, neighbours and the general public.  Also, on drone footage and aerial surveillance which was undertaken in the week following the storm across the forest estate. The full extent of the damage to woodlands has still to be quantified and will require follow-up ground surveys, mapping and analysis of spatial data which is ongoing and will take a number of weeks to complete. In the immediate aftermath of the storm, the focus was to prioritise the Department’s, and available contractor capability, in clearance works to reinstate access for neighbours and businesses.  Also, my Department worked with other agencies to reopen public roads and assist in reconnecting electricity and utility supplies.  We are continuing our work, including with recreational partners, to expedite tree clearance works in areas most affected by reduced recreational provision.My Department’s country park sites were also significantly affected by Storm Éowyn which resulted in damage to woodland areas and many fallen trees.  While access to numerous sites was initially blocked, and some sites were closed in the immediate aftermath of the storm, arboriculture contractors were engaged by my officials and these sites are now open to public access.  There were reports of damage to neighbouring property, however officials continue to work with affected landowners to resolve this.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households living below the poverty line, defined as 60 per cent of median income, that are paying back an advance payment or budget loan issued by his Department in each year for the last five years, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Official measures of absolute and relative poverty for Northern Ireland are derived from the Family Resources Survey. The Family Resources Survey does not hold information on repayment of budget loans or advanced payments issued by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the report or rationale he used to base his decision to set aside the findings and recommendations of the 2022 report from the Anti-Poverty Strategy Co-Design group.
answer_text: The Anti-Poverty Strategy is still in development and final decisions on its content will be a matter for the Executive.Work undertaken by the Co-Design Group under the previous mandate has provided a strong basis for taking this work forward. The final content of the Strategy will be based on and draw from a broad range of resources including inputs from the Anti-Poverty Strategy Expert Advisory Panel and Co-design Group alongside other research.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the targets and measures taken by his Department since it was formed as a result of the Departments Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, to achieve the intentions of the Child Poverty Act 2010.
answer_text: The responsibility for producing and reporting on a Child Poverty Strategy arose from the Child Poverty Act 2010. This was amended and renamed as the Life Chances Act 2010 following the Welfare Reform and Work Act 2016. The legislative responsibility under the Child Poverty/Life Chances Act expired in 2020.
