DfC’s responsibility was to review the Strategy every three years and to report on it annually. Annual reports were collated and published throughout the life span of the Strategy. Annual reports for the last two years of the Strategy  are available at the following links https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-child-poverty-annual-report-2021-2022.pdf  and https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/communities/dfc-child-poverty-annual-report-2020-2021.pdf 	The Executive’s Child Poverty Strategy 2016 was laid before the Assembly on 16 March 2016. On 8 July 2020, it was agreed to extend the term of the Child Poverty Strategy 2016-2019 until May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will add (i) sign language to the modern languages curriculum to deliver access to learning British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language for Key Stage 3 pupils; and (ii) an introduction to sign language in primary schools.
answer_text: Within the statutory requirements there are a range of opportunities for schools to include Sign Language within their curriculum provision. The Area of Learning ‘Language and Literacy’ places value on the ways in which learning another language such as sign language can improve children’s communication and literacy skills and can help them to develop cultural understanding, which is one of the key elements that underpin the Northern Ireland Curriculum objectives.Schools can currently choose to teach Makaton, British Sign Language and Irish Sign Language. They have the opportunity to access resources and support from organizations including the National Deaf Children’s Organisation and Action Deaf Youth. Additionally, schools can use their delegated budget to engage the services of any organisation for delivery purposes.My Department recognises the importance of British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) in fostering inclusivity and accessibility within our education system. While sign languages serve as vital communication tools, they are distinct from spoken modern languages in terms of structure and use.I will continue to evaluate the role of sign language in supporting communication and accessibility for all learners. With the Sign Language Bill currently progressing, my officials will want to consider the impact and operational practicalities of any proposals including the impact on the Northern Ireland Curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the number of higher education teacher training places, requested by the Department of Education for the new academic year starting September 2025, compares to the number of teaching jobs expected to be available in Northern Ireland in 2028.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, full-time Postgraduate Certificate in Education (PGCE) Initial Teacher Education (ITE) courses are one year in duration, while Bachelor of Education (BEd) ITE courses are four years. This means that student teachers commencing a BEd ITE course in September 2025 would qualify in 2029.Each year, my Department sets the overall intake numbers for ITE programmes in Northern Ireland. This process is informed by the Teacher Demand Model (TDM), which projects primary and post-primary teacher vacancies in future years to estimate the necessary intakes into ITE. However, the TDM is only one of several factors considered in decision-making, providing an indicative figure for the upcoming academic year and the subsequent year. Effective workforce planning is crucial to ensuring that Northern Ireland has an education workforce of the right size, with the right skills and structured effectively to deliver the best possible education for our children and young people. My Department, along with colleagues in the Education Authority, is currently developing an education workforce strategy to address these long-term needs. In particular, we will put in place medium and long-term strategic monitoring of teacher supply to anticipate and remediate issues and also review how best to attract high quality graduates to subject shortage areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has determined, in conjunction with the Minister for the Economy, the qualification requirements and student numbers needed to fill the mental health care gap in every Health and Social Care Trust to ensure sufficient places are available in further and higher education to meet need.
answer_text: The Mental Health Workforce Review 2022-2032 was delivered in response to Action 32 of the Mental Health Strategy (MHS). The purpose of the review was to consider the existing workforce and new models of working, with a view to providing a future workforce profile that will meet the needs of the population into the future. It makes several recommendations relating to pathways into the workforce which includes improving undergraduate access to mental health training.Work is ongoing to progress Recommendation 1, to fully cost the future workforce profile and, Recommendation 2, to establish a task and finish group to oversee the prioritisation of service developments and recruitment. It is expected this work will be completed by June.I have not held any specific discussions with the Minister for the Economy on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions that will be taken by the Historic Environment Division to save the historic Kircubbin Harbour following further damage to the quay and retaining walls during recent storms and the lack of restoration and preventative measures taken by the owner.
answer_text: My officials have inspected the historic Kircubbin Harbour, a scheduled historic monument, since Storm Eowyn and have noted further ongoing erosion.  My department has no responsibility for maintaining scheduled monuments in private ownership but does advise and support owners in doing so. Historic Environment Division officials will be writing to the owner, once again, to bring the deteriorating condition of the harbour to his attention. The letter will also make him aware that, on application, the department can offer a small financial contribution for emergency holding works under its Historic Environment Fund Monument Regeneration stream.  My officials will also continue to work with the local community who have a particular interest in this site, in conjunction with the owner and Ards and North Down Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to help homeowners and landlords, especially in rural areas, to move away from fossil fuels, particularly oil dependence, to an energy efficient alternative.
answer_text: At present, financial assistance is available through the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP), for fully supported, and part funded schemes, for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with most of the funding targeted at lower income customers.My department has recently consulted on future support of low carbon technologies in residential properties, which closed 10th January 2025. The views provided will support my department in the development of policy to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heat, which will include energy efficient technologies like heat pumps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of unpaid carers registered as of 31 January 2025, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: At present, carers’ registers are in place regionally across Northern Ireland and maintained by the Health and Social Care Trusts, which carers can voluntarily choose to join.  These registers are primarily a means of sharing information and advice with carers and for supporting carers through networks and activities.  The Department does not hold information as to the numbers registered, this is held locally at Trust level, and we do not have the breakdown of the numbers by Trust on 31st January 2025.  However, the Department is aware that at September 2023 these registers listed approximately 13,000 carers.  The breakdown for each Trust is noted below and whilst there will have been some expected fluctuation as carers join and leave the registers, we do not expect the numbers registered to have significantly changed.SHSCT – 1596SEHSCT – 2700BHSCT - 3648WHSCT – 2670NHSCT - 2500

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there will be a review of the existing housing points allocated to Housing Selection Scheme applicants after 31 March 2025, as any applications after this date will not be able to compete with those that had intimidation points applied.
answer_text: Following 31 March 2025 all applications will be assessed using the same criteria. Transitional arrangements will be in place for only those who already have intimidation points or whose application is in train until such times as they are re-housed or refuse two reasonable offers.		Further changes to the Housing Selection Scheme are planned as part of the ongoing Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. Two implementation stages remain which will now include a review of Primary Social Needs criteria and points.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding stream within his Department that the network of Men’s Sheds can apply to in 2025-26 to cover their core operating costs.
answer_text: Community Empowerment Division provide funding of approx. £10k per annum to one men’s shed project, located in Ballymena. Subject to progression of the People & Place review process, there are no new funding opportunities available for the network of Men’s Sheds in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any legislation or regulation that empowers his Department to censor the type or content of books, periodicals and other written materials included in Libraries NI stock.
answer_text: My Department’s role and powers in relation to the NI Library Authority are set out in the Libraries Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review NI Water’s priorities to add housing as a key consideration for how and where investment is spent.
answer_text: In October monitoring, NI Water received £32m, of which £19.5m has been used to successfully unlock capacity for 2,300 properties across Antrim, Derry and Tyrone.I plan to continue that focus on unlocking capacity and can advise that my officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to contribute to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy.    I will also continue to develop the three-pronged approach that is already in place.  Firstly, working with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, officials are examining the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions with a view to consulting on the proposals in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many points will be issued for Housing Selection Scheme applicants who qualify for Primary Social Needs after intimidation points end on 31 March 2025.
answer_text: Points that are issued for applicants who qualify for Primary Social Needs after 31 March 2025 will remain as they are until the review of criteria and points has been completed. This will be completed as part of the ongoing Fundamental Review of Allocations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the review and next steps of the Together: Building a United Community Strategy will be published.
answer_text: We are currently considering recommendations from the T:BUC Review and these will shape the timeline for the development of a refreshed approach to Good Relations. In the meantime, the delivery of T:BUC continues to positively impact those most in need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any preparatory work undertaken to support employees with disabilities, and their employer, in the event of the cessation of the Employment Support Scheme.
answer_text: An EQIA was published on 25th November 2024 for 12 weeks seeking the views of the public on the proposal to cease funding for the Employment Support Scheme.  This public consultation closed on 17th February 2025 and no final decision will be made until the public consultation report is completed.There has been ongoing engagement with the two providers of the Employment Support Scheme prior to and throughout the EQIA process. The providers continue to provide support and guidance to the scheme participants and their employers within their role as my department’s delivery partners. My department will continue to engage with and provide guidance to the scheme providers throughout, as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there has been research to consider what percentage of after-school activities in mainstream schools include children who have a statement of special educational need.
answer_text: Neither the department nor the Education Authority (EA) hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will update his Department’s communications policy to ensure all consultations and communications include accessible options such as, but not limited to, Easy Read, as common practice rather than on request.
answer_text: My Department currently follows and will continue to follow best practice in this area. All published departmental information and communications are considered for alternative formats, including Easy Read. For example, Easy Read documents were published for the Department of Education’s Draft 2023-2028 Corporate Plan consultation, the Children and Young People’s Strategy 2019-2029 consultation, the Making School Uniforms More Affordable consultation, the Free School Meals and Uniform Grant consultation, the Deferring School Starting Age consultation, and the Children & Young People's Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) also publish easy read versions or pupil friendly versions.Easy Read documents are produced at first publication whenever it is appropriate to do so. Any alternative formats, when requested, are provided promptly for those that require them. This process is in keeping with the ‘Accessible communication formats’ guidance provided by the Cabinet Office and Disability Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will update his Department’s communications policy to ensure all consultations and communications include accessible options such as, but not limited to, Easy Read, as common practice rather than on request.
answer_text: My Department currently follows and will continue to follow best practice in this area. Officials strive to ensure that all published departmental information and communications are considered for alternative formats, including Easy Read, producing these at first publication whenever it is appropriate to do so. Thereafter, any alternative formats requested are provided promptly for those that require them. This process is in keeping with the ‘Accessible communication formats’ guidance provided by the Cabinet Office and Disability Unit.Officials also work to ensure that all digital communications are compliant with the Public Sector Bodies Accessibility Regulations to ensure those using assistive technologies are not disadvantaged in any way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will meet the redundancy costs for those in supported employment with wage subsidy through the Employment Support Scheme.
answer_text: Redundancy is not necessarily a direct impact of the Employment Support Scheme funding ceasing. My department will continue to engage with and provide guidance to the scheme providers throughout, as required.If the employer decides that a job can no longer be sustained, and redundancy is the only viable option, my department will pay the relevant percentage portion for the statutory rate of any redundancy payments and the employer must pay their percentage. The wage subsidy is based on the person’s agreed output/productivity in their specific job role with a split contribution from my department and the Employer. Any redundancy costs is based on the same principle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Draft Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan, particularly with regards to offshore wind energy.
answer_text: Question 11 – AQO 1520/22-27My Department, in collaboration with other departments and key stakeholders, have developed an Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) to enable offshore renewable energy development in the north’s marine area from 2030.  DfE have appointed RPS Ireland to undertake an update to the 2012 Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) and Habitats Regulation Assessment (HRA) of Offshore Renewable Energy in the north’s Marine Area. The SEA/HRA will enable us, in collaboration with key delivery partners, to identify suitable areas for offshore renewable energy development across various technology types including wind.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the decision to remove Intimidation points from the Housing Selection Scheme, whether a review of the median housing points across each Common Landlord Area will be completed.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “The Housing Executive plan to carry out modelling and analysis of Waiting List data in relation to the cumulative impact of the agreed points changes which are being implemented under the Fundamental Review of Allocations, including the removal of Intimidation Points. Given the inter-dependencies, further modelling and analysis will examine the collective impact of all these key points changes on the Waiting List. This work will inform decisions on the remaining changes to be implemented, including the introduction of banding.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on actions being taken to deliver a scheme to enable people with health conditions to try a job for thirty days without losing benefit entitlements.
answer_text: The Department for Communities continues to deliver the Work Experience Programme, which offers short work experience placements of two to eight weeks with local employers for people aged 16-65 years. Participation in the programme is voluntary, and participants will retain their benefit entitlements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the public will be informed of the change to how people who are deemed economically inactive are categorised.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for categorising economically inactive people or informing the public of associated changes to these processes in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when Land and Property services will be available online for the public to access as part of the Open Government commitment.
answer_text: There are a number of existing Land & Property Services (LPS) online services that can be found through the NI Direct website. These include Paying Rates Online, Searching Valuation Lists (https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/guide-rates)  and Mapping Services (https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/osni-online-map-shop). Additionally, many LPS Open Datasets are available through the OpenDataNI portal (https://admin.opendatani.gov.uk/group/property?organization=land-property). The LPS Transformation Programme will increase the level of online service provision across Land Registration, Valuation and Rating services.  The first of these projects will implement a full range of transformed online services for Land Registration between October 2026 and October 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will develop a data sharing agreement between the Housing Executive and the Department for Work and Pensions to ensure that when a change to benefits or pension payment occurs, the client's file is updated to reduce the number of housing benefit payments.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive, and I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that: “The Housing Executive can confirm that Section 116C and 116D of the Social Security Administration (NI) Act 1992 enables the reciprocal sharing of information between benefit agencies for the purpose of administering their respective benefits, including Housing Benefit. For Housing Benefit purposes, most benefit entitlement details are updated automatically via the Automatic Transfer to Local Authority Systems (ATLAS).  This is a secure platform for the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) to electronically share benefit information between government agencies for efficient benefit administration.  In 2023/24, the Housing Executive’s Housing Benefit Department dealt with 3,538,526 changes notified via the Department for Work and Pensions.Where benefits are excluded from the ATLAS process, regular reports are run by Housing Executive decision makers to identify Housing Benefit customers in receipt of those benefits.  Housing Executive decision makers have approval to access a digital portal used by the DWP, to verify information about individuals' benefit claims.An obligation however remains with Housing Benefit customers to notify the Housing Executive of changes in circumstances, including changes to income.  This duty to notify changes in circumstances is entailed in the Housing Benefit regulations. The customer signs a declaration on their application form to this effect and is reminded of the requirement in all Housing Benefit notifications.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any support packages that will be made available for businesses unable to trade due to a lack of power caused by Storm Éowyn, including any specific criteria that will be used.
answer_text: ANSWERCompensation for the loss of power would appear to be a matter for NIE networks in the first instance.The Executive requested that NIE networks considers possible goodwill payments for customers who have been affected by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support packages that will be made available for businesses unable to trade due to a lack of power caused by Storm Éowyn, including any specific criteria that will be used.
answer_text: It has been agreed in principle that a compensation payment will be made to those customers impacted.I have written to the Utility Regulator to ask them to work with NIE and my Department on providing a compensation payment to those customers who have been affected. Their work will cover what will be paid, how it will be paid and when it will be paid. It is important to note that NIE’s current priority is to restore all of those who are still without power.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to review the Building Back Better scheme; and (ii) whether a similar project to help residents could be put in place in Northern Ireland for those impacted by (a) flooding; (b) storm damage; and (c) other adverse weather incidents.
answer_text: Financial services, including insurance, are reserved matters so reviewing the Building Back Better scheme including its extension to storm and other adverse weather damage to homes would be a matter for the British Government.My Department already administers the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme (HFPGS) in the North of Ireland to support victims of flooding as well as helping to create a sustainable environment resilient to the impacts of our changing climate, whilst sharing knowledge and industry best practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions that will be taken to secure the power network across Northern Ireland, given the move away from fossil fuels will increase residential dependency on electricity and the increasing in frequency and impact of adverse weather incidents.
answer_text: My department has been working with DAERA and all departments to finalise the third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3) in response to the risks and opportunities set out in the most recent UK Climate Change Risk Assessment (CRA3). The draft NICCAP3 will go to the Executive before a public consultation.   The planning and maintenance of the electricity grid is the collective responsibility of SONI and NIE Networks.  NIE Networks is working towards the 2080 change models that were developed by the Met Office in its UK Climate Projections (UKCP 18).The Utility Regulator has recently approved an unprecedented £2.23 billion for the development and maintenance of the electricity grid over the period April 2025 to March 2031.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of requests for regulative review of a Housing Association have been received by his Housing Regulation Branch in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Housing Regulation Branch has not received any requests from residents or tenant representative bodies for regulative review of a housing association in each of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans to bring management agents and companies employed by Housing Associations into the scope of the Housing Regulation Branch, particularly in respect of tenant relations.
answer_text: I have no plans to bring management agents and companies employed by Housing Associations into the scope of the Housing Regulation Branch.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people registered as a carer as at December 2024, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A carer is defined as someone who provides regular and substantial care on an informal (unpaid) basis. Health and Social Care Trusts hold a database, the ‘Carer Register’, which is a mailing list of any informal, unpaid carer who has opted for their name and details to be kept on this Register.  Carers can request to have their details removed at any time.  These registers are primarily a means of sharing information and advice with carers and for supporting carers through networks and activities. The breakdown for each Trust is noted below to reflect registered numbers in December 2024.  Please consider that there will have been some expected fluctuation as carers join and leave the registers and current registered numbers may therefore be different.BHSCT5000NHSCT2830SET3926WHSCT3063SHSCT3568

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a copy of the written process that GP practices must follow to ensure the local Health and Social Care Trust’s Carers Support is notified when a patient’s record is updated to include their role as a carer.
answer_text: There is no contractual requirement for GPs to record that a patient of the practice is a carer or to inform the local Health and Social Care Trust.However, in the Northern Local Commissioning Group (LCG) area there is a Local Enhanced Service for Carers Health, where service providers are required to consider signposting carers to other organisations, including the local Carers’ Hub.  Contracting to provide this service is discretionary - 93 per cent of the 67 GP practices within the LCG area are currently providing the service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the (i) work of his Housing Regulation Branch; and (ii) role of the NI Public Service Ombudsman are promoted to tenants of Housing Associations.
answer_text: The role of my Department’s Housing Regulation Branch (HRB) is primarily promoted to tenants of registered housing associations (RHAs) via the Regulatory Framework for Registered Housing Associations in Northern Ireland, which is readily available on the Department’s website. This framework, effective from 01 April 2024, was subject to a recent high-level effectiveness review which was carried out in consultation with the sector, including tenants. The Department was assisted during the review by Supporting Communities NI (SCNI), an independent charity that champions tenant and community participation. A stakeholder workshop, held specifically for tenants, was facilitated by SCNI to help ensure that the views of tenants were taken on board. SCNI is also a member of the Department’s Stakeholder Reference Group which meets biannually to discuss emerging issues and exchange good practice. The group consists of representatives from sectoral organisations, several Chief Executives of RHAs and Housing Policy colleagues. HRB also regularly attends various sectoral forums to promote its role. This includes a tenant forum facilitated by SCNI, namely the Housing Policy Panel, which acts in a consultative function to the Department, focusing on social housing-related matters. It is made up of tenant representatives from the social housing sector.   The role of the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman (NIPSO) is also set out in the Regulatory Framework and on the Department’s website. HRB does not deal with individual service-related complaints about RHAs. These should be progressed through the individual RHA’s complaints process in the first instance, and then through NIPSO if tenants remain unhappy. Each RHA has in place an individual complaints policy which clearly sets out the process for tenants. HRB ensures, during the regulatory process, and in keeping with the desired outcomes and expectations under Consumer Standard 2 of the Regulatory Framework, that RHAs make this information readily available to their tenants.  NIPSO, in fulfilling its role in the process, will inform HRB of any concerns or systemic issues identified within an RHA, or the whole sector, to allow HRB to consider if the Department needs to take any follow up action in response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any solutions being introduced for patients with epilepsy that are having difficulty accessing anti-seizure medications such as Zonisamide, particularly Zonegran, Phenytoin, Lamotrigine and Carbamazepine.
answer_text: Medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions can occur for a variety of reasons and are not uncommon. My Department is aware that there have been some UK-wide supply disruptions affecting various strengths and preparations of epilepsy medications in recent times, including carbamazepine, phenytoin and lamotrigine products, although most of these disruptions appear to have now been resolved. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department and other operators within the supply chain to put in place tried and tested tools and processes should any disruptions arise. These existing tools and processes include communication to healthcare professionals such as National Patient Safety Alerts, Medicines Supply Notifications, NHS Specialist Pharmacy Services (SPS) resources including SPS medicines supply tool, and other mitigations such as Serious Shortage Protocols, additions to the export and hoarding ban list, additions to the NIMAR list, and the importation of medicines from other markets. Several of these tools and processes have been implemented to mitigate the impact of anti-epileptic medicine shortages when supply disruptions have occurred.It is important to note that the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) has issued updated advice following a review by the Commission on Human Medicines (CHM), that categorised anti-epileptic medicines into one of three risk-based categories, which healthcare professionals can use to decide whether it is necessary to maintain continuity of supply of a particular manufacturer’s product. Prescribing recommendations for Health and Social Care (HSC) healthcare professionals, outlined in the Northern Ireland Formulary, incorporate this MHRA guidance, and my Department’s policy that medicines should be prescribed generically in all appropriate circumstances as set out in the Northern Ireland Formulary. Therefore, it can also be the case that patients can sometimes misconstrue not obtaining their preferred brand such as Zonegran® (zonisamide) as a supply disruption when their medication is prescribed generically. In such cases, it would be more appropriate for patients to discuss their treatment options and concerns with their clinicians.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when guidance will be provided to local councils and sports clubs in relation to the transition away from Artificial Grass Pitches; and (ii) any financial support that will be made available following the EU REACH restriction on the sale of rubber infill for 3G Artificial Grass Pitches.
answer_text: Guidance regarding Artificial Grass Pitches is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:On 25th September 2023, the European Commission announced the introduction of new rules on microplastic materials which will apply following an eight-year transition period, ending October 2031. Sport NI has shared this information with the sports sector, advising of the proposed changes and what this means for playing pitches.Sport NI has published an Information Paper and a Position Statement at https://www.sportni.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Microplastics-Information-Paper-November-2023.pdf informing the sports sector of upcoming changes and advising on alternatives to rubber infill and reducing the impact of existing rubber infill.  In addition, Sport NI:Have engaged with Councils and Private Sector Integrated Consultancy Teams (ICT).Have attended and delivered workshops on the subject, including inviting the leading expert in this field to speak to delegates in NI.Provide on-going advice to the sports sector, via  https://www.sportni.net/facilities/microplastics/ Contribute to a UK working group that is testing alternative (organic) infill products, and we are monitoring results.  Sport NI are also monitoring the development of new pitches in Republic of Ireland and are awaiting the availability of performance data. Sport NI are not recommending a specific organic infill product (as an alternative to rubber), as there are now multiple such products available. However, we will provide information to enable others (i.e., ICTs) to select the most appropriate product on behalf of their clients.At this time, my Department does not offer any programmes that disburse financial support to assist owners for maintenance or replacement of artificial grass pitches.  In addition, the Chief Executive of Sport NI has also advised as follows:Many synthetic pitches in Northern Ireland have been funded using public money.  Subsequently there should be an expectation that owners of these assets have operated them in a financially sustainable way.  This includes setting aside a portion of revenue each year to maintain their pitch and replace the asset at the end of its economic life (i.e. an Asset Replacement Fund or Sinking Fund).  Synthetic pitches funded by Sport NI would have included this specific stipulation as part of the terms and conditions of award.Since September 2024, new Sport NI investment programmes have not funded 3G synthetic pitches with rubber infill.  However, there is no financial support specifically to address the EU REACH restriction on the sale of rubber infill for 3G Artificial Grass Pitches.As per my answer to AQW18949/22-27, my officials are currently engaging with other stakeholders and assessing the impact of the ban on the sale of rubber crumb for 3G artificial grass pitches from 2031 in Northern Ireland and I await an update on next steps.   For any fu

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department will progress legislation to (i) enable the Charity Commission to clarify the financial and reporting obligations for Section 167 charities registered elsewhere on these islands; and (ii) remove barriers to local funding for these charities
answer_text: Policy development for a Charities Bill is currently in progress.  This work includes legislative amendments to address known flaws in relation to the operation of section 167 of the Charities Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.  It is anticipated that the Bill will be presented to the Assembly in the current mandate, followed by regulations to clarify financial and reporting obligations for section 167 charities and potentially remove barriers to local funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of charities that are recognised as Section 167 charities for the purposes of the Charities Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Charity Commission for Northern Ireland (the Commission). I have sought the requested information from the Commission, which has advised:		‘There are currently no recognised section 167 charities for the purposes of the Charities Act (Northern Ireland) 2008 as this legislative provision has yet to be commenced.	The Commission’s list of “section 167s” (last updated on 24 December 2024) details the 661 organisations which are established outside of Northern Ireland, but which have indicated they operate for charitable purposes in or from Northern Ireland and wish to register here. There may also be other organisations operating in Northern Ireland which fall under this category, but which have not made themselves known.’		For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Commission directly. Making direct contact with the Commission will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.  A list of section 167 organisations is published on the Charity Commission’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will put in arrangements in place, along with the Housing Executive, to require all developments of ten social housing units or more to include a bus stop, at least two accessible and sustainable bus shelters and road space to enable access by an Ulsterbus-sized public transport vehicle to travel in and out of the residential space.
answer_text: There are no plans to make these a requirement for social housing schemes with 10 or more units. Requirements for public transport are contained within the “Creating Places” document used by the Planning Service when assessing the planning principles for residential developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will review the necessity to provide car parking space on Balloo Road, Bangor, opposite the former Rathgael House, given the building has been demolished and the area could be used for parking, allowing the land on Balloo Road to be used for social housing.
answer_text: The construction works at Rathgael House are being taken forward in two stages. The first stage was the demolition of the tower block which was completed in December 2024. The second stage will include the refurbishment of the front entrance and the provision of car parking spaces. This work is scheduled to be completed by the end of May 2025. The land on Balloo Road adjacent to Rathgael House, currently used for car parking, will then become surplus. Land & Property Services’ internal disposal process will then be initiated allowing it to be considered for alternative use by relevant public and third sector bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the link to the current Transformation to Integrated Status process guidance and templates as of January 2025.
answer_text: Transformation is the legal process in which a non-Integrated school becomes integrated. This process is set out in the legislation in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (link).  The supporting Transformation guidance can be accessed on the Department of Education website at - Integration Works Transforming Your School (link). My officials are currently reviewing the Transformation guidance to ensure it is fit for purpose. Any new guidance will continue to reflect the statutory Transformation process set out in the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children that have failed to secure a place in an integrated post-primary school in the North Down and Ards area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why his departmental officials are developing a new Transformation process for schools making a Case for Change to become integrated; and (ii) to provide an indication of when the public consultation on the proposed changes will be held.
answer_text: Transformation is the legal process by which a non-Integrated school becomes integrated. This process is set out in the legislation in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.  In line with the commitment in the Integrated Education Strategy, my officials are reviewing the supporting Transformation guidance, Integration Works Transforming Your School to ensure it is fit for purpose. Any new guidance will continue to reflect the statutory Transformation process set out in the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the outcome of the parental ballot asking if they supported their school transforming to integrated status held at (i) Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College; and (ii) Rathmore Primary School.
answer_text: This detail is referenced throughout the published reports and appendices on which I took my decisions on Development Proposals 727 and 728 and may be accessed at the following link: Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will be publishing the next report on integrated education, as required under Section 10 of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The initial Section 10 report was published in April 2024 and in line with Section 10 (1)(b) of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 the next report will be published in April 2027 at the earliest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the policy or regulations he used when he took the Ministerial decision not to uphold the departmental recommendation to approve the application for transformation to integrated status for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College and Rathmore Primary School.
answer_text: All legislation and policies relevant to the assessment of the Development Proposals for these schools are included within the published reports and appendices on which I took my detailed decisions and may be accessed at the following link: Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the refusal to approve the Transformation to Integrated Status Development Plan for Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College and Rathmore Primary School is in line with the statutory requirements of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and his duty to encourage, facilitate and support integrated education.
answer_text: My detailed reasons for not approving Development Proposals 727 and 728 clearly reference the considerations I gave to the provisions of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  The decisions and detailed reports on which they were taken may be accessed here: Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how he is ensuring his Department is providing sufficient places in integrated schools to meet demand, such as in the North Down and Ards area, as required by legislation.
answer_text: Section 1(1) of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 defines what integrated education means. It provides,“Integrated education” means the education together, in an integrated school, of—(a) those of different cultures and religious beliefs and of none, including reasonable numbers of both Protestant and Roman Catholic children or young persons;(b) those who are experiencing socio-economic deprivation and those who are not; and(c) those of different abilities.”My Department will aim to meet demand, as required by legislation, as set out in the Section 10 report published in April 2024. In this report six locations, including North Down and Ards (Strangford Integrated College), were identified as having oversubscribed schools. On conclusion of work to update guidance for schools considering transformation to Integrated status, my Department will commission the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) to engage with these oversubscribed integrated schools and the Education Authority (EA) in the six localities identified. The affected schools will be encouraged to review their enrolment numbers and school capacity and prepare and bring forward DPs to increase their enrolment numbers (if such changes are significant in nature). DE acknowledges that while this approach may partially address oversubscription issues, it is unlikely to resolve them in full. DE will therefore commission NICIE, working in partnership with EA and CSSC to issue a ‘Call for Transformation’ suggesting schools in the identified localities consider Transformation to integrated status to meet demand. To further monitor demand across Northern Ireland, Departmental officials have included societal level questions on demand and the factors parents consider when makings school admission decisions in the annual Northern Ireland Life and Times survey.  Results will be available in late Spring with a report in the Summer.Officials are also working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority to develop and conduct a survey of parents who have recently made school preference decisions through the admissions portal. This survey will be carried out in April 2025 and will ask parents and guardians, in line with the Integrated Education Act, whether they have a preference for Integrated Education and how various factors have influenced their school preferences. Both surveys will allow my Department to consider existing and future demand and provide data on which existing Area Planning structures can take action to aim to meet that demand in the context the overall sustainability of the school estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the proactive measures that his Department will take to deliver the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy, particularly in relation to (i) water and sewerage; and (ii) public transport connectivity consideration within planning and road infrastructure.
answer_text: I allocated an additional £19.5m capital DEL during October Monitoring to NI Water, specifically to address constraints in wastewater infrastructure and to unlock capacity for housing connections. NI Water has advised me that the outcome of this investment is projected to deliver capacity for some 2300 new properties to connect to the water and sewerage infrastructure during this price control period. I have been working with Executive colleagues to increase wastewater investment and to focus on unlocking further wastewater capacity to enable properties to connect to the sewerage network during this price control period until 2027. The Housing Supply Strategy highlights the importance of locating housing where it has convenient access to key services by walking, cycling and public transport.  My officials are currently preparing a new suite of Transport Plans alongside the Councils’ preparation of their Local Development Plans.  This integration of transport and land use planning, with accessibility analysis for both active and public transport, is key to achieving sustainable development. The planning system plays an important enabling role in facilitating the delivery of housing. My Department’s Strategic Planning Policy Statement (the SPPS), requires councils, in taking forward their local development plans (LDPs), to facilitate an adequate and available supply of quality housing to meet the needs of everyone and promote more sustainable housing development within existing urban areas. This approach supports the need to maximise the use of existing infrastructure and services, and the creation of more balanced sustainable communities.My Department also provides specialist safety information and transportation advice to the appropriate Planning Authority on road related matters associated with proposed development applications. Major Road Infrastructure schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.  They also offer opportunities to improve public transport connectivity within the region through the provision of improved access to high quality Park and Ride facilities extending travel choices for all sections of the community.  My Department has identified a list of priority bus and rail-based Park and Ride sites and further potential sites will be considered within the new Transport Strategy and transport plans currently being developed.  Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the proactive measures that his Department will take to develop and deliver sustainable energy solutions to deliver climate change targets as part of the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My department is fully committed to playing its part in developing and delivering long term sustainable energy solutions that support the achievement of climate change targets in the north while contributing to the Executive’s recently announced Housing Supply Strategy.The regional Energy Strategy: Path to Net Zero Energy provides the policy framework to decarbonise energy and to mobilise the skills, technologies and behaviours required to transition our economy to net zero. In this context, my department has recently published a consultation on Low carbon heat support for domestic properties, which will ultimately seek to introduce a support scheme like those operating in Britain and Ireland. This proposed policy initiative is intended to provide households with grants to support the installation of low carbon heating technologies, including heat pumps. In tandem with this approach, we are bringing forward policies to gather evidence on the production of Biomethane and liquid biofuels, to better understand their respective role in the energy transition. This represents a vital step forward in helping households transition to more sustainable and efficient heating systems.In addition to support for low carbon heating, my department is also preparing a consultation on domestic energy efficiency support. This consultation will help to inform the design, rules, and guidance of future support that aims to address multiple priorities, including support industry growth, reducing energy bills, and cutting carbon emissions through enabling the transition to low carbon heat. In the interim, ahead of any proposed support scheme, my department will continue to work collaboratively with a range of local partners to leverage and promote existing financial support mechanisms, for heating and energy efficiency, that are already available through the Utility Regulator’s NISEP Scheme. To support the transition to sustainable energy in housing, we are investing in skills development and innovation to build the workforce needed for renewable energy and construction technologies. This ensures long-term sustainability and growth in the green economy while developing initiatives to encourage growth of the local industry as well as developing an appropriate pipeline of a highly skilled and diverse workforce.  As we transition to net zero, we are mindful of the need to protect consumers from undue financial burdens and to ensure that these solutions are sustainable and equitable, delivering long-term benefits to residents while meeting our climate obligations. Measures such as supporting affordable energy pricing, targeted financial assistance for low-income households, and ensuring ‘just energy transition principles’ are central to our approach. My officials will continue to work collaboratively across government, the private sector, and the community to deliver on these commitments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the proactive measures his Department will take to develop and deliver a sustainable supply chain of construction materials with industry partners to support the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: Invest NI provides a range of specialist supply chain advisory and financial support to companies across the north. This includes the construction materials supply chain.  The support helps businesses build resilience and capability into their supply chain and develop their supply-chain function to enhance value, reduce risk, improve their competitive advantage and support development of a wider supply chain ecosystem in the north.The Supply Chain Resilience and Development Service (SCRDS) helps companies identify supply chain risks, opportunities and capability development. Depending on business circumstances, financial assistance is also available towards the salary costs of an employee dedicated to supply chain development.  Operational Excellence (OPEX) provides support to embed principles, tools and techniques to address areas such as waste identification and elimination, practical problem solving, daily continuous improvement, and workflow improvements.Additional support is available to assist companies to upskill and reskill existing staff; facilitate collaboration with partners to develop new business models, address challenges and exploit new opportunities; and enhance leadership capability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress being made to review and update planning legislation and regulations to support the delivery of the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My Department has been working collectively with local government and other stakeholders to improve the planning system through the Planning Improvement Programme (PIP). The PIP has and will deliver a number of changes to planning legislation which will enhance and make a tangible difference to the planning system here in the North. These changes include the introduction of legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists (Statutory Rule has been made and will come into operation on 1 April 2025) which will improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process. My Department is also conducting a review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to ensure that they are practical and appropriate to support current and future development trends, streamline the planning application process, and encourage greater participation in the planning process.I am acutely aware of the challenges around housing supply in the North and the importance that the planning system plays in helping to meet current and foreseeable housing needs. My Department will continue to work with stakeholders to ensure that we have a planning system that delivers the necessary socio-economic and environmental outcomes for our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to review the rates charged for vacant domestic properties as part of her rating policy review.
answer_text: I would draw the member’s attention to my Oral Ministerial Statement of Monday 9 December (Rating Policy – Strategic Roadmap):https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/minister-finance-oral-statement-rating-policy-strategic-roadmap

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she is considering any review of rates relief for businesses that are registered as charities, but would not qualify for small business rates relief.
answer_text: I would draw the member’s attention to my Oral Ministerial Statement of Monday 9 December (Rating Policy – Strategic Roadmap):https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/minister-finance-oral-statement-rating-policy-strategic-roadmap In addition, under current rating legislation where a property is occupied by a charity, and is valued as being used for the objectives and purposes of that charity, then it will receive charitable exemption. Where this does not apply the occupier may be eligible for small business rate relief provided it falls below the NAV threshold and meets criteria for the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the actions she will be delivering in the year ahead to meet the requirements relating to data sharing, particularly public land availability, as part of the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS), within my department, has been developing a centrally held database - The Government Land and Property Register (GLPR) – to record all government owned and/or occupied land and property assets in Northern Ireland. As of March 2024, 119,756 Hectares of assets has been identified and mapped as part of the ongoing GLPR project. This work is scheduled to continue into 2025. The data within the GLPR is freely available to all government departments and can be used by departments to assist in identifying public land availability, as part of the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.Also, within LPS, Ordnance Survey (OSNI) is supporting the Housing Led Regeneration Subgroup and the Rural Housing Steering Group by providing geospatial information on publicly owned land and property for further analysis by the groups to ascertain suitability for housing development. Both OSNI and the GLPR team are represented on these groups. There is work ongoing, by OSNI, to develop an internal application (online map viewer) where Departments can better access and view information on publicly owned land.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the proactive measures that will be taken by his Department to develop and deliver skills and jobs required to deliver the Executive's agreed Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: My department delivers a range of training interventions to support the construction sector.  This includes the ApprenticeshipsNI programme which offers vocational qualifications in a range of trade occupations including electrical, plumbing and construction. In addition, Higher Level Apprenticeships offer qualifications at Level 4 and above in the areas of Construction, Planning, Architecture and the Built Environment.A new Skill Up programme is available as part of my Departments Skills Agenda. The programme has 50 free courses related to Construction and Green Skills. These accredited courses range from Level 2 to Level 7 and are available through the six Further Education Colleges, the Open University, Queens University and the University of Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he is taking within his Department to deliver effective partnership working, as set down in the Concordat agreement, with community and voluntary sector providers for access to employment support and jobs for people with disabilities.
answer_text: The Department values partnership working with a wide range of stakeholders, including the community and voluntary sector, to provide access to employment support and jobs for people with disabilities.This partnership working extends across co-production of a new Disability and Work Strategy; membership, commissioning and delivery across Labour Market Partnership structures; and delivery of programmes such as Workable (NI) and Access to Work (NI). There is also regular engagement across the sector on priority areas of concern or opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions his Department has had with the Department for Infrastructure to address transport barriers to access to employment for people with disabilities, including how this will be addressed in his Disability and Employment Strategy.
answer_text: In developing the Disability Strategy and considering the needs of Deaf and disabled people, my Department is working with all Executive departments. Engagement with the Department for Infrastructure is ongoing and I can confirm that this will include consideration on how transport issues can be addressed.Additionally, the Department continues to deliver the Access to Work (NI) Programme which supports disabled people to access employment, including travel to and from work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any actions he is taking to introduce a Disability Confidence Scheme to encourage inclusive employment, similar to what is provided in Great Britain.
answer_text: My department’s draft Disability and Work Strategy contains an action, under a theme of ‘Supporting and Enabling Employers’, to explore a scheme for employers that grows their awareness of disability employment, and supports and enables implementation of best practice in relation to the recruitment and employment of disabled people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action being taken to ensure disability awareness training is a requirement for employers in the Disability and Employment Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Disability and Work Strategy, under a Theme of Supporting and Enabling Employers, includes commitments and actions to address employer misconceptions, to build their awareness, and to support and enable implementation of best practice approaches to employing disabled people

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the process introduced into schools to ensure the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 is being delivered; and (ii) where equality records for individual schools is published.
answer_text: To ensure the smooth implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 a working group was established with management representatives from all of the education sectors.This group worked in conjunction with the Equality Commission and guidance for schools was published with the support of Management Side of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee. The Equality Commission delivering a series of training webinars specifically on the recruitment and selection guidance. The target audience for the webinars was those school governors, principals, heads of departments involved in the recruitment and selection of school teachers. The Chair of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) management side wrote to all schools explaining the requirements for the implementation of the Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.The Education Authority is responsible for preparing and submitting the annual monitoring return to the Equality Commission for teachers in controlled schools and CCMS is responsible for carrying out this function for teachers in Catholic maintained schools. All other schools with over ten staff are required to submit monitoring returns to the Equality Commission.There is no requirement to publish equality records for individual schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will update the accessibility rating of tourism accommodation to include the necessary provision of adjustable mobility beds.
answer_text: Under the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Order”), all visitor/tourist accommodation in the North must be certified by Tourism NI to legally operate. The scope of the 1992 Order, and Tourism NI’s Certification Scheme for Visitor Accommodation, is tightly defined, with Tourism NI’s role limited to inspecting each property to ensure that it meets a minimum standard of safety, cleanliness and the provision of facilities.Officials in my Department are currently working with Tourism NI to review the certification scheme and its criteria for each of the existing eight statutory accommodation categories. Accessibility ratings are currently outside the scope of the accommodation certification scheme, with accessibility requirements in this area primarily sitting within building regulations. However, my Department and Tourism NI are looking to see if accessibility issues can be considered as part of the certification criteria review.As part of this review process, we welcome additional proposals for changes to the certification regulations which can be considered in line with my Department’s remit, and once my Officials have finalised proposed amendments, it is our intention to publicly consult on these in the first half of 2025 to ensure that we get public and industry views on same.In the meantime, Tourism NI continues to work proactively to encourage accommodation providers and other tourism businesses to be accessible and inclusive to all visitors. A series of industry webinars have been delivered, and a toolkit of useful information and advice is due to be launched in spring 2025. A range of online guidance is also available at https://www.tourismni.com/business-guidance/business-support/accessibility/.Information on accessibility features is also displayed on individual business listings on the Tourism NI consumer website (Discover Northern Ireland) which can help visitors make an informed choice when deciding where to stay on their trip etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions being taken to future-proof tourist accommodation provision by increasing the number of accessible beds that can be used by the aging customer and people with disabilities.
answer_text: Under the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 (“the 1992 Order”), all visitor/tourist accommodation in the North must be certified by Tourism NI to legally operate. The scope of the 1992 Order, and Tourism NI’s Certification Scheme for Visitor Accommodation, is tightly defined, with Tourism NI’s role limited to inspecting each property to ensure that it meets a minimum standard of safety, cleanliness and the provision of facilities.Officials in my Department are currently working with Tourism NI to review the certification scheme and its criteria for each of the existing eight statutory accommodation categories. A series of industry webinars have been delivered, and a toolkit of useful information and advice is due to be launched in spring 2025. A range of online guidance is also available at https://www.tourismni.com/business-guidance/business-support/accessibility/.Information on accessibility features is also displayed on individual business listings on the Tourism NI consumer website (Discover Northern Ireland) which can help visitors make an informed choice when deciding where to stay on their trip etc.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans to introduce a regulatory framework or professional regulation for the operation of (i) property management companies; and (ii) letting agents.
answer_text: I have previously indicated that management companies must act in accordance with existing companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department. However, I also recognise that shared property management also attracts my Department’s interest in substantive land law, and I have been keen to make progress in examining this issue.I have engaged with officials to ensure the appropriate expertise in this complex policy area is secured. Resource, has been identified and a study of the issues relating to the operation of property management companies and agents will shortly commence. I will consider the outcome of that work, which I have asked to be completed by Easter, before determining the way forward.In relation to the regulation of letting agents, this is an area that also falls outside my remit and is a matter for my colleague, the Minister for Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the actions his Department is taking to address the shortage of skills in construction, specifically skills related to retrofitting and passive house type new builds.
answer_text: My Department, in collaboration with the Green Skills Delivery Group, will launch the Green Skills Action Plan in early 2025. This plan will seek to address the skills shortages required to reduce our carbon emissions.Domestic low carbon technologies and energy efficiency has been identified as a priority area within the Plan and work has already commenced to develop new retrofitting courses. Courses in this area are available to SME's through the Department's Skills Focus programme. I would encourage employers, within the construction sector, to engage with their local FE college to avail of these opportunities. In addition, several free retrofitting courses, including passive house, are already available through the Skill Up programme. CITBNI, an NDPB of the Department, is also active in this space and has delivered training courses, awareness sessions and business improvement events in energy efficiency and the wider green skills area.The construction sector will play a significant role in the reduction of carbon emissions and my Department, through its range of programmes will continue to address the skills need of this important sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he is working with the Minister for Communities to create sustainable jobs for people with disabilities and to support employers to provide those jobs.
answer_text: Whilst the Department for Communities (DfC) has policy responsibility for employment, my department has a significant contribution to make through our skills and education remit. DfC is currently developing a Disability and Work Strategy which will aim to improve employment outcomes for disabled people and people with health conditions. The scope of the Strategy includes those who are not currently active in the labour market, those who are already in work, and young disabled people aged 14-19 who will transition out of educational settings.  Since 2021, my officials have been working closely with their DfC colleagues in the development of the draft Strategy, to ensure that Department for the Economy commitments and contributions are reflected.My department also works with DfC to support individuals into employment through the Labour Market Partnerships, where officials from my Skills team and Careers Service are represented.Improving support for young people with Special Educational Needs (SEN) is a priority in my Business Plan and the Executive’s draft Programme for Government. I have tasked my officials with scoping the existing provision and providing me with options to improve access and outcomes for our young people with SEN. My department, along with the departments for Education, Health, and Communities has submitted a bid to the Interim Transformation Fund, aimed at strengthening skills and training provision and support for young people with SEN.My department also funds the Labour Relations Agency which provides an advice, guidance and early conciliation service for employers and their employees. This includes an employment document toolkit resource to assist employers in the drafting of workplace policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the publication of the Northern Ireland Housing Bulletin July - September 2024, to detail the number of individuals (i) currently on the social housing waiting list; (ii) that have Full Duty Applicant status and homelessness status, broken down by (a) age; (b) household type; and (c) local government area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 19059/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether additional support will be provided to the Housing Executive to ensure it can meet its statutory duty to provide emergency homes, given the pressure over recent days when the Housing Executive was concerned that it had reached full capacity of available emergency accommodation.
answer_text: Discussions have been ongoing with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who strongly refute comments around their failing in their statutory duty to homeless presenters recently. Following the October monitoring round, I have provided an additional £6.7m to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to prevent the risk of homeless service closures and ensure statutory obligations can continue to be met.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission why successor arrangements for the Independent Financial Review Panel, tasked to review members' pay, have not been established.
answer_text: Further to AQW 15535/22-27, the Assembly Commission is currently considering introduction of the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) whether an MLA can retain and use their current Assembly issued printer for the constituency office; and (ii) whether an MLA can buy toner and a printer from another constituency office that is moving to use a new printer.
answer_text: MLAs may retain and use the existing Assembly Commission-issued printer provided for their constituency office, until such time as a new Assembly Members expenses determination is published by the Assembly Commission, allowing for an appropriate transition period to the new printer arrangements.In relation to the purchase of printers, an MLA cannot buy an Assembly Commission-issued printer from another MLA, as the printer is the property of the Assembly Commission. While it is possible for MLAs to buy toner from another MLA, the process for doing so should be agreed with the Finance Office. MLAs can however return unused and unopened toners to Office Resources for a refund.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) the policy in relation to the use of the Members' Bar; and (ii) whether changes have been made to allow staff, MPs or visitors to access the area without being accompanied by an MLA.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission last reviewed the list of those eligible to access the Members’ Bar and Coffee Lounge on 7 May 2013.  The list agreed by the Assembly Commission at that time (provided below) included that Northern Ireland based Members of either house within the palace of Westminster and Assembly staff (Assembly Grade 4 and above) could access the Members’ Bar and Coffee Lounge without being accompanied by an MLA.  I provide the list agreed by the Assembly Commission on 7 May 2013 below, which has not been changed since that time. Members Head of Northern Ireland Civil Service and Permanent Secretaries Assembly Commission Staff (AG4 and above)Attorney General Solicitor to Attorney GeneralNorthern Ireland Assembly Commissioner for StandardsParty Nominated Users (Each Party may nominate one member of their permanent staff.  Larger parties of 12 MLAs or more may nominate an additional member of their permanent staff)Special AdvisersNorthern Ireland based Members of either house within the palace of Westminster (and/or European Parliament – this is no longer relevant)Comptroller and Auditor General and Audit Manager/Public Accounts Committee LiaisonIndependent Financial Review Panel MembersChair and Members of Assembly Commission Audit and Risk CommitteeNorthern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman and DeputyDepartmental SolicitorFirst Legislative CounselAccompanied guests of the above

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional funding that will be available to support regulatory and environmental health functions in local authorities.
answer_text: My Department currently supports regulatory and environmental health functions across our District Councils, such as through the Local Air Quality Management Grant.However, at present, I do not have plans for the Department to further fund District Councils beyond what is currently available in the 2024/25 year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on any discussions he has had with the Minister for the Economy on the recommendations for short-term lets and Airbnbs.
answer_text: As part of the work to finalise the Housing Supply Strategy, I met with the Minister for the Economy on 11 November to discuss the need for further collaboration on actions to address key issues for which DfE has a remit.The issue of short-term lets/Airbnb’s was one of the issues raised, which may be considered as part of any future strategy implementation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of World Aids Day on 1 December 2024, to detail any actions he is taking to achieve the UNAIDS target of no new HIV diagnoses by 2030.
answer_text: While a specific target date has not been adopted by my Department, aiming to eliminate HIV transmission in Northern Ireland at the earliest possible stage continues to be a priority for my Department. The Sexual Health Action Plan, launched in November 2023, aims to co-ordinate the ongoing direction for sexual health improvement in Northern Ireland. Within the Plan, striving to eliminate HIV is one of the seven strategic objectives, and a delivery group has been established to take forward work in this area. This group brings together policy leads, clinicians, health improvement leads and advocates from the community and voluntary sector.One of the early actions of this delivery group was to explore Northern Ireland participation in the global Fast Track Cities initiative, which focuses on ending HIV transmission.
