I am very pleased that earlier this year, both Belfast City and Derry and Strabane District Councils signed up to this civic initiative, and I will be exploring how I might encourage other Councils to consider what they can do in their local areas to support HIV elimination. To tackle HIV transmission and support those living with HIV to live well, a number of subgroups have been established through my Department’s Action Plan to lead on efforts to tackle stigma associated with HIV, explore testing opportunities and consider additional actions that can be taken to minimise late HIV diagnoses. Additionally, in line with World Aids Day, the Public Health Agency is leading on communication efforts to strengthen public messaging around the importance of HIV testing, how to prevent transmission and the impact of engaging in effective HIV treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to address the stigma associated with HIV, which can place a significant mental health burden on people living with the virus.
answer_text: The Sexual Health Action Plan, launched in November 2023, aims to co-ordinate the ongoing direction for sexual health improvement in Northern Ireland. One of the key objectives of the Action Plan is to ‘Strive to eliminate new HIV transmissions in Northern Ireland and support people living with HIV to live well’. During my address at the recent 29th Northern Ireland Regional Sexual Health Conference, I emphasised the importance of collaboration to ensure that messaging around HIV and sexual health are consistent and widespread. Through the Sexual Health Action Plan, an overarching communications group has been established to deliver a programme of awareness raising activities, focused around four key annual events across sexual health and wellbeing. One of these events is World Aids Day on 1 December. The joint focus of messaging in Northern Ireland will be on striving to eliminate HIV transmission and tackling stigma.  To raise awareness, a communications plan sets out a schedule for social media activity to encourage and maximise sharing, engagement and awareness on agreed campaign messages. A press release and communications toolkit have also been developed and disseminated via stakeholders. Although HIV treatment has come a long way over the years, fear and misinformation remain prevalent in our society. It is vital that public health messaging is consistent across the region to address misconceptions, and there is more to do in this space. As part of the Sexual Health Action Plan, a delivery group has been established to focus on tackling the transmission of HIV and sexually transmitted infections. The group brings together policy leads, clinicians, health improvement leads and advocates from the community and voluntary sector. Within this, a subgroup has been set up to specifically consider how stigma can be measured and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to his commitment to prevention, to detail his plans to invest in HIV awareness campaigns that could reduce the long-term cost burden on the NHS.
answer_text: The Sexual Health Action Plan, launched in November 2023, aims to co-ordinate the ongoing direction for sexual health improvement in Northern Ireland. One of the key objectives of the Action Plan is to ‘Strive to eliminate new HIV transmissions in Northern Ireland and support people living with HIV to live well’. During my address at the recent 29th Northern Ireland Regional Sexual Health Conference, I emphasised the importance of collaboration to ensure that messaging around HIV and sexual health are consistent and widespread. Through the Sexual Health Action Plan, an overarching communications group has been established to deliver a programme of awareness raising activities, focused around four key annual events across sexual health and wellbeing. One of these events is World Aids Day on 1 December. The joint focus of messaging in Northern Ireland will be on striving to eliminate HIV transmission and tackling stigma.  To raise awareness, a communications plan sets out a schedule for social media activity to encourage and maximise sharing, engagement and awareness on agreed campaign messages. A press release and communications toolkit have also been developed and disseminated via stakeholders. Although HIV treatment has come a long way over the years, fear and misinformation remain prevalent in our society. It is vital that public health messaging is consistent across the region to address misconceptions, and there is more to do in this space. As part of the Sexual Health Action Plan, a delivery group has been established to focus on tackling the transmission of HIV and sexually transmitted infections. The group brings together policy leads, clinicians, health improvement leads and advocates from the community and voluntary sector. Within this, a subgroup has been set up to specifically consider how stigma can be measured and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on a time frame for the enactment of section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: I have instructed my officials to take forward the necessary work to commence the power to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12-month period, with 3 months notice, as permitted by Section 7 of the Private Tenancies Act. I expect to commence this power early next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the (i) actions; (ii) timetable; and (iii) staff resource investment to ensure his Department is fulfilling its statutory duties as outlined in the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, in each year since 2022.
answer_text: Since 2022, my Department has undertaken a number of actions to meet the statutory duties set out in the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  These actions are set out in the table below. Notably, the Department has, as required by the Act, published a Section 10 Report on the delivery of Integrated Education outlining how it has fulfilled its duties under the Act since its passage through the Assembly. In terms of staff resources, the Integrated Education Act Implementation Team was established within the Department to take forward implementation of the Act and its associated duties. It is of course supported by other policy teams, including the designated Irish Medium and Integrated Education Team, and supported by the Integrated Education implementation governance structure of the Steering and Working Groups.My Department also provides funding in excess of £600K per annum to the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education and continues to work with the Education Authority and ARK to investigate demand for Integrated Education. I also note the recently announced £730,000 financial commitment to the Integrated sector from the Northern Ireland Office to support ongoing work to develop Integrated Education.ActionDateResourced departmental Integrated Education Act Implementation TeamOctober 2022 Published Strategy for Integrated EducationApril 2023Integrated Education Action Plan ConsultationLaunched August 2023 – closed November 2023Published Section 10 ReportApril 2024Development of Admissions portal survey October 2024Letter to S3 Bodies issued with a revised strategy and action plan ‘Vision 2030’ – for consideration and commentsComments from S3 bodies by Nov 2024Future ActionsConsideration of comments from S3 bodiesDecember 2024Finalise and publish revised Vision 2030 January 2025Parent survey via School admission portalFebruary/March 2024Public consultation on Vison 2030 - Integrated Education Action PlanCompleted by end March 2025Review of public consultation on Integrated Education Action PlanApril 2025Prepare and publish a report on demand for Integrated EducationMay 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional funding that will be provided to the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education to deliver research to identify methods of establishing parental demand for integrated education.
answer_text: The duty to monitor, assess and aim to meet parental demand falls to the Department of Education (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) not the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE). Section 4 of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 states - 4.—(1) In Article 64(1) of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (Department’s duties in respect of integrated education) for “to encourage and facilitate” substitute “to encourage, facilitate and support”. (2) In section 2 of the Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2014 (functions of Education Authority) after subsection (3) (Authority’s duties in respect of shared education) insert— “(3A) It is a duty of the Authority (so far as its powers extend) to encourage, facilitate and support integrated education.”.NICIE has been asked to undertake an engagement exercise in a number of localities where the Department has identified demand as part of their ongoing work programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of meetings, including dates, he or his Department has had with officials in the Department of Health to encourage progression of the Help with Travel Costs scheme for people in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs Scheme is the responsibility of the Department of Health (DoH). In addition to ongoing email communications, my officials have met DoH officials on five occasions to discuss matters relating to progressing the Help with Health Costs scheme regulations to include people in receipt of Universal Credit.  Meetings were held on 19 October 2022, 30 November 2023, 20 March 2024, 14 May 2024 and 12 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has been made of the amount of money his Department would save if the Help for Travel Cost scheme was updated to include people on Universal Credit, therefore removing the requirement to process HC1 forms.
answer_text: Help for travel costs is available through the Health Service Low Income Scheme (HS LIS) which is the responsibility of the Department of Health (DoH).Therefore, my department has not completed any assessment of the potential financial savings of including people on Universal Credit in the Scheme and removing the requirement to process HC1 forms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any financial assistance is available to enable children with disabilities to access in-patient dental treatment elsewhere in the UK or Ireland.
answer_text: The relevant process to enable all NI patients, not just children with disabilities, to access inpatient dental treatment elsewhere in the UK or Ireland is through the Strategic Planning and Performance Group’s (SPPG) Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process. The SPPG’s Extra Contractual Referral (ECR) process enables local Trust consultants to seek funding approval to transfer a named patient to a provider outside Northern Ireland.  This applies in cases where there is requirement for assessment or treatment which the consultant considers necessary, but is not available in local health and social care facilities.  This may be either because the treatment needed is of a specialist nature, or because there is a clinical reason why the local service is not appropriate.It should be noted however that most inpatient dental treatments are available within Northern Ireland and most therefore, would not be eligible under the Extra Contractual Referral process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children with disabilities awaiting in-patient dental treatment; and (ii) the average waiting time for in-patient dental services for children with disabilities, as of the end of October 2024, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is the only Trust in Northern Ireland to provide inpatient dental treatment for children with disabilities.Belfast Trust has advised that it has a waiting list for all paediatric general anaesthetic (GA) patients. This includes a mix of inpatient and day-cases. However, the waiting list is not broken down by disability/ absence of disability so it is unable to provide this information.Belfast Trust has advised that the average waiting time for all paediatric general anaesthetic (GA) patients at the end of October 2024 was 40 months. The waiting list is not broken down by disability/ absence of disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to reduce waiting times for children with learning disabilities, who are living in pain, to access in-patient dental treatment.
answer_text: The Community Dental Service specialises in providing dental care to patients who, for various reasons are not suitable for treatment in the primary care setting. For young children, those with additional needs or particularly complex cases, it may be necessary for the treatment to be take place under General Anaesthetic (GA). Some patients may require all or most of their dental care under GA.The Community Dental Service (CDS) in all five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts offer dental treatment under GA to children with additional needs. However, for those children with the most complex needs a GA Daycase in a district general hospital may not be suitable. Instead, for both patient safety and complexity of care reasons, a referral to the regional Paediatric Consultant-led service in BHSCT may be required.The CDS operate a strict triage and prioritisation process which is based on clinical need. This ensures waiting times for acute patients are less than those for urgent patients as they are given a higher priority grading; those patients with a routine grading have the longest waiting times.The CDS waiting lists for dental treatment under GA were severely impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic and associated infection prevention and control restrictions. Regionally, in 2023-24, the number of children receiving dental treatment under GA was approximately 90% of the 2019-20 figure. Trusts currently report that the main barrier to achieving pre-pandemic levels of dental GA activity is theatre access and in particular a shortage of theatre nursing staff.Throughout the later stages of the pandemic some waiting list initiatives were undertaken by some of the Trust CDS teams. These included additional lists (including weekends), as well as a successful cross border initiative in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust for children requiring timely extraction of permanent teeth. Attempts to maximise the use of alternatives to GA are in place in some Trusts. These included the use of inhalation sedation (twilight sleep) and advanced sedation techniques (Penthrox). These approaches are administered in a dental surgery thereby overcoming the issue of lack of theatre availability.Another method that has been successfully utilised by some Trusts is to have a GA team and patient list on standby to take advantage of any free theatre space that becomes available at short notice due to list cancellation by another specialty.The Southern Health and Social Care Trust have also successfully changed their model of delivery of paediatric theatre services recently.  They have trained a Paediatric Perioperative Nurse for the Paediatric theatres in Daisy Hill Hospital as well as undertaking international recruitment to increase the number of theatre nurses.Last year, my Department consulted upon the Oral Health Improvement Plans for children and older persons. This document made a number of strategic recommendations to prevent dental decay among children. If these recomm

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of applications; and (ii) the amount of funding secured from Financial Transactions Capital for projects to improve or build new homes in each of the last five financial years, including 2024-25.
answer_text: Allocations of Financial Transactions Capital are made to departments as part of the normal budgetary process. The table below shows the allocations of Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) for the last five financial years to the Department for Communities to improve or build new social housing.                                                                                                                                                                                                     £000sYear20202021202220232024Allocation39,25045,75036,25014,25032,590

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of pensioners that have made applications for Pension Credit as a result of local promotion actions following the UK Government decision to restrict criteria for the Winter Fuel Payment; and (ii) how he will ensure all pensioners who can avail of Pension Credit will be proactively contacted by his Department.
answer_text: In the three months following the Chancellor’s announcement on 29th July 2024, my Department received 3026 Pension Credit applications, a 56% increase when compared with the applications received in same period in 2023.My Department continues to undertake a wide range of activities to promote Pension Credit uptake including: regular social media and broadcast campaigns; promotion work with age-based stakeholders; provision of confidential benefit assessment and help to complete forms; and identifying potential Pension Credit entitlement when a person makes their State Retirement Pension claim.  In addition, in conjunction with the Department for Work and Pensions, a letter has been issued to all pensioners regarding the Winter Fuel Payment entitlement changes which signposts to Pension Credit.  My Department is also finalising plans with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to issue a letter to all older Housing Benefit recipients not already in receipt of Pension Credit, to make them aware about the benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has given any consideration to support for the hospitality sector through the rating system.
answer_text: The business rate relief in England for Retail, Hospitality and Leisure was reduced from 75% to 40%, meaning the cost of support is lower in 2025-26. The Executive has received a reduction of £58.2 million in 2025-26 in relation to business rates.The Chancellor’s actions to reduce support in this area serves to confirm the affordability issues that I have consistently referred to with this particular business rates scheme. Those affordability issues are exacerbated if implanted into our local taxbase and economy which is heavily comprised of small and medium-sized enterprises.My Department is assessing the details and implications of the wider policy announcements in the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget Statement as well as the Transforming business rates paper published by Treasury following that Statement. Further work will then be progressed in this area once the Executive finishes considering the proposals I have put forward in relation to the strategic short, medium and long-term steps for the rating system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any positive actions included in the Civil Service Skills Academy and student placement programme to attract people with disabilities and those over 50 to work in the Civil Service.
answer_text: Positive actions applied to encourage disabled persons to apply for the Civil Service Skills Academy included the use of Positive Action Advertising (PAA) and targeted outreach with the disability sector via an information session on the Skills Academy. Actions to promote the Civil Service Student Placement Programme to disabled persons included outreach with Queens’ University Belfast, Ulster University and the Open University; promotion of the Programme to the disability sector as part of the recruitment advertising campaign; and an equal opportunities statement welcoming applications from disabled people. The over 50 age demographic is not under-represented in the Civil Service workforce therefore PAA is not targeted at that group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether there is a requirement to consider the requirements of each Department to enable a change of use to meet wider Executive priorities, such as Housing, as part of her review of the Civil Service office estate.
answer_text: The policy for identifying a ‘public need’ for surplus public sector assets prior to selling these on the open market is well established through the ‘Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property in Northern Ireland (November 2018)’ process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail why a Citizens' Assembly has not progressed in line with the commitment in New Decade New Approach.
answer_text: Civic engagement plays an important role in supporting policy development. We continue to assess options for the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why water infrastructure costs are not included in Social Housing Grants.
answer_text: The Housing Association Guide sets out clearly what Registered Housing Associations can claim as part of the Housing Association Grant. As per the Guide, any infrastructure costs (including sewers, connections or sustainable drainage systems (SUDs) which are within the boundaries of the Social Housing development will be deemed as qualifying scheme costs and therefore eligible for Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on all discussions held with the new UK Government regarding the UK Shared Prosperity Fund, given the cut-off date for current funding is 31st March 2025.
answer_text: Executive departments continue to engage and work collaboratively in seeking to secure the best possible outcomes for the people and sectors here. The Finance and Communities Ministers met with the Minister for Local Growth Alex Norris MP on the 17th September 2024 to discuss the loss of EU funds and successor funding options and the role of the Executive. The Minister for Housing, Communities and Local Growth (MHCLG) was pressed on the need for urgent clarification on successor funding particularly due to the recurrent cliff-edge faced by the Community and Voluntary Sector.DoF are engaging regularly with MHCLG and are pressing for clarity on the allocations and interventions to be delivered for the SPF transition year and for early and full engagement on successor funding from 2026 onwards pressing for devolved design and delivery of any future fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether both the Strangford and Portaferry ferries will be fully operational when the Strangford ferry service is reinstated later this month.
answer_text: I can confirm that Strangford Lough Ferry Service will return to a full sailing schedule upon completion of the essential slipway works being facilitated by the current temporary suspension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what action he will be taking to encourage self-employed people to have a pension.
answer_text: Finding effective, durable, retirement savings solutions for self-employed individuals is a long-term challenge not just nationally but globally. Whilst automatic enrolment into workplace pensions does not apply to the self-employed, individuals can arrange their own pension provision including through National Employment Savings Trust (NEST), personal pensions, stakeholder pensions and self-invested personal Pensions (SIPP). However there are specific barriers to pensions savings experienced by many self-employed people, including irregularity of income.The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) is exploring the feasibility of retirement savings solutions for the self-employed, however this work is long term.  My officials continue to liaise with DWP officials on this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is being removed from Portaferry Surgery; and (ii) whether CBT services will continue to be provided by primary care via GP surgeries within all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: (i) The Northern Ireland Local Enhanced Service (NI LES) for Mild to Moderate Depression enables practices to employ practice-based counsellors to provide counselling for patients (aged 18 years and above) with mild to moderate depression. This service ensures that patients receive care from competent practitioners for mild to moderate depression with low intensity psychosocial interventions such as individual guided self-help based on the principles of cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT).Portaferry Surgery is among 185 practices throughout NI contracted to provide this service in the 2024/25 financial year.The service has not been withdrawn from Portaferry Surgery.All practices contracted to provide this service are allocated funding to provide mild to moderate (MMD) depression counselling sessions. With regards to Portaferry Surgery the practice was allocated additional funding in year that will allow the practice to continue providing counselling sessions.(ii) The NI LES Mild to Moderate Depression service is an enhanced service which covers enhanced aspects of clinical care of a patient - all of which are beyond the scope of essential General Medical Services. They are therefore not mandatory and as independent contractors GP Practices have the choice to contract to provide this service or not.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what considerations have been put in place for maternity services to ensure when a stillbirth or a miscarriage occurs in hospital, that mothers are not placed in wards where other patients or families are celebrating a birth.
answer_text: Early Pregnancy Clinics are available in most maternity/gynaecology units across Northern Ireland.  These clinics will have telephone triage and self-referral triage in place that successfully help to divert those experiencing pregnancy or loss away from ED units, however it is not possible at this time to provide 24/7 Early Pregnancy Clinics and this can mean that not all patients avail of this service in their time of need.On Maternity wards, maternity service staff are very aware of the sensitivities for mothers and families around the time of a loss of a baby and how important it is to provide a safe environment to allow a family to grieve without being exposed to others celebrating.  While all Trusts endeavour to provide a room in a quieter ward e.g. Early Pregnancy where there is less likelihood of babies in the vicinity, on some occasions a mother may be placed in a ward where there are babies but staff do their utmost to provide a private room as well as all other psychological and physical supports available.As the infrastructure varies across Trusts, SPPG have summarised each Trust’s response below:Belfast HSC TrustThe Belfast Trust Maternity Service staff are very aware of the sensitivities for mothers and families around the time of a loss of a baby and how important it is to provide a safe environment to allow a family to grieve without being exposed to others celebrating.At this time the current facilities in Belfast Trust are outdated and the Service had hoped to be availing of a new maternity hospital but this is delayed.  For Mums who have had a stillbirth the Trust provides a side room called the Snow Drop room in the Delivery Suite which offers privacy but it is not sound proofed.  Other women who have had early pregnancy loss are cared for in quieter wards e.g. Early Pregnancy where there is less likelihood of babies in the vicinity.  However, on some occasions a mother may be placed in a ward where there are babies but staff do ensure that a private room is provided as well as all other psychological and physical supports available.South Eastern HSC TrustWithin the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust miscarriages are managed within the Early Pregnancy Problem Clinic (EPPC).  This clinic is based from Ward 4E which is our Gynaecology ward.  Ward 4E is located in a separate building from the Maternity Hospital.In the event a woman has a still birth she is cared for in our Bereavement Suite which is located in a specific area of Labour Ward which is designed specifically for this purpose.Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Trust cares for women who have a stillbirth in the ‘Willow Suite’ which is a bereavement room opened in 2023,  outside of the birthing suite.  This room was specifically commissioned and designed to provide compassionate and sensitive bereavement care to women and their families during the sad loss of their baby.  The woman will remain in the room after the birth until she is discharged home. Regrettably if

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what actions he will take to require all Local Government Development Plans to be completed and published by as part of his work to reduce homelessness.
answer_text: Council Local Development Plans (LDPs) are the main vehicle for housing delivery by assessing future housing land requirements and managing housing growth to achieve sustainable patterns of residential development.  This process allows councils to address local housing issues within their communities.  Ultimately, councils have the important responsibility for setting a vision for the long-term future development of their areas through the preparation of LDPs.  In doing so councils have the flexibility to bring forward bespoke approaches to dealing with any local issues which affect their communities. In the North, the promotion of healthy housing policy and the provision of decent, affordable and sustainable homes is the responsibility of the Department for Communities (DfC).I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way. The Planning Improvement Programme is progressing, working closely with local government and partners, to improve current processes and the performance and delivery of the system. This wide-ranging improvement programme has already delivered important changes, all of which will continue to improve the overall efficiency and effectiveness of our planning system. I want to build on the positive work that has been taken forward to date and have asked my officials to arrange a series of meetings with councils with a specific focus on improving performance; identifying challenges; recognising success and promoting key learning across the system. Within my Department, steps have already been taken to increase staffing, mitigate and address performance of consultees. However, there is more to do, and I am delighted the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees and to appoint persons to undertake work that the Planning Appeals Commission has been unable to progress. I am hopeful that this bid will ultimately be successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the enactment of section 11 of the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department is working on commencing longer Notice to Quit periods, as provided for in Section 11 of the Act. However, as you are aware, the Act stipulates that regulations to specify those circumstances where longer notice periods will not apply must be in place before this happens.  My Department commissioned the Chartered Institute of Housing to produce a research report on those circumstances. The report and subsequent stakeholder discussion revealed a number of complexities which required further consideration, such as, in cases of domestic violence or when anti-social behaviour is as a result of a mental health condition. To this end, my Department is in the process of finalising a robust Equality Impact Assessment, with consultation planned for the coming months. Subject to the EQIA and other work completing as planned, I anticipate that draft regulations will be published for consultation in the early part of 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the Apprenticeship Challenge Fund.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the median gap between rent and the Local Housing Allowance for Universal Credit and Housing Benefit claimant households in the private rented sector.
answer_text: In May 2024, the average (median) gap between rent and the Local Housing Allowance for households in private rented sector for claimants on Universal Credit was £79.In September 2024, the average (median) gap between rent and the Local Housing Allowance for households in private rented sector for claimants on Housing Benefit was £78. Data source: Universal Credit Full-Service system (UCFS) May 2024. Note that due to retrospection of UCFS data UC figures are subject to change. NIHE Housing Benefit Data is taken from September 2024 to ensure that 2024 LHA rates are accounted for.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how any income generated from a removal of rates exemption for university accommodation will be used.
answer_text: No decision has been taken on the removal of this measure. As a general rule, any revenue savings from rating support that is removed would be a matter for the Executive to decide in line with budgetary processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any reports and research (i) completed; and (ii) planned to determine the affordability, suitability and standard of student accommodation provided by higher and further education facilities.
answer_text: There are no reports or research completed or planned. However, the interim report of the Magee Taskforce (available here: Magee Taskforce interim report | Department for the Economy) does highlight the lack of student accommodation as a key constraint on the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus and suggests action from the local council and the university to help address this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any estimate that has been made on the amount of income that would be generated if the removal of rates exemption for university accommodation is progressed as part of her budget sustainability plan.
answer_text: Any removal of the stated exemption would raise around £2M in revenue, shared between the Executive and District Councils.No decision has been taken on the removal of this measure. As a general rule, any revenue savings from rating support that is removed would be a matter for the Executive to decide in line with budgetary processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the Ulster University Magee Taskforce plans to meet affordable and appropriate future student accommodation requirements; and (ii) whether private investment has been secured to deliver and manage these units.
answer_text: My officials will continue to work with Ulster University and other partners to support the development of affordable, appropriate student accommodation.There has been significant growth in privately-owned purpose-built student accommodation in Belfast in recent years, it is anticipated that similar developments will come forward  to build student accommodation for students attending Magee.Since the report of the Taskforce was published a planning application has been submitted for construction of a purpose-built student accommodation on the waterfront, conveniently located near to the campus. The Taskforce is working with Derry City and Strabane District Council and others to drive campus growth including student accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is considering how the removal of rates exemption from university accommodation will impact students and the affordability of third level education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials have discussed this issue with stakeholders such as the universities. The removal of rates exemptions on university accommodation will ultimately be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether the Shared Housing policy will be led by his Department or the Housing Executive; and (ii) to detail any plans there are to review the Shared Housing programme.
answer_text: As of 1st February 2024, operational delivery of the Shared Housing Programme has migrated to the ‘Housing for All’ Shared Housing team in the Housing Executive. The new team is now established and developing new policies and procedures. My Department has completed two reviews of the Shared Housing Programme, and these have resulted in 28 recommendations which have informed and increased shared housing delivery.  The Shared Housing Programme transferred to the Housing Executive on 1st February 2024, and therefore, any future reviews will be managed by the Housing Executive.If their evaluations result in changes to policy or Programme guidance, the NIHE will draft and agree changes with the Registered Housing Associations and share the final document(s) with my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps she has taken to remove the second religion question from the census questions being planned for 2031.
answer_text: The work required to develop census questions for 2031 has not yet been initiated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the investment agreed and planned for new Shared Housing for (a) 2024-25; (b) 2025-26; and (iii) 2026-27; and (ii) the number of houses this investment will create.
answer_text: A budget of £26.5m has been allocated for Shared Housing in the financial year 2024/25. Funding for future years will be based on budget allocation as agreed by the Executive.My Department has an annual delivery target of 200 shared social units per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to ensure fair and equitable access for people who are deaf and hard of hearing to all services provided by (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) GPs.
answer_text: Health and Social Care TrustsThe Health and Social Care trusts are committed to delivering services that are accessible to people who are deaf and hard of hearing.  Trusts work proactively to ensure that services comply with the statutory Equality and Disability duties, for example Section 75 of the NI Act 1988, Disability Discrimination Act 1995, and promote best practice.  Some of the work within Trusts includes:Dedicated Trust Sensory Support TeamsTrust Disability Consultation PanelsVideos which are available in BSL and ISL and subtitled versionsDeaf Awareness Training / Toolkits for staff / GuidesStaff mandatory regional training, for example, Equality, Good Relations and Human Rights; Making a Difference eLearning.Promotional material, such as Posters / leafletsProvision of assistive listening devicesAttendance at Deaf Service Users groupsJAM (Just A Minute) Card, providing support to those with communication difficulties.Care OpinionHSC Disability Action Plan 2024 – 2029HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland worked collaboratively on the development of their new five-year Disability Action Plan and Equality Action Plan (2024-2029).  The plans outline actions and initiatives to address section 75 inequalities, improve equality of opportunity, promote positive attitudes for disabled people and improve their participation in public life.  The Actions in the Plan have been shaped by an audit of inequalities, engagement with experts by experience and key stakeholders and formal consultation.A specific Action is included in the 2024 – 2029 Plan in relation to deaf and hard of hearing service users.  This commits all Trusts to continue the roll out of the Health and Social Care Regional Communication Support Service for People who are d/Deaf, d/Deafblind and Hard of Hearing and improve access to Sign Video within hospitals using computers and phones accessing the Trust Wi-Fi.Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS)The RCSS was introduced on 1st April 2023 and offers a range of in-person and remote sign language interpreting services for health and social care appointments.  The service is provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI) and is delivered through one central booking service.  SPPG have set up an Advisory Board to help advise and shape the RCSS for people who are deaf and hard of hearing in Northern Ireland.(ii)	GP’sThe GMS Contract states that for GP services, ‘a contract must contain a term which requires the contractor in core hours- to provide – essential services, and additional services funded under the global sum, at such times, within core hours, as are appropriate to meet the reasonable needs of his patients’.It also stipulates that the practice leaflet must detail, ‘Whether the practice premises have suitable access for disabled patients and, if not, the alternative arrangements for providing services to such patients.All patients accessing an HSCNI service including GPs and their hospital appointments can access Sign Language Int

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what action his Department is taking to protect and improve conditions for seabird populations.
answer_text: My Department recently launched a public consultation on a Seabird Conservation Strategy and Action Plan for Northern Ireland which outlines a number of objectives and associated actions that are required to address the various threats and pressures faced by seabirds to improve their resilience and make steps towards recovery. This includes increasing the evidence base, in particular, for at-sea records, and delivering targeted management for issues related to habitat loss from sea level rise or reducing impacts from invasive non-native mammalian predators. This strategy, alongside others my Department has produced, was developed in a co-design approach with key stakeholders, and it is by working together that we can deliver the actions outlined.The consultation closes on 16th December and I would like to take this opportunity to strongly encourage anyone who feels they can contribute, in words and also in actions, to respond.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any meetings she has had with the new UK Government to confirm their changes to fiscal rules.
answer_text: Following the election, I had introductory meetings with both the Chief Secretary to Treasury and the Chancellor of the Exchequer in mid-July. Then, along with the First and Deputy First Minister I met with the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 12 September to discuss the 2024-25 financial position, including a request for early clarification of any additional Barnett consequentials for 2024-25.Together with the Devolved Administration Finance Ministers I met with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury most recently at the Finance: Interministerial Finance Committee on 03 October, where I took the opportunity to highlight the need to invest in public services, infrastructure and public sector workers following years of Tory austerity and I also raised the fiscal rules, particularly in respect of investment in infrastructure.Given the wider message on public finances socialised by the British Government, the Chief Secretary to the Treasury indicated that the new Labour Government were undertaking a wide ranging review on public finances in advance of decisions for the Autumn Budget due on 30 October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any meeting requests from organisations that he has turned down since taking up office.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the rationale used for not providing local government with hardship funding for vulnerable residents; and (ii) whether he will be bidding for funding to support vulnerable people through local councils in the next monitoring round.
answer_text: Due to the financial constraints on my Department’s budget, I am currently unable to provide any hardship funding for 2024/25 and due to the continuing uncertainty in funding, it would not be possible to consider a recurrent funding model at this time.The Department of Finance have commissioned the 2024/25 October Monitoring Round and there are no opportunities to bid for Non Ring-Fenced Resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any preventative expenditure and investment measures being considering as part of his departmental budget planning for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: Heavily constrained, single-year budgets severely limit my Department’s ability to consider longer term preventative expenditure and investment measures.  Efficiencies have however been progressed to live within Budget, including rationalising my Department’s office estate and exiting four Belfast office buildings to provide recurrent savings, and progressing the transformation of Health Assessments for welfare benefits which longer term will also provide savings.  Work is also ongoing on an Asset Management Strategy for my Department, and input has been provided to inform the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland (ISNI). My Department has submitted both 2024-25 In-Year and Budget 2025-26 requirements and will continue to ensure its expenditure and investment plans make best use of public funds.  This includes seeking to secure additional funding in key areas, such as investment in New Build Social Homes and Homelessness Prevention measures, which if secured at a meaningful level with help tackle homelessness in a sustainable manner and drive down the amount spent annually on temporary accommodation.  Additional funding has been provided to the Supporting People programme in 2024-25 in recognition of the invaluable support to vulnerable people and the important work of these schemes in preventing additional demands on budgets elsewhere, particularly health and justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any consideration is being given to introducing a preventative expenditure working group to identify how best to utilise budgets across Departments to achieve societal change, such as ending paramilitarism or ending homelessness.
answer_text: I would be happy to consider any proposals from departments to participate in a preventative expenditure working group.I would encourage all departments to work collaboratively to drive societal change. In terms of funding, Departments may fund joint programmes from their existing baseline or may bid for additional funding through the Budget process. Each department may bid separately, or a lead department may bid on behalf of departments. It is then a matter for the Executive to consider which bids can be prioritised given the funding available. In terms of ending paramilitarism, an Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime has been in place since 2016. This cross Departmental Programme is chaired by the Head of the Civil Service and comprises representatives from Departments and Agencies who can deliver societal change in tackling this issue. The Executive provided £8 million in the 2024-25 Budget to support this work, along with an additional £1.5 million of funding to The Executive Office in June Monitoring. Funding for the Programme has also been provided by the British Government.In relation to homelessness, the Housing Executive has a statutory responsibility and has published its Homelessness Strategy 2022-27. To aid the Housing Executive, in June Monitoring, I along with Executive Colleagues, provided an additional £20 million for the social housing development programme and a further £2 million Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) was provided for the ‘Move-on’ accommodation scheme, which supports those who have been experiencing homelessness. I also have encouraged the Communities Minister to make more use of the FTC funding that is available. Our joint ambition is to do more, and the further use of FTC has the potential to be part of the long-term solution, reflecting the shared priority set out in the draft Programme for Government around providing more social, affordable and sustainable housing.As Homelessness is strongly linked to poverty, it is essential that we as an Executive work closely together in developing an agreed Anti-Poverty Strategy. I look forward to the Communities Minister providing his draft Anti-Poverty Strategy, which we as an Executive can then consider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the traffic management measures he will put in place to prevent commuters from causing congestion and parking pressures in Portaferry while the foot passenger ferry is in operation.
answer_text: Essential resurfacing works of The Strand, Castle Street, and Loughshore Road in Portaferry have been carefully planned to take place during the period in which the vehicle ferry is not operational beginning on Monday 14 October 2024.  Traffic control arrangements will be in place with limited use of full road closures to facilitate the final resurfacing phases. The passenger ferry will be using the pontoon opposite Ferry Street at the end of the resurfacing works.  Pedestrian access will be maintained during the resurfacing works.Anyone wishing to park and use the passenger ferry service will be directed to the free council car parks at Meetinghouse Street and The Rope Walk. If there are any concerns regarding motorists parking vehicles in such a manner that causes an obstruction, or blocking access or egress, then please refer the matter to the PSNI who are the enforcement authority using their non-emergency number 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department is taking to stop the advertising of vapes (i) in close proximity to schools; and (ii) where confectionery can be purchased.
answer_text: There are existing restrictions on advertising of nicotine vapes as set out in the Tobacco and Related Products Regulations 2016.  The King’s speech in July this year included a commitment to introduce a new Tobacco and Vapes Bill to progressively increase the age at which people can buy cigarettes and impose limits on the sale and marketing of vapes.   The Government has also stated a desire to ban vapes from being branded and advertised to appeal to children in order to stop the next generation from becoming addicted to nicotine. I am supportive of the extension of these legislative measures to address youth vaping to Northern Ireland, however final decisions on Northern Ireland’s inclusion in the Tobacco and Vapes Bill will be subject to Executive and Assembly agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to recruit and train more GPs.
answer_text: Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the GP workforce.  This includes funding to support the recruitment and retention of GPs through a number of initiatives comprising the “Attract, Recruit, Retain, Scheme” the GP Retainer Scheme, the GP Mentor Scheme, the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme and the GP Fellowship Scheme.  My Department has taken action to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland and we are currently updating the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher schemes to align with the new General Medical Council (GMC) pathways for the recruitment of GPs from other countries joining the GMC GP Register.  My Department has also introduced a Streamlined Returner process with the aim of removing unnecessary barriers to those wishing to return to the GP workforce.   Over recent years, my Department has prioritised investment in the training of future GPs.  The annual number of GP training places has been increased from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  In addition, my Department is working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) on innovations to the local GP training programme with the objective of enhancing the recruitment and retention of trainees in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on plans to address the waiting list for paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tubes surgery.
answer_text: From the outset, let me assure the Member and the parents of these young people that I fully appreciate the pressure that delays are causing.  This procedure is complex and it requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  There are 83 children currently on the waiting list with the longest wait over three years.  The Trust is currently examining every means to address this issue and it is hoped that this can assisted with additional measures being put in place from this month.  For example, at the moment a paediatric surgeon is validating the list so waiting times and numbers on the list may change dependent on the clinical priority. This validation process will identity which children are suitable for possible transfer to Alder Hey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.  Once the initial scoping is done the parents of the eligible children for potential transfer to Liverpool will be contacted about the procedure.Belfast Trust are also hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in the coming weeks, to carry out additional sessions this month. As well as this, discussions are underway to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon a gastrostomy surgery list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this if it can be actioned.There is also a review underway of work performed in RBHSC and in District General Hospitals to ascertain what work could be redirected to District General Hospitals.  This may free up further capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department, in conjunction with the Education Authority, is identifying, assessing, monitoring and aiming to meet the demand for the provision of integrated education in area planning, as required by Section 5 (1) (a) of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) where this information is publicly available to review.
answer_text: The Integrated Education Act Section 10 Report was issued on 26 April 2024 which outlined the department’s analysis of demand for Integrated Education. This analysis concluded that although there was broadly sufficient provision of Integrated Education at primary school level, demand was not being met at the post-primary level. On conclusion of work to update guidance for schools considering transformation to Integrated status, my Department will commission the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education (NICIE) to conduct an engagement exercise with those local communities with oversubscribed integrated schools. NICIE will then report on whether there is sufficient interest to merit development of integrated provision and/or encourage Transformation. Their findings will be published in due course.My Department will shortly be issuing an updated Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan, which will deal specifically with the issue of meeting the demand for Integrated Education. My officials continue to work closely with colleagues in the Education Authority and other stakeholder groups to develop and implement a process to assess demand. This includes the conducting of a survey to establish the preferences of parents for Integrated Education within and outside the mainstream school admissions process. It also includes an annual survey of attitudes towards Integrated Education within the Northern Ireland Life and Times survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health to progress the changes needed to the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme to enable people on Universal Credit to avail of this support without having to complete the Health Costs Form HC1.
answer_text: My officials are in regular contact with the relevant Department of Health (DoH) officials on the changes required to the Help with Health Costs Scheme for Universal Credit claimants.  In the meantime, Universal Credit claimants should continue to apply for Help with Health Costs by completing a HC1 application form and forwarding it to the Department for Communities. Further information can be found on nidirect: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-you-have-health-condition-or-disability#toc-8

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the additional cost to his Department to bus pupils from Portaferry to schools in Ballynahinch and Downpatrick during the five weeks when the Strangford ferry service is suspended.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport. It has advised me that the Department of Infrastructure has agreed that Translink will provide any additional bus transport needed for the five-week period while the Strangford Ferry service is suspended. There is no additional transport assistance required from the EA Transport Service and therefore no additional cost for bus transport will be incurred by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the action his Department is taking, in conjunction with the Education Authority, to examine evidence of expected future demand for integrated education; and (ii) the methodology used to collate this evidence.
answer_text: Officials in my Department are working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority to develop and conduct a survey of parents who have recently made school preference decisions through the admissions portal. This survey will ask parents and guardians, in line with the Integrated Education Act, whether they have a preference for Integrated Education and how various factors have influenced their school preferences. This data will allow my Department to consider existing and future demand and provide data on which existing Area Planning structures can take action to aim to meet that demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the role he and his Department have in relation to the reform of the Housing Executive, including whether he has the ability to set a timetable for completion.
answer_text: I am actively pressing for a solution to enable the Housing Executive to access borrowing which does not score in Public Expenditure terms. I am seeking a solution that does not require reclassification or significant reform and which will put the Housing Executive on a sustainable footing to increase routine and planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes. This work is ongoing, and it is not possible for me to confirm a timetable for completion at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to clarify his statement to the Assembly on 1 October 2024 that Community Transport could provide transport to hospital, given (i) those operators are not permitted to apply for contracted health transport services under their Section 10B permit; and (ii) the Department for Infrastructure Dial-a-Lift programme states the service may not be used for hospital transport outside the local area.
answer_text: On launching the public consultation on the document - 'Hospitals — Creating a Network for Better Outcomes' – I acknowledged the importance of having in place suitable transport arrangements to underpin the hospital network and there is a section in the document dedicated to this.  The potential need for additional travel for some is carefully balanced against the need to provide more timely access to care and a more efficient service, which is better able to meet the health needs of the entire population. Importantly, my comments were made regarding wider transport options that are, or could be, available for our communities. The provision of transport is, of course, a cross-departmental issue and I have already been engaging with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to discuss the full range of transport options available.  We have instructed officials to recommission the Cross-Departmental Working Group to explore how to provide the best, value for money services.  I understand that this Group will be meeting again in October and will provide further options for Ministerial consideration, in due course. In the meantime, I would encourage all those with an interest in the hospital network document, including those with a perspective on transport provision, to respond to the consultation, which is now live.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider introducing a companion pass to enable carers to travel for free on public transport with a person with a disability or aged 75 and over.
answer_text: The NI Concessionary Fares Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2023 and included an option which would introduce companion passes for disabled people unable to travel alone. While public support for this option was strong (with 79% of respondents in favour of the change), unfortunately, due to the severe financial pressures facing the Department, I am not in a position to introduce changes which would increase the cost of the Scheme at this time.  However, I will review my decision on the introduction of companion passes for disabled people unable to travel alone and also consider the other options included in the consultation again at the next budget. I am not, at this time, considering introducing companion passes based on age alone.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) what their Department is doing to ensure co-production and co-design of the Together: Building a United Community consultation questions; and (ii) who they are engaging with to co-produce and co-design the consultation.
answer_text: An extensive stakeholder engagement process has been completed as part of the review of T:BUC, providing a number of opportunities for stakeholders across the community and voluntary sector, local government, central government, academics and representative groups to contribute to the Review. Officials are currently finalising a draft of the Review for our consideration with a view to developing a refreshed approach to Good Relations to build on progress to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of Housing Executive decisions to refuse new housing development in areas when the waiting list for social homes is projected to hit 50,000 households in the coming months.
answer_text: It is my assessment that this issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are a number of factors to consider when supporting / committing expenditure to new social housing developments.  The location and pattern of housing need varies in different locations across Northern Ireland.  To that end, the Housing Executive complete housing assessments annually which consider the location and type of housing needs by housing need area / settlement level.  The Commissioning Prospectus is then published (annually) which communicates this information to the sector and is an important document to direct housing associations in their site search to identify development opportunities in locations where there is a projected social housing need and a shortage of new build proposals. The Housing Executive engages with housing associations at an early stage in the site identification process and assess every new build proposal against the 5-year projected need for the area and on the suitability of the location and the type and mix of housing proposed.  Those schemes which have been supported are then assessed for inclusion on the 3-year Social Housing Development Programme to ensure they are capable of meeting identified housing need and to ensure they meet Housing Association Guide Standards.   All schemes are programmed in line with strategic guidelines and availability of grant funding.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the number of new social homes required in each local council area; and (ii) to detail the number of applications for social housing that have been refused by the Housing Executive in each local council area in (a) 2022; (b) 2023; and (c) 2024 to date.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The table below provides projected social housing need, i.e. the number of new homes required over the 5 year period 2023-28 to meet identified social housing need, in each Council area.2023-2028 HNA Projections Local Government District5-year social housing needAntrim & Newtownabbey1229Ards & North Down1390Armagh, Banbridge & Craigavon1498Belfast8551Causeway Coast & Glens1638Derry City & Strabane 4066Fermanagh & Omagh 905Lisburn & Castlereagh1636Mid & East Antrim 863Mid Ulster 912Newry, Mourne & Down 2234Northern Ireland24922The Housing Executive engages with housing associations at an early stage in the site identification process and assess every new build proposal against the 5-year projected need for the area and on the suitability of the location and the type and mix of housing proposed. Those schemes which have been supported are then assessed for inclusion on the 3-year Social Housing Development Programme to ensure they are capable of meeting identified housing need and to ensure they meet Housing Association Guide Standards. All schemes are programmed in line with strategic guidelines and availability of grant funding.  There are cases where it is not possible to support a housing association proposal due to a lack of evidenced housing need, unsuitability of location e.g. outside development limits, or where there are sufficient supported, programmed and on-site schemes to meet projected need. Such issues are normally raised at a very early stage in the process to avoid nugatory investigative work on the part of the association. At this stage the communication is generally informal, by email or telephone, and these discussions are not recorded in a manner which can be readily quantified.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work his Department has been undertaking to identify capital funding investment from non-government sources to support new social house builds.
answer_text: New social homes are currently delivered via grant funding provided by the Department through the Housing Association Grant and private finance arranged by the Housing Associations.My Department is supportive of any initiatives which might improve the delivery of new social or affordable housing given the significant housing need.A two-day Strategic Insights Lab was held in June, which brought together key stakeholders to share knowledge and experience on how to increase social housing delivery in the current challenging environment. I am considering a number of recommendations which will form part of an action plan to be taken forward. A number of these recommendations include proposals on funding methods which will be given further consideration and require engagement with the relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the effectiveness of Belfast City Council’s Employment Academy interventions; and (ii) whether he will be encouraging councils to take forward such access to employment and skills interventions.
answer_text: In 2023/24, Employment Academies funded through the Belfast Labour Market Partnership and Belfast City Council actively supported 610 participants across a wide variety of sectors. Of the 329 participants who have completed an Employment Academy to date, 289 have successfully gained employment. The remaining 10 Councils across Northern Ireland, through their local labour market partnerships action plan, also provide equally successful Employment Academies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he has received the Northern Ireland Local Government Association recommendations in relation to councillors; and (ii) whether an Independent Panel will be appointed to consider councillor remuneration, in a similar same way the Assembly Commission maintains transparency by appointing an Independent Panel to review MLA remuneration.
answer_text: I have received a report from the Review of Roles and Responsibilities of Councillors Working Group.  The Chair and one other member were appointed using the public appointment process.  Other members of the group included representatives from the NI Local Government Association; National Association of Councillors; Irish Congress of Trade Unions/Northern Ireland Public Service Alliance, and officials from my Department.I have not made any decisions yet on how councillor remuneration should be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the retrofitting of social homes is a key strategic element of his Housing Strategy.
answer_text: Decarbonisation and a just transition to low carbon homes is one of the five key objectives of the Housing Supply StrategyThe Strategy recognises the key role that retrofitting of all existing homes will play in achieving this objective.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the input his Department has to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: My Department does not have any input to the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill. However, I have agreed in principle with the Minister for Economy, that my officials can use the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill as a vehicle for progressing Gender Pay Gap reporting in NI.  I will make an announcement in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans she has to bring forward regulation of property management companies.
answer_text: Management companies must act in accordance with existing relevant companies legislation, which falls outside the remit of my Department.However, the issue of how shared property is managed also has an interface with substantive land law, and is one that I am keen to see some progress in developing policy proposals. I am currently exploring with officials how to secure the appropriate expertise in what is a complex area. Further progress will depend on that and competing legislative priorities over the remainder of this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any unused ink cartridges will be reimbursed to office cost allowances when printers in constituency offices and MLA offices in Parliament Buildings are replaced.
answer_text: When printers in constituency offices and MLA offices in Parliament Buildings are replaced, Members will be able to return unopened and unused ink cartridges purchased from the Northern Ireland Assembly Commission supplier only.  Please note that ink cartridges acceptable for return must have been purchased within 12 months preceding the date on which the printer was replaced. The stock value will then be reimbursed to the Member’s office cost allowance.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) for an update on its work to establish an independent panel to review MLA salaries; and (ii) whether this panel will be required to review and compare the remuneration of Statutory Committees and Standing Committees with other devolved regions, to include a review of remuneration for chairpersons and deputy chairpersons of Standing Committees.
answer_text: Following the resumption of normal Assembly business and the reconstitution of the Assembly Commission in February 2024, the Assembly Commission has considered the detail of a draft Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill at a number of meetings and asked for further papers and advice from officials. The Assembly Commission is scheduled to have a further discussion about the draft Bill and the timing of its potential introduction at its next meeting.   As part of these discussions, the Assembly Commission is giving consideration to the merits of benchmarking the position at the Assembly in relation to all salaries and allowances with other relevant institutions.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it will consult with MLAs as part of its process in reviewing constituency office expenses.
answer_text: Over recent months and years, the Assembly Commission has had ongoing discussions about issues which could be included in a review of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, as amended by the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) (Amendment) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2020, (‘the Determination’).  A number of policy changes relating to the Determination had already been agreed in principle by the Assembly Commission and, following further work over the Summer Recess, the Assembly Commission is currently finalising a new determination.  As is usual, the views of parties and Members have been contributed to the process through the elected Members of the Assembly Commission.  Assembly Commission Members have reflected the experiences and issues raised with them by colleagues and the Assembly Commission has taken account of this feedback to seek to reach balanced outcomes which also take account of value for money and robust processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to confirm that all repair and maintenance work on the Strangford II and Portaferry II ferries will be completed during the five weeks from 14 October 2024 when they are out of service.
answer_text: The ferries require regular and routine maintenance throughout the year. Each vessel must also undergo an annual survey and associated works prior to the issue of a Passenger Ship Safety Certificate by MCA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any arrangements in place with Translink to provide additional buses from Strangford to Downpatrick during the period when the Strangford ferry service is cancelled from 14 October 2024.
answer_text: My Officials are in consultation with Translink about intended alternative arrangements during the temporary suspension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any scoping exercises completed to establish possible alternatives to cancelling the Strangford ferry for five weeks from 14 October 2024.
answer_text: My Department considered a number of options before deciding on this period, which coincides with school half term and when passenger numbers are 40% lower than July and August.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether customers who have unused ferry tickets due to expire during the period when the ferry service is cancelled will be granted an extension to the use by date of those tickets, at least equivalent to the length of time the ferry service is cancelled.
answer_text: I can confirm that during the period of temporary suspension journeys which expire will be honoured by My Department. Officials have issued a SMS message to all current subscribers advising of this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the staff employed on the Strangford ferry service will be redeployed or furloughed during the period when the ferry service is cancelled from 14 October 2024.
answer_text: During the temporary suspension of the ferry service it is intended that crew members will assist with the passenger only service if successfully procured and complete any necessary training.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department plans to replace each of the Strangford ferries.
answer_text: From 01 April – 31 August 2024 at least 1 vessel has been available for 99.96% of over 9,000 scheduled sailings. My Department currently has no plans to replace either ferry. Any future plans will take into consideration decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the roll-out of the Fundamental Reform of Allocations scheme, including a timeline for the completion of phase 3 and phase 4 of the project.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing with the Fundamental Review of Allocations. Stage One was implemented on 31 January 2023. I gave approval to Stage Two in June 2024, and my officials are now working with the Housing Executive on the early stages of Stage Three. The fourth and final phase was due to be completed in 2025; however, timelines are currently being reviewed and are subject to available budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the application process to install a pedestrian road crossing; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to install a pedestrian crossing at Ballynahinch Primary School.
answer_text: Requests or proposals for Controlled Crossing facilities are assessed against the criteria and assessment procedure set out under our current policy RSPPG E005 (Controlled Crossings: Assessment Procedures) which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/controlled-crossings-assessment-procedures-rsppg-e005v6.pdfMy officials undertook a pedestrian survey at Ballynahinch Primary School in February 2024, using the criteria set out in policy document: RSPPG E005.  Following a second stage assessment, I am pleased to advise that this location has been added to our list for pedestrian crossings in the Ards, North Down and Down areas. Unfortunately, however, due to constrained resources, the scheme does not score highly enough to be included on our planned programme for 2024/25 but will be considered for inclusion in future works programmes, subject to available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the timeline; and (ii) the proposal for the pilot for Housing First allocations in 2024-25.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.
