I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 Year 3 Action Plan includes a commitment to publish independent research on the feasibility study for Housing First for Northern Ireland. The research is due to be submitted to the Housing Executive’s Executive Team for consideration in the coming weeks and will be used to shape ongoing development work on any Housing First allocations. Additional Housing First allocation will be subject to engagement with a range of partners and the provision of appropriate funding to support any increased delivery.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration that has been given to implement bowel cancer screening from age 50.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee recommend that bowel cancer screening is offered to individuals aged 50-74. Currently, in Northern Ireland, anyone aged 60-74 years old, who is registered with a GP, is eligible for screening. Action 7 of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. Work to meet the commitments to lower the age range and/or further reduce the sensitivity level is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services.  It is important to note that screening programmes are a pathway and not just a ‘test’. As such, where a Screening Programme makes changes to its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, to include diagnostic capacity and any treatments that may be required, are aligned. Collaborative work has been ongoing between the Public Health Agency (PHA) and Queen’s University Belfast on the development of a bowel cancer screening model (taking into account both age extension and further reduction in FIT threshold). This model will contribute to informing the future approach, including how these are best phased into the programme to maximise benefits and outcomes and to provide more detailed cost information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the implementation of recommendations set out in the Review of Jointly Commissioned Supported Accommodation Projects.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the remit of; (ii) the timeframe of work; and (iii) what has been achieved by the Housing Executive’s Housing Solutions Task and Finish Group.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive and been advised that:“The Accommodation Solutions Task and Finish Group provides strategic oversight and direction for work strands which aim to address temporary accommodation demand and are directly referenced as actions within the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation 2022-27.Individual timeframes are set for specific short, medium, and long-term actions with regular updates on progress provided to the Group. The work being taken forward by the group and across the Housing Executive includes:engaging with Housing Associations regarding bringing their long-term voids into use as temporary accommodation.    the introduction of Houses with Multiple Occupation as a model for providing temporary accommodation.exploring longer term leasing arrangements with accommodation providers, to provide better security of supply. exploring options to provide an additional accommodation and support solution for complex cases in the West Area. exploring options to acquire additional properties for use as temporary accommodation.review exercises with customers aged over 55 in temporary accommodation.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide (i) an update on the remodeling programme for large-scale congregate hostels; and (ii) a timeline for the work on this programme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and as such I have been advised that:“The Housing Executive’s Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation 2022-27 contains an action to work with providers and Supporting People on a remodelling programme for large-scale congregate hostels.The Housing Executive has conducted an analysis of Supporting People funded homeless hostels and has formed a steering group with Supporting People and a provider of one of Northern Ireland’s largest hostels which is considering options for the remodelling of the service.The remodelling of large-scale congregate hostels is likely to extend beyond the period of the current Action Plan.  There are several factors which will affect the timeframe for remodelling, such as demand for temporary accommodation, budget availability, design processes and, where required, the availability of suitable, alternative properties and planning approvals.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding reduction in (i) cash terms; and (ii) real terms to the Housing Executive’s Regional Services Revenue Programme since 2015-16.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Major Adverse Incidents recorded by Supporting People providers in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) type of adverse incident; and (ii) Supporting People thematic group.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of (i) households; and (ii) individuals that were living in temporary accommodation on 31 March and on 1 September in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) age; (b) sex; (c) local council area; (d) District Electoral Area; and (e) type of temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024 contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024  	Section 3 provides information on temporary accommodation placements. As the information is not published in the format you have requested, I am unable to provide you with a more detailed response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the average length of stay for households placed in temporary accommodation on (i) 31 March; and (ii) 1 September, in each of the last five years, broken down by (a) Assembly constituency; (b) local council area; and (c) District Electoral Area.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2023 – March 2024 contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2023-march-2024  	Table 3.5 provides details on households in temporary accommodation by banded length of stay and accommodation type. As the information is not published in the format you have requested, I am unable to provide information by constituency or geographic area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to introduce paid carer's leave in his proposed Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: I am supportive of a paid entitlement and met recently with CarersNI to discuss their proposals. It is important to be honest about the financial constraints placed upon the Executive. In addition to the annually recurring costs of providing paid carer’s leave, there are other possible implications such as the availability of funding for any HMRC system changes that would be required. These changes would require considerable set up costs and the ongoing annual costs could be substantial. Therefore I am urging the British Government to adopt paid carers leave as part of its workers rights agenda. This would mean similar support could be provided here without an impact on the block grant. The Good Jobs Employment Rights Consultation closes on 30 September 2024. Finalisation of policy proposals can only properly be undertaken once all responses to the consultation have been duly evaluated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the action she is taking to ensure Building Regulations are updated to include energy efficiency and retrofitting standards that includes both Energy Performance Certificate and Building Energy Rating systems.
answer_text: My Department is progressing its response to consultation on energy efficiency and related parts of the building regulations which ran from 26th July to 15 December 2023, for publication early next year. This considers relevant metrics and will inform subsequent uplifts to energy efficiency standards. A comprehensive fitness review of Energy Performance Certificates is currently being undertaken by the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government with support from the Department of Energy Security and Net Zero, the outcomes of which are anticipated to be applicable here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans to update and improve the passenger shelter facilities at Portaferry quay for passengers waiting to use the Strangford ferry.
answer_text: The existing shelter at Portaferry quay is owned and maintained by Translink and used by bus and ferry passengers. The Department currently has no plan to update or improve this facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to reintroduce cash payments on the Strangford ferry.
answer_text: Following a period of temporary suspension during the Covid-19 pandemic, cash payments were reintroduced at Strangford Ferry during April 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12140/22-27, in relation to the total spend of £40 million, to detail the amount that was allocated to reimbursements for individuals using taxis to travel to appointments under the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme.
answer_text: All six Health and Social Care Trusts have advised that the expenditure reported in AQW 12140/22-27 did not include expenditure under the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme.Reimbursement under the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme is based on public transport fares. If public transport is unavailable or impractical, an approved private car or taxi journey would be reimbursed at a capped public transport fare rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12140/22-27, in relation to the total spend of £40 million, to detail the amount spent on taxis procured and organised directly by (i) Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Three of the Health and Social Care Trusts [Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT); South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) and Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT)] have advised that they do not hold this level of detail within their central systems and it would require an extensive manual exercise to extract such information at a disproportionate cost.Three of the Health and Social Care Trusts [The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS); Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) and Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT)] have advised that the reported figures within the £40 million total spend, relating to their Trust [Table 1], would be attributable to taxis procured and organised directly by their Trust, in each of the last five years.2019/20£000s2020/21£000s2021/22£000s2022/23£000s2023/24*£000sSouthern Trust1,5739381,5181,9092,152Western Trust819695844853599NIAS2067958532,5882,287     Table 1

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether all contracts, tenders or grants to deliver frontline services in the community includes provision for mental health support for the contractor’s staff.
answer_text: Since the introduction of the Procurement Policy Note 0121 Scoring Social Value, all service contracts procured since June 2022 must include 10% award criteria for scoring social value. SPPG is now fully compliant with PPN 0121 Scoring Social Value. As a result, all above threshold Social Care Service tenders include a minimum I 0% award criteria on Scoring Social Value. For some contracts, the scoring will include social value themes one of which is about supporting the health and wellbeing, including physical and mental health, in the contract workforce.SPPG ensures that when above threshold contracts and services are tendered, those providers that do provide or include support of this sort, have this acknowledged in their application and this will be rewarded within the Contracts Adjudication Group (CAO) scoring scheme. SPPG will consider the option to include for the provision of a requirement to consider the mental health support for the contractor's staff in future early years grant schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review and update the Section 10B bus permits to replicate the Section 22 and Section 19 permits for Community Transport, as used elsewhere in the UK, enabling the sector to provide not-for-profit routes in rural areas and services that will meet Department of Health procurement rules.
answer_text: I am awaiting further submissions from my officials on this matter which will help inform me on what if any further action in regards this matter is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he plans to review the number of dentists providing NHS dental services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area; and (ii) to detail any action he is taking to ensure there is sustainable provision of NHS dentists in the area.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any plans to bring forward legislation to include local councils and council services within the remit of the Public Services Ombudsman.
answer_text: District councils are already included in  Schedule 3 to the Public Services Ombudsman Act (NI) 2016 as a listed authority and therefore fall under the remit of the Public Services Ombudsman.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will (i) carry out a review into the safety of rural bus stops, to identify those that do not have a shelter or appropriate lighting; and (ii) produce a report that includes recommendations for safety improvements.
answer_text: I recognise that all bus shelters, whether in rural or urban communities, are an integral part of the public transport service and provide passengers with shelter and safety from the elements whilst waiting for bus services. I know that Translink regularly receive requests for the provision of new bus shelters at stops throughout the North from passenger focus groups, members of the public, community and elected representatives. There is also a need to replace/refurbish the existing shelters in order to maintain their levels of condition.My officials are currently considering a business case from Translink seeking to replace/refurbish existing bus shelters over a ten-year period. This will also include installing several new bus shelters across the public transport network. This work will be progressed subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail any actions being taken to address the rise in complaints from patients who are unable to access GP services; and (ii) whether he will provide a contact telephone number or email address for patients to be able to raise concerns about GP surgeries.
answer_text: I recognise that being able to access GP practices when we need to do so is important for all of us.  As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.GP services have been experiencing sustained, high levels of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic and in response Practices have continued to adapt how services are delivered. GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care. Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  As part of supporting work in this area, my Department established a GP Access Working Group, which includes GP and service-user representation, to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  Should a patient or service user wish to raise a formal complaint about the service received from a GP, this should be raised with the GP practice itself in the first instance.  All practices are required to have a complaints procedure to address any issues raised by patients.Alternatively, the Complaints Team in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) of the Department of Health can act as an intermediary between a patient and the Practice, further information is available here: FPS Complaints - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  The SPPG Complaints Team can be contacted for further advice and assistance by e-mail: complaints.sppg@hscni.net or by telephoning 028 9536 3893. Independent advice and support on making a complaint about health and social care services is also available from the Patient and Client Council, who offer a free, confidential, patient advocacy service.  Further information, including contact details, is available via the Patient and Client Council website: Contact Us - Patient and Client Council Northern Ireland (hscni.net) or by telephoning Freephone 0800 917 0222.If, following a response to any complaint, a patient remains unhappy they can bring their complaint to the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman (NIP

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to (i) review the Direct Payments system to include a standardised criteria; and (ii) introduce an appeals system within each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: (i) A Direct Payment, which a Health and Social Care Trust has agreed to fund, must be used to purchase services in accordance with assessed need. This individualised approach ensures that care packages are tailored to each person, therefore making a standardised, one-size-fits-all criteria would be inappropriate. Each person’s care requirements are unique, and Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) must be flexible and able to respond to differing needs within the framework provided by legislation, such as the Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002. Direct Payments are utilised across a number of Programmes of Care within Health and Social Care. I have asked my officials to consider and provide me with advice on the current Direct Payments system in Northern Ireland. This will help identify those areas where improvements may be necessary. (ii) Currently, my Department has no plans to introduce a formal appeals systems for Direct Payments which would differ to systems currently in place within HSCTs. The operational management and day-to-day running of Direct Payments, including handling complaints and assessments, remain the responsibility of individual HSCTs. They are tasked with administering the system in line with existing legislation and ensuring that decisions reflect local needs and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why there is a disparity between the criteria used by each Health and Social Care Trust when awarding Direct Payments.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether all providers of contracted or grant funded services that receive funding from his Department, or through an arm’s length body or non departmental public body, are sufficiently funded to enable all employed staff to receive, as a minimum, the Living Wage.
answer_text: My department’s directly contracted services have required all suppliers appointed since June 2022 to ensure that those who are delivering services to DfC are paid at least Real Living Wage to comply with Executive accreditation as a Living Wage employer and deliver on Social Values Policy.Grant funding provides a contribution towards eligible costs, which may include salaries and running costs incurred in the delivery of a range of agreed policy outcomes. My department is not the employer of staff in any arm’s length body or non-departmental public body it funds.  Terms and conditions and pay policies are a matter for the employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether all contracts, tenders or grants issued directly by his Department, or on behalf of his Department through an arm’s length body or non departmental public body, includes provision to enable mental health support to be provided for frontline workers delivering services in the community.
answer_text: All tenders and contracts above threshold (£138,760 inc. VAT), issued by my Department or by its Associated Bodies comply with PPN 01/21 - Scoring Social Value.Since 1st September 2021 this includes a mandatory requirement for suppliers to adopt fair work practices, which should include specifically 'health, safety and psychosocial wellbeing'.Additionally, there is a Supplier Code of Conduct which requires all contracted Suppliers of services to Government to confirm acceptance of the code. This includes the following provision: Provide a safe and healthy workplace for their employees.All contract awards above £30,000 are managed by Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) and they are responsible for development of standard NICS contract terms.The Policy does not mandate inclusion of Social Values in Grant awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people in receipt of Direct Payments in each of the last ten years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A Direct Payment is payment made to an individual as an alternative to services which would have been provided, or purchased by a Health and Social Care Trust.  A Direct Payment must be used to purchase services, in accordance with assessed need and which the Trust has agreed to fund. The use of Direct Payments runs throughout a number of Programmes of Care within the area of Health and Social Care.Details of Direct Payments paid out by Trusts are published by the DoH, these figures can be accessed by following the enclosed link Quarterly direct payments statistics June 2024 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will (i) have the updated Concordat agreement signed by all Executive Ministers; and (ii) ensure that it is circulated to all Departments.
answer_text: The Joint Forum between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector has been working together to develop a draft Partnership Agreement which will renew the commitments in the 2011 Concordat and support the relationship between the sectors.  This draft is currently being finalised.An updated agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector will need to be endorsed by all Executive Ministers and embedded across all departments and local government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Blue Badge application system will accept printed evidence, supplied by applicants, from Encompass rather than their GP.
answer_text: Currently Blue Badge Unit will accept evidence, submitted by applicants, from any suitably qualified medical professional i.e. GPs, physiotherapists, chiropractors, consultants, specialist nurses, MacMillan or Marie Curie nurses, etc. Applicants sometimes send in copies of their medical notes which, in some cases, can suffice if they demonstrate clearly that the person applying has a permanent and substantial disability which means they cannot walk or that they have considerable difficulty walking. Given that the Encompass system is not yet available across all the Trust areas, the efficacy of evidence supplied by the system has yet to be evaluated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Queens Hall and Newtownards Library capital project.
answer_text: Further to my responses to AQW 5873/22-27 and AQW 8963/22-27, Libraries NI have submitted an updated business case supporting the proposed redevelopment of the library which is currently being considered by my Department. Any decision to proceed with a new library facility will be dependent on prioritising of capital investment in a very difficult budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a list of dentists accepting NHS patients in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the Department of Health is unable to hold and maintain a list of dental practices currently accepting new Health Service patients. Patents who are unregistered and require a dentist can make use of the HSC service finder to search for dental practices by area and is available at https://online.hscni.net/hsc-service-finder/ . Practices can be sorted by those closest to an address and then extending further afield.  A contact number for each dental practice is available and will hopefully help your constituent find a practice to register with.		A Northern Ireland-wide Dental Access Scheme (DAS) commenced on 1st August 2024.  This new scheme aims to improve access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent, or pressing condition.  There are six practices in the South Eastern Trust area involved in the scheme and they are listed on the scheme website. Full details of the scheme (including participating practices) can be viewed at:  Dental Access Scheme - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to introduce legislation in relation to estate agents.
answer_text: My Department has no responsibility for estate agents and therefore has no plans to introduce legislation in relation to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on funding for Down Community Transport.
answer_text: I understand the importance to those within our rural communities who rely on community transport provided by Down District Accessible Transport and others, for accessing goods and services, enabling them to be more independent and remain connected to their communities.  Funding for 2024/25 is at the same level as that awarded for 2023/24.  This is subject to the Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the budget and so this allocation is indicative at this stage.  I do, however, expect to be able to confirm opening budget allocations, once I have taken final decisions following consideration of responses to the Equality Impact Assessment after an initial four-week period ending 8th July 2024.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions he will take to ensure children and young people are taught about pensions in the school curriculum.
answer_text: Financial capability, including money management and savings, is currently embedded as a statutory and integral element of the Northern Ireland curriculum.  One of the requirements of the statutory cross-curricular skill of Using Mathematics is that, at each of the four Key Stages throughout compulsory education, pupils should be enabled to develop financial capability skills.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the preventative measures his Department will put in place to reduce the number of children from deprived areas being placed into care.
answer_text: The joint Department of Education and Department of Health Life Deserved Strategy for Looked After Children recognises the link between deprivation and entry to care, and includes a number of commitments aimed at supporting families at an early stage, and providing targeted and timely support to help keep families together where possible.My Department provides funding support totalling £809,732.00 per annum to a multi-agency network of 29 Family Support Hubs across Northern Ireland to provide early intervention services locally to help families address a range of emerging issues, and to signpost families with specific needs to appropriate services. Through the Public Health Agency, my Department also provides funding of £1,332,000.00 per annum to the Early Intervention Support Service, designed to support families with emerging vulnerability, with the aim of de-escalating issues of concern, achieving sustainable change, and promoting capability and capacity within families to resolve difficulties they are experiencing. An Edge of Care Service is also in place in each Health and Social Care Trust, providing intensive and targeted support to young people identified as being at particular risk of entry into care. The overarching aim of this service is to support children to be safe within their family and community.The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland was clear about the need for more help for families to assist them to care well for their children and made a number of recommendations aimed at providing greater focus and attention to family support. In response to these recommendations, a dedicated workstream has been established as part of the ongoing Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, working with partners in the voluntary and community sector, to improve outcomes for children, young people and their families through earlier intervention. While still in its early stages, it is intended that this workstream will develop proposals to build on and extend the range of early intervention and support services in place for children, young people and their families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the eligibility criteria for the Hospital Travel Cost Scheme, whether community transport is considered as an available form of public transport.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for the Hospital Travel Costs Scheme is published on the NI Direct website:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/help-health-costs A person is either automatically entitled to a refund if they are in receipt of certain benefits or they have to apply through the Low Income Scheme.  Guidance for refunding travel expenses refers to ‘reasonable travel expenses’ and advises that travel costs should be calculated on the basis of the cheapest form of public transport available to the patient, but it does not specifically mention community transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total funding allocation for the Hospital Travel Cost Scheme, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) type of vehicle used; (iii) average journey mileage; and (iv) number of journeys, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not provide a specific funding allocation for the Hospital Travel Cost Scheme and as such does not maintain any specific data relating to the scheme.Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts, usually through individual hospitals, administer the Hospital Travel Cost Scheme on behalf of the Department of Health. This scheme is funded through their overall budget allocation. HSC Trusts are responsible for informing patients of their entitlement, checking the appropriate travel costs and refunding travel costs to which patients are entitled.My Department has made enquires with the HSC Trusts and has been informed that they do not centrally compile data on overall spend on this scheme nor do hospitals routinely retain data relating to the type of vehicle used, the average journey mileage or the number of journeys made. However, if you would like to pursue this matter further you will need to contact the HSC Trusts directly.

answer_text: Five of the Health and Social Care Trusts have advised that they do not hold this level of detail (i. individuals, ii. journeys and iii. mileage) within their central systems and it would require an extensive manual exercise to extract such information at a disproportionate cost.The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NAIS) have advised they do not hold this level of detail (i. individuals and iii. mileage) within their central systems and it would require an extensive manual exercise to extract such information at a disproportionate cost. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people that have applied to the Healthcare Travel Cost Scheme in each of the last five years, broken down by health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how many of those applicants were approved as eligible for the scheme.
answer_text: My department administers the Health Service Low Income Scheme (LIS) on behalf of the Department of Health (DoH).  The role of the Department for Communities (DfC) is to assess claims, issue certificates setting out entitlement, and dealing with all related matters.  People apply to the scheme rather than an element of the scheme such as travel costs or dental charges. The tables included below provide details of the claims received and processed by DfC business area. It is not possible to provide a further breakdown by Health and Social Care Trust, or the type of support the claimant was seeking help with. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the removal of funding from Eating Disorders Association NI, what action he is taking to ensure those who need one-to-one support for an eating disorder can avail of the appropriate support.
answer_text: There are currently four adult and four children’s Eating Disorder Specialist Teams across the five Health and Social Care Trusts.  The Belfast Trust and South Eastern Trust is represented by one team.  For individuals requiring one to one support, these teams have developed community based psychological therapy services using evidence-based treatments as per National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidance.  They work to the Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders (2016). The care pathway outlines what people can expect if they are referred for treatment and provides summary of clinical advice derived from evidence-based practice. It also includes information to assist people with self-care and accessing other supports before, during and after treatment.The Regional Eating Disorder Network Group (REDNG) chaired by officials from my Department ensures local developments are coherent and standardised as far as possible. Representation on the REDNG is lifespan. It includes individuals from Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and policy, Public Health Agency, Health and Social Care Trusts including, Eating Disorder Service Clinical Leads, Team Leads and Clinical Service Managers, Psychology nominee (nominated on behalf of psychology services in all Trusts), experts by experience encompassing the voluntary sector, service users and carers, Trust service managers.The role of the REDNG is to promote the uniformity of approaches to patients across Trusts and share best practice and learning from service developments.  It provides a consistent and equitable approach across NI to the planning and commissioning of high quality, effective and accessible treatment for service users. Standards are based upon those laid out by the Royal College of Psychiatrists and NICE (National Institute for Clinical Excellence).  REDNG currently fulfil that function, aiming to: •	improve outcomes for those who require Eating Disorder Services•	promote collaborative working between the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, Trusts, Department of Health Policy, Statutory Services and the Community & Voluntary Sector•	involve service users and carers in service developments•	ensure workforce development and sustainability•	address any teaching and training needs•	establish/promote gold standard clinical practice  Currently a clinical lead and a 0.5wte Network Manager are being recruited for the REDNG in order to improve eating disorder identification and treatment. Action 29 of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 commits to ensure there are specialist interventions available to those who need it. 29d commits to enhance the regional eating disorder service. A business case is in development in relation to Action 29 d The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 notes provision of services for people with an eating disorder, recognising the benefits of REDNG, whilst acknowledging outcomes for these patients could be impro

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether housing associations are required to meet the standards outlined in the Housing Association Guide to receive funding from his Department; and (ii) what process the Department follows to ensure that the standards specified in the Housing Association Guide in relation to the completion of electrical safety inspections have been met before allocating funding.
answer_text: The Department's Housing Association Guide sets out the design standards required by the Department for all new build social housing schemes that receive Housing Association Grant (HAG). There are no standards specified in the Housing Association Guide in relation to the completion of electrical safety inspections in new build social housing properties. This would be a matter for individual Housing Associations (HA). At practical completion stage, Housing Associations will submit to Housing Executive a claim for payment which is conditioned on self-certifications by the HA to include that the dwellings produced comply with my Department’s design and procurement requirements. The Housing Executive do not seek or check electrical testing certificates before payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant AQW 11728/22-27, to detail the terms and conditions of the review.
answer_text: The terms of reference for the review of the Departmental guidance “Integration works” are as follows:Terms of Reference for the Review of “Integration Works” guidanceIntroductionSchools transform to integrated status through the statutory Development Proposal process, which is laid out in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.  A proposal is published, consulted on and a decision taken by the Education Minister or, in the absence of a Minister, by the Permanent Secretary of the Department of Education.The Department’s current guidance to support schools considering transforming to either controlled integrated or grant maintained integrated status “Integration Works” was published in 2017.The Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 gained Royal Assent on 26 April 2022 following its passage through the Northern Ireland Assembly. The Act came into operation on 26 October 2022. The Act amends the previous duty on the Department under Article 64 of the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 to encourage and facilitate the development of integrated education.  Now the duty includes “support” which is defined in Section 5 of the Act. The Act also amends the definition of integrated education to mean “the education together, in an integrated school, ofthose of different cultures and religious beliefs and of none, including reasonable numbers of both Protestant and Roman Catholic children or young persons;those who are experiencing socio-economic deprivation and those who are not; and those of different abilities.”The Act further defines an integrated school as one which: “a) intentionally supports, protects and advances an ethos of diversity, respect and understanding between those of different cultures and religious beliefs and of none, between those of different socio-economic backgrounds and between those of different abilities, and(b)has acquired—(i)grant-maintained integrated status, or(ii)controlled integrated statusunder the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989.”Objective of the ReviewThe objective of the review of the “Integration Works” guidance is to ensure it:reflects the new definitions, requirements and statutory duties of the Integrated Education Act; contains up to date and relevant information on the transformation process in line with the statutory framework set out in the Education Reform (Northern Ireland) Order 1989, as amended by the Integrated Education Act;reflects experience gained since the last review; is clear and easily understandable by parents, schools, Boards of Governors and relevant Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) providing support and information to schools seeking to transform, including highlighting sources of funding and advice; aligns with key Departmental policies, in particular the implementation of the Sustainable Schools Policy (SSP) through Area Planning; andis clear about the information that will be considered as part of the Area Planning process once schools have reached th

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline which school capital projects will be taken forward in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: (10) AQO 519/22-27 Question:To ask the Minister of Education to outline which school capital projects will be taken forward in the 2024-25 financial year.Kellie Armstrong, MLA - StrangfordAnswer: I was very pleased to announce on 28th May 2024, that 15 important new build and extension projects, for schools from all sectors, can now proceed to construction procurement. This is in addition to those capital build projects that are already onsite and under construction. This announcement will see 15 schools benefiting from new state of the art facilities for young people from Belfast to Ballymena to Enniskillen and beyond. It will also provide a welcome boost to the construction industry over the next number of years.This announcement is in addition to the over £50 million which has been earmarked to support Special Educational Needs and the planned commencement of construction of the flagship Strule Shared Education Campus in Omagh, which will provide five new post-primary schools.I also intend to launch a new programme of curriculum led investment to support and enhance the delivery of the curriculum in our schools.  Potential schemes in this area include specialist curriculum accommodation at post-primary; outdoor play and PE in the primary phase and curriculum resources and equipment. It is my priority to ensure the educational experiences of as many children and young people as possible across Northern Ireland are impacted positively by capital investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the actions he is taking along with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure all adults have access to an NHS dentist.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients in General Dental Services were the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers had meant that it had not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  In March 2024, a 6% pay uplift for General Dental Services for 2023/24, in line with recommendations contained in the 51st Doctors and Dentists Review Board report, was announced. This uplift comes with a 7.3% uplift to non-staff expenses for 2023/24, with a 3% recurrent award in future years.In March 2024 the Department also announced a £9.2m investment package for General Dental Services to include:£1m for newly registered child patients through the reestablishment of the Enhanced Child Examination Scheme. This scheme provides children aged 0-10 who have not been registered with a dentist with an examination, individualised oral health advice and age-specific fluoride application to teeth to assist with preventing dental decay;£4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for health service fillings, extractions and root canal treatment for 2024/25, to support public access to priority treatments; and£3.9m for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients through a Dental Access Scheme.Until the Dental Access Scheme is implemented, a pilot scheme will continue to run to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group office for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how much opticians are permitted to charge for the Northern Ireland Primary Eyecare Assessment and Referral Service; and (ii) whether he is monitoring the charges.
answer_text: NI PEARS is an HSC funded clinical assessment service provided to eligible patients which remunerates optometry contractors for the clinical assessment and management of a patient presenting with a new and acute eye problem such as red eye, sudden visual loss, etc. For eligible patients there is no charge to the patient for the NI PEARS clinical assessment. Eligible patients may receive one NI PEARS assessment and, where clinically necessary/indicated, one follow-up NI PEARS assessment for a new ophthalmic clinical condition once per year. Some patients accessing NI PEARS will be advised, in line with clinical management guidelines, to use ophthalmic medications and treatments to manage their eye condition. This recommended treatment(s) may incur a cost to the patient. Where a treatment is available to purchase as an ‘over the counter’ item this is recommended in line with current HSC guidelines.NI PEARS service activity levels and associated costs are monitored on a monthly basis by the Directorate of Primary Care, SPPG.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, further to the meeting with her precedessor and the Minister of Education, whether she intends to reinstate the Careers Advisory Forum.
answer_text: Oral Question No 4 – AQO 498/22-27At my meeting with the Education Minister, we discussed the need to enhance the careers ecosystem. Collaboration and co-design with users and stakeholders will be essential to the success of this work. This is why a Careers Portal stakeholder event is taking place on 5th June with attendees coming from across the system. More broadly, as part of our work to enhance the careers services, officials in both Departments are currently considering the potential role of the Careers Advisory Forum to embed system level engagement as part of a joint programme of work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his Department's process for assessing the income of persons in receipt of Carer's Allowance; (ii) how often this process is carried out; and (iii) the timeframe for notifying a carer of an overpayment.
answer_text: A person’s income is assessed using information provided on their application form or reported in a change of circumstances, which is then verified using His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs information. Where earnings are intermittent, they can be averaged.  Allowable deductions are considered in all cases and include National Insurance Contributions, Income Tax, half of any personal or occupational pension contributions, and other expenses related to a person’s work.		Earnings are checked for each new claim and any time a change to a customer’s circumstances is reported. Where a customer has an irregular pattern of earnings, for example seasonal workers, monthly earnings checks are put in place to ensure that earnings do not exceed £151 per week after deductions. The income of self-employed people is subject to an annual review in line with the financial year for their business.		In 2023/24, overpayment notifications were issued to Carers Allowance customers within one week of an overpayment being identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he or his officials met Department for Work and Pensions officials to discuss a review of Carer's Allowance.
answer_text: My Department regularly engages with the Department for Work and Pensions on a range of social security policy and legislation matters, including those relating to Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to provide a list of all dental surgeries; and (ii) to detail which of these dental surgeries no longer accept NHS patients.
answer_text: The HSCNI Service Finder currently lists 378 dental practices in Northern Ireland. The Service Finder can be accessed at: https://online.hscni.net/hsc-service-finder/The number of dental practices listed in the HSCNI Service Finder does not include a small cohort of practices which have no Health Service commitment whatsoever.Unfortunately, the Department of Health is unable to hold and maintain a list of dental practices which are no longer accepting new Health Service patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the criteria used by Health and Social Care Trusts to determine whether patients are eligible to receive a funded public taxi for travel to or from hospital.
answer_text: Each Health and Social Care Trust has its own policies and procedures which adhere to eligibility criteria set out within the Healthcare Travel Cost Scheme (HTCS).  There are 3 overarching criteria used to assess eligibility for help with travel costs under the HTCS:-In receipt of one of the qualifying benefits or allowances or meet the eligibility criteria for the NHS Low Income Scheme.In receipt of a referral for specialist care from a healthcare professional to a specialist or hospital for further NHS treatment or tests.Appointment must be on a separate visit to when the referral was made.The HTCS provides for re-imbursement of full travel costs only when using the cheapest form of public transport available, including any concessions or promotions.  This means that patients should endeavour to use the cheapest, most appropriate means of transport to attend an appointment. If it is not possible to use public transport or a private car and dependent on the patients’ individual circumstances, a reasonable taxi fare may be refunded on production of a valid receipt from the taxi firm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is plans to review the grant fund for churches and listed buildings.
answer_text: Yes. My department has commissioned research on the condition of listed buildings, including churches, and separate research on the issues faced by owners of listed buildings and scheduled monuments. My Department is due to receive this research by September 24 and it will help to inform decisions on future grant funding for listed churches and buildings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trusts have a (i) policy; and (ii) statutory responsibility, to provide transportation to patients who require, or would benefit from, access to day opportunities.
answer_text: There is a statutory responsibility to provide transport for service users attending buildings-based day care.In 2014, the Department of Health finalised a model for Day Opportunities, aligned to the aspirations of Equal Lives and in collaboration with NI Executive Departments. It aimed to promote a culture of social inclusion, greater independence, and a good quality of life.These services should be discrete from traditional buildings-based Day Care facilities and access to the Day Opportunities services should be in non-segregated general transport provision. Day Opportunities should aim to empower individuals to access and participate in activities independently.It is recognised that access to public transport can be difficult on occasion. Trusts do explore alternative transport methods on a case by case basis to enable access to Day Opportunities. The Department is aware that people supported use Community Transport to access Day Opportunities. Officials have engaged in a cross-departmental working group with DfI and DAERA to explore opportunities for better collaboration.
