question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to upskill the voluntary and community sector to ensure that they can access Financial Transactions Capital funding.
answer_text: Using Financial Transactions Capital, my department is making £13m available over three years to Community Finance Ireland, for social loans to organisations in the Voluntary, Community & Social Enterprise (VCSE) sector.My department will continue to invest in and support the voluntary and community sector through all available funding streams, including Financial Transactions Capital, where that mechanism is appropriate.My department is happy to engage with the Sector where new proposals for use of Financial Transactions Capital funds are submitted. We will work collaboratively to assess, and where appropriate, develop proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8961/22-27, whether the Minister will commit to increasing the number of shared houses to 300 per year to meet the growing demand for this type of housing development.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Department of Communities (DfC) and Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) are committed to supporting the delivery of a minimum 200 shared social housing units per year as part of the annual Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) target. The Programme for Government Shared Housing Programme is named ‘Housing for All’.The ‘Housing for All’ Shared Housing Programme is currently supporting 2,476 shared social housing units across 76 developments”. This is detailed in table 1 below. The benefits of shared housing, however, extend far beyond those 2476 units as the Housing Associations work with 32 Local Advisory Groups across the shared housing communities. The Advisory Groups support the development and delivery of Good Relations Plans which benefit many communities and individuals, of any tenure, who live within a five-mile radius of the new development. The learning and networks developed through the “Housing for All” Shared Housing programme can therefore be applied widely.Table 1 - ‘Housing for All’ Shared Housing Programme Schemes and Units by Year including the initial 10 shared housing developments through the T:buc strategy.YearNumber of Schemes Actual Units2023/248539 (283 Units receive GRS funding as cap of 50 per scheme)2022/23112542021/22122232020/21206162019/202972018/1951052017/188159T:BUC 1010483It is expected that this level of shared housing provision will continue, subject to funding availability”. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the action his Department is taking to ensure that Housing Associations are aware of Financial Transactions Capital funding.
answer_text: My Department has used Financial Transactions Capital (FTC) as low cost, long term government loan to help increase the supply of affordable housing products for some time now. It has been working very effectively and is primarily used to support the delivery of shared ownership housing, provided by our delivery partner, Co-Ownership.We have made a large commitment to Co-Ownership, allocating £158 million in FTC funding to assist over 4,000 people into home ownership, between 2020 and 2025, through the Co-Own product, as well as providing £12.5m in funding for Rent to Own and £8m for Co-Own for over 55s.Beyond Co-Ownership, my Department has also previously allocated over £28m in FTC funding to Apex, Choice and Clanmil Housing Associations for the Affordable Housing Loan Fund (AHLF) and Empty Homes Loan Fund (EHLF). My officials are also currently working with the Registered Housing Association sector to consider development of a New Build Shared Ownership product which could help increase the supply of affordable homes. We are currently undertaking research to test the viability of such a product and would seek to access FTC funding to support delivery if such a scheme were to be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9489/22-27, for an update on the actions his Department is taking to progress the 26 recommendations contained in the Recommendations to advance Community wealth building in Northern Ireland, published in October 2022.
answer_text: I have not yet considered the Independent Advisory Panel Report, which I understand makes wide ranging and cross cutting recommendations.  I am meeting with my officials in due course to discuss potential next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will provide a revised answer to (i) AQW 5499/22-2; (ii) AQW 5501/22-27; and (iii) AQW 5782/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 5499/22-27 was answered on 21st May 2024. AQW 5501/22-27 was answered on 23rd May 2024. AQW 5782/22-27 was answered on 11th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department has taken to work with (i) the Health and Social Care Trusts; (ii) charities; and (iii) other stakeholders, to increase awareness of ovarian cancer.
answer_text: Cancer prevention is one of the strategic themes within the Cancer Strategy and improved awareness raising of the potential signs and symptoms of cancer is essential in ensuring this.   Work is ongoing through the Cancer Programme Board and Cancer Steering Group to progress these important activities.  This includes wider collaboration and co-design and co-delivery with a wide range of stakeholders from across the Health and Social Care System, charitable, community and voluntary sectors on the implementation of the related cancer strategy priorities.The Public Health Agency also provided a press release in March 2024, to raise awareness of the signs and symptoms of Ovarian Cancer during Ovarian Cancer month.Officials from my Department continue to engage with key stakeholders across the sector and most recently met with representatives from Target Ovarian Cancer, the UK's leading ovarian cancer charity, on 24th April 2024.   The continued working with this and other key stakeholders is an important part of how we can increase awareness of the signs and symptoms of cancer and to allow for earlier diagnosis, treatment and improved outcomes for those with a cancer diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the length of time a school transforming to become an integrated school is permitted to take to achieve the appropriate breakdown of children from protestant, catholic, other and no faith backgrounds; and (ii) where this guidance is published.
answer_text: The Department provides guidance on transformation (Integration Works) for schools going through the process. This covers: Planning for TransformationThe Statutory Transformation ProcessAfter Approval – Transformation in PracticeandSources of Advice and FundingThe guidance is under review (as per action 10 of the Integrated Education (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 strategy and action plan). The review will consider the length of time a school transforming to become an integrated school should take to achieve the appropriate breakdown of children from Protestant, Roman Catholic, other and no faith backgrounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how parental demand data will be collated as required by the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The ARK research report, issued by the Integrated Education Fund (IEF), titled "Assessing Demand for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland", noted that demand for integrated education can be categorised in two parts; (i) societal level inclination towards integrated education, which reflects the general openness of parents and guardians when considering integrated education for their children, and (ii) parental preference made for school admission, which reflects the specific pragmatic choices that parents and guardians make when applying to schools.The conclusion of the report states “As outlined in the recent Strategy for Integrated Education in Northern Ireland (Department of Education, 2023b), no single data source or measure will provide the type or range of information needed to measure demand, resulting in the need for a suite of measures.”To measure this (and track it over time) DE will:Include societal level questions on demand and the factors parents consider when makings school admission decisions in the annual Northern Ireland Life and Times (NILT) survey.Assess and monitor parental demand for Integrated Education as expressed through school admission preferences through the Education Authority school admissions portal.Use the EA admissions portal to obtain permission from parents to be surveyed in respect of school admissions preferences and associated factors and,  Annually carry out a Northern Ireland wide survey of such parents to assess and monitor demand for Integrated Education.Carry out ad-hoc local surveys of parents to further assess and monitor demand as required.Consider the outcomes of parental ballots for transformation carried out by Electoral Reform Services.Consider any other relevant survey data available.Report on this within the statutory report on Integrated Education as required by the Act.Review and amend the Integrated Education Strategy following the publication of the Section 10 Report on Integrated Education and all subsequent iterations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the 2021 consultation on the Local Government Staff Commission.
answer_text: On 15 November 2021 the former Minister for Communities sought the views of stakeholders on whether there were any reasons that the Executive decision in 2014 to dissolve the Local Government Staff Commission should be reconsidered. However, due to the collapse of the Executive, a decision on whether the Local Government Staff Commission should be dissolved was not considered by the former Minister.Any decision to dissolve the Local Government Staff Commission would need to be considered by the Executive due to its cross-cutting nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the uniform grant will be reviewed to take account of the adaptations and changes required to school uniforms for children with disabilities.
answer_text: The current guidance on school uniform policy states that schools should consult widely on proposed uniform policies including with groups representing pupils with special educational needs or disabilities. I plan to launch a public consultation exercise on the review of school uniform policy in June with a view to setting the current non-statutory guidance on a statutory basis.  The consultation will set out proposals to strengthen the guidance, including ensuring schools take account of particular adaptations or adjustments that may be required for certain groups of pupils as well as consideration of the bands used to determine the level of the uniform grant available to different pupil cohorts.   The Education Authority is responsible for the determination of the rates of uniform grant. Following the completion of review of school uniform policy, it will be for the EA to take account of the available funding in determining the rates of uniform grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) the build progress; and (ii) an expected date of completion for each integrated school being supported through Fresh Start capital monies.
answer_text: The following table summarises the status and actual or expected completion dates for projects funded through Fresh Start.  Please note this includes shared education campuses as well as integrated schools.SchoolStatusCompletionDrumlins IPSCompletedSeptember 2017Braidside IPSCompletedJuly 2020Enniskillen IPSCompletedJanuary 2023Limavady SECCompletedAugust 2023Roe Valley IPSCompletedDecember 2023Mill Strand IPSIn constructionSummer 2024Phoenix IPSIn constructionSummer 2024Parkhall ICIn constructionAutumn 2024Ulidia ICIn constructionEnd 2024Shimna ICIn constructionSummer 2025Rowandale IPSIn constructionAutumn 2025New Bridge ICIn constructionEnd 2025Strangford ICIn constructionAutumn 2026Ballycastle SECIn constructionSummer 2027

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the number of children who were unable to secure a place in an integrated primary school in September 2023 will be published.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on the allocation of school places. The allocation of school places is an operational function for the Education Authority. As part of the accountability framework underlying delivery of the Integrated Education Strategy and Action Plan, the Department will however be publishing the percentage of children who obtain their 1st preference Integrated school compared to other sectors by phase. We consider this a more appropriate measure, as the numbers of children in each cohort will change with population trends.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the actions his Department has taken to deliver on each statutory provision of the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: As required by legislation, the Department published the Section 10 Report on Integrated Education on 26 April 2024.  The report provides details on the work my Department is undertaking to implement the Act. It evaluates the performance of the Department and other education bodies in respect of integrated education, as well as the levels of demand for and supply of integrated education. The report also identifies successes and challenges in respect of the provision of integrated education and assesses the extent to which the purpose of integrated education has been achieved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of the minutes from the Partnership Panel for Northern Ireland for each year in the last five years.
answer_text: The Minutes for the Partnership Panel for Northern Ireland are published online on the Department for Communities website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/minutes-partnership-panel-northern-ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the functions of the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers’ Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC); and (ii) whether he has any plans to review the operation of NILGOSC.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Local Government Officers' Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC) is a statutory body established by the Local Government (Superannuation) Act (Northern Ireland) 1950 to:  administer a pension scheme for local authorities and admitted bodies; and to manage and maintain a fund out of which the benefits of the scheme are met. An independent reviewer has been tasked with conducting a review of NILGOSC as part of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to review Arms’ Length Bodies in Northern Ireland. This review is due to be completed in July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of the minutes of meetings held between the Minister, or departmental officials, and the Chief Local Government Auditor for each year in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department holds minutes of meetings with the Local Government Auditor that took place on 24 January 2020 and 30 August 2023 respectively. Copies of these minutes are attached.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) , as part of his oversight role of local councils, whether any internal reports, updates or communications, including emails, have been produced in relation to Local Development Plans; and (ii) to provide a copy of any such documents issued in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In relation to Local Development Plans (LDPs), my Department has main oversight responsibilities to agree Council’s timetables, to consider plans at submission stage ‘to cause’ an Independent Examination (IE) and also following receipt of Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) IE Reports to direct Councils to adopt to their plan documents.  The above request to release ongoing policy development and engagement with local councils in the past five years in respect of Local Development Plans, has produced a significant volume of reports, updates, communications, including emails etc and to provide copies of all these communications would be at a disproportionate cost. For those plans which are currently under active consideration, the internal consideration process also includes material used as part of the decision-making process.  This is subject to my final determination as Minister and therefore my Department cannot disclose information to this extent.However, my Department when issuing Councils with a direction to adopt their plan documents, does provide Explanatory Notes to accompany the directions (where necessary) which explains the Departments reasoning and rationale, if modifications are at variance with the PAC reports. All of this information is available on each of the councils’ websites, once the LDP has been adopted.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the oversight role he and his Department has for local councils.
answer_text: District councils are democratically elected independent bodies and as such, neither my Department nor I have an oversight role. However, my Department does have a number of powers of intervention, some of which are also available to other Departments.These powers of intervention are summarised in the following table:	The Local Government Act (NI) 2014		Section 98	Section 98 of the 2014 Act permits the Department to direct the Local Government Auditor (after consultation with the Auditor) to conduct a special inspection of a council where it believes that a relevant council may fail to comply with the Performance Improvement provisions set out in Part 12 of the 2014 Act.		Section 100	Where the Department is satisfied that the council is failing or likely to fail with the requirements of Performance Improvement, the Department may direct a council to-	prepare or amend an improvement plan or to follow specified procedures in relation to such a plan; 	carry out a review of its exercise of specified functions;	enter into specified arrangements with another council;	set specified improvement objectives for itself under section 85.		Any Department may direct a council to take any action it considers necessary to secure the council’s compliance with the requirements of Performance Improvement.		Where this section applies, any Department may direct-	that a specified function of a council must be exercised by that Department; and	the council must comply with any instructions of that Department in relation to the exercise of that function and must provide assistance as required by that Department.		Section 104	Any Department may direct a council to make a report or return or supply specific information with respect to the exercise of the council’s functions.		Section 105		Any Department may cause such local and other inquiries to be held along with such investigations it sees fit in connection with the administration of any statutory provision relating to the functions of a council/committee/sub-committee.			Section 106		Any Department may, after an inquiry or investigation has been completed and the Department is satisfied that the council has failed to discharge any if its functions, make an order that the council is in default and direct a council to take action, within a specified period.		If the direction is not complied with the Department may under an order empower an officer of the Department to exercise or procure the exercise of, the function in question.		The Department must pay the expenses of an officer, but these must be re-paid by the council.			Section 107	If any Northern Ireland department considers that any action proposed to be taken by a council would be incompatible with any international obligations, that department may direct that the proposed action must not be taken.		If any Northern Ireland department considers that any action capable of being taken by a council is required for the purpose of giving effect to any international obl

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on discussions in relation to when any independent review panels will be recruited.
answer_text: The Independent Financial Review Panel (IFRP) was not reconstituted when the term of the outgoing panel members expired on 1 July 2016. In the last Assembly mandate, the Assembly Commission introduced the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill on 14 December 2020 which, amongst other things, sought to rename the IFRP as the Renumeration Board and remove its power and responsibility for determining the allowances payable to Members. This followed the conferral of power on the Assembly Commission to make determinations on Members' allowances, by the Assembly on 30 June 2020.While the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill was introduced it was not progressed to second stage, as the Assembly Commission was mindful of prioritising legalisation associated with or arising from COVID-19. Following the resumption of normal Assembly business and the reconstitution of the Assembly Commission in February 2024, the Assembly Commission reconsidered the draft Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill at its first meeting on 19 March 2024. At that meeting, the Assembly Commission asked officials to prepare a further paper and a revised Bill for consideration at its next meeting, which is scheduled for 16 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when the minutes of the most recent Assembly Commission meeting will be published.
answer_text: Minutes of Assembly Commission meetings are tabled and agreed at the following meeting.  Once agreed they are then published on the Assembly website.  The Assembly Commission is next scheduled to meet on 16 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail (i) when MLAs and their staff will receive a pension statement; and (ii) whether a pension statement will be issued to MLAs annually.
answer_text: The issue of pension statements for Members is a matter for the Assembly Members Pension Scheme Trustees.  However, the Assembly Commission is aware that this matter was discussed by the Trustees at their meeting on 1 May 2024.  Work to produce statements up to and including 31 March 2024 is underway, and they should be ready for distribution in 10-12 weeks.The pension statements for MLA staff is a matter for each of the appropriate pension schemes and not a matter for the Assembly Commission.  Staff should contact their pension providers if they have queries regarding their schemes or associated benefits.Once these current statements are issued, annual statements will once again be issued to MLAs going forward.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the work that has been carried out through the North South Ministerial Council to ensure that international adoption that is recognised in Northern Ireland is also recognised in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: The matter of international adoption has not been discussed by the North South Ministerial Council. We do not hold any further information.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) to detail the status of MLAs in relation to health and safety and accessibility policies that cover all users of Parliament Buildings; and (ii) whether there are any plans to review this status.
answer_text: (i)	The Assembly Commission’s Health and Safety at Work Policy applies to all users of Parliament Buildings including Members.  The Health and Safety at Work Policy is available for Members via the Members Portal. The policy is reviewed annually or more frequently if necessary. The Assembly Commission also has an Inclusive Customer Service Policy which covers all building users and promotes accessibility services within the Northern Ireland Assembly. The Inclusive Customer Service Policy is available on the Northern Ireland Assembly website.(ii)	There are no plans to review the current arrangements under these policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will work with rural communities to review the current need for rural transport.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with councils and stakeholders in local communities to produce a new suite of local Transport Plans which will set out the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035. These transport plans are centred on local transport solutions and will seek to ensure that people are able to access employment, healthcare and other services.My Department is also undertaking a phased review of the community transport schemes which it funds, including the Dial-A-Lift Scheme provided by the Rural Community Transport Partnerships. In order to inform the review’s first phase, my officials worked with rural service providers, transport users and some key stakeholders to obtain their views on a number of issues, including current and future demand for their services. I anticipate there will be further engagement with rural communities and rural transport providers in the next phase of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the resources his Department is putting in place to support rural transport.
answer_text: My Department awarded grant funding of £2.236M in 2023/24 to support the delivery of rural Dial-a-Lift Scheme services which are delivered across the North by rural community transport providers.  Funding of £55K was also awarded to the Community Transport Association to provide advice and services across both the rural and urban community transport sector.  Due to the delay with the 2024/25 Budget, I have approved funding for community transport providers for the first quarter of 2024/25 to support the continuance of community transport in rural areas.  Translink do not account for the operation of public transport on the basis of rural and non-rural services.  Ulsterbus provides the majority of the rural network, as well as inter-urban services and rural and smaller town services.  On that basis I can confirm that Ulsterbus received £46.779M of bus route subsidy during 2023/24. Allocations for 2024/25 are in the process of being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what meetings he has had with Translink in relation to rural bus routes.
answer_text: I have recently hosted the annual Ministerial and bi-annual accountability meeting with the Northern Ireland Transport Holding Company Board.  This includes updates on many aspects of performance and details of upcoming plans across the public transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether checks of electrical installations are carried out in dwellings in the social rented sector; (ii) what legislation or regulation requires the checking of electrical installations; (iii) whether these are provided by both the Housing Executive and registered housing associations; (iv) how often these checks are conducted; and (v) whether an Electrical Installation Condition Report is completed as part of these checks.
answer_text: In respect of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, it has responsibility for electrical wiring in relation to its tenanted dwellings under Schedule 4 of the General Conditions of Tenancy 1989 Edition, which sets out the rights and duties which must be observed by the NIHE and the tenant in all secure tenancies. Since 1992 the NIHE has carried out fixed electrical inspection testing to all its domestic properties on a 10-year cycle and at every change of tenancy. Whilst there is no specific, direct legislative requirement to undertake electrical safety inspections on domestic properties, the NIHE in 2017, reviewed its processes for delivering fixed electrical testing and, in line with established “best practice” as recommended in IET Regulations Guidance Note 3, decided to migrate from the 10-Year cycle of inspection to a new reduced 5-Year cycle which it is currently working towards. On completion of each electrical inspection an Electrical Installation Condition Report is completed and is uploaded onto the NIHEs electronic database for the relevant NIHE dwelling.In respect of registered housing associations, checks of electrical installations are undertaken as per the guidance set out in the IET Wiring Regulations in that all fixed electrical installations within properties should be inspected and tested on a 5-year cycle in line with best practice.  Currently there is no specific/dedicated legislation in Northern Ireland compelling anyone or any organisation to undertake electrical checks on the social rented sector. However, most Associations should have a policy in electrical safety to protect tenants. Best practice recommends a frequency of every 5 years for domestic rented properties (as indicated above) or otherwise directed in the EICR. The EICR is part of the inspection process.  In the Housing Association Guide, the frequency of electrical inspections is stated as 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the interventions the Minister is putting in place to support people with disabilities, in particular those furthest from the labour market.
answer_text: My Department offers an extensive range of supports to those with disabilities, including across areas such as welfare, employment, housing, culture, arts and leisure, and through funding partnerships with Local Councils and the voluntary and community sector. I am committed to delivering for disabled people throughout Northern Ireland and, as one of my key priorities, I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the development of an Executive Disability Strategy. Final decisions on the content and implementation of the Disability Strategy will be subject to Executive agreement.In terms of employment, my Department has undertaken extensive work to co-develop a draft Disability and Work Strategy for Northern Ireland. This will include interventions to support people with disabilities and health conditions to move closer, into, sustain and progress in employment. There are also a number of existing provisions delivering interventions to support people with disabilities and health conditions, particularly those furthest from the labour market, including JobStart Phase 2, Labour Market Partnerships, Condition Management Programme, Workable, Access to Work, Work Experience and Adviser Discretion Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has conducted an (i) external evaluation; and (ii) a value for money exercise on the JobStart Scheme.
answer_text: Both phases of the JobStart Scheme were subject to a rigorous evaluation and value for money exercise, including the development and subsequent approval of separate economic appraisals by the Department of Finance. A Post Evaluation Review of JobStart Phase 1 was conducted in July 2023, it concluded that the scheme fully achieved its objectives and performance against planned costs. The JobStart Phase 2 evaluation review is planned for completion at scheme close (end of March 2025).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has considered alternative schemes to JobStart.
answer_text: The JobStart scheme is only one of my Department’s employment programmes. It is specifically aimed at young people aged 16 to 24 who are at risk of falling into long-term unemployment, providing job opportunities offering 25 hours of paid employment per week at the appropriate National Minimum Wage.There are a number of alternative employment programmes and schemes available to help and support young people, long term unemployed and people with a disability and/or health condition into work and to stay in work including, Local Labour Market partnerships, Condition Management Programme, Workable, Access to Work, Work Experience and Adviser Discretion Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider launching a similar scheme to the Universal Support scheme that has recently launched in Great Britain.
answer_text: My Department has a range of employability provisions to support people to move closer, into, sustain and progress in work. This operates in conjunction with other supports delivered by Central and Local Government, and the Voluntary and Community Sector.   My officials are working with colleagues in the Department for Work and Pensions to gain a greater understanding of Universal Support and the help it will provide to overcome barriers to entering or staying in employment.  I will consider the outcome of these discussions and the resources required to implement Universal Support when my Department’s budget position has been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the tragedy that occurred in Ballynahinch in April 2024, whether the Ballynahinch bypass is being considered for capital investment in 2024-25.
answer_text: I wish to send my heartfelt condolences to the family and friends of  Mr Giovanni Buggea who lost his life in a road traffic collision in the Square, Ballynahinch on Monday 8 April 2024. The PSNI are investigating this tragic incident and anyone who can assist the police with their enquiries should do so.Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety. The A24 Ballynahinch Bypass will deliver a 3.1km bypass to the east which will improve traffic conditions in the town centre.The publication of the prioritised list of major road schemes, that will continue to be progressed, explains that development work on the Ballynahinch Bypass is currently paused. A decision to proceed to procurement will be considered following confirmation of the Department’s Transport Plans and the availability of funding.Details of the current status of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A24 Ballynahinch Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what preparatory work has been completed on the Ballynahinch bypass, to ensure the project is shovel-ready when capital investment is available.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety. The A24 Ballynahinch Bypass scheme has progressed through the statutory processes with the Direction Order being made in July 2021, however, the Vesting Order for the scheme cannot be made until funding is secured.  The scheme has been developed to a stage that if a decision was made to progress the project it would take in the region of 24 months to commence construction; 12 months to refresh environmental information, prepare contract documents and complete the economic appraisal (Business Case) and 12 months for the procurement process.Details of the status of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A24 Ballynahinch Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Ballynahinch bypass is on the priority list of capital projects to be taken forward by his Department.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.The publication of the prioritised list of major road schemes, that will continue to be progressed, explains that development work on the Ballynahinch Bypass is currently paused. A decision to proceed to procurement will be considered following confirmation of the Department’s Transport Plans and the availability of funding.Details of the current status of the A24 Ballynahinch Bypass project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A24 Ballynahinch Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number; and (ii) the percentage of the total of individuals living in (a) owner-occupier; (b) private rental; (c) hostel accommodation; (d) Housing Executive accommodation; (e) Housing Association accommodation; (f) informal arrangements with friends or family; (g) on the streets or night shelter; (h) supported living; and (i) any other form of accommodation, prior to committal to prison, in each of the last five calendar years.
answer_text: At committal, an assessment of needs is completed for each prisoner against a number of resettlement pathways, including accommodation.  This information enables Prison Service staff in Prisoner Development Units to put interventions in place, such as a referral to the Housing Rights advocacy service to examine accommodation options for a prisoner on release.Details relating to accommodation are gathered from Prisoner Needs Profile (PNP) questionnaires completed by prisoners.  These details are self-reported and not verified by Prison Service staff.  It is important to note that not all prisoners will provide a response.  Some may refuse to engage with the PNP process; others may refuse to answer the accommodation questions and others are released prior to providing a response.Table 1 below highlights the requested accommodation type breakdown based on prisoners who completed a PNP.  The 2021 data is from 10 February onwards when the current PNP was implemented.  Table 2 highlights responses from the previous version of the PNP.  However, due to differences in the response categories and in methodology, the data from the previous version of the PNP should not be directly compared to the current PNP.Table 1: Accommodation Type prior to custody (Current Revised PNP)Accommodation Type2021120222023Hostel10%8%7%JJC Custody0%0%<1%NIHE/Housing Association38%39%32%On the streets/night shelter2%3%5%Owner Occupied12%12%12%Private Rented24%21%22%Residential Care0%<1%<1%Supported Living1%2%2%Temporary with friend/family12%15%19%Travellers Site0%<1%<1%Total100%100%100%Table 2: Accommodation Type prior to custody (Previous version of PNP)Accommodation Type20192020Homeless7%6%Hostel12%10%JJC Care<1%0%NIHE/Housing Association39%43%Owner Occupied15%17%Private Rented24%21%Residential Care<1%<1%Roofless2%1%Supported Living1%2%Total100%100%Source: PNP (Current Revised and Previous version)Notes:The current version of the PNP has been in use since 10th February 2021.  2021 data in Table 1 does not include any PNPs prior to 10th February.Totals may not sum to 100% due to rounding.“Refuse to answer” and blank responses are excluded from the totals.Responses are for individuals per year.  If an individual was committed more than once in a calendar year, they would have one response included if they selected the same accommodation type each time.  However, if they selected a different accommodation type in a separate PNP then this would also be included separately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how much funding will be provided to the Housing Executive to support Shared Housing in 2024-25; and (ii) how many new start homes this will equate to.
answer_text: It will be for the Executive to decide on the level of funding allocated to each NI Department. However, I will seek to maximise the funding allocated to my Department through my engagement in the 2024-25 Budget Process.My Department has an annual delivery target of 200 shared social units per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times the Portaferry II ferry has provided crossings in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The MV Portaferry II has sailed a total of 49,854 times in the last 3 years. In 2021/22 it sailed 15,714 times, in 2022/23 it sailed 23,865 times, and in 2023/24 it sailed 10,275 times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when building regulations will be reviewed and updated to ensure climate change objectives will be met
answer_text: Building Regulations contribute to climate change objectives in setting minimum standards for building work that is being undertaken or in certain cases of a change of use.  My Department has been working on a phased review of the relevant parts of the Building Regulations as set out in the Energy Strategy published by the Department for the Economy in 2021.Consultation on a Phase 2 discussion document, published by my Department in June 2023, closed on 15 December and a departmental summary and response is planned for publication in Summer 2024. This will inform subsequent timings for changes to the Building Regulations here.  I can assure you that this is a priority for my Department, however, the complexity, scope and detail involved in this work means it is likely that the Phase 3 uplift will be in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 17087/17-22, (i) for an update to the figures provided for (a) 2022-23; and (b) 2023-24; and (ii) how many parents have chosen not to consent to paediatric pathology and post-mortem services in the same period.
answer_text: The number of hospitals consented post mortems are as follows:YearNumber of Post Mortems202219920232022024 (January-March)41  The number of coronial post mortems are as follows:YearNumber of Post Mortems2022262023202024 (January-March)7In line with our pathway, all parents are currently afforded the opportunity to avail of a post mortem who experience a pregnancy loss from 12 weeks gestation. Whilst we can provide the number of post-mortems undertaken, it is only possible to provide the total number of parents who did not avail of this option from 20 weeks gestation as this data is collected to support the Mothers and Babies: Reducing Risk through Audits and Confidential Enquiries across the UK (MBRRACE-UK) perinatal mortality surveillance programme. Maternity information is collected on the Northern Ireland Maternity System (NIMATS) at booking, however pathways for pregnancy loss also include gynaecology services which use NIMATS. As such it is not possible to demonstrate if all parents experiencing loss less than 20 weeks were offered a post mortem.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he will ensure Make the Call is not duplicating the work of the Independent Advice sector.
answer_text: My Department’s Make the Call Service aims to ensure that individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. This Service is unique in that it has direct access to benefit systems enabling efficient and effective potential entitlements to social security benefits to be identified. Make the Call also has a team of Community Outreach officers, who conduct home visits with people who are vulnerable through age, disability, illness, or adverse circumstances to provide face to face assistance and support and who can also assist with benefit claim completion.	Make the Call and the Independent Advice sector both have important complementary roles in helping to ensure individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. My officials are currently developing a fresh policy framework for independent advice and debt services which will review the existing collaborative partnerships between the advice sector and government services including Make the Call to ensure citizens receive the necessary support and advice from the organisation best placed to provide it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for an update on the delivery of the social value procurement policy; and (ii) whether she will update the procurement policy note 01/21 to increase the minimum total award criteria.
answer_text: The Executive Policy on scoring social value in public procurement has been significant in delivering meaningful economic, environmental and social outcomes here. To date these have included:3,500+ people who were long-term unemployed or disadvantaged in the labour market have gained employment.75+ social enterprises have been part of the supply chain in public sector contracts2,700+ hours of volunteering with the VCSE sector6,500+ hours of skills development activities 2,800+ weeks of work experience deliveredThe Strategic Investment Board has recently completed a review of the implementation of the Scoring Social Value Policy and has provided a report which recommends enhancements to help small and micro businesses to deliver social value, and to improve the effectiveness of the policy to better align to a new Programme for Government; Climate Change Commitments; and/or the Investment Strategy NI. The outcome of the review will be bought to the Executive over the coming months to help inform the decision to increase the minimum weighting for social value to 20%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9492/22-27, to detail, during the five week wait for Universal Credit, (i) how many weeks those claimants in receipt of Housing Benefit will not receive this benefit to cover their rent; and (ii) the average rental debt for people living in (a) a Housing Executive property; (b) a housing association property; and (c) private landlord property.
answer_text: how many weeks those claimants in receipt of Housing Benefit will not receive this benefit to cover their rent;When a person makes a claim for Universal Credit, this will result in their Housing Benefit award ending, effective from the previous day. All such claimants will, however, receive an additional two weeks ‘run on’ of the rent element of Housing Benefit as part of the stopping Housing Benefit process (this payment excludes rates). Universal Credit will be paid from the date that Housing Benefit stops but is paid on a different arrears cycle. Although claimants will receive the relevant benefit to cover rent costs throughout the transition from Housing Benefit to Universal Credit the first Universal Credit payment will be made around five weeks after a claim is made (around three weeks after the two weeks ‘run on’ is paid). Customers who are entitled to Universal Credit may be able to claim extra financial support to help while waiting on their payment to help with essential costs.  This includes:Applying for a Universal Credit Contingency Fund grantApplying for an Advance loan Applying for Discretionary Support.the average rental debt for people living in (a) a Housing Executive property.When a Housing Executive tenant in receipt of Housing Benefit makes a claim for Universal Credit (UC) the rent account will only accrue a technical arrear due to the fact UC and Rate Rebate for UC pay in arrears. The Housing Executive does not take action to recover technical debt. If a tenant later moves off UC, any debt left outstanding after their final UC payment is reclassified as a recoverable arrear. The technical debt accrued in a case where a tenant moves to UC from Housing Benefit is usually relatively small, between the equivalent of approximately 1 to 3 weeks rental element (i.e. around £80 to £240). the average rental debt for people living in (b) a Housing Association property.The Department does not hold information on the average rental debt for people living in a housing association property.the average rental debt for people living in (c) a private landlord property.The Department does not hold information on the average rental debt for people living in a private landlord property.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the co-ownership band for limits across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Property Value Limit (PVL) is the maximum market value of a property that can be purchased through Co Ownership. It is reviewed annually by my Department.  The one PVL rate is used for all Northern Ireland, which is currently £195,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how he is ensuring social value procurement policy is being delivered by his Department; and (ii) to detail the successful awards made since 1 June 2022.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) complies with NI procurement policy, and in relation to social value ensures compliance with PPN 01/21 – Scoring Social Values. This PPN requires that all supplies and services, and construction contracts above certain values must include social values initiatives with a 10% weighting applied to bidder responses at tender evaluation stage.  This minimum weighting will apply to the thresholds set out in the Public Contracts Regulations.  That is, service contracts valued above £138,760 and construction contracts valued above £5,336,937.Construction Procurement Delivery (CPD) as the Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) for DfC provides the Department with a range of services designed to ensure procurement processes, tender documentation, evaluations and contract awards comply with NI Public Procurement Policy and the UK Procurement Regulations.Since 1 June 2022 the total number of DfC awarded contracts is 122.  The Department’s Commercial Business Unit awarded 55 of those and CPD the other 67.  Social Value initiatives were included with those meeting the relevant thresholds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions his Department is taking to progress the 26 recommendations contained in the Recommendations to advance Community Wealth Building in Northern Ireland, published October 2022.
answer_text: I have not yet considered the Independent Advisory Panel Report, which I understand makes wide ranging and cross cutting recommendations.  I am expecting an update on this in April.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action his Department is taking to ensure that Housing Solutions is appropriately resourced.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has quantified the Housing Solutions requirement as part of the 2024/25 budget information gathering exercise. The NI Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s settlement against key priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, when corresponding with claimants in relation to their move to Universal Credit, whether they will include information on potential debt they may incur during the five-week wait and a number to call for support.
answer_text: The Move to UC migration notice letter contains information about the support available to help people make the move to Universal Credit, including:information about the Universal Credit Contingency Fund non-repayable grant which can be applied for during the five week wait;the Department’s Freephone number 0800 012 1331 if they need support or if they think their Universal Credit will be impacted by certain types of debt;Available face to face support in their local Jobs and Benefits office; and Signposting to Independent Welfare Advice.Additional information is also available on the nidirect website at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/universal-credit-if-you-receive-migration-notice-letter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions are being taken to assist historic monuments in meeting climate change targets.
answer_text: My Department’s Historic Environment Division (HED) is the policy lead with regard to historic monuments in NI. There are 3 principal strands that guide HED’s approach to climate change: mitigation (achieving net zero); managing risks (understanding threats) and adaptation (preparing for climate change). HED collaborates with a number of partners regarding the impact of climate change on historic monuments and the Department is a member of the UK Heritage Adaptation Partnership (UKHAP).  My Department has part funded research projects working in partnership with the National Trust around evidence-based decision making, adaptation and an ongoing project identifying risks to monuments along our coastline.HED recently completed a carbon audit on its State Care Monuments and is taking forward recommendations to better understand its carbon footprint and help to establish annual carbon reduction targets.HED also utilises the evidence-based assessment of hazards commissioned by the National Trust to help provide a strategic picture of potential climate-related risks to State Care Monuments and embed climate change considerations in State Care Monument condition surveys. My Department is committed to adapting and mitigating the impacts of climate change on Historic Monuments. We will continue to consider how we best conserve our monuments for future generations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when nominations for appointment will be sought for Members to the Boards of the North South Implementation Bodies.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of his Department’s ability to achieve a reduction in emissions by the transport sector by 48% lower than the baseline by 2030.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he will protect and improve the terms and conditions through the commitment to real living wage rates for voluntary and community sector posts; and (ii) whether he has had any discussions with other departments to ensure they do the same.
answer_text: My department’s grant funding provides a contribution towards eligible costs, which may include salaries and running costs incurred in the delivery of a range of agreed policy outcomes. Funding allocations for 2024/25 will be considered against competing priorities and within my overall Departmental budget position once the Executive sets a 2024/25 Budget.  My department is not the employer of staff in any voluntary and community sector organisation it funds.  Terms and conditions and pay policies are a matter for the employer.I am pleased that my officials have been supporting the work of the Joint Forum, with representatives of other Executive departments and local government, to co-develop a set of Funding Principles aimed at improving the funding relationship with the Sector, recognising the fundamental difference that an enabling and consistent funding environment can make in terms of organisations being able to offer improved terms and conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the review of Libraries NI will take place, in accordance with New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: In line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment my Department has commissioned reviews for three of its Arm’s Length Bodies so far - the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People (NICCY), the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland (COPNI) and the Northern Ireland Local Government Officers Superannuation Committee (NILGOSC).  Consideration will be given to further reviews in line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment, but no timeframes have been agreed as yet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the capital projects in the Strangford constituency that will be progressed using capital funding allocations.
answer_text: The Department has not yet received its capital budget allocation for 2024/2025 and beyond. As in prior years, once capital budget allocations are known, projects will have to be prioritised across the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to provide financial support to Housing Associations to ensure future retrofitting and climate change targets can be met.
answer_text: My officials, with support from the Strategic Investment Board, have been engaging with the Northern Ireland Federation of Housing Associations (NIFHA) in relation to options for future financial support for retrofit and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in line with climate change obligations on the pathway to Net Zero by 2050.  Potential future funding will be subject to the required business case processes and approvals and budget availability.  My officials will continue to support NIFHA through the development of the necessary economic appraisals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his commitment to hold talks with the British Medical Association on pay and working conditions.
answer_text: My officials met with the three Chairs of the BMA Committees (Consultants, SAS and Junior Doctors) on 15th February 2024 to initiate and open discussions on the way forward.  At the meeting the BMA were advised that I had agreed to implement the DDRB recommendations for 2023/24 as a matter of urgency. My officials also reiterated my commitment to further negotiations when I have greater clarity on any final settlement in the ongoing pay negotiations in England and Wales. The BMA indicated that this commitment was insufficient to meet their demands for above inflationary uplifts and full pay restoration.I further wrote to the Junior Doctors’ Committee on 20th February and again on 5th March outlining my concerns the impact of strike action would have on patient care, and reiterating my belief that there are grounds for productive negotiations on several fronts which include 2024/25 pay, and potentially reforming the Junior Doctor contract, as well as addressing concerns on working conditions. Further meetings with my officials and representatives from the Junior Doctors’ Committee took place 26th March and are planned for April. A meeting between officials and representatives of the BMA Consultant Committee has also been scheduled for 10th April 2024.I have throughout advised that I am more than prepared to enter into discussion to see if there is a way where we can move forward without the need for further industrial action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what action his Department will take to require private owners of hedging along the section of the A20 Portaferry Road between Teal Rocks and the Flood Gates to cut back hedges from the Department for Infrastructure-owned footpath;  and (ii) whether his Department will take legal action against those private owners of hedging to force footpaths to be cleared to the legal minimum.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular routine maintenance inspections on all roads. Inspectors will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction, obscuring road signs, or have the potential to create danger for the road user. In those instances where overgrown hedges are identified as causing a danger, officials will contact the landowner requesting their co-operation in having the necessary work undertaken.  If the landowner fails to respond, the Department can instigate legal action to have the danger presented by the overgrown hedges addressed, although this action is not usually required. The Department seeks to work with landowners to proactively manage safety issues in this way, including by placing notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding landowners of their responsibility regarding hedges and the need to carry out maintenance during the early part of the year, avoiding where possible the bird nesting season from 1 March to 31 August. My Department is not aware of any current issues with overgrown hedges on the Portaferry Road, although it is acknowledged that the footway is narrow and it takes very little growth before hedges start to encroach onto the footway. The area will be kept under review and contact will be made with landowners as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department will review the effectiveness of the 20mph speed limit zones at schools; and (ii) when he will provide an update on new 20mph speed limit schemes.
answer_text: There have been two tranches of part time 20mph speed limits delivered, which introduced these measures at 215 schools, an investment of about £4m. A review is already underway to determine if a further tranche is justified. The findings of this review will be made public later this year.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when the Historical Environment Division last inspected Kircubbin Harbour; and (ii) when officers will review the state of this historic quay.
answer_text: (i) Kircubbin Harbour was last inspected by a Field Monument Warden of the Historic Environment Division in January 2024. (ii) Each Field Monument Warden report is reviewed by curatorial staff within the Division.  Condition and risk are scored and any changes to the overall condition score are logged and tracked.  Reports over recent years have noted the deteriorating condition of the harbour and this has been brought to the attention of the owner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when the review of Sport NI will be completed; and (ii) whether there will be a change in objectives to ensure grassroots sports will be supported.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of Sport NI, arranged at the request of the Sport NI Chair and Board, is to identify and understand the cause of governance and control issues so that they can be addressed. The draft report of findings is due in April/May 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why the Housing Executive was not included in the list of arm's length bodies being reviewed by the Department at their presentation to the Committee for Communities on 7 March 2024.
answer_text: When the Executive agreed to proceed with the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) commitment to review ALBs; individual Ministers were asked to conduct the assessment of their respective ALBs and determine how best they should be rationalised and governed. The Northen Ireland Housing Executive’s (NIHE) was already in the midst of a long-term review of the delivery of social housing so it was agreed that the organisation would not be encompassed within an overall DfC Review as well.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 8316/22-27, to detail the specific legislation that confirms a recall day is treated as seven days.
answer_text: The orders which extended the expiry date of section 78 of the Coronavirus Act 2020 were made by the confirmatory procedure under section 90(2) of that Act and are subject to the procedure laid out in section 96 of the Act.  Section 96(7) of the Coronavirus Act specifies that an order made under the confirmatory procedure ceases to have effect at the end of the period of 40 days beginning with the day on which the order is made unless, during that period, it is approved by resolution of the Assembly.   Section 96(8)(c) states that, when calculating the period of 40 days, no account is to be taken of any time during which the Northern Ireland Assembly is adjourned for more that 6 days.  Any day when the Assembly is recalled would count towards the 40-day period and, in accordance with section 96(8)(c) (following the adjournment of the Assembly at the end of a recall day) the following 6 days would also count towards the 40 days as these are days when the Assembly has not been adjourned for more than 6 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to ensuring that roads reports, provided to local councils, are also emailed to MLAs who serve the same area.
answer_text: Following the presentation of my Department’s Roads Reports to Councils, the reports are published on my Department’s website. A link to the report is then issued to each Member for their respective council areas. The use of links to the information allows Members to quickly and easily access the reports for each of the relevant council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to (i) review and identify financing to convert both ferries operating on Strangford Lough to electric; and (ii) work with Queens University, who have harnessed tidal energy in Strangford Lough, to potentially power the ferries.
answer_text: I am committed to taking steps to reduce carbon emissions in line with the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. My Department will be looking into options to potentially decarbonise the ferries, these will be subject to consideration and budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the provision of £1500 cost of living payment by the Minister for Communities to voluntary and community sector organisations, whether he will provide an equal cost of living support payment of £1500 to the voluntary and community sector organisations funded through his Department, such as the Rural Community Transport Partnerships and the urban community organisations.
answer_text: I have approved the provision of funding to community transport and shopmobility organisations for April and May 2024, subject to public expenditure processes and completed grant applications.  This grant will be based on the overall level of funding provided in 2023/24 and will allow continuation of services until the incoming 2024/25 budget position is confirmed.  I have no plans to provide a cost of living support payment at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to (i) repairing; or (ii) resurfacing Shore Road, Portaferry, between Lough Shore Road and Cook Street, in advance of the 2024 tourist season.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council in due course.My Department will continue to monitor this area and all defects meeting current intervention levels shall be recorded for repairs in line with set maintenance policy guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the budget allocated for homelessness prevention in each of the last five financial years; (ii) what proportion of the overall homelessness services budget this represented; and (iii) what proportion of the revenue expenditure of the regional services budget this represented.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me that the table below reflects the Housing Executive expenditure in relation to homeless prevention as requested:“2019/20£k’s2020/21£k’s2021/22£k’s2022/23£k’s23/24 Budget£k’sRevenueIncome(3,201)(1,785)(2,220)(3,492)(3,739)Expenditure130,932148,402145,140157,585156,133Total Net Revenue Expenditure 127,731146,617142,920154,093152,394Total Homeless Programme Cost (included in above)12,60520,85024,08026,38133,548Homeless Programme as a portion of Total Net Revenue Exp.9.9%14.2%16.8%17.1%22.0%Homeless Prevention (Part of overall Homeless Programme)Community Prevention and Support Initiatives 3,0122,8443,5114,3564,352Strategic Prevention Programme 3367851,293976302Total Homeless Prevention Programme3,3483,6294,8045,3324,654Homeless Prevention as a % of total Homeless Programme26.6%17.4%20.0%20.2%13.9%Homeless Prevention as a % of total Net Revenue Exp.2.6%2.5%3.4%3.5%3.1%”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients each of the five regional homeless nurses have supported, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally by the Department; the data was provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.  Please find the 5 Trust responses below:Belfast Health and Social Care TrustInformation on the specific number of homeless patients is not available for Belfast HSCT as the homesless nurses work within the Inclusion Health Service multidisciplinary Team.  Northern Health and Social Care TrustThe NHSCT homeless health nurse has been in post since October 2022. Since commencement of post, full health assessments have been completed on 229 clients living in homeless hostels.As part of these assessments all clients have been offered HepC and HIV screening. A large proportion of clients require ongoing review and referrals.  A smaller proportion of clients have required a higher level of enhanced support for several months.
