question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support his Department provides in school to children who are living in temporary accommodation.
answer_text: The Department of Education works alongside the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES) to provide funding and support to Asylum Seeker and Refugee (ASR) children residing in temporary contingency and dispersal accommodation in Northern Ireland. IES staff visit ASR contingency hotels on a regular basis to meet with families and discuss educational provision and requirements. This includes ensuring children with Special Educational Needs, who need the support of a specialist placement, can access the statutory assessment process.  IES also provide support for these children through their ‘Schools Trauma Advisory and Referral Service’ (STARS); trauma training and other emotional wellbeing advice that support children who have experienced trauma to help ensure they are supported in the continuation of their education.  Further to this, in 2022/23 the Department provided £460k funding for the provision of 60 Welcome Club places (30 primary; 30 post-primary) at two schools (Malvern Primary School (PS) and Integrated College Glengormley (ICG)) in the Greater Belfast area, in response to ASR children being accommodated in contingency hotels.  Whilst the post-primary provision has now ended with places having been found in mainstream provision both in ICG and other post-primary schools closer to the young peoples’ accommodation, welcome provision continues to support ASR pupils attending Malvern PS.During the 2023/24 financial year, the Department provided £795,000 to the EA and Schools from TEO’s ASR Dispersal Fund and a further £40k from the UK Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) to cover additional costs associated with the provision of education for children from the ASR community.  Funding was directed toward welcome provision, language skills, transport, IT equipment, uniform assistance, and other costs in removing barriers to education for these children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, when he will answer (i) AQW 5785/22-27; (ii) AQW 5499/22-2; (iii) AQW 5501/22-27; (iv) AQW 5781/22-27; and (v) AQW 5782/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 5785/22-25 was answered on 3rd May 2024. AQW 5499/22-27 was answered on 21st May 2024. AQW 5501/22-27 was answered on 23rd May 2024. AQW 5781/22-27 was answered on 3rd May 2024. AQW 5782/22-27 was answered on 11th November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact the work of the five regional homeless nurses has had on (i) the number of Emergency Department visits; (ii) the number of ambulance call-outs; and (iii) the number of patients leaving hospital before they could be treated, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) do not hold service user data at the granular level required to enable such an analysis and it is therefore not possible to quantify the impact of the regional homeless nurses’ work in terms of the numbers of attendances to Accident & Emergency Departments, the number of ambulance call-outs; or the number of patients leaving hospital before they could be treated, in the last three financial years.  The impact of the regional homeless nurses stretches beyond unscheduled care attendances in supporting the complex needs of the homeless population. They play an important role in bridging the gap between community and hospital care to the street homeless and those who are in adult homeless hostels by supporting, advocating, educating, empowering, and caring for those within the homeless population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many homes will be included in the Shared Housing programme.
answer_text: My Department has an annual delivery target of 200 shared social units per year.  The Shared Housing Programme is currently delivering 2,501 shared social housing units across 76 developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide the website diagnostics for the number of uses of homelessness resources provided by CCEA in each of the last five years.
answer_text: DE has contributed to the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plans by providing support to schools, children and young people, through resources provided by CCEA. The downloads to date for the Learning Resources ‘Understanding Homelessness’ are as follows:Key Stage 1 & 2 total downloads 1,545Key Stage 3 & 4 total downloads 3,853DE’s support for this work via CCEA will continue despite the absence of a budget for Departments in respect of the Interdepartmental Homelessness Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Newtownards library based in Queen's Hall.
answer_text: Further to my response to AQW 5873/22-27, my officials continue to work with Libraries NI to progress the business case for the redevelopment of Newtownards Library on the existing site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he will ensure appropriate investment will be provided for shared housing in 2024-25.
answer_text: It will be for the Executive to decide on the level of funding allocated to each NI Department.  However, I will seek to maximise the funding allocated to my Department through my engagement in the 2024-25 Budget Process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will bring forward a strategy to move away from fossil fuels for home heating.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy, "The Path to Net Zero Energy," (published on 16 December 2021) outlines the policy direction for decarbonising the economy and phasing out fossil fuels, including gas, heating oil, coal, and certain other solid fuels used for heating. An update to progress on the strategy is produced annually and outlines the key strategic priorities and energy policy initiatives which are being developed and delivered with partners across government. My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for domestic and non-domestic consumers to move away from fossil fuels for heating and adopt low carbon heating technologies including heat pumps.  Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the difference between the Integrated Education Strategy compared to the requirements contained in the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) why the strategy does not reflect the requirements defined in the Act.
answer_text: In line with its duty under the Integrated Education Act 2022, the Department published an Integrated Education Action Plan on 26 April 2023 as a constituent part of the first strategy for Integrated Education. That action plan committed DE to fifteen specific actions in delivering implementation of the Act and forms the basis of the Department’s implementation plans. The Strategy and Action Plan are available here: Strategy-integrated-education-northern-ireland and Integrated-education-strategy-action-plan.Though published before I became Minister, I am satisfied that the Integrated Education Strategy is compliant with the requirements of the Integrated Education Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will review the premia payments to teacher training colleges.
answer_text: I recognise the important role of both St Mary’s University College and Stranmillis University College.  It is clear that there are significant financial pressures facing all our higher education institutions and their current and prospective students. I have asked my officials to update me on work to date on a potential review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward. Any review of Higher Education Funding could give consideration to the issue of premia payments to University Colleges along with all other aspects of their funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of teachers required to meet future school needs in (i) 2024; and (ii) 2025.
answer_text: ANSWERThe estimated number of teachers (based on projected number of pupils) required for (i) 2024 is 19,859.3 full-time equivalent teachers and for (ii) 2025 is 19,677.0 full-time equivalent teachers. However, the Board of Governors (BoG) of each school is entitled to determine the number and type of staff required. For a controlled school the Education Authority (EA) appoints the teaching and non teaching staff and is also the employing authority for non-teaching staff in catholic maintained schools. The Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) appoints the teaching staff in catholic maintained schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact that suitable bail accommodation has had on prison population.
answer_text: There are a range of socio-economic factors that have an impact on reoffending, including poverty and social deprivation, mental health issues, addictions, homelessness, and a lack of educational attainment and employment opportunities.  These factors contribute to people becoming involved in crime in the first place and are often exacerbated through contact with the criminal justice system, leading to a cycle of offending which can cause significant harm to victims and communities.  Management information captured by Court Service indicates that a small percentage of defendants are refused bail by the Courts due to having no suitable accommodation.  Information disclosed by individuals upon committal to prison, both on remand and sentenced, indicates that the availability of stable accommodation may be a significant issue.  33% of those committed to Maghaberry during 2023 had either come from temporary arrangements with a friend or family member; a hostel; had been living on the streets; or in a night shelter. Evidence indicates that one of the critical success factors in desistance from reoffending is the provision of stable accommodation on release.  It is for this reason that NIPS takes a proactive approach to preventing homelessness by ensuring all prisoners assessed as having accommodation needs are provided with appropriate advice and interventions.  Our aim is to ensure that, as far as reasonably practicable, appropriate accommodation, temporary or permanent, and associated support services are in place for each person leaving custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the action plan, as set out in the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, will be published.
answer_text: In line with its duty under the Integrated Education Act 2022, the Department published an Integrated Education Action Plan on 26 April 2023 as a constituent part of the first strategy for Integrated Education. That action plan committed DE to fifteen specific actions in delivering implementation of the Act and forms the basis of the Department’s implementation plans. The Strategy and Action Plan are available here: Strategy-integrated-education-northern-ireland and Integrated-education-strategy-action-plan.Though published before I became Minister, I am satisfied that the Integrated Education Strategy is compliant with the requirements of the Integrated Education Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the funding for the Access and Inclusion Fund for 2024-25.
answer_text: Given that the Department’s capital budget for 2024/25 has not yet been confirmed no information regarding breakdown of funds for the Access and Inclusion programme is available.  The capital funding that will be allocated for my Department will need to be prioritised firstly to meet contractual commitments and other inescapable pressures that include health and safety maintenance work.  If sufficient funds are available to deliver a viable Access and Inclusion programme in 2024/25, I plan to launch the scheme at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether answers from the Housing Executive, as requested via Assembly Written Questions, will be made available to the Assembly for publication on its website.
answer_text: Further to comments made by the Speaker on 12 March 2024 I have been seeking information requested by Members from the NI Housing Executive and passing this on to Members.  My officials have also been working with NIHE regarding the information available on its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of all additional monies allocated by him to external partners since 3 February 2024.
answer_text: Since taking up my post, on 3 February, I have been able to allocate £4.6m of funding to external partners. This is broken down in the table below: Additional Funding to External Partners (since 3 Feb 24 to 13 March 2024)£’000Resource DELSupporting People  3,000 Library Bookstock  1,000 Voluntary & Community sector     428 Independent Library Bodies        65 Volunteer Now - 40th Anniversary Event       40 Capital DELMusical Instruments Programme100Total 4,633

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered changing prisoner release days to the start of the week, enabling accommodation and support to be put in place to coincide with release from custody.
answer_text: My Department appreciates that prisoner release days particularly at the weekends can present challenges for individuals accessing vital support services linked to accommodation.Prison Rule 30(6) allows remission to be credited to certain prisoners to ensure that they are not released at weekends or on any public holiday in Northern Ireland.  Articles 8(6), 13(7) and 14(12) of the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland Order) 2008 precludes those sentenced under its provisions from availing of remission under this Rule.  However, Section 12(2) of the Prison Act 1898 still remains in force for those prisoners.  Under this section prisoners due to be released on a Sunday, Christmas Day or Good Friday, are discharged on the day next preceding.   The Northern Ireland Prison Service currently has no legislative authority to shorten further any custodial period, any change would require further legislation. With a particularly congested legislative programme across all of the Executive Departments, it will not be possible for me to bring forward any additional Assembly Bills beyond the three that I am committed to delivering to modernise aspects of the justice system; to reform sentencing arrangements following a review of sentencing policy; and to tackle hate crime.  I have asked officials to monitor ongoing legislative developments in England and Wales, including whether their introduction has had a material impact on reoffending. And, cognisant of the challenges of weekend releases, the Lady Chief Justice has helpfully agreed to move her High Court Bail listings from a Friday commencing 8th April 2024.    Whilst we cannot affect the day of release, the Prison Service does take a proactive approach to prevent homelessness, by ensuring that everyone exiting custody has accommodation on release or is signposted to available options. ‘The protocol for the management of the accommodation and related support needs of people in custody in Northern Ireland’, published in December 2023, sets out a collaborative approach to addressing accommodation needs from NIHE, PBNI and NIPS. To support the implementation of the protocol we will also be introducing NIHE Housing Advisers into prisons in April 2024 to enhance the accommodation advocacy role currently undertaken by our VCS partners, Housing Rights.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of what the additional monies allocated to external partners is being used for since 3 February 2024.
answer_text: Since becoming Minister for Communities, on 3 February, I have been able to allocate £4.6m of funding, as at the time of your question, to external partners. Detail on what the funding is being used for is set out below:Resource DELA £3m allocation to the Supporting People Programme that will help sustain housing-related support to vulnerable people within Supporting People schemes. This will assist vital services to some of the most vulnerable people in our society.A £1m investment in library bookstock which will have a positive impact on lending, membership and participation in Libraries.A £0.4m allocation to Voluntary and Community Sector that will help sustain the provision of services to individuals and to groups, by helping them with running costs to maintain opening hours.  Additional funding totalling £0.07m for Independent Library bodies to provide support given the rising costs they have faced.  This year represents the 40th year of Volunteers Week and Volunteer Now and a £0.04m allocation I have provided will contribute towards reward and recognition events to thank volunteers. Capital DELAn additional £0.1m allocation to the Arts Council NI to fund additional awards under the Musical Instruments Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will announce the Access and Inclusion Fund in quarter one of the financial year to give organisations the best chance possible to be able to submit and complete applications in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Department’s capital budget for 2024/25 has not yet been confirmed. The capital funding that will be made available for my Department will need to be prioritised firstly to meet contractual commitments and other inescapable pressures that include health and safety maintenance work.  If sufficient funds are available to deliver a viable Access and Inclusion programme in 2024/25, I plan to launch the scheme at the earliest opportunity to allow organisations ample time to consider potential projects and submit applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the funding for the Access and Inclusion Fund for 2023-24.
answer_text: The overall funding made available for the Access and Inclusion Programme for 2023-24 was £720,121. This includes a contribution of £300,000 from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs towards rural projects. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department avails of online programmes to facilitate meetings.
answer_text: All Northern Ireland Civil Service departments, including the Department for Communities, have access to software to support the conduct of remote meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will lay regulations in the Assembly to extend hybrid working for councils using the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 regulations.
answer_text: Regulations to provide councils with the flexibility to hold meetings by remote or hybrid means are being developed at pace.  I am committed to keeping the gap in provision for remote and hybrid meetings to a minimum.I will confirm the timeframe for laying the regulations in the Assembly with the Communities Committee as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a copy of his instruction to officials, setting out his approach to refer Members asking Assembly Written Questions about the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly  to that body for an answer.
answer_text: Further to comments made by the Speaker on 12 March 2024 I have been seeking information requested by Members from the NI Housing Executive and passing this on to Members.  My officials have been instructed accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how his answers to Assembly Written Questions, referring Members to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly, complies with paragraph 1.5(iii) of the Ministerial Code.
answer_text: This approach was adopted to ensure clarity between the Department’s policy responsibilities and Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s (NIHE) operational remit, as well as to ensure that MLAs receive accurate, timely and full responses.  I consider this process to be compliant with paragraph 1.5(iii) of the Ministerial Code.Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions he will take to ensure Northern Ireland receives a fair allocation from the Household Support Fund allocated in the budget statement delivered on 6 March 2024.
answer_text: Any Barnett Consequential provided to Northern Ireland from the Household Support Fund announced as part of the Budget Statement on 6 March will be unhypothcated.I will, however, continue to clearly set out the case for increased funding for all the sectors my Department supports as part of the Executive discussions on the 2024-25 Budget, including Discretionary Support, which is the Northern Ireland equivalent of the Household Support fund. Therefore, I have bid for an additional £12.7m for Discretionary Support as part of the 2024-25 Budget exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how the budget delivered on 6 March 2024 will directly benefit people in Northern Ireland on benefits.
answer_text: My officials have completed an initial assessment of the Spring Budget 2024 and concluded that there are no announcements that will directly benefit people in Northern Ireland who are claiming benefits delivered by the Department for Communities.It was announced that the repayment period on budgeting advance loans taken out by claimants on Universal Credit will increase from 12 months to 24 months. This change may be beneficial for some people depending on their personal circumstances and will apply to new budgeting Advances taken out from December 2024.It is anticipated that the change will apply in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be any cost of living financial support for students from Northern Ireland who are studying outside Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Eligible students studying in the south and in Great Britain can access student support funding, including maintenance loans and grants.  Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. I am, however, acutely aware of the impact that cost of living increases are having on students, including those who are studying away from home. I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available in future years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why his Department stopped the Coronavirus Act 2020 and The Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 before the 40-day approval period for the current extension.
answer_text: During the period that the Assembly was dissolved, a number of orders were made, by the confirmatory procedure, to extend the expiry date of section 78 of the Coronavirus Act 2020.The Coronavirus Act specifies that an order made under the confirmatory procedure ceases to have effect at the end of the period of 40 days beginning with the day on which the order is made unless, during that period, it is approved by resolution of the Assembly.    In calculating the 40-day period, no account is taken of any time during which the Assembly is dissolved, in recess for more than 4 days, or adjourned for more than 6 days.The Department received advice in calculating the 40-day period which indicates that times when the Assembly was recalled count as 7 days towards the 40-day period i.e. the day of the sitting and the 6 days following, during which the Assembly is adjourned.  Section 78 of the Coronavirus Act therefore ceased to have effect on 7 March 2024.For clarification the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (NI) 2021 remains in force and provides the vehicle for my department to make more permanent regulations for remote meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for all written documents, including all emails and departmental correspondence, sent between 3 February 2024 and 4 March 2024 that mention or pertain to Section 78 (local authority meetings) of the Coronavirus Act 2020 and The Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 to be laid in the Assembly library.
answer_text: Copies of the relevant papers have been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether staff working in his Department who have disabilities, caring responsibilities or a long-term illness are permitted to work from home and to attend meetings remotely using online meeting platforms.
answer_text: In accordance with NICS HR policy, there are several flexible working arrangements available to all DfC employees, including those with disabilities, caring responsibilities, or a long-term illness, in appropriate circumstances. If a staff member requires an adjustment due to a disability, they can make a reasonable adjustment application to their line manager.  All Northern Ireland Civil Service departments, including the Department for Communities, have access to software to support the conduct of remote meetings for all staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consultation he has had with local councils in relation to ending hybrid meetings.
answer_text: Departmental officials wrote to Council Chief Executive Offices on Friday 1st March 2024 informing them of my decision regarding hybrid meetings.  The Orders to extend section 78 of the Coronavirus Act, which provided councils with the flexibility to hold meetings by remote/hybrid means during the pandemic, were temporary provisions made under emergency legislation.  I did not consider there to be any justification to continue to extend provisions of the Coronavirus Act.I am committed to providing councils with powers for remote/hybrid meetings and I will be bringing forward regulations, in as quick a timeframe as possible, using powers in the Local Government (Meetings and Performance) Act (NI) 2021.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he intends to review the Shared Housing Programme; and (ii) what consideration he has given to updating the criteria to include all Section 75 groups.
answer_text: My Department has completed two reviews of the Shared Housing Programme, and these have resulted in 28 recommendations which have informed and increased shared housing delivery.  The Shared Housing Programme transferred to the Housing Executive on 01st February 2024, and therefore, any future reviews will be managed by the Housing Executive. The Shared Housing Programme criteria does not consider Section 75 groups as all shared housing schemes are selected from the Social Housing Development Programme which considers and allocates social housing based on an applicant’s housing need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what free mental health counselling is available to those going through publicly funded IVF treatment.
answer_text: All patients accessing publicly funded fertility treatment at the Regional Fertility Centre are offered free counselling sessions. The sessions are available before, during or after patients embark on investigations or treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of how much his Department will be investing in the Shared Housing Programme.
answer_text: My Department is establishing the overall position of the former Fresh Start funding with the Department of Finance, and it has identified a £26.5M capital funding requirement to deliver the 2024/25 Shared Housing Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his announcement that publicly funded IVF treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women, to provide the rationale for the decision that, to be eligible, a woman has to be on the waiting list on or after 1 October 2022.
answer_text: I established a clinically led Project Board to deliver on the New Decade New Approach commitment to provide three funded cycles of IVF. The Board considered a number of retrospective dates for the increase to one full cycle for eligible women, including back to January 2020 when the NDNA commitment was announced.  However, as the passage of time has meant that many of the original options are no longer feasible, following extensive discussion, the Project Board agreed that offering the additional treatment to women who were placed on the waiting list for treatment on or after 1 October 2022 allowed the ability of the service to deliver additional treatment, within current funding and physical capacity, without the complexity that setting an earlier date could cause, including increasing the waiting lists that the Regional Fertility Centre had worked hard to reduce following the COVID-19 pandemic. Whilst I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the Regional Fertility Centre will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the full implementation of the New Decade, New Approach commitment for In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF).
answer_text: On 15 February, I announced that publicly funded In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women. Until that point, those women eligible for publicly funded IVF treatment were entitled to receive one fresh and one frozen embryo transfer, even if they achieve a live birth with the first transfer. Under the recent change, any eligible woman who does not achieve a live birth with either her fresh or first frozen embryo transfers, and still has frozen embryos, will be entitled to further treatment until either she has a live birth or she has transferred all her frozen embryos. It is expected that this will benefit approximately 150 women each year.A woman will be eligible if she meets the published access criteria for IVF and has been placed on the waiting list for publicly funded IVF/ICSI treatment on or after 1 October 2022. Whilst I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the Regional Fertility Centre will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision. A copy of the press release can be accessed at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/increase-funded-ivf-provision .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,  given so many practices have either closed their registration for non-NHS patients or have long waiting lists for new patients, what actions he will take to ensure everyone has access to NHS dentistry services.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.An additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.In addition, a pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Northern Ireland will receive a Barnett consequential from the £25 million announced for Women's Health Hubs in England.
answer_text: This was funded from within Department of Health and Social Care’s existing budget and therefore there is no Barnett consequential associated directly with this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the transfer of Shared Housing from the Executive Office to the Housing Executive; and (ii) whether any changes to programme priorities will happen as a result of the transfer.
answer_text: I can confirm that the transfer of the Shared Housing Programme to the Housing Executive completed on 01st February 2024, and that there will be no changes to the programme priorities as a result of the transfer

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will bring forward a voluntary scheme to allow parents to record and receive a certificate to provide recognition of their baby loss in early pregnancy, as is currently available in England.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will update the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services to include young carers.
answer_text: The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services concluded and reported in June 2023 and the focus at this stage is turning to implementation, subject to the outcome of public consultation on the Review’s recommendations. Work is ongoing to analyse the responses to consultation and it is intended to complete that work in mid-late March 2024.My Department recognises the need to support young carers to, among other things, protect their health and development in childhood. A Regional Young Carers Service (NIYCS) has been in place since 2007. It is delivered in partnership by Action for Children and Barnardo’s Northern Ireland under contract with my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group. The service offers young carers individual support, information about illness, peer support groups, personal development, short breaks, advocacy, advice, and signposting to other relevant services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he will take to progress a formal register of carers that will include both adult and child carers.
answer_text: To advance the reform of adult social care the Department has established the Social Care Collaborative Forum. The purpose of the Collaborative Forum is to provide a formal mechanism to support opportunities for greater co-operation, co-production and joined up working between the Department, the social care sector, and across Government, to implement the proposals arising out of the Consultation. The work of the Forum is being taken forward through a number of workstreams which meet on a regular basis. A Supporting Carers Workstream, co-chaired by Carers (NI) and my Department, has been established with a workplan focused on Carers specific issues including exploring potential options to further develop a centralised register, inclusive of both adult and young carers.  Many of the purposed reforms to adult social care are subject to confirmation of funding and will therefore require prioritisation and planning, and longer-term budget stability to ensure realistic delivery.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether they will be supporting the Young Carers Covenant; and (ii) to detail how they will ensure support for young carers is built into the next Programme for Government.
answer_text: While, The Executive Office is not responsible for carers policy, we will work with all Ministers to develop an ambitious and impactful Programme for Government and will make sure that this issue is given proper consideration during that process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he will take to ensure teachers receive mandatory training to understand and support pupils who are young carers.
answer_text: As part of the Education Welfare Service (EWS) Transformation Programme a need has been identified to utilise specialist practitioners to provide specific support for pupils who are young carers.  This role would involve the provision of training to teachers and support staff, working in collaboration with health colleagues and others as appropriate.  Implementation of a transformed approach to the EWS is subject to funding.  My Department continues to work closely with stakeholders to improve the understanding in schools around the role of young carers, the challenges they face, and the support available.  In January 2024, my Department issued a ‘Young Carers in Education Digital Awareness Pack’ to all schools.  The pack was developed in a collaborative approach with young carers and included a digital poster,  guidance on ‘Supporting Young Carers in School’ and a research report produced by Action for Children’s Young Carers Reference Group.  My Department will continue to provide assistance to young carers in our educational settings by working closely with colleagues in the Education Authority, other Departments and stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when the work experience programme will be restarted.
answer_text: Between 2010 and 2014, Assembly Commission staff in the Education Service coordinated work experience requests. Delivered in response to ad hoc requests, the experience was uneven for participants, depending heavily on the availability of staff in various Assembly Commission business areas. Challenges around the Assembly Commission’s responsibilities to young people and managing expectations as the Assembly faced into the final two years of the mandate meant that this ad hoc programme was halted.   Periods during which the Assembly was not operating normally, together with the COVID-19 years, have limited the potential for a work experience programme since then. However, the development of a new work experience programme is being considered as part of the Assembly Commission’s forthcoming Engagement Strategy. This draft Strategy has not yet been considered or approved by the Assembly Commission.Such a new work experience programme, if feasible, will seek to strike the balance of a positive and consistent learning experience for the participants against the demands of Assembly business in a public building. An egalitarian and inclusive programme which offers opportunities across our society will underpin our efforts.  Subject to the feasibility of the proposed work experience programme, consideration of resourcing implications, and consideration and approval of the Engagement Strategy by the Assembly Commission, officials anticipate that a work experience programme could commence in the next 12-18 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on Project Stratum; and (ii) what actions are being taken to ensure rural dwellers are able to connect to the new system where connection by overhead lines is not possible.
answer_text: (i) Of the more than 81,000 eligible premises which are scheduled for intervention under Project Stratum, over 71,000 have been addressed. 25,000 eligible premises have ordered a broadband service made available through Project Stratum. (ii) To maximise the number of premises that can connect to the Hypertfast NI full fibre gigabit capable network within the Project Stratum budget, the roll out of the Hyperfast NI network is designed to use existing Openreach infrastructure wherever possible. Where Fibrus need to extend the network or fill in gaps of existing Openreach infrastructure to enable a connection they will install Fibrus poles and other infrastructure in accordance with the original network design and so as to ensure they adhere to contractual obligations to deliver the network on time as well as to maximise the public purse investment in Project Stratum. Where householders or businesses wish to deviate from the original network design there may be an additional cost e.g. if they would prefer an underground solution versus an overhead connection using poles. Where connection to the Hyperfast NI network via poles is physically not possible other methods including underground duct connections will be investigated. No installation will take place without the agreement of the household or business and relevant signed wayleaves.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work is being undertaken to create a strategy to move away from the dependence of fossil fuels for home heating.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy provides the overall strategic context for energy decarbonisation, including the objective to move away from fossil fuels for home heating.  An update to progress on the strategy is produced annually and outlines the key strategic priorities and energy policy initiatives which are being developed and delivered with partners across government.  		My officials are currently progressing policy development on a number of strands relating to residential heating includng a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme, a call for Evidence on Biomethane, and a call for evidence on the use of liquid biofuels which are all key components to move households away from fossil fuels.  We are also progressing geothermal demonstrator projects at the Stormont Estate and Greenmount Campus, to explore our geothermal potential in supporting heat decarbonisation.		The pathway to decarbonise heat is complex and will include a range of low carbon technologies. Through this evdience based policy development, all options will be fully considered to ensure that they do not lead to adverse impacts on human health or the environment. Our focus to 2030 will be ensuring that low-regret pathways remain open while focusing on removing the most carbon intensive sources of heating.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider updating the NI Direct reporting system to (i) allow members of the public to (a) report the flying; and (b) request the removal, of unauthorised flags; and (ii) allow his Department to record data on how many complaints are made about the flying of flags on lamp-posts.
answer_text: . Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.Members of the public can report issues with flags erected on DfI infrastructure through the direct line number 0300 200 7899 and call centre staff will add these details to the mapping system.. This allows all reports to be recorded and directed to the relevant maintenance office for investigation.Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have any plans to update the Joint Protocol on the display of flags in public areas.
answer_text: Currently there are no plans to update the Joint Protocol in Relation to the Display of Flags in Public Areas (2005).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration has been given to the introduction of hospitality rates relief.
answer_text: I have met with hospitality sector representatives, along with representatives of other sectors and look forward to continuing this work.65% of the hospitality sector is in receipt of support through my Department’s Small Business Rate Relief scheme which provides businesses with a reduction of between 20% to 50% on their rates. I will be looking at strategic options for the business rates system in the coming weeks and months. It will be for the Executive to make decisions on any potential specific interventions in line with its overall fiscal capacity and policy priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Equality Act.
answer_text: There are extensive legal protections in place in respect of equality in this jurisdiction.  There is also work being undertaken that will enhance the current legislative framework specifically in respect of the Review of the Race Relations (NI) Order 1997.  There is no agreement on a single Equality Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the regulations needed for the implementation of the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (“the Act”) is being implemented on a phased basis over five years, from 2022-2027.  This involves the drafting of, and consultation on, approximately twenty-five Statutory Rules and accompanying statutory guidance.  A number of Commencement Orders will also be required.  To date, two Statutory Rules and one Commencement Order have been made, relating to the renaming of Guardians ad Litem and the Northern Ireland Guardian ad Litem Agency. Work is ongoing on the drafting of the remaining Statutory Rules which will be required.  In April 2025, my Department will publish and lay before the Assembly its first report on the implementation of Parts 1 and 2 of the Act, in accordance with its duty under Section 158.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps he is taking to help voluntary and community sector organisations that have stopped providing services due to budget cuts.
answer_text: My department did not make cuts to community and voluntary sector grants as part of last year’s budget exercise and my officials are not aware of any community organisation that we fund having stopped providing services.I recognise the role that the voluntary and community sector plays in providing a wide range of support to people across Northern Ireland and would like to see more organisations being supported, should budget become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether a pay award to Further Education lecturers will restore equity and fairness by matching the salary of school teachers.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the (i) Anti-Poverty Strategy; (ii) Disability Strategy; (iii) Gender Equality Strategy; and (iv) Sexual Orientation Strategy will be published.
answer_text: I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive’s Social Inclusion Strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any consideration has been given to moving responsibility for the Further Education sector from his Department to the Department of Education.
answer_text: Partnership working and collaboration between the two departments is critical to support all our learners, and the Education Minister and I will be engaging on areas of mutual responsibility and interest.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will work with the Minister of Education to consider formally linking future pay awards for Further Education lecturers to the pay awards for school teachers.
answer_text: While Further Education lecturers’ pay is a matter for the Further Education colleges as employers, I am committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will end the Housing Executive's Right to Buy scheme.
answer_text: Legislation would be required to end the Housing Executive’s Right to Buy Scheme. The Executive’s legislative programme has yet to be determined.

answer_text: Please see details of grant funding awarded under the Rural Transport Fund, broken down by recipient below. 2016/172017/182018/192019/20CDM Community Transport£443,085£442,308£431,533£417,983Down District Accessible Transport£333,543£322,975£312,758£301,787Easilink Community Transport£334,000£333,414£322,867£381,690Fermanagh Community Transport£350,613£349,998£338,926£326,180Lagan Valley Rural Transport£125,587£125,366£121,400£119,064North Coast Community Transport£419,122£418,387£405,152£477,194South Antrim Community Transport£128,354£128,129£124,075£120,205Southern Area Partnership£310,630£310,085£300,275£294,128Community Transport Association£60,000£58,000£55,100£55,100 2020/212021/222022/232023/24CDM Community Transport£422,611£406,900£406,900£393,337Down District Accessible Transport£297,120£297,120£297,120£287,216Easilink Community Transport£306,723£306,723£306,723£296,499Fermanagh Community Transport£321,980£321,980£321,980£311,247Lagan Valley Rural Transport£115,330£115,330£115,330£111,486North Coast Community Transport£384,894£384,894£384,894£372,064South Antrim Community Transport£117,871£117,871£117,871£113,942Southern Area Partnership£285,262£285,262£285,262£275,752Community Transport Association£55,100£55,100£55,100£55,100

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will update the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 to enable people in receipt of Universal Credit to avail of free dental treatment, sight tests and support for hospital travel.
answer_text: Preparatory work has recently commenced in advance of updating the Travelling Expenses and Remission of Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004. The focus of work is to reinstate automatic passporting for those eligible to receive free sight tests and free Health Service dental treatment through the Help with Health Care (HwHC) scheme following the migration of their benefits to Universal Credit by the Department for Communities (DfC).The Department, in consultation with DfC, has implemented an interim solution to help ensure that people do not lose out on their entitlement. However, this is no longer an automatic system. It is paper based, and individuals must apply for an annual certificate. This has obvious implications for some of our most vulnerable people. While the Department is currently liaising with officials in DfC to explore options for streamline this process, it is clear that there is an urgent need to update our legislation to reinstate automatic passporting to those eligible.The Department will seek to maintain, as closely as possible, the caseload of recipients who are eligible for HwHC, balancing the continued protection of the health of members of society who need additional support without adding pressures on HSC budgets. My Department is currently liaising with other NI departments and colleagues in GB. This will inform the decision on setting the income threshold for UC becoming an automated passport benefit. A short public consultation will be conducted in the coming months on proposed changes to our legislation in relation to UC, with the associated legislative process commencing in due course thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of Carers NI research showing that (i) 46 per cent of Carer’s Allowance recipients in Northern Ireland are living in poverty; and (ii) the rate of food bank use among Carer’s Allowance claimants in Northern Ireland is nearly twice as high as the general carer population.
answer_text: I recognise the important role unpaid carers play within the wider community, as well as the vital support they provide to those who they are caring for.  I am mindful of the particular challenges that this group faces, particularly in light of recent events including the COVID-19 pandemic and cost of living crisis. Carer’s Allowance is the main benefit in the social security system targeted towards unpaid carers – its primary purpose is to provide a measure of financial support for people who give up the opportunity of full-time employment in order to provide regular and substantial care for a severely disabled person.  It was never intended to be a wage for caring or to replace lost or forgone earnings in their entirety.  As part of the annual benefits up-rating exercise the weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance was increased by 10.1% from April 2023 and will increase by a further 6.7% from April 2024. Depending on individual circumstances carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and their associated carer premiums/additions.The Discretionary Support Scheme can offer help towards short-term living expenses or with the purchase of household items for those people in an extreme, exceptional or crisis situation.  Depending on personal circumstances applicants could be offered either an interest-free loan or a non-repayable grant.In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.I have noted the recommendations relating to additional support for carers that were made in the Independent Advisory Panel’s Report into the future of welfare mitigations that was published in October 2022.The recommendations made in that report and any future mitigation package will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a copy of the (i) Equality Impact Assessment; and (ii) Rural Needs Impact Assessment, carried out in relation to setting fares for the Strangford ferry.
answer_text: The Equality Impact Assessment is available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/strangford-lough-ferry-service-fare-review-2023-s75-screening-form-final.pdfRural Needs Impact Assessments are not normally published by DfI therefore I have asked my officials to forward you a copy directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide the policy used to determine the fare increase for the Strangford ferry; and to confirm where this policy is published.
answer_text: Fares for Strangford Ferry have been unaltered since November 2009. Due to budgetary pressures all Departments were asked to reduce costs or increase revenue where possible, by the Secretary of State. Following the guidance contained in MPMNI published by DoF and the legislation within The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 my officials carried out a review of fares and cost recovery. The decision was made in accordance with the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 on 26 January 2024 that all fares would increase by 33.41%.The full price single fare is now £1.30 for a passenger and £7.70 for a car and driver.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list each occasion between 1 April 2016 and 31 January 2024 when the Strangford ferry service did not provide a crossing; and the reason why the crossing was unable to be provided.
answer_text: During the period in question, Strangford Lough Ferry Service was scheduled to sail on 170,770 occasions, and for a variety of reasons was unable to do so on 1,979 occasions providing an overall availability of 98.85 %. There were 1,502 missed sailings that were not within the control of DfI such as fog and industrial action, while there were 446 missed sailings that were within the control of DfI such as technical issues or maintenance.  I have asked my Officials to supply a full list of these occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are in place to ensure that people with disabilities can access public transport.
answer_text: The accessibility and inclusivity of our public transport network is vitally important for me and I am fully committed to providing inclusive and accessible Public Transport and facilities for all customers. I am pleased to advise that both my Department and Translink work closely with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) and the Royal National Institute for the Blind (RNIB) and value the contributions and advice of public transport users with lived experience of disabilities to inform how we can continually make improvements to the accessibility of the network.  Translink is currently carrying out a Rail Halt Accessibility Assessment Project which will carry out an accessibility audit of all stations and provide proposals on how a programme of accessibility improvements can be developed and implemented. The project will establish a strategy for the improvements in accessibility at all Rail Halts and IMTAC have been consulted as part of this project and this assessment is expected to be completed in 2024. Translink’s website has been independently accredited by The Shaw Trust charity achieving 100% compliance with (WCAG 2.1 AA) web accessibility standards. For any passengers who are deaf and require support from Tranlink’s Contact Centre, an online interpreter service is provided via their website, offering both British and Irish Sign Language services via a three-way video conversation between the Contact Centre Agent, the interpreter and customer.As you aware, my Department also provides grant funding to support the delivery of two transport schemes: (1) the Dial-a-Lift (DAL) Scheme, a rural service delivered by11 Rural Community Transport Partnerships and (2) the Disability Action Transport Scheme (DATS), which operates in 29 urban towns and cities.   These services complement wider public transport services and provide accessible public transport options to those who have difficulty accessing public transport.  Vehicles used in the delivery of DAL and DATS have been adapted to suit the needs of those with a disability and drivers have also been trained in dealing with those who have a disability.  The main user groups of these services are those with a disability or older people.The Concessionary Fares Scheme provides free travel on public transport services to those who are registered blind or who are in receipt of a regular War Disablement Pension or Guaranteed Income Payment from the Service Personnel and Veterans Agency.  A half fare concession is available to those who are known to their local Health and Social Care Trust as having a learning disability or are partially sighted, those who are in receipt of the mobility element of the Personal Independence Payment or, those who due to medical reasons, have either been refused a licence to drive a motor vehicle or have had an existing licence revoked.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) number of households in housing arrears to the Housing Executive; and (ii) the average household arrears, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency and council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive; however, I previously sought the requested information from the Chief Executive in response to the same question you asked in AQW 5499/22-27 and to which I replied on 21 M	ay 2024.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the cost to the Department of providing taxis for patients to attend outpatient appointments for the last three years.
answer_text: It is not possible for my Department to provide this information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are in place to ensure that Deaf people can give informed consent to receive Covid-19 vaccines.
answer_text: Like anyone else, a deaf person can access information on the COVID-19 vaccination programme via the NI Direct webpage.  In addition, they can access all the relevant vaccination information leaflets via the PHA link on the same page.  If they need to speak to a health professional, a free remote interpreting service for British Sign Language (BSL) and Irish Sign Language (ISL) users in Northern Ireland has been introduced which provides the facility for deaf people to communicate with any health care professional via an interpreter. This should enable a deaf person to make an informed consent decision as to whether or not they wish to book a vaccination slot.  If they have been invited by their own GP to receive a COVID-19 vaccine, then their GP should take into account their patient’s particular needs.  When attending a vaccination appointment, arrangements can be put in place to ensure additional time is given and if necessary, interpreter services can be arranged.

The pay settlement for Housing Executive workers is agreed at U.K. Level through collective bargaining arrangements.These are wholly matters for the Housing Executive, TUS and its employees. I will, in line with DoF guidance, need to approve the pay award once an agreed settlement has been reached.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what measures she has taken to ensure welfare mitigation measures are protected in the absence of an Executive post 28 October 2022.
answer_text: On 31 January 2022 the Assembly approved the Welfare Supplementary Payment (Extension) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022.  This legislation extended the period for which all Welfare Supplementary Payments, excluding the Social Sector Size Criteria, could be paid up to 31 March 2025.  Further legislation extended the Social Sector Size Criteria mitigation payments for an indefinite period.The Welfare Supplementary Payments (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 introduced a statutory requirement on my Department to produce a report setting out an assessment of the operation of each of the ongoing Welfare Supplementary Payment schemes by 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work is being undertaken by her Department to ensure opportunities arising from PEACE PLUS funding can be accessed.
answer_text: My Department, along with other the government departments, both North and South, have been working closely with the Special European Programmes Body (SEUPB), the Managing Authority for PEACEPLUS, on the content and implementation of the new programme.This is particularly reflected in Theme 1 (Building Peaceful and Thriving Communities) Investment Areas 1.2, Empowering Communities (€30m) and 1.4 Reimaging Communities (€75m) for which the Department for Communities will be an Accountable Department.In July 2023 the Department participated in a hybrid pre-application workshop for Investment Area 1.4 which provided advice, guidance, and practical support in relation to preparing an application.  The event attracted over 400 attendees either in person or on-line. The call for applications to Investment Area 1.4 closed in September 2023.Pre-application support for Investment Area 1.2, Empowering Communities, will also be undertaken prior to the opening of the call for applications later in 2024.Whilst there is specific action being taken in relation to the PEACEPLUS Programme it should be set within the context of the ongoing work that my Department takes to incentivise, support and enhance community empowerment through an innovative and sustainable community and voluntary sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what processes are used to measure the effectiveness and progress of the Shared Housing funded projects across NI.
answer_text: Shared housing developments are supported through Good Relations Support funding to develop five-year Good Relations Plans, which are delivered to new shared housing tenants and communities within a five-mile radius of each development.  The Plans’ events and projects are subject to formal monitoring, including participant feedback and submission of six-monthly monitoring returns to the Department for Communities.  Plans are subjected to the requisite Post Project Evaluation process.In addition, shared housing tenants complete Year 1, Year 3 and Year 5 Tenant Surveys; it is anticipated that these will provide longitudinal data of ‘distance travelled’ in relation to shared living.  The Year 1 survey focuses on tenants’ reasons for choosing to live in a shared housing environment and their experiences after a twelve-month period.  The Years 3 and 5 surveys focus on tenants’ participation in the Good Relations Plan activities.The Department has also undertaken two evaluations of the Shared Housing Programme with key stakeholders to measure the effectiveness of the Programme and its reach. These evaluations have resulted in recommendations which have informed and developed the Programme guidance and supporting documents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how will the loss of European Social Fund funding affect the services which her Department provides.
answer_text: The loss of European Social Funding has the potential to impact negatively on our ability to provide two business critical programmes which currently receive ESF funding.  These programmes are NIACRO Working Well and Quest Advantage:NIACRO Working Well supports people with convictions into employment, education or training. Quest Advantage equip young people with the skills and qualifications to effectively integrate into employment. Both these programmes are core elements of the rehabilitation of people in the care of the NI Prison Service and it is vital that we are able to continue the important work currently being delivered through these projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the level of GP services for the Ards Peninsula; and (ii) whether the level of service provision available in October 2022 is meeting contractual levels.
answer_text: I can confirm that all GP Practices in the Ards Peninsula are opened and that all patient lists in the area are currently open with no restrictions in regards to patient registrations.  All GP Practices in the Ards Peninsula are providing the full range of services that they are required to provide under their GMS Contracts and the service they are providing is meeting their current contractual requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many GP surgeries across the South Eastern Trust area have requested to close patient lists in the last year; and (ii) to provide (a) the name of the surgery; (b) the date the request was submitted; and (c) the response provided.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many patients in the South Eastern Trust area live in a geographical area not covered by a GP surgery; and (ii) to provide a list of the geographical areas affected.
answer_text: All patients in the South Eastern Trust area live in a geographical area covered by a GP Surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many GP surgeries across the South Eastern Trust area have requested to reduce the area they are contracted to cover; and (ii) to provide (a) the name of the surgery; (b) the date the request was submitted; and (c) the response provided.
answer_text: The SPPG have received three applications from South Eastern area GP practices to reduce their practice boundary in 2022/2023.The applications were received on 5/5/2022, 7/4/2022 and 17/5/2022. For confidential reasons the SPPG cannot provide the GP Practice details.  The three GP practices will be advised of the SPPG decision when applications for Belfast, Northern, Southern and Western area practices have been reviewed and agreed by SPPG. All applications are discussed with Local Medical Committee representatives

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to ensure there is appropriate primary care health provision in the Ards Peninsula, following GP retirements and closures of GP surgeries due to staffing issues.
answer_text: GP practices are independent contractors and responsibility for replacing doctors who have retired rests with practices themselves.  I am aware however that some practices are experiencing difficulties recruiting replacement GPs, particularly in remote areas such as the Ards Peninsula.I am committed to continuing to work to address the pressures facing GP practices.  On 26th September, I announced a £5.5m package of support for GPs.  This package provides targeted help to parts of the service that are most under pressure by investing £1m in a new Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme to help attract GPs in hard-to-recruit areas. It also boosts the support team for GP practices who are experiencing difficulty by an additional £680k.  The SPPG and Department of Health are aware of the current issues with the GP workforce and an expert working group is currently undertaking a detailed analysis in order to plan for future required GP training places.  This is a complex piece of work as it relates not only to the financial resource required to increase GP training places but also to other constraints such as the number of potential eligible applicants in the medical workforce and the time required to increase capacity in the necessary infrastructure required to provide GP training.  All these issues are currently being taken into consideration as a matter of urgencyI would like to confirm that no GP Practices have closed in the Ards Peninsula. The SPPG have previously approved a merger between two practices in the area - Windmill Surgery and Cuan Family Practice - on 1st October 2020.  The practice merger was approved in order to increase their resilience and help promote stability. I recognise that being able to access GP services is very important for people across Northern Ireland.  My Department has therefore convened a working group to explore issues around access to services, including how technology can be better used to provide improved access for patients.  An initial report from the group is anticipated in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what financial support is being planned to support private home owners in moving from home heating oil to a more environmentally sustainable alternative.
answer_text: My Department published the Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, The Path to Net Zero Energy, in December 2021. A key policy within this strategy is the phase-out of fossil fuel home heating oil, which will be necessary to achieve our vision of net zero emissions.One pathway available to consumers of home heating oil to reduce emissions is to switch to a low carbon heating technology, for example a heat pump.The Energy Strategy commits to a pilot support scheme to help domestic and small business consumers switch to these more environmentally sustainable, low carbon heat technologies. This pilot scheme, that will launch in 2023, is currently being developed by my Department. It is intended that this will be followed by a wider rollout of support in future years.Further information on the pilot support scheme will be published at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy why he has not included sustainable tidal energy production in his Energy Strategy.
answer_text: My department is working towards a target of 80% of electricity consumption coming from a diverse range of renewable generating technologies by 2030. The Energy Strategy does not define which renewable electricity generating technologies will be used to achieve this target, allowing the most efficient solutions that provide locally produced, affordable, renewable energy for consumers to be brought forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions he is taking with the Minister for Communities to ensure all social homes will be included in the future roll out of Smart Meters.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – Path to Net Zero Energy - Action plan published inJanuary 2022 commits to delivering the following action this year:Action Plan 20: “Carry out a cost benefit analysis of electricity and gas smartmeters and other technologies in order to access half-hourly and dailyconsumption information”. The assignment is due to be completed by the end of this year. I will review the recommendations thereafter.Any future implementation of smart metering systems in Northern Ireland will have due to regard to the needs of all energy consumers regardless of home tenure arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action is being taken with the Department for the Economy to develop efficient untethered roadside electric vehicle charging.
answer_text: My Department established an Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task-Force, in December 2021, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network. In developing its Action Plan the Task-Force will consider EV Infrastructure in the context of the overarching principles of prioritising active travel (walking, wheeling and cycling) and public transport towards decarbonising our transport system. The Action Plan will be submitted to me shortly.The EV Task-Force is led by my Department and the Department for the Economy is also represented on it along with other key stakeholders. At this stage, no specific action is being taken with the Department for the Economy to develop efficient untethered roadside electric vehicle charging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times utility providers have been instructed by his Department to make good the road surface in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Where reinstatements by utility providers do not meet the necessary standards, they are notified to the utilities as requiring remedial action during the guarantee period which in most cases is two years. Beyond that period responsibility reverts to my Department when the intervention criteria for repairs reflects the Department’s current Limited Service policy.My Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis, rather information is managed on a Council or DfI Roads Section Office area basis. Therefore, details of the number of notifications to utility companies for sub-standard reinstatements carried out within the Ards and North Down area for each year from 2018 until 2022 to date are shown in the table below:YearNo. of failed Inspections2022 (to date)2892021498202017420195182018319Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans his Department has to ensure all Northern Ireland Housing Executive and social housing tenants are included in the roll out of smart meters.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – Path to Net Zero Energy - Action plan published inJanuary 2022 commits to delivering the following action this year:Action Plan 20: “Carry out a cost benefit analysis of electricity and gas smartmeters and other technologies in order to access half-hourly and dailyconsumption information”. The assignment is due to be completed by the end of this year. I will review the recommendations thereafter.Any future implementation of smart metering systems in Northern Ireland will have due to regard to the needs of all energy consumers regardless of home tenure arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support (i) each management type of school; and (ii) youth services, will receive this winter to ensure they can afford to heat their buildings.
answer_text: I can assure you that I fully recognise the challenges that rising heating and electricity costs present for our schools and youth services, and my officials seek regular updates from the Education Authority (EA) in relation to this. The most recent update estimated an additional pressure of £13.3m relating to rises in natural gas, heating oil and electricity costs in the 2022-23 financial year. This was after factoring in the anticipated impact of the UK government’s recent announcement of an Energy Bill Relief Scheme for the period October 2022 to March 2023. These, and other pressures have been communicated to the Department of Finance in anticipation of an agreed budget for 2022-23 in due course. In addition to this, since August 2020, the EA has proactively sought to mitigate the impact of increasing utility and energy costs by engaging an independent energy advisor to provide advice on purchasing energy, tariff changes and hedging purchase prices for electricity and gas on behalf of our schools. Notwithstanding all this, if any school or youth facility is facing financial difficultly, I would encourage them to contact the EA in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 3339/22-27, (i) what is the qualifying date for the second Low Income Cost of Living Payment; and (ii) when will the public be notified of this date.
answer_text: Details of the qualifying date for the second cost of living support payments are available at: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-supportThese details were published on 3 October.
