question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to the announcement of a 35 million euro package to aid Irish sports clubs with energy costs this winter, what support is being made available by the Northern Ireland Executive and the UK Government to aid sports clubs in Northern Ireland with the rise in energy costs.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.	Details of current funding opportunities available from Sport NI can be found on its website Sport NI – Funding (www.sportni.net/funding/). Additionally, details of Government Grants paid to the Voluntary and Community Sector are accessible via the Government Funding Database (https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions her Department is taking, in conjunction with the Education Authority, to address issues experienced by disabled children on school transport.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) delivers home to school transport on behalf of the Department of Education to over 9,500 children who have additional transport requirements identified on their Statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN). Over 20% of these pupils were newly referred to the transport service this year as SEN transport referrals are received on an ongoing basis. When new transport referrals are received, further assessments are carried out for pupils who use wheelchairs or other physical aids, and for those who have specific medical requirements to ensure any aids are suitable for use on school transport vehicles and that the pupil’s needs can be safely accommodated.  Where a pupil cannot be accommodated on school transport a parental payment is offered to assist the parents transporting their children to school. Routes and services have to be adjusted and new taxi and private hire contracts are put in place.   Confirmation of SEN transport arrangements, therefore, often takes place later in the summer.   Around 5,200 pupils with a transport requirement identified on their Statement of SEN travel to school by bus. The EA has developed specialist vehicle specifications for SEN transport to ensure the comfort and safety of children and young people using the service.    Bus Escorts travel as standard on services to special schools and additional assistance is provided where needed for pupils to board and disembark the vehicle. One to one escorts are also put in place where a specific medical or behavioural need has been identified. All transport frontline staff must complete an Access NI check prior to working on home to school transport services and all EA bus drivers and escorts are expected to receive staff inductions and regular training relating to their role.  Over 3,500 pupils with SEN transport needs travel to and from school each day in a taxi and the EA have faced challenges in providing taxi transport at the start of the school year which led to some instances where contracted routes were cancelled at short notice and some pupils did not receive transport assistance as they began the new academic year.  The EA recognises the impact that this disruption has had on the learning and routine of pupils and the inconvenience it has caused to parents and it is continuing to work hard to ensure sufficient capacity is available on the school transport network, and to improve communication procedures with parents.The EA is progressing several digital transport solutions that will help to improve safeguarding for pupils and the communication of information to parents relating to their child’s journey to school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will provide redundancy support for all Community and Voluntary Sector organisations that have been supported to deliver services for her Department through the European Social Fund.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for providing a tailored redundancy support and advice service to employers and people being made redundant, including information about benefits and support to find jobs. This includes Community and Voluntary Sector organisations.The Department’s Redundancy Service offers both virtual and face to face support to best meet the needs of both the employer and employees. A Redundancy Clinic Webcast has been developed, along with Redundancy Factsheets which help signpost employers and employees to useful information and sources of on-line support. The Department’s Work Psychology Services (WPS) has also developed a Redundancy Support booklet to help people cope with the psychological and emotional impacts of redundancy. On-site Redundancy Clinics can also be arranged. Details of the Redundancy Service can be found at www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/finding-staff/redundancy-serviceMy Department also manages the JobApplyNI website. This is a recruitment and advertising platform which lists current job vacancies. The site is available for jobseekers 24 hours a day and 7 days a week. Registering with JobApplyNI www.jobapplyni.com allows jobseekers to apply for vacancies directly to employers. Further support is available throughout the Department’s 35 Jobs and Benefits office network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions she is taking to ensure the Shared Prosperity Fund allocation for Northern Ireland will be flexible to meet the needs of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Finance leads on this matter through its EU Future Policy and Finance Workstream. Executive Departments have, however, taken a joined up approach to the issue and have made a number of representations to the British Government raising concerns about proposals for delivery of the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). Our main concern is that the proposed SPF allocation does not currently match equivalent EU funds and we are facing a shortfall in excess of £65m over the three year funding period. We also have concerns around proposals for local delivery of the Fund but will continue to engage with the British Government to try to resolve these issues and to ensure that there is no impediment to funding flowing to local organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many occupational therapists are currently employed as assessors of Personal Independence Payments.
answer_text: Capita Business Services Limited employ 14 occupational therapists for the delivery of Personal Independence Payment (PIP) assessments here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the living wage will be extended to all organisations that are in receipt of a grant through her Department, including where her Department's monies are distributed by another party, for example Local Government.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 9382/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to outline the (a) process; and (b) frequency, for reviewing the Funeral Expenses Payment; and (ii) when was the last time the payment was increased.
answer_text: (i) The Social Fund Funeral Expenses Payments scheme makes a contribution towards a funeral. It meets the reasonable necessary costs of a cremation or burial including the purchase of a grave with exclusive burial.(a) While there is no formal planned review of the scheme, my Department monitors the existing policy/ legislation. (b) There is no specific frequency for this work. 	 (ii) In relation to any increase of the payment the amount allowed for other funeral expenses had been capped at £700, however in April 2020, my Department increased this cap from £700 to £1000. Any further increases would break parity with the Department for Work and Pensions and require Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people with disabilities have been through JobStart scheme since the scheme began; and (ii) of that number, how many have secured a permanent job.
answer_text: At 30 September 2022, 1,697 young people had started a JobStart opportunity of which 527 (31%) have a disability. I am delighted to advise you that, of the 206 young people with a disability who have completed their JobStart opportunity, 159 (77%) have sustained employment and secured a permanent job.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why the Workable programme is currently capped for people with disabilities; (ii) how many people have completed the Workable programme and secured a permanent job; and (iii) how many have dropped out in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: The Workable programme is not capped for people with disabilities.  The 3 organisations that deliver the programme on behalf of my Department do so ensuring they remain within their respective annual funding allocation. Currently 703 people with disabilities are being supported by the programme.The Workable database contains data for the last 7 years therefore the Department cannot provide information for the last 10 years.745 people left the Workable programme in the last 7 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people with disabilities and whose hours of work have dropped below 10 hours, have had to leave the Workable programme to date; and (ii) when this happens, what employment support programme is provided for people with disabilities working less than 10 hours.
answer_text: Since 2016 to date 18 people with disabilities whose hours of work have dropped below 10 hours per week, have had to leave the Workable programme.The Condition Management Programme is available to people who are struggling to remain in work due to their health condition or disability. The Condition Management Programme is a work-focused rehabilitation programme delivered on behalf of my Department by five Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what contingency plans are in place to ensure the Strangford ferry service can continue to operate in the case of staff shortages.
answer_text: A minimum of four qualified crew members, including two that are licenced by the Maritime and Coastguard Agency to pilot the vessel, must be on board for the ferry to legally sail.  The Strangford Ferry Service operates using three crews and contingency has been built in as each crew has six members. This level of staffing is usually sufficient to keep the service running however when a member of staff is unavailable for a shift, other staff can be brought in on voluntary overtime or may be asked to change shifts.  In addition there are other staff who have been trained to provide cover, and agency staff can be recruited, provided they have the relevant qualifications. Part of our contingency arrangements is also to keep the travelling public informed as soon as possible so they can make alternative arrangements if the ferry service is to be suspended.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the matters outside his Department's control that prevented the Strangford ferry service from operating after 7.45pm on 24 September 2022.
answer_text: The Strangford Ferry was withdrawn from service at 7.45pm on 24 September 2022 due to crew unavailability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many staff are employed to provide the Strangford ferry service, broken down by role; and (ii) when the last recruitment was held for staff for the Strangford ferry service.
answer_text: There are currently 28.8 staff employed in the direct provision of the Strangford Ferry service as set out in the table below. RoleNumber of staff currently in postSenior Engineer (Manager)1Ferry Manager1Harbour Master1Marine Maintenance Supervisor1Shift Manager (Senior Skipper)3Skipper3Senior Purser4Purser8 (2 from an Agency)Marine Fitter1Ferry Admin Manager1Admin Support1.8Night Watch3 (1 from an Agency)TOTAL28.8In addition to those listed above there are 3 Duty Ferry Managers who provide cover outside of normal office hours. These posts are filled by staff from other sections within DfI. The last external recruitment competitions were advertised on 17 August 2020 for Skipper and Senior Purser. A senior Engineer was seconded to the service in February 2022. Agency staff have more recently been employed in late April 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in Northern Ireland were excluded from a Cost of Living Support Payment due to sanctions or other reasons, broken down by council area.
answer_text: As these payments were administered by the Department for Work and Pensions and Revenue and Customs, my Department does not hold the information required to respond to this question

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when his Department knew the Strangford ferry service would not be operational after 7:45pm on 24 September 2022; and (ii) when the first public notice was issued confirming withdrawal of the ferry service.
answer_text: Officials became aware of a potential issue with the Saturday evening sailings at approximately 12:30pm on Friday 23 September 2022. My Private Office was advised at 3:41pm on Friday 23 September 2022 that it had been concluded that suspension was unavoidable. The first public notice was issued at 4.58pm on Friday 23 September 2022 on the DfI Facebook and Twitter accounts. A few minutes later a SMS was issued to over 4000 Ferry SMS alert subscribers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when was the Support for Mortgage Interest rate last reviewed.
answer_text: The Support for Mortgage Interest rate is reviewed on a monthly basis as the standard interest rate is set at a level equal to the Bank of England's published monthly average mortgage interest rate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has plans to review Northern Ireland Housing Executive maintenance costs to ensure there is effective and efficient use of public money and in line with market values.
answer_text: The Housing Executive is a Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE) and continues to work closely with its contractors across a range of disciplines to ensure that contract pricing fairly reflects current inflationary pressures being experienced by contractors, whilst also safeguarding the use of public funds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities will the Support for Mortgage Interest rate be reviewed as a result of the increase in interest rates.
answer_text: Please refer to AQW 3442/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what responsibility the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has to maintain its stock, given the Householder's Guide booklet appears to require tenants to sustain maintenance.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the “Maintaining your home - a householder's guide” document has advice for the housing sector generally on what householders can do to manage and maintain their home and keep repairs to a minimum.The nature and extent of the responsibility of the Housing Executive to maintain its stock - in respect of tenanted properties - is to be found in its standard tenancy agreement which can be found at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Documents/Customer-charter/conditions-of-tenancyThe Housing Executive’s contractual responsibilities or obligations as a landlord are set out in Part II of that agreement, while Schedule 4 provides more details on the responsibilities of both the Housing Executive and the tenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she has plans to make an automatic payment of £500 from the Discretionary Support fund, for the remainder of this financial year, to new claimants of Universal Credit during their initial five week assessment and until the move to the Universal Credit is completed.
answer_text: The introduction of a guaranteed automatic payment to new claimants of Universal Credit would require amendments to current legislation. Amending primary legislation is not possible due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has any plans to review and put on hold all debt recovery from people on benefits, including Pension Credit and Personal Independence Payment in Northern Ireland, from 1 October 2022 to 30 September 2023.
answer_text: Any pause in debt recovery would require a break in parity from the Department for Work and Pensions and the cost of any divergence would require Executive agreement. The Department for Communities recognises the financial hardship that many people are experiencing due to the cost of living and is fully committed to supporting and helping individuals who may be finding difficulty with the repayment of social security benefit debt. Individuals should contact the Department’s Debt Management team, who will work with them to agree an affordable repayment plan. Contact details for Debt Management can be found on https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/debt-management-northern-ireland

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when, during this Autumn, the next Energy Support payment will be issued to people on relevant benefits.
answer_text: The second Low Income Cost of Living Payment qualifying date will be no later than 31 October 2022.The link below for NI Direct will be updated with the dates for the second Low Income Cost of Living Payments when they are announced.https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/cost-living-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the number of former Northern Ireland Housing Executive maisonettes or flats, now in private ownership, that are currently required under contract to pay the Northern Ireland Housing Executive for maintenance.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that the total number of flats/maisonettes that it formerly owned but are now in private ownership are 6,138. Of these 5,666 are flats and 472 are maisonettes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will bring forward regulations to enable the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to buy back maisonettes and flats.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has given the Housing Executive approval to offer a buy back option to leaseholders with vacant possession where they are part of a mixed tenure complex and the cost of the works is over £10k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will fix the flood alleviation measures taken along the Strangford Lough coastline behind the Saltwater Brig public house and surrounding properties.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the inspection and maintenance of approximately 26 kilometres of sea defences in the North.  However, these sea defences do not include the measures taken along the Strangford Lough coastline behind the Saltwater Brig public house and surrounding properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions she is taking to provide support for sporting organisations to be able to afford increasing electricity and heating costs in 2022/2023.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to add the Saltwater Brig area of the Strangford Lough Coastline to the Rivers Agency hydrometric section, to ensure monitoring of the water levels given the increasing flooding in the area.
answer_text: My Department currently monitors the water levels at the northern and southern ends of Strangford Lough by stations located at Portaferry Road, Newtownards and at the Quoile Barrier, Downpatrick.The station at Portaferry Road Newtownards notifies my officials when predetermined thresholds are breached allowing time to prepare for any flood response as appropriate. My Department also receives daily information from the Met Office on coastal surges and high tides and this information is disseminated to relevant agencies to allow an appropriate flood response. There are no plans to add further monitoring stations at any other locations around Strangford Lough.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions she is taking to provide support for (i) museums; and (ii) heritage sites to help them to afford increasing electricity and heating costs in 2022/2023.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact across our community. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.My Department will do all it can to support, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions she is taking to provide support for (i) artists; (ii) arts organisations; and (iii) community arts facilities to help them to be able to afford increasing electricity and heating costs in 2022/2023.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions his Department is taking to ensure home and business insurance is available for coastal communities where sea or Lough flooding is becoming a more common occurrence.
answer_text: My officials have worked closely with the Association of British Insurers (ABI) in relation to insurance and flood risk issues. The UK Government and members of the ABI operate Flood Re, which is a joint initiative established in 2014 and is effectively a not-for-profit flood insurance scheme. Its aim is to make the flood cover element of household insurance policies more affordable, particularly to householders whose homes are in areas at high risk of flooding. Flood Re has reported that 3,047 households in the North benefitted from the scheme in 2020. The Flood Re scheme does not include businesses, which require commercial insurance which is often bespoke and based on the individual nature of the business.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department could request the Drainage Council to consider a flood defence scheme at the Strangford Lough coastline of the Ards Peninsula, to protect the homes and businesses of the upper Ards Peninsula from being cut off from essential services.
answer_text: My Department has developed the 2nd Cycle Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) 2021 -2027 which has highlighted the most significant areas of risk in the North from rivers, the sea and surface water.  The Ards Peninsula has not been identified, through that work, as an area of potentially significant flood risk and there are presently no plans to carry out any additional works in this area. However, my officials have worked with partner organisations to develop a coastal flooding emergency response plan to detail the arrangements for a co-ordinated multi-agency emergency response to anywhere coastal flooding may occur here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a copy of the maintenance schedule for the drain located on the Strangford Lough coastal wall behind the Saltwater Brig public house and surrounding properties.
answer_text: The Cookstown Drain located on the Strangford Lough coastal wall behind the Saltwater Brig public house is maintained on a four year cycle.  The last inspection on 25 November 2020 highlighted the need for maintenance work.  The works were programmed to be carried out during the 2021/22 financial year. However, at that time, the landowners did not allow access for the works to be carried out. My officials will contact the landowners again with a view to including the previously identified works in this year's works programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how often is the map which his Department produce to evidence the asset value along the Strangford Constituency coastline, including around Strangford Lough, is updated; and (ii) where this map can be accessed by the public.
answer_text: My Department does not hold a map detailing the asset value along the Strangford Lough coastline.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions his Department is taking to ensure bids to the Contracts for Difference are submitted in time for NI to claim part of the available £20 million per year.
answer_text: The CfD scheme is a GB scheme and does not extend to Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what actions he is taking alone, or in partnership with other Departments, to map potential offshore wind turbine sites for companies to bid for.
answer_text: My Department maintains the DAERA Marine Mapviewer which brings together a range of marine related spatial datasets to support marine related decision making. As set out in the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022, the Department for Economy (DfE) is bringing forward an action plan to deliver 1GW of offshore wind from 2030 in Northern Ireland (NI) waters. DfE is leading an Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group and working with key partners (DfE, DAERA, Department for Infrastructure, The Crown Estate, Utility Regulator, the electricity networks, and RenewableNI) to address the barriers and accelerate offshore wind deployment in NI to happen by 2030 if practicably possible. This involves the commissioning and direction of an update to the Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulations Appraisal for the NI marine area that was completed in 2012 by consultant firms AECOM and Metoc. As part of this update work, key resource areas for offshore wind development will be identified. In parallel with the work of the OREAP Steering Group, an Offshore Renewable Energy Forum (OREF) has been established. The OREF will engage with wider stakeholder groups such as environmental organisations, local communities and industries that will be impacted by offshore wind development in NI to gain their input and engagement with the development of the OREAP. Therefore, alongside the environmental and habitats impact assessments, thorough stakeholder engagement is taking place to ensure that offshore wind projects are sited in the best places for Northern Ireland’s communities and businesses, and the environment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what investment has been made to the Centre for Advanced Sustainable Energy to ensure NI has trade representation at the Ocean Energy Europe event.
answer_text: In 2019 the Centre for Advanced Sustainable Energy (CASE) received a further offer of support totalling up to £3.6 million through the Invest NI Competence Centre Programme, to cover the period up to 2024. In March 2022 CASE was selected to manage a £4.5 million Green Innovation Challenge Fund pilot on behalf of the Department for the Economy. Budget is available through these funds to attend relevant events as deemed appropriate by CASE.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of an Off Shore Energy Strategy.
answer_text: My Department is leading an Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan (OREAP) Steering Group.  We are working with key partners to address the barriers and accelerate offshore wind deployment in Northern Ireland to happen by 2030 if practicably possible.As the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2022 sets out, the OREAP will deliver 1GW of offshore wind from 2030.We will publish the roadmap to Offshore Renewable Energy deployment for Northern Ireland this year, and a more detailed Action Plan in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will review the gap in service between primary and secondary care providers, given that patients are going private for ear wax removal when National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines states this should be done in primary community care settings.
To ask the Minister of Health for a breakdown of the waiting lists for first appointments with an audiology service for each Trust, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: This information is not held centrally so the information was requested from the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. The HSC Trusts advised that completed waits for hearing aid fitting is already available to the Department of Health, but anything else would require significant resources and therefore only be supplied at disproportionate cost, or in some HSC Trusts that the information is not recorded as required. The Department collects and publishes community audiology waiting times for hearing aid fittings annually. The Audiology completed waits 2021/22 report was published on 23rd May 2022 and is available and published at the link provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to confirm (i) how many audiology consultants are employed by each Trust; and (ii) the number of hours per week for first appointments for audiology services are provided per Trust.
answer_text: Audiologists are not doctors but a separate category of healthcare professional, therefore audiology consultant is not a term used within the Health and Social Care (HSC) system. Information about audiology services has been provided by each HSC Trust as below. Belfast HSC Trust:(i) 15.6 whole time equivalent (WTE) audiology staff (including 2.55 WTE associate and trainee associate audiologists) and 10 ENT consultants.  (ii) 12 hours for audiology and 60 hours for ENT consultants per week (p/w), excluding audiology cover for ENT consultant-led clinics.South Eastern HSC Trust: (i) 14.22 WTE audiology staff.(ii) Average 130-160 hours p/w, excluding audiology cover for ENT consultant-led clinics.Southern HSC Trust: (i) 14.5WTE audiology staff (including 0.6WTE associate audiologist and 2.0 WTE trainee associate audiologists). (ii) Average of 140 hours p/w, equivalent to 39% of audiologist time.Western HSC Trust: (i) 10.87 WTE audiologists bands 4-8A, plus one band 4 pending a start date.(ii) 73.5 Trust audiology sessions p/w; 37% relate to new appointments.Northern HSC Trust: (i) 14.3 WTE audiologists bands 4–8A.(ii) On average 24% of clinical time for adults and 20% of clinical time for paediatrics p/w relate to new audiology appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department requires primary care providers to provide alternatives to telephone triage for people who are deaf or hard of hearing; and ii) who monitors the accessibility of primary care services in each Trust.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of ensuring that our HSC facilities and services, including primary care services, are accessible to everyone who needs to use them.  GP practices in Northern Ireland are expected to address need within the requirements of equality, disability, human rights and data protection legislation and, as independent contractors, each practice can determine how best to do this within their local context.The Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) rewards contractors for the provision of quality care and helps to standardise improvements in the delivery of primary medical services. The 2022/23 Framework includes an indicator and guidance to support improved access for patients with hearing loss which includes the development and maintenance of a register of patients who have hearing loss and the adoption of a consistent approach to identify, record, flag and share relevant information with other healthcare providers as appropriate.The Guidance asks practices to review their access arrangements for patients who are deaf or who have hearing loss and provides advice for good practice.  The Guidance also asks practices to consider offering a range of different methods to contact the surgery eg text messaging, email and Video Relay Service (VRS).Funding for equipment has also been provided for surgery Jayex boards and T loops (audio induction) which will improve the quality of access for people with hearing difficulties.GP practices have received information in relation to communication support for patients who are deaf and hard of hearing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what actions are being taken by his Department to bring home heating oil within the scope of the Utility Regulator.
answer_text: Given the recognised competitive market in home heating oil in Northern Ireland, there is currently no consideration being given to regulating the sector.			If we were to take such intervention, it would require primary legislation to establish the regulatory framework.  The Competition and Markets Authority has jurisdiction to investigate and take action to address anti-competitive practices in this market. In terms of consumer protection against unfair trading practices, my Department’s Trading Standards Services (TSS) has the power to investigate complaints of this nature, and can take enforcement action where consumer protection regulations have been breached, including around the sale and delivery of home heating oil. 		It is also worth noting that price controls will do nothing to tackle the underlying causes of oil price rises as the price regulator would have to allow the pass-through of legitimate input costs, most notably the crude oil price. Additionally, the costs of regulation, including administrative and other indirect costs, could add further upward pressure on consumer’s bills.				The Northern Ireland Energy Strategy, “The Path to Net Zero Energy”, sets out a pathway to decarbonising our society and gradually phasing out fossil fuels.  This will ensure we reach our net zero targets and protect consumers by decoupling our sources of heating from the global fossil fuel markets, which are subject to price shocks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to amend Regulation 8(b) of the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 to allow for an MOT test to be booked more than three months in advance.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA)  has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.Extending the window for customers to book a test in advance of their MOT expiry date would increase demand on the service reducing the availability of appointments for those customers who require one more urgently.  Therefore, the Department has no plans to amend Regulation 8(b) of the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 to allow for an MOT test to be booked more than three months in advance.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Supporting People funding.
answer_text: Due to the continued absence of a functioning Executive, a 2022/23 budget cannot currently be approved, therefore the Housing Executive has been allocated an indicative budget.  £72.8m has been allocated for 2022/23 to fund the Supporting People Programme. This is consistent with the baseline funding allocation of previous financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 644/22-27, to list the seven churches that the Education Authority paid rent for between April 2016 to 31 March 2022.
answer_text: The names of the seven churches that the Education Authority paid rent for between April 2016 to 31 March 2022 are as follows:1          First Presbyterian Church Donaghadee 2          Trustees of Straid Congregational Church 3          St Lukes Church Loughgall4          Desertmartin Parish Church 5          St Columb's Diocesan Trust 6          Magheralin Parish Church 7          First Islandmagee Presbyterian Church

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for the level of premia payments made to (i) Stranmillis University College; and (ii) St Mary's University College per year, over the last five years.
answer_text: The level of premia payments made to Stranmillis University College and St Mary's University College for the last five academic years are outlined in the table below.			Academic Year		2017/18 	2018/19	2019/20	2020/21	2021/22	Stranmillis University College	£1,106,612	£1,126,822	£1,119,280	£1,172,292	£1,187,078	St Mary's University College	£1,121,231	£1,132,963	£1,118,707	£1,197,318	£1,214,072

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when will the MOT Test Centre in Newtownards be closed for refurbishment.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has no plans to close Newtownards test centre for refurbishment during this financial year.  The DVA is developing a programme of work to refresh existing vehicle testing equipment across its network, which will require the staggered closure of tests centres.  Once the programme is agreed with the equipment provider, and the dates of test centre closures are confirmed, this will be communicated to customers through the normal channels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the evidence required to demonstrate eligibility for the highest mobility rate of Disability Living Allowance, under the severe mental impairment criteria, for a child with Down Syndrome.
answer_text: Legislation does not define what evidence is required to support applications for Disability Living Allowance. However, people are encouraged to provide any evidence they have on their health condition or disability and how it impacts their daily life, in terms of care needs and mobility restrictions.   		Legislation stipulates that an award of the higher rate of the mobility component under the severe mental impairment criteria can only be made if the child is severely mentally impaired, displays severe behavioural problems and is entitled to the highest rate of the care component.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what training staff receive on the assessment of Disability Living Allowance for children who have Down Syndrome.
answer_text: Decision Makers within Disability and Carers Service are extensively trained on the eligibility criteria for Disability Living Allowance and how to apply it when deciding claims. They also have access to online guidance, developed by leading medical experts, which provides the most up to date information on medical conditions. Health Professionals are also available to provide a daily advice service to Disability Living Allowance Decision Makers.		Officials are consistently striving to improve their knowledge and understanding of all aspects of their work and opportunities for improvement are always kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the policy which takes into consideration mental capacity when assessing the mobility of a Disability Living Allowance claimant with Down Syndrome.
answer_text: Entitlement to the mobility component of Disability Living Allowance depends on the effects that a disability or health condition has on a child’s ability to walk, and not on the particular disability or diagnosis. Every claim is determined in line with the assessment criteria, relevant legislation and information provided.In addition to where a child is unable to walk or virtually unable to walk, the mobility component is also payable where a child is able to walk but needs extra guidance or supervision when outdoors; or is severely mentally impaired with severe behavioural problems and qualifies for the highest rate of the care component.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether staff who determine Disability Living Allowance awards for children with Down Syndrome are health professionals; and (ii) for a breakdown of the type of medical professional that determines these awards.
answer_text: Decisions on entitlement to Disability Living Allowance are made by Decision Makers appointed by the Department. These Decision Makers are civil servants and not health professionals but are extensively trained in Disability Living Allowance legislation and how to apply the eligibility criteria when determining claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many children who have Down Syndrome are currently in receipt of Disability Living Allowance; and (ii) how many are on the (a) standard; and (b) higher rate.
answer_text: As at February 2022 (latest published figures) there were 700 Disability Living Allowance recipients aged under 17 with Down Syndrome. The table below provides the breakdown of awards in these cases.		Rate of Award	No.	Higher rate care only	80	Middle rate care only	40	Lower rate care only	*	Lower rate mobility only	*	Higher rate care and higher rate mobility	350	Higher rate care and lower rate mobility	110	Middle rate care and higher rate mobility	50	Middle rate care and lower rate mobility	70	Total	700	Figures are rounded to the nearest 10.	*Less than 5

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has collated a breakdown of the reasons for last year's 45 per cent increase in homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing Executive notes that the 45% increase referred to at the All Party Group on Homelessness was referring to a specific reason for presenting as homelessness rather than an overall increase. There has been a significant increase in presentations due to loss of rented accommodation with 1,689 presentations in 2020/21 compared with 2,463 presentations in 2021/22, an increase of 45.8%. A key driver in this increase has been an increase in the sub-category loss of private rented accommodation due to property sale with 636 presentations in 2020/21 compared with 1,380 presentations in 2021/22, an increase of 117%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work is being done by her Department to assess the impact of the cost of living crisis on housing and homelessness.
answer_text: The current situation is uncertain for all concerned including those experiencing homelessness. The absence of a functioning Executive and no budget in place makes the challenge of dealing with these issues all the greater.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many households in the private rented sector are in receipt of Local Housing Allowance, through universal credit housing costs and through housing benefit; (ii) how many of those households have rents that exceed the Local Housing Allowance; and (iii) of these households what is the median average gap between the rent and the Local Housing Allowance, for each broad rental market area, for the most recent period for which data is available.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised with regard to Housing Benefit:There are 27,096 Private Sector tenants in receipt of Housing Benefit that are assessed under Local Housing Allowance rules.In relation to rent exceeding the Local Housing Allowance, the Housing Executive is unable to obtain the exact numbers although believe the vast majority of claims will have a shortfall.The Housing Executive are unable to answer this question as they are unable to extract details of claims that have a shortfall between rent charged by the landlord and that eligible under Local Housing Allowance rules. The Department can advise that at the end of March 2022:(i) There were 32,673 households in the Private rented sector in receipt of Universal Credit(ii) Of these, 26,797 were in receipt of an LHA-capped housing payment that was lower than their declared rent(iii) The median gap between rent and LHA for each of the broad rental market areas is shown below: Broad Rental Market Area           Median gapBelfast                                        	£93.05Lough Neagh Lower                    	£105.84Lough Neagh Upper                     	£96.08North                                             	£92.86North West                                   	£97.76South                                             	£111.23South East                                     	£101.97South West                                   	£108.33

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people in housing stress in the Strangford constituency broken down by (i) age; (ii) gender; and (iii) length of time on the housing waiting list.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 2489/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress of the Year 1 actions set out in the Cancer Recovery Plan, published on 24th June 2021, including the actions have been implemented and any slippage that has occurred.
answer_text: The Cancer Recovery Plan ‘Building Back; Rebuilding better’, that focused on recovering from the impact of COVID-19 has been superseded by the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032.Officials in my Department are currently in process of progressing the key actions as outlined in the Cancer Strategy and delivery will be dependent on budget availability. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care and as Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.Despite the current and significant challenges in 2021, I introduced a grant scheme using COVID-19 funding of £10.1 million. This funding will afford cancer charities the opportunity to bid under a number of criteria which are aligned with recommendations in the Cancer Recovery Plan and the Cancer Strategy. The grant is managed through the Community Foundation Northern Ireland and between April 2021 and March 2022 there were 21 grants awarded at a total of £8.1million.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are being considered to improve road safety at the crossroads at Ballyblack Road East, Ballyblack Road and Grangee Road, Carrowdore.
answer_text: I am aware that there have been some minor collisions at this location over a number of years and my Department has implemented a number of collision remedial measures including provision of enhanced warning and directional signs, advanced slow road markings and high friction surfacing, all of which combine to enhance driver awareness of and safety on the approaches to this junction.Although my Department has no current plans to implement further measures at this junction, I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the junction and to implement any further safety measures that are considered appropriate.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the report on the Independent Review of Welfare Mitigations will be published.
answer_text: The Welfare Mitigations independent advisory panel have presented their final report, including recommendations for new mitigation measures, to the Department. The report will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many Blue Badges are currently issued in Northern Ireland, broken down by council area.
answer_text: My Department has issued 121,273 Blue Badges in the North, although as the system does not record the Council area of the applicant, the information cannot be provided by Council area.The Blue Badge Unit can provide details of the number of badges issued by postcode. If that would be useful you can request it by emailing: bluebadges@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Department makes a payment to GPs if they are consulted as part of their patient’s application for a Blue Badge.
answer_text: My Department makes a payment of £22.85 for every Blue Badge GP Questionnaire that is satisfactorily completed and returned to the Blue Badge Unit for assessment as part of the Blue Badge application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current time from application to issue of Blue Badges in NI.
answer_text: During the period from 1 April 2022 to 30 June 2022 my Department’s Blue Badge Unit made decisions on 12,269 applications with 12,243 being made within the target date of 40 working days which equates to 99.8%.The Blue Badge Unit are currently working on applications which were received during week commencing 20 June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether people in receipt of Carers Allowance, who are not on other benefits, will receive a Cost of Living Payment.
answer_text: The package of measures announced by the Chancellor does not include a payment to those in receipt of Carer’s Allowance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans are in place to ensure women’s centres will continue to be funded.
answer_text: In the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget, all options are being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of house repossessions by mortgage lenders in each council area, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information. 		UK Finance holds information regarding mortgages and publishes quarterly data regarding mortgage arrears and possessions – see link: https://www.ukfinance.org.uk/data-and-research/data/arrears-and-possessionsThe Department of Justice (DoJ) publishes a quarterly bulletin on mortgage possessions which provides statistics for cases received (i.e. writs and originating summonses issued), cases disposed and final orders made in respect of mortgages in the Chancery Division of the High Court. The most recent bulletin covers the period from January to March 2022 – see link: https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/justice/mortgages-bulletin-january-to-march-2022_0.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many households are spending 30 per cent or more of their income on housing costs.
answer_text: In the financial year 2019/20, it was estimated that 8% of households had weekly housing costs that accounted for 30% or more of weekly household income. This estimate is based on data from the annual Family Resources Survey (FRS). The next edition of the FRS, covering the survey year of 2020/21 is due to be released in Autumn 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the additional payments announced by the Chancellor to help people on benefits with the cost of living, will be disregarded and not treated as income when calculating those benefits.
answer_text: The additional payments announced will not have any impact on existing means-tested social security benefit awards (including Universal Credit) or tax credits either as “income” or “capital”. These payments will be tax-free and will not count towards the benefit cap.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what financial support he will be providing to community transport organisations to help them continue to deliver Rural Transport Fund objectives, while fuel prices are increasing.
answer_text: I recently met with representatives of some of the rural community transport providers funded by my Department to discuss the challenges they are facing including the continuing rises in fuel costs and the impact this has on the delivery of Dial-a-Lift.  I fully appreciate and understand the real value and impact of this service ensuring that some of the most vulnerable people in our society are able to access essential local services and more actively participate in society Although I have issued interim 6 month grant awards my Department is facing significant resource pressures in 2022-23, impacted by a decade of austerity budgets from Westminster, the unprecedented increases in energy costs and rising inflation. The refusal of the DUP to form an Executive means over £420m of funds being held at the centre cannot be released and I therefore cannot bid for additional in year funding. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment. However, I am determined to do all I can to protect these services and my Department will continue to work closely with the community transport organisations to address their concerns and consider how best we can support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to provide an update on the Residents' Parking Schemes; and (ii) whether there are plans to extend to other areas.
answer_text: I can confirm that the review of residents’ parking schemes is complete and I plan to make the report public once I have had the opportunity to discuss its findings with my officials.  The review report will set out the lessons learnt from the Rugby Road scheme in Belfast, and the other schemes that did not make it to implementation, and will be used to establish the policy for such schemes going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to update the section 10B community transport minibus permit to match the section 19 permit used in GB.
answer_text: In 2019 the High Court handed down a judgment in respect of Section 19 Permits regarding the meaning of “non-commercial” and the guidance published by the Department for Transport in GB.  My officials are currently reviewing this decision, after which I will consider the arrangements here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions he is taking to address the backlog in MOT tests across NI.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.Official vehicle testing statistics are published on a monthly basis and can be accessed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics.  The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many cars are now untaxed as they have been unable to get an MOT appointment due to the backlog.
answer_text: Vehicle Excise Duty (Motor Tax) is an excepted matter and is administered and enforced by the Driver & Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA) in Swansea on behalf of HM Treasury. The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) does not hold information on how many vehicles are now untaxed waiting on an MOT appointment. MLAs, acting on behalf of constituents, can contact the DVLA directly through a dedicated email address at DVLAMinisterials@dvla.gov.uk. The DVA has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what progress has been made on the proposed designation of the East Coast (Northern Ireland) and Carlingford Lough as Special Protection Areas.
answer_text: Nature conservation in Northern Ireland is a devolved matter with the exception of the marine environment, where some functions are reserved matters. Therefore, decisions taken on marine SPAs require the consideration and approval of the Secretary of State for DEFRA.I am pleased to confirm that both the proposed East Coast (NI) Marine SPA and proposed marine extension to the existing Carlingford Lough SPA have been referred to the Secretary of State for DEFRA seeking his approval for their classification.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the process for formulating the first Sectoral Plan for Fisheries, as outlined in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My Department is in the early stages of planning the formulation of the first Climate Action Plan for Northern Ireland. Consideration is being given to the design principles and it is intended that development of the draft Climate Action Plan for Northern Ireland, which will include the fisheries sectoral plan, will begin in August 2022. The Climate Change Act requires departments to consult with relevant sector-specific advisory groups and these will include representatives from the fisheries sector. It is anticipated that the draft Climate Action Plan will be published for public consultation in March/April 2023.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he plans to publish the first Sectoral Plan for Fisheries, as outlined in the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 does not specify a specific timeframe for developing the first sectoral plans however the intention is that sectoral plans will be included within Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan. The Climate Action Plan is required to be laid before the Assembly by the end of December 2023 and the sectoral plan for fisheries will be taken forward in line with the overall approach to and timeline for producing the Climate Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) number of households in housing arrears to the Housing Executive; and (ii) the average household arrears, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency and council area.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 1567/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what actions are being considered to reduce families' dependency on foodbanks.
answer_text: The Department has been running a Social Supermarket Pilot Programme since 2017.  Following a positive evaluation of the programme and working on a co-design basis with local Councils, the Social Supermarket model will be rolled out. A Social Supermarket provides holistic support through the provision of food alongside wraparound services to address the root causes of poverty. This includes, but is not limited to, debt and benefits advice, budgeting, healthy eating, cooking on a budget, training and volunteering opportunities to enhance future employability.  I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced, which could help to reduce dependency on foodbanks. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need.  I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Anti-Poverty Strategy will include actions to end the use of food banks and the food crisis.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work on the co-design of the Executive’s Anti-Poverty Strategy. I am committed to delivering long-term, sustainable solutions to poverty in all its forms, including food insecurity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what protections are in place to ensure that, where there is a conflict of interest between an organisation being investigated and one or more Charity Commissioners, appropriate Departmental processes are in place to ensure openness and transparency is maintained and that any person with a potential or confirmed conflict of interest is not involved in the process.
answer_text: Charity Commissioners agree to abide by the seven principles of public life and agree to declare any personal or business interests which may, or may be perceived to, influence their judgement when performing their duties as a member of the Commission. Failure to do so could lead to their appointment being withdrawn.The Commission’s Code of Conduct and the Standing Orders for Board meetings are publicly available on the Commission’s website.  These require that Commissioners must abide by the rules of the Commission in handling conflicts of interest, including that all interests are made publicly available and maintained on the Commission’s website. Should a specific issue arise through the course of a Commissioner’s work that could give rise to a potential conflict, they are required to disclose their interest and withdraw from any discussion or consideration of the matter. The Commission has committed to a review of its approach to conflicts of interest flowing from recommendations of a number of recent governance reviews.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the reasons behind the absence of a standardised approach and curriculum for Relationship and Sexuality Education in schools across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age and it is the responsibility of the Board of Governors of each school to ensure that a comprehensive programme is delivered which meets the needs of its pupils.The curriculum gives schools flexibility to adapt what they teach and use a variety of approaches and curricular resources that reflect, and are responsive to, the learning needs of their pupils.  This ensures that young people are both safe and well-informed about all of the issues they face in modern society.Providing detailed lists of compulsory subject content within a curriculum does not in any way guarantee that a subject will be well or appropriately taught.   Further, in an area such as RSE, prescribed content may rapidly become out of date or new issues may emerge.  A wide range of resources have been developed and are available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s RSE Hub which will include the publication of an RSE Progression Framework.  This approach is designed to improve confidence and capacity in the teaching of RSE.  I have asked my officials to prepare additional guidelines for schools setting out the importance of ensuring the issues highlighted by the Gillen Review, including serious sexual offences and consent, are covered within the Curriculum.  This will be issued before the end of the current school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what progress is being made to create a standardised approach to Relationship and Sexuality Education; and (ii) whether pupils are being consulted as part of this process.
answer_text: Relationships and Sexuality Education (RSE) is mandatory for all pupils of compulsory school age and it is the responsibility of the Board of Governors of each school to ensure that a comprehensive programme is delivered which meets the needs of its pupils.The curriculum gives schools flexibility to adapt what they teach and use a variety of approaches and curricular resources that reflect, and are responsive to, the learning needs of their pupils.  This ensures that young people are both safe and well-informed about all of the issues they face in modern society.Providing detailed lists of compulsory subject content within a curriculum does not in any way guarantee that a subject will be well or appropriately taught.   Further, in an area such as RSE, prescribed content may rapidly become out of date or new issues may emerge.  A wide range of resources have been developed and are available on the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment’s RSE Hub which will include the publication of an RSE Progression Framework.  This approach is designed to improve confidence and capacity in the teaching of RSE.  I have asked my officials to prepare additional guidelines for schools setting out the importance of ensuring the issues highlighted by the Gillen Review, including serious sexual offences and consent, are covered within the Curriculum.  This will be issued before the end of the current school year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action will she take to ensure schools provide free period products to pupils in schools as part of the Period Dignity scheme.
answer_text: School engagement with the “Piot Scheme to Address Period Dignity in Schools” was significant in 2021/22.  Eighty three percent of the budget was utilised.  70% of grammar schools, 82% of secondary schools and 89% of special schools placed orders by the end of March 2022.  All primary schools either placed orders or received a stock allocation.  The scheme has been welcomed by 98.2% of pupils (1,680) and 98.8% (760) of teachers responding to CCEA’s baseline survey in June 2020. A follow up survey in January 2022 also received high response levels (1,722 pupils and 391 teachers), demonstrating a high level of engagement with addressing the issue of period dignity in schools.In March 2022, the Department of Finance Supply Team approved expenditure for the pilot scheme to continue until June 2024. Based on the evidence gathered in the early stages of the scheme, DoF Supply was content that the need for the intervention had been demonstrated. Total budget for 2022/23 is £643k.CCEA’s activity in 2022/23 will include: building its database of Period Dignity Champions; further Teacher Professional Learning opportunities; revising guidance to include Northern Ireland based case studies of best practice in implementing the scheme; developing educational resources for primary, post-primary and special schools; and promotional activity, including social media, videos and posters.  CCEA will also issue an annual teacher and pupil questionnaire towards the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the uptake of the Period Dignity scheme within schools.
answer_text: School engagement with the “Piot Scheme to Address Period Dignity in Schools” was significant in 2021/22.  Eighty three percent of the budget was utilised.  70% of grammar schools, 82% of secondary schools and 89% of special schools placed orders by the end of March 2022.  All primary schools either placed orders or received a stock allocation.  The scheme has been welcomed by 98.2% of pupils (1,680) and 98.8% (760) of teachers responding to CCEA’s baseline survey in June 2020. A follow up survey in January 2022 also received high response levels (1,722 pupils and 391 teachers), demonstrating a high level of engagement with addressing the issue of period dignity in schools.In March 2022, the Department of Finance Supply Team approved expenditure for the pilot scheme to continue until June 2024. Based on the evidence gathered in the early stages of the scheme, DoF Supply was content that the need for the intervention had been demonstrated. Total budget for 2022/23 is £643k.CCEA’s activity in 2022/23 will include: building its database of Period Dignity Champions; further Teacher Professional Learning opportunities; revising guidance to include Northern Ireland based case studies of best practice in implementing the scheme; developing educational resources for primary, post-primary and special schools; and promotional activity, including social media, videos and posters.  CCEA will also issue an annual teacher and pupil questionnaire towards the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people waited more than (i) 4 hours; (ii) 8 hours; or (iii) 12 hours, from admission to treatment, in Accident and Emergency departments in each Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department currently monitors the time waited, from arrival to discharge, for attendances at Emergency departments against the four and 12 hour waiting time targets. Information on patients who waited more than 8 hours is not recorded. Performance at each Emergency department is published on a quarterly basis at the link below:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-waiting-time-statistics-emergency-care-waiting-times-january-march-2022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to support the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service to meet the target for response for life threatening, category 1 calls and emergency calls and ensure the safe delivery of patients to Emergency Departments.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 2390/17-22, to outline the reasons for the difference between the ADHD diagnosis wait times in different Health and Social Care Trusts, in particular the difference in waiting times for an appointment in the Northern Trust and the Belfast Trust.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether registration of birth forms could include a tick-box option for parents to declare in which education sector they wish their child to be educated.
answer_text: The purpose of a birth registration is to provide a permanent and official record of a child’s birth and is information on a life event at a point in time.  The registration of the birth enables a birth certificate to be issued to the parents that will be required for various purposes throughout the child’s life and into adulthood, such as for claiming benefits, registration for school or opening a bank account.   It would not be appropriate to include a tick-box on the birth registration form for parents to declare in which education sector they wish their child to be educated as gathering this information would not be necessary for the birth registration and would be outside the role of the civil registration service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has any plans to review and improve benefits assessors' knowledge and understanding of children with Down Syndrome.
answer_text: Decision Makers within Disability and Carers Service are extensively trained on the eligibility criteria for Disability Living Allowance and how to apply it when deciding claims. They also have access to online guidance, developed by leading medical experts, which provides the most up to date information on medical conditions, including Down’s Syndrome and the likely effects it would have on a person’s care and mobility needs. Health Professionals are also available to provide a daily advice service to Disability Living Allowance Decision Makers. 		In addition, senior officials from Disability and Carers Service met with the campaign group ‘Families with Ups and Downs’ in December 2021 to hear first-hand and address any concerns they had in relation to the administration of Disability Living Allowance. 		My Officials are consistently striving to improve their knowledge and understanding of all aspects of their work and opportunities for improvement are always kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has any plans to introduce a standardised Disability Living Allowance award system for children with Down Syndrome.
answer_text: Entitlement to DLA depends on the extent to which a child needs help with personal care, requires supervision or has difficulty getting around; and not on a particular disability or diagnosis. The amount of additional care and supervision a disabled child needs compared to that for a non-disabled child of similar age is taken into consideration. Every claim is determined in line with the assessment criteria, relevant legislation and information provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will review the transition from Disability Living Allowance to Personal Independence Payments for young people with Down Syndrome, to ensure it doesn’t cause any undue stress.
answer_text: My Department aims to provide a quality service for all people transitioning from Disability Living Allowance to Personal Independence Payment (PIP) that causes the least amount of stress, irrespective of their age, disability or health condition. This process is subject to ongoing review and enhancement

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has plans to review the rollout of smart meters in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – Path to Net Zero Energy - Action plan published in January 2022 commits to delivering the following action this year:Action Plan 20: “Carry out a cost benefit analysis of electricity and gas smart meters and other technologies in order to access half-hourly and daily consumption information”.Planning for the Cost Benefit Analysis is underway with the intention of completion by the end of this year. I will review the recommendations thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has any plans to review the benefits system to ensure that Disability Living Allowance awards for children with Down Syndrome are never reassessed.
answer_text: There are measures in place in each of the benefit areas to ensure that people are not required to undertake unnecessary reviews.  These processes are subject to ongoing review and enhancement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has any plans to ensure children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs are included in a school’s total pupil numbers instead of being referred to as supernumerary.
answer_text: My Department did examine this issue some years ago with the managing authorities and sectoral body representatives and consulted with schools and Trades Union Side (TUS) on a draft proposal.  This work was discontinued as factors such as annual growth or decline in pupil numbers at individual schools and the increasing number of children presenting with statements resulted in changeable modelling outcomes from year-to-year.  This would have created difficulties for schools to manage any flexibilities they had around approved enrolments and how they could manage available places and in the assessment of suitable school sizes when planning new builds or extensions to existing school stock.  There are no plans to review this practice at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of how much rent has been paid for (i) land; and (ii) premises belonging to churches in Northern Ireland, over the last 10 years.
answer_text: The Education Authority paid rent of £172,812 for land/premises owned by churches for the period 1 April 2016 to 31 March 2022. Details are not available for the period prior to April 2016, and it has not been possible to break this figure down further between land and premises, as rents for land and premises are not separated but are coded jointly to a single finance code. The records show that the payments were made to seven churches for land/premises owned by them.
