question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Local Housing Allowance was last reviewed; and when it will next be reviewed.
answer_text: In November 2021 the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions announced that the rates would be frozen at the rate determined for 2020/21 and would remain at this level in future years.  I wrote to Therese Coffrey, Secretary of State for Work and Pensions, on 4 February 2022 requesting that urgent consideration be given to increasing the rates. I will continue to urge Secretary of State for Work and Pensions to reconsider and increase LHA rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new social homes that will be built in the Strangford constituency in (i) 2022; and (ii) 2023, broken down by type of house and number of bedrooms.
answer_text: 79 new social housing homes are currently under construction in the Strangford parliamentary constituency, with an anticipated practical completion date in 2022 or 2023. 32 new social housing homes are currently programmed to start through the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) in 2022/23 and 2023/24 in the Strangford parliamentary constituency. Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to outline the criteria that is used to place a child with a Statement of Special Educational Needs in a post-primary school; and (ii) whether that process takes into account the physical space needed for both the child and their classroom assistant.
answer_text: Where the Education Authority (EA) makes a statement in a case where the parent of a child concerned has expressed a preference for a grant-aided school pursuant to Article 44 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 and Paragraph 5 of Schedule 2 to the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, the EA shall specify the name of that school in the statement unless:The school is unsuitable to the child’s age, ability or aptitude or to his special educational needs, or; The attendance of the child at the school would be incompatible with the provision of efficient education for the children with whom he would be educated or the efficient use of resources.The legislation is not drafted in such a way as to allow for consideration of the collective effect or impact of the admission of a number of pupils with statements of SEN on resources, including physical space, or on the efficient instruction of other children.  Nor is the legislation drafted in such a way as to allow any further filtering of admissions, beyond that which is expressly set out above.Children with a Statement of SEN are ‘supernumerary’ to the enrolment numbers and admissions numbers fixed by the Department for each school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many of the 244 pupils who were not allocated a post-primary place had (i) a statement of special educational needs; and (ii) a reported medical need.
answer_text: The post-primary admissions process places children who do not have a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN); children who have a statement are placed through a different process.  As a result, none of the 244 children who were initially not allocated a school place held a statement.  Figures on the number of children who have a reported medical need is not collected as part of the admissions process.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when a facility to provide subtitles during Assembly debates and Committee meetings will be available to Members via Starleaf.
answer_text: The StarLeaf system used by Members to make remote contributions to Committee meetings and Plenary sittings does not currently support automatic captioning and has no plans to do so. Assembly Commission officials will continue to monitor developments with the current supplier and the wider market, to determine the feasibility of ‘real time’ subtitling Assembly debates and committee meetings for Members.It is however worth noting that continuous transmission of Assembly proceedings is now available via the headphones in the Chamber during Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation guidance for booster jabs includes unpaid carers in the same category as paid carers.
To ask the Minister of Education what action she is taking to provide mental health support to the 244 children who were not provided with a post-primary school place.
answer_text: Emotional health and wellbeing is high priority for my Department and this has been even more so the case since the pandemic which has significantly impacted on the lives of our children and young people.In February 2021, my Department and the Department of Health (DoH) published the ‘Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework’ which aims to:-Ensure children and young people are empowered and assisted to understand and take care/manage their emotional health and wellbeing.Identify needs early and address effectively when required.Establish an integrated model that supports an early help, support and intervention focus.Reduce the numbers of children and young people requiring specialist intervention from Mental Health Services.A number of initiatives under the Framework may be particularly beneficial to support children’s mental health at key points such as the transition from primary to post-primary school.  These include:-Text A Nurse service - a secure and confidential text messaging service for young people aged 11-19 providing access to a school nurse.  REACH – ‘Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen’ programme for 6-19 year olds to develop positive emotional health, increase their readiness for learning and build their resilience.Healthy Happy Minds Therapeutic and Counselling Services pilot – providing therapeutic and counselling support for primary school pupils. School pastoral care teams and the Education Authority’s (EA) Youth Service Youth Online are also available to provide emotional help and support in a safe and secure way.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission when the review of Members' rental allowance will take place; and whether that review will consider backdating the allowance to the start of this mandate.
answer_text: As noted in the replies to AQW 22266/17-22 and AQW 30680/17-22, the Assembly Commission agreed at its meeting on 8 July 2021 that any new Determination on Members’ allowances could only be published alongside progress on legislative reform of the system of setting salaries and allowances for MLAs.You will be aware that the Assembly Commission had introduced the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill in the last Assembly mandate. However, the Bill was not progressed in that mandate and it fell once the mandate ended. Therefore, a review of allowances payable to Members, to include the amounts of rent recoverable for a Member’s constituency office, can only be finalised once progress has been made on the legislative reform noted above. While the Assembly Commission remains in place following the recent Assembly election, no provision presently exists for the Assembly Commission to introduce a similar Bill to the Assembly Members (Remuneration Board) Bill in this Assembly mandate. It is envisaged that a newly appointed Assembly Commission will determine the exact scope of the review.  It is also anticipated that the review will consider a number of different aspects of the present Determination including market rates for rental payments and also the timing of the implementation of any changes that might be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the drop-out rate of pupils at Year 13 across all schools for (i) 2019/2020; (ii) 2020/2021; and (iii) 2021/2022.
answer_text: An analysis of available data in the School Leavers Survey and in the Northern Ireland School Census indicates that approximately 2.2% of Year 13 pupils had a leaving date before the end of the 2019/20 academic year. The respective figure for 2020/21 was 2.6%. Information for 2021/22 will not be available until May 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an up to date organisational chart for her Department.
answer_text: Minister - Deirdre Hargey MLAPermanent Secretary - Colum BoyleBeverley WallDeputy Secretary Strategic Policy & Professional ServicesCentral PolicyFinancial ManagementFinancial PlanningEqualityGovernance & Commercial ServicesCommunicationsPrivate OfficeMark O’DonnellHousing, Urban Regeneration and Local GovernmentRegional DevNW Dev OfficeHousing Supply PolicyHousing Policy & OversightLocal Gov & Housing RegBelfast RegenerationMoira DohertyEngaged CommunitiesCulturePRONICommunity EmpowermentHistoric EnvironmentC&VSportsPaddy Rooney(TP) Deputy Secretary Work & HealthWork & WellbeingWorking age & Universal CreditSocial Security Policy & LegislationBrenda Henderson(TP) Deputy Secretary Supporting PeopleChild MaintenancePensions DisabilityBenefits SecurityDebtInformation ServicesProperty Management

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work to modernise the Review of Old Mineral Permissions legislation.
answer_text: Work to modernise the review of old mineral permissions (ROMPs) legislation will require amendments to primary legislation to comply with the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Directive and to ensure appropriate transparency and public participation and that the full quarrying process is covered. An Executive must be in place in order to bring forward these legislative amendments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will review the planning permission for the quarry license being used at Fishquarter Quarry, Kircubbin to ensure that it covers the activities being performed at the site.
answer_text: Following the transfer of responsibility for the majority of planning functions to local councils in April 2015, it is a matter for the local council to determine whether the quarry is operating within its planning permission.It should be noted that the local council has powers to control nuisance vibrations (from blasting), noise emissions and dust from quarries under the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act 2011. These powers are separate from the council’s powers under planning legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the drop-out rate for first year students at all universities in Northern Ireland in (i) 2019/2020; (ii) 2020/2021; and (iii) 2021/2022.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect information on drop-out rates for first-year higher education students. Instead, this is collected and published by the Higher Education Statistics Agency (HESA) on its website: https://www.hesa.ac.uk/data-and-analysis/performance-indicators/non-continuation The latest available HESA data is for the 2020/21 academic year. However, data regarding non-continuation rates can only be determined the following academic year and, as such, the most recent non-continuation rates available are for first degree entrants during the 2019/2020 academic year. 			For ease of reference, Table A below shows non-continuation following year of entry of UK domiciled full-time first-degree entrants at NI Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) from 2017/18 to 2019/20. Table B shows the information for part-time first-degree entrants at NI HEIs from 2016/17 to 2018/19.  These are supplied separately, as the full-time rates are based on dropouts after one year and the part-time rates are based on dropouts after 2 years.TABLE A - UK domiciled full-time first-degree entrants at NI HEIs - 2017/18 to 2019/20 Percentage no longer in HE one year after entry   2019/202018/192017/18Institutionyear of entryyear of entryyear of entryQueen's University Belfast3.3%4.9%4.9%St Mary's University College3.9%1.2%4.4%Stranmillis University College6.1%5.8%2.4%Ulster University4.9%6.6%9.3%Total Northern Ireland4.2%5.7%7.1%TABLE B - UK domiciled part-time first-degree entrants at NI HEIs - 2016/17 to 2018/19 Percentage no longer in HE two years after entry   2018/192017/182016/17Institutionyear of entryyear of entryyear of entryQueen's University Belfast14.6%14.3%32.0%The Open University in Northern Ireland30.3%33.4%42.2%Stranmillis University College7.7%3.4%9.1%Ulster University21.4%21.7%24.4%Total Northern Ireland25.4%26.9%32.5%

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has met with HMRC to raise the need to increase basic mileage rate allowances for employees during the fuel price crisis.
answer_text: I have not met with HMRC on the topic of mileage rate allowance as this would not fall within the responsibility of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many children with a statement of special education needs are due to start (a) pre-school; (b) primary school; and (c) post-primary school; and (ii) of those children how many have yet to be provided with a place.
answer_text: As of 8 June 2022:3345 children with a statement of SEN are due to start pre-school, primary school or move to post-primary school, including:Pre-school: 211 children with a statement of SEN;Primary School: 1037 children with a statement of SEN; andPost-primary (P7-Yr8): 2097 children with a statement of SEN.Of the 3345 children with a statement of SEN in pre-school, primary and post-primary 2708 have a confirmed place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many companies in Northern Ireland do not pay their staff or volunteers the HMRC business mileage rate.
answer_text: My Department does not hold or have access to this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the underspend resulting from the Emergency Home Heating scheme administered by Bryson; and what has happened with that underspend.
answer_text: My Department made a contribution of £2 million to the scheme which was fully spent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why children with a statement of special education needs were not given notice of their secondary school on the same day as their peers; and (ii) has this policy ever been subject to an equality impact assessment.
answer_text: The transfer from primary to post primary education for children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) is managed through a separate process associated with the Annual Review of that Statement rather than the Transfer Admissions arrangements.The SEN Transfer process administered by the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) is reflective of the requirements of schools and the Education Authority (EA) to make arrangements for the Annual Review of children with SEN as prescribed in legislation, specifically the Education (NI) Order 1996 (“The 1996 Order”), the Special Educational Needs and Disability Order (SENDO) 2005 and the Education (SEN) Regulations (NI) 2005.Article 4 of “the 1996 Order” also places a duty on the EA to have regard to the provisions of the Code of Practice. For that reason, SARS also takes cognisance of the Code of Practice on the Identification and Assessment of Special Educational Needs (1998) and the Supplement to the Code of Practice (2005).The SEN Transfer process, as prescribed in legislation, consequently manages each child’s placement on a case by case basis, ensuring each child receives a suitable placement that will appropriately support and cater to their individual needs.  Children are informed once their school placement has been finalised, rather than on a fixed date.The Transfer of pupils with Statements of Special Educational Needs does not arise from an EA Policy.  This process is administered by the Statutory Assessment and Review Service in accordance with the Education (NI) Order 1996 (“The 1996 Order”), the Special Educational Needs and Disability Order (SENDO) 2005 and the Education (SEN) Regulations (NI) 2005.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many short breaks were provided to unpaid carers by each Health and Social Care Trust during the first quarter of 2022; and (ii) how this compares to the first quarter in (a) 2021; (b) 2020; and (c) 2019.
answer_text: Short Breaks remain limited as Trusts are dependent on access to respite beds in care homes, which are subject to public health guidance in relation to admissions from the community. The Public Health Agency recently issued a communication to care homes, to relax testing and admission guidelines, to improve the availability of short breaks.In addition, the Department is aware that several respite beds in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. Due to the limited availability of bed based Short Breaks over the last two years the frequency of access to this service has been determined by the social work assessment of need.  While the total number of short breaks provided to unpaid carers is not routinely collected, therefore, presented in Table 1 below is the number of individual adult carers who received a short break during the period by each Health and Social Care Trust.Table 1: The number of adult carers provided with a short break, by each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT), for the first quarter in 2019, 2020, 2021 and 2022.HSCT NameJan - Mar 2019Jan - Mar 2020Jan - Mar 2021Jan - Mar 2022Belfast1,4411,1552,1271,894Northern3,1295,4112,9612,354South Eastern1,5831,7311,4031,225Southern1,7451,7291,5991,389Western885900763637Northern Ireland8,78310,9268,8537,499Source: Quarterly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).It should be noted that the figures above are representative of the number of adults receiving a short break during the specified time period. Trusts only report on adult short break care where an adult is caring for another adult and excludes young carers or children being cared for. The information presented excludes day care activity. Additionally, please note that there may be an element of double/over counting as carers potentially can get more than one form of short break during the period. The above information is sourced predominantly from manual systems and therefore should be treated with caution as figures are subject to change.Further information on the remobilisation of learning disability services can be found on the Department’s website:	https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-services

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of the average school attendance rates in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary schools for 2021/2022.
answer_text: The following sets out the most recent average year to date school attendance rates in both primary and post-primary schools for 2021/2022.	September 2021 to April 2022 - Primary attendance rate: 91.4%	September 2021 to April 2022 - Post Primary attendance rate: 88.8%

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what actions her Department has taken to address the shortage of classroom assistants.
answer_text: The majority of Classroom Assistants working in schools in Northern Ireland are employed by the Education Authority (EA). The EA has advised that it has taken a number of actions at an operational level to support schools in addressing short-term and emergency gaps in Classroom Assistant cover, including the establishment of a relief register for all grades of Classroom Assistant.  When a school has a need for emergency cover for a Classroom Assistant post, the EA's Emergency Resourcing team works with the school to help them secure emergency cover. At a strategic level, the EA is in the process of establishing a project to review the current employment model for Classroom Assistants working with pupils with special educational needs or disabilities (SEND) and provide recommendations for strategic change. The primary focus of the review will be on how to optimise the capacity and development of the Classroom Assistant workforce to support pupils with SEND, but the resulting employment model will extend to cover other general assistant roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many people were supported by day centres in each Health and Social Care Trust during the first quarter of 2022; and (ii) how this compares to the first quarter in (a) 2021; (b) 2020; and (c) 2019.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information centrally and it was requested from the Health and Social Care Trusts. Please find information detailed below.Please note that the figures provided from the HSC Trusts are compiled from varying categories of care, and some categories of care are only available for some of the years requested. Belfast HSC Trust and Western HSC Trust provided information for mental health, learning disability, older people’s services and physical and sensory disability categories of care, and Western HSC Trust’s older people’s services information was estimated for 2021 and 2022. Northern HSC Trust provided information for learning disability, and community care (2021 and 2022 only) categories. South Eastern HSC Trust provided adult services information without older people’s services data as this could not be provided, but no further detail on the included categories of care has been provided. Southern HSC Trust did not provide information on the categories of care included in their information.i) Number of people who were supported by day centres in each Health and Social Care Trust during the first quarter of 2022Table 1. Number of attendances at / people who attended day centres in each Health and Social Care Trust from January to March 2020January – March 2020Number of attendances at day centres1Belfast41,747Western32,760Number of people who attended day centresNorthern3809South Eastern2525Southern866Source: Health and Social Care Trusts1 Belfast and Western HSC Trusts provided the number of attendances at day centres.2 Older People’s day centre information could not be included in the S

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health at what capacity day services are currently operating in each Health and Social Care Trust, in percentage terms.
answer_text: Data on day care, short breaks and transport is provided on the Department’s website and is updated on a monthly basis. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesTo improve the level of provision my officials have worked with the Public Health Agency to issue updated guidance, which will enable Trusts to better balance Covid-19 considerations with wider risks to the mental health and wellbeing of service users, families and carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the local producers license; and when local producers can apply for a license through the courts.
answer_text: Section 10 of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act 2021 (‘the Act’), which allows local producers of alcoholic drinks to apply to the courts for a licence to sell alcohol for consumption off the premises, came into effect on 6 April 2022. The first local producer’s licence was granted on 25 April.Section 11 of the Act, which allows licensed local producers to apply to the courts for a suitability order and authorisation for consumption in the premises, will commence on 1 June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action he has taken to develop a Register of Carers.
answer_text: As you will be aware, I have previously expressed my wish to examine the possibility of developing a more robust central carers register when pressures allow. Carers registers are maintained by the Health and Social Care Trusts, and serve primarily as a means of sharing information and advice. However, they rely heavily on self-referral and do not therefore provide any true reflection of the actual number of carers in Northern Ireland. The consultation on the Reform of Adult Social care includes proposals to develop and maintain a voluntary central register of carers with the aim of supporting improved identification and data availability of carers. I look forward to seeing views on this as the consultation proceeds.  There are undoubtedly improvements to be made and a more robust list could facilitate the provision of support to carers. We should not however underestimate the challenges of developing, and maintaining, such a comprehensive register and it is likely to be a long term piece of work that would require sustained attention and additional resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the independent report on the Welfare Mitigation Review will be published.
answer_text: The independent Advisory Panel has provided its final report on the Review of Welfare Mitigations to my Department and this will be published at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the social inclusion strategies will be published; and why they have not yet been published.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s social inclusion strategies. 		The drafting of the strategies is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. I am committed to the delivery of meaningful strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what promotional activities are planned to ensure that claimants are aware of the discretionary support non-repayable grant.
answer_text: An entirely redesigned Finance Support NI Direct campaign page has been developed using stakeholder feedback, enabling users to obtain further information on all aspects of Discretionary Support and Social Fund.  The campaign page went live in February 2022 and people visiting the page can make an online claim to Discretionary Support or request a Social Fund form to be issued.  The page has seen a significant rise in hits since its redesign.Awareness sessions have also been conducted with representatives from Housing Executive and their Homelessness Local Area Group which includes many third party organisations as well as people from statutory organisations.  Universal Credit journals have been updated with a reminder on how to claim Discretionary Support and the Universal Credit Contingency Fund grant if claimants are in need of short term financial support. An advertising campaign was recently launched, between 28 February and 31 March 2022, which specifically targeted older people aged 65 and above, promoting awareness of Discretionary Support and how to apply.  Promotion of the Discretionary Support scheme will continue to ensure those in need are fully aware of the help available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why Health and Social Care Trusts have yet to fully reinstate respite and day centre provision for people with disabilities; and what action he is taking to ensure the resumption of full provision of services for people with disabilities.
answer_text: In February 2022, the Chief Social Work Officer wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to remobilise respite services. To date, significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19. The latest position reported by Trusts shows that the majority have increased availability in adult day care to at least 70% of pre-pandemic capacity. However, this has not been reflected in the volume of attendances as a number of service users have been unable to attend due to health protection guidance that outlines that close contacts should not attend a Health & Social Care setting for a period of 10 days. Short Breaks provision across statutory health & social care settings has significantly increased. However, overall capacity has reduced as a number of respite beds available in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. Transport continues to be provided on a reduced basis as there is limited scope to increase capacity while adhering to 1m physical distancing guidelines, aligned to recommendations by the World Health Organisation.While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restore to pre-pandemic levels. Therefore, my officials have written to the PHA asking them to commence an urgent formal review of the Pathway and associated health protection guidelines. Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website in June 2022. I have clearly outlined that this data will be collected and published on a regular basis to monitor remobilisation against rebuild timeframes. Furthermore, I will commit to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts. It is regrettable that services have yet to be fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity and I sincerely apologise to those families that continue to receive a partial service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many children will not be provided with classroom assistance, as assigned to them as part of their statement, broken down by primary and post-primary provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) may make an allocation of additional adult assistance hours to a school to assist staff in meeting the objectives outlined in Part 3 of a child’s Statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN). The recruitment of SEN staff within schools is, however, the responsibility of individual Boards of Governors.  Neither the Department nor the EA, are therefore able to provide this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the collapsing wall at Portaferry Integrated Primary School has not been fixed.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Maintenance Service have confirmed that the stone wall remains sectioned off for safety reasons. Commencement of remedial works on site will be subject to legal agreement between the EA and the adjacent landowner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what provision will be made for children who are unable to get to school to sit their exams as a result of a public transport strike.
answer_text: I have written to the Minister for Infrastructure highlighting the significant impact that a public transport strike will cause to school transport services and particularly the potential disruption for pupils taking public examinations.Whilst potential contingencies are limited by the nature of such a strike, my Department has developed a scheme whereby the Education Authority (EA) will provide financial support to schools to mitigate costs incurred in providing alternative transport support to pupils at risk of missing public exams due to industrial action.  Schools were notified of the scheme in advance of the industrial action that was scheduled to commence on 17th May 2022.
